topics.title: Definition and Significance
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT635 - MACHINE LEARNING FOR CPS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Definition and Significance

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Aligned)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Machine Learning (ML)** is a sub-field of Artificial Intelligence (AI) that develops algorithms and statistical models enabling computer systems to **learn patterns from data**, **generalize to unseen inputs**, and **improve their performance on a defined task** over time — *without being explicitly programmed for every scenario*.

When this paradigm is applied within the context of **Cyber-Physical Systems (CPS)** — systems that tightly integrate computation, networking, and physical processes — ML becomes the *cognitive engine* that allows physical systems to perceive, reason, predict, and adapt autonomously.

### 1.1.1 The Two Foundational Definitions You MUST Memorise

**Arthur Samuel (1959) — Pioneering / Informal Definition:**
> *"Machine Learning is the field of study that gives computers the ability to learn without being explicitly programmed."*

**Tom Mitchell (1997) — Textbook / Formal Definition (EXAM FAVOURITE):**
> *"A computer program is said to learn from experience **E** with respect to some class of tasks **T** and performance measure **P**, if its performance at tasks in T, as measured by P, improves with experience E."*

This is expressed mathematically as a mapping relation:

$$
\text{Performance}(T, P) \;\propto\; f(E)
$$

In simple words — as **experience (data)** grows, **performance on the task** must measurably improve.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** A 3-mark question is frequently asked asking you to *"Explain Tom Mitchell's definition with an example"*. Always cite the **Task (T), Experience (E), Performance (P)** triplet explicitly. Missing any one component = partial marks lost.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition

Imagine you are teaching a **young child** to recognise a **cat** in photographs.

- **Traditional Programming Approach:** You sit down and write an exhaustive list of *if–else rules* — *"if it has pointy ears AND whiskers AND a tail AND four legs… then it is a cat."* This breaks the moment you encounter a hairless cat, a sleeping cat, or a cat shown from an unusual angle.

- **Machine Learning Approach:** You instead **show the child 10,000 photographs labelled "cat" or "not-cat."** The child's brain (or the algorithm) *automatically discovers the underlying patterns* — fur texture, ear geometry, eye spacing — and builds its own internal decision rule.

**ML is essentially that child, but with mathematics instead of neurons.**

### 1.2.1 Why is this REVOLUTIONARY for CPS?

A Cyber-Physical System — a self-driving car, a smart power grid, a robotic surgical arm — operates in an environment that is:

1. **Too complex** to model with hand-coded equations (e.g., chaotic traffic)
2. **Too dynamic** to hard-code responses for every scenario
3. **Too data-rich** for rule-based systems to exploit efficiently

ML solves all three by allowing the *physical* part of the CPS to *learn* directly from sensor streams, actuator logs, and historical operational data.

| Dimension | Traditional Program | ML-Enabled CPS |
|---|---|---|
| Knowledge Source | Human-written rules | Patterns in data |
| Adaptability | Static (breaks on novel input) | Dynamic (generalises) |
| Scalability of Logic | Poor (rules explode combinatorially) | Excellent (scales with data) |
| Physical-World Robustness | Brittle | Resilient to noise |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Metric to Remember:** A frequently cited indicator of ML's significance is its ability to deliver performance gains that scale approximately **logarithmically with dataset size** for many algorithms, making data the new *engineering raw material* for CPS design.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 Deconstructing the Mitchell Formal Definition

Mitchell's definition is the *gold-standard* answer expected in KTU viva and ESE questions. It is composed of **three explicit components**, and every legitimate ML problem must have all three defined clearly.

### 2.1.1 The Three Pillars

**1. Task — T**
The specific, well-defined objective the system must accomplish.

$$T \in \{ \text{Classification},\ \text{Regression},\ \text{Clustering},\ \text{Control},\ \text{Anomaly Detection} \}$$

**2. Experience — E**
The data from which the system learns, typically expressed as a dataset:

$$E = \{ (x_1, y_1),\ (x_2, y_2),\ \ldots,\ (x_n, y_n) \}$$

where $x_i$ is an input feature vector and $y_i$ is the corresponding label (supervised case).

**3. Performance Measure — P**
A quantifiable metric evaluating how well the system performs $T$.

$$P : \text{Output} \rightarrow \mathbb{R}, \quad \text{e.g., Accuracy},\ F_1\text{-Score},\ \text{MSE},\ \text{Reward} $$

### 2.1.2 The Logical Flow — Why → How

- **Why** are T, E, P needed? Because without them, the statement *"the program improves"* is ambiguous — improvement *in what?* *with what data?* *measured how?*
- **How** are they used? The learning algorithm ingests experience $E$, optimises its internal parameters $\theta$ to maximise (or minimise) $P$, and is then evaluated on the task $T$.

The implicit objective of any ML system can be summarised as:

$$
\theta^* = \arg\max_{\theta}\ P\bigl( M_\theta,\ T,\ E_{\text{test}} \bigr)
$$

where $M_\theta$ is the model parameterised by $\theta$, and $E_{\text{test}}$ is unseen data.

---

## 2.2 The Significance of Machine Learning in Cyber-Physical Systems

ML is not merely "another tool" — it is the *enabling technology* behind several classes of CPS that would otherwise be infeasible. Its significance is best understood along **four engineering axes**.

### 2.2.1 Axis 1 — Perception (Sense → Understand)

Physical sensors (cameras, LiDAR, accelerometers) produce raw, noisy data. ML converts these signals into *semantic* information: object detection, scene segmentation, fault signatures.

### 2.2.2 Axis 2 — Prediction (Present → Forecast)

CPS must anticipate — predict equipment failure (predictive maintenance), forecast traffic flow, predict power demand. ML models trained on temporal data perform this forecasting.

### 2.2.3 Axis 3 — Decision & Control (Understand → Act)

Reinforcement learning and imitation learning enable autonomous agents (drones, robots, vehicles) to derive optimal control policies from interaction data rather than hand-crafted controllers.

### 2.2.4 Axis 4 — Adaptation & Resilience (Static → Evolving)

A CPS that learns online can adapt to *drift* — changes in environment, wear-and-tear of components, evolving threat landscapes — without manual re-engineering.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Reality Check:** In industrial CPS deployments, ML models are typically retrained on cycles of **24 hours to 7 days**, balancing model freshness against computational cost. This is known as the **Model Refresh Interval** and is a frequent interview topic.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet — Definition & Significance

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates every exam-essential term. Memorise the *meaning*, *role*, and *context* of each.

| # | Term | Formal Expression / Symbol | Meaning | Role in CPS |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Task | $T$ | The objective the system must achieve | Defines *what* the CPS must do |
| 2 | Experience | $E = \{(x_i, y_i)\}_{i=1}^n$ | The dataset used for training | Sensor/operational logs |
| 3 | Performance Measure | $P$ | Quantitative metric of success | Accuracy, MSE, reward |
| 4 | Model Parameters | $\theta$ | Internal learnable variables of the model | Weights of a neural net |
| 5 | Generalisation | Performance on $E_{\text{test}} \neq E_{\text{train}}$ | Ability to handle unseen inputs | Critical for real-world CPS |
| 6 | Inductive Bias | $\mathcal{H}$ (hypothesis class) | Assumptions guiding learning | Determines model suitability |
| 7 | Training Error | $E_{\text{train}}$ | Loss on training data | Diagnoses underfitting |
| 8 | Generalisation Gap | $\vert E_{\text{test}} - E_{\text{train}} \vert$ | Difference between test and train error | Diagnoses overfitting |
| 9 | Data Drift | $\Delta P_{\text{data}}(X, t)$ | Change in data distribution over time | Triggers model retraining |
| 10 | Online Learning | $\theta_{t+1} = f(\theta_t, x_t)$ | Continuous update from streaming data | Enables real-time CPS adaptation |

> [!WARNING]
> **LaTeX Safety:** Notice the absolute value notation $\vert \cdot \vert$ — never use the literal pipe symbol $\vert$ inside markdown table cells, as it breaks table parsing. This is a common typographical pitfall in KTU digital submissions.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Analysis: From Traditional Programs to Learning Systems

## 3.1 The Paradigm Shift — A Mathematical Walkthrough

The clearest way to grasp the significance of ML is to **contrast** it mathematically with traditional programming.

### 3.1.1 Traditional Programming Paradigm

In classical software engineering, the programmer provides:

- The **program** (logic, rules, algorithms)
- The **input data**

…and the computer deterministically produces the **output**.

$$
\text{Program} : ( \text{Program Logic},\ \text{Input Data} ) \longrightarrow \text{Output}
$$

Expressed more formally:

$$
\text{Output} \;=\; f_{\text{hand-coded}}(\text{Input Data})
$$

where $f_{\text{hand-coded}}$ is a function explicitly written by a human engineer.

### 3.1.2 Machine Learning Paradigm

In ML, the roles are inverted. The system provides:

- The **input–output pairs** (experience $E$)
- A **hypothesis space** $\mathcal{H}$ of candidate functions

…and the algorithm *learns* the program.

$$
\text{ML Algorithm} : ( \text{Input-Output Pairs},\ \mathcal{H} ) \longrightarrow \text{Learned Function } \hat{f}
$$

Formally:

$$
\hat{f} \;=\; \arg\min_{f \in \mathcal{H}}\ \mathcal{L}\bigl( f(x_i),\ y_i \bigr)
$$

where $\mathcal{L}$ is the loss function measuring how badly $f$'s predictions deviate from the true labels $y_i$.

### 3.1.3 Side-by-Side Logical Comparison

| Aspect | Traditional Programming | Machine Learning |
|---|---|---|
| What human supplies | Logic + Rules | Data + Hypothesis class |
| What machine returns | Output | Program (parameters $\theta$) |
| Knowledge representation | Explicit (if–else, equations) | Implicit (learned weights) |
| Generalisation | Limited to programmed rules | Inferred from data |
| Engineering cost | High for complex domains | High data cost, low coding cost |

This inversion is **the single most important conceptual takeaway** from Module 1.

---

## 3.2 Worked Illustrative Example — Spam Detection

### 3.2.1 Traditional Approach (Rule-Based)

Engineers hand-craft rules:

- If the email contains the word **"lottery"** → mark as spam.
- If the sender is in the user's **blocklist** → mark as spam.
- If the subject has more than 5 exclamation marks → mark as spam.

This produces:

$$
y_{\text{spam}} \;=\; \mathbb{1}\!\bigl[ \text{rule}_1 \lor \text{rule}_2 \lor \text{rule}_3 \bigr]
$$

**Drawback:** Spammers easily evade these rules with misspellings ("l0ttery"), image-based text, or novel phrasing.

### 3.2.2 Machine Learning Approach

A dataset $E$ of labelled emails is provided:

$$
E \;=\; \bigl\{ (x^{(1)}, y^{(1)}),\ (x^{(2)}, y^{(2)}),\ \ldots,\ (x^{(n)}, y^{(n)}) \bigr\}
$$

where $x^{(i)}$ is a feature vector (bag-of-words, embeddings, sender metadata) and $y^{(i)} \in \{0, 1\}$ indicates ham or spam.

A classifier — say, logistic regression — is trained:

$$
\hat{y} \;=\; \sigma\!\bigl( \theta^{\top} x \bigr) \;=\; \frac{1}{1 + e^{-\theta^{\top} x}}
$$

The parameters $\theta$ are learned by minimising binary cross-entropy loss:

$$
\mathcal{L}(\theta) \;=\; -\frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} \Bigl[ y^{(i)} \log \hat{y}^{(i)} + \bigl(1 - y^{(i)}\bigr) \log \bigl(1 - \hat{y}^{(i)}\bigr) \Bigr]
$$

The learned $\theta$ implicitly encodes thousands of nuanced patterns — far beyond any hand-written rule list.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mitchell's Triplet Filled In:**
> - **Task (T):** Classify incoming email as spam or ham.
> - **Experience (E):** A corpus of $n$ pre-labelled historical emails.
> - **Performance (P):** Classification accuracy (or $F_1$-score) on a held-out test set.
>
> This triplet structure is what you write in a 7-mark KTU answer whenever asked for *"Explain ML with an example"*.

---

## 3.3 Significance in CPS — Quantitative Justification

The significance of ML for CPS is not merely philosophical — it is measurable. Consider three representative engineering scenarios:

**Scenario A — Predictive Maintenance in Manufacturing CPS**

A rotating machine has $m$ sensors producing readings at frequency $f$. Over $T$ operational days, the volume of data accumulated is:

$$
D \;=\; m \cdot f \cdot 86400 \cdot T \quad \text{(samples)}
$$

For $m = 10$, $f = 1\,\text{kHz}$, $T = 30$ days:

$$
D \;=\; 10 \times 1000 \times 86400 \times 30 \;=\; 2.592 \times 10^{10} \text{ samples}
$$

No human can write rules over 25 *billion* data points. ML scales effortlessly — this is the essence of its significance.

**Scenario B — Autonomous Vehicle Perception**

An autonomous car's camera produces approximately $30$ frames/second. Over a 1-hour drive:

$$
D_{\text{cam}} \;=\; 30 \times 3600 \;=\; 108{,}000 \text{ frames}
$$

ML models (CNNs, transformers) process these frames and recognise pedestrians, signs, lanes — a task *literally impossible* to encode by rule.

**Scenario C — Smart Grid Load Forecasting**

A regional grid serving $N$ households must predict next-hour load $L_{t+1}$ from historical demand and weather. ML regressors learn the mapping:

$$
L_{t+1} \;\approx\; g\bigl( L_t,\ L_{t-1},\ \ldots,\ T_t,\ H_t,\ W_t \bigr)
$$

where $T$, $H$, $W$ are temperature, humidity, and wind speed respectively. Forecasting errors are reduced by typically **15–40 %** compared to statistical baselines — a direct quantification of ML's significance.

---

## 3.4 The Historical Evolution — A Timeline of Significance

| Year | Milestone | Why it Mattered |
|---|---|---|
| **1950** | Turing's *Imitation Game* paper | Proposed that machines could *learn* |
| **1959** | Arthur Samuel coins *"Machine Learning"* | Formalised the field |
| **1980s** | Expert systems & decision trees | First practical ML deployments |
| **1997** | Tom Mitchell publishes textbook | Established the canonical T-E-P definition |
| **2006** | Deep Learning renaissance (Hinton) | Enabled hierarchical feature learning |
| **2012** | AlexNet wins ImageNet | Triggered the modern AI revolution |
| **2015+** | ML integrated into CPS (Industry 4.0) | Smart factories, autonomous systems |
| **2020+** | Foundation models & edge ML | ML embedded into CPS edge devices |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Frequently Asked:** *"Discuss the evolution and significance of machine learning."* Always anchor your answer with **Samuel (1959)** and **Mitchell (1997)** — these are the two dates examiners expect.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — ML Pipeline in a Cyber-Physical System

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates how machine learning slots into a CPS architecture. Data flows from the physical world, through ML cognitive layers, and back as control actuation.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PHYS[Physical World]
        sensorA[Sensor 1 Temperature]
        sensorB[Sensor 2 Vibration]
        sensorC[Sensor 3 Vision Camera]
    end

    subgraph CLOUD[Edge Gateway and Cloud Layer]
        preproc[Data Preprocessing and Feature Extraction]
        datastore[(Time Series and Image Database)]
        mltrain[ML Model Training Engine]
        mlmodel[Trained ML Model theta]
    end

    subgraph CPS_DEC[Decision and Control Layer]
        infer[Real Time Inference Module]
        policy[Control Policy Output]
    end

    subgraph ACT[Actuator and Physical Response]
        motor[Motor Actuator]
        valve[Hydraulic Valve]
        alert[Alarm and HMI Display]
    end

    sensorA --> preproc
    sensorB --> preproc
    sensorC --> preproc
    preproc --> datastore
    datastore --> mltrain
    mltrain --> mlmodel
    mlmodel --> infer
    infer --> policy
    policy --> motor
    policy --> valve
    policy --> alert

    motor -. Feedback .- sensorB
    valve -. Feedback .- sensorA
    alert -. Feedback .- sensorC
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read this diagram (for exam writing):**
> - **Left block (Physical World):** Sensors capture signals.
> - **Middle block (Cloud/Edge):** ML training happens offline on stored data.
> - **Third block (Decision):** Inference runs online on live data.
> - **Right block (Actuators):** Control actions act on the physical world.
> - **Dotted feedback loops:** Closing the cyber-physical control loop.

---

## 4.2 Concept Map — The Three Pillars of Mitchell's Definition

This concept map shows the inseparable relationship between Task, Experience, and Performance — the cornerstone of any rigorous ML definition.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    ML[Machine Learning System]
    T[Task T]
    E[Experience E Dataset]
    P[Performance Measure P]

    ML --> T
    ML --> E
    ML --> P

    T --> T1[Classification]
    T --> T2[Regression]
    T --> T3[Clustering]
    T --> T4[Control]

    E --> E1[Labelled Samples]
    E --> E2[Unlabelled Samples]
    E --> E3[Reward Signals]

    P --> P1[Accuracy and F1 Score]
    P --> P2[Mean Squared Error]
    P --> P3[Cumulative Reward]

    T1 --> P1
    T2 --> P2
    T3 --> P1
    T4 --> P3

    E1 --> T1
    E1 --> T2
    E2 --> T3
    E3 --> T4
```

---

## 4.3 Comparative Flow — Traditional Programming vs Machine Learning

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph TRAD[Traditional Programming]
        A1[Human Engineer] --> A2[Hand Coded Rules and Program Logic]
        A2 --> A3[Input Data]
        A3 --> A4[Deterministic Output]
    end

    subgraph MLPRG[Machine Learning Paradigm]
        B1[Input Data] --> B2[Input Output Pairs Experience]
        B2 --> B3[Learning Algorithm]
        B3 --> B4[Learned Function or Program]
    end

    A4 -.Contrast.- B4
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Visual Takeaway:** Notice how in the **traditional path**, the *program* is the input and the *output* is the answer; in the **ML path**, the *input–output pairs* are the input and the *program itself* is the answer. This inversion is the *defining significance* of ML.

---

## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture — ML Significance in CPS

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    CPS[Cyber Physical System]
    SIGN[Significance of ML in CPS]

    CPS --> PERC[Perception Layer]
    CPS --> PRED[Prediction Layer]
    CPS --> CTRL[Decision and Control Layer]
    CPS --> ADAP[Adaptation Layer]

    PERC --> PERC1[Object Detection from Camera Feeds]
    PERC --> PERC2[Acoustic Anomaly Classification]
    PERC --> PERC3[Sensor Fusion and State Estimation]

    PRED --> PRED1[Remaining Useful Life Estimation]
    PRED --> PRED2[Load and Traffic Forecasting]
    PRED --> PRED3[Failure Probability Prediction]

    CTRL --> CTRL1[Reinforcement Learning Policies]
    CTRL --> CTRL2[Imitation Learning from Expert Demos]
    CTRL --> CTRL3[Model Predictive Control via Learned Models]

    ADAP --> ADAP1[Online Learning from Drift]
    ADAP --> ADAP2[Federated Learning Across CPS Nodes]
    ADAP --> ADAP3[Transfer Learning Across CPS Domains]
```

This block diagram provides a **taxonomy of ML significance** — every block in the right column is a concrete engineering use-case that justifies why ML is indispensable to modern CPS.

---

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define Machine Learning. State Arthur Samuel's definition.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Machine Learning is a branch of Artificial Intelligence that enables computers to learn from data and improve their performance on a given task over time, without being explicitly programmed.

In **1959**, Arthur Samuel, a pioneer in the field of AI, defined Machine Learning as:

> *"Machine Learning is the field of study that gives computers the ability to learn without being explicitly programmed."*

This definition highlights the *self-improving* nature of ML systems — they derive knowledge from data rather than relying on hand-coded instructions.

*(Word count target for 3-mark answer: ~80–100 words.)*

---

### Question 2 — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Explain Tom Mitchell's formal definition of Machine Learning. Identify the three components.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Tom Mitchell (1997) gave the canonical, formal definition:

> *"A computer program is said to learn from experience E with respect to some class of tasks T and performance measure P, if its performance at tasks in T, as measured by P, improves with experience E."*

The three explicit components are:

1. **Task (T):** The well-defined objective (e.g., classification, regression).
2. **Experience (E):** The dataset from which the system learns.
3. **Performance Measure (P):** The quantitative metric evaluating success (e.g., accuracy, error).

All three components must be specified for any legitimate ML problem.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 / Model Paper]`

**a)** *(7 Marks)* **Discuss in detail the significance of Machine Learning in the context of Cyber-Physical Systems (CPS). Illustrate with at least three engineering examples.**

**Model Answer:**

Machine Learning (ML) has emerged as the *cognitive backbone* of modern Cyber-Physical Systems (CPS). A CPS integrates sensing, computation, networking, and physical actuation into a closed-loop system. The significance of ML in CPS can be understood along four engineering axes:

**(i) Perception — Sense → Understand**
CPS sensors generate vast, noisy raw signals. ML models — particularly deep convolutional networks — translate these into semantically meaningful information such as object detection in autonomous vehicles, defect identification in smart manufacturing, and acoustic anomaly detection in power plants.

**(ii) Prediction — Present → Forecast**
Industrial CPS rely on predictive analytics. ML regressors and time-series models forecast equipment Remaining Useful Life (RUL), enabling predictive maintenance that reduces unplanned downtime by **30–50 %**.

**(iii) Decision & Control — Understand → Act**
Reinforcement Learning (RL) enables autonomous agents (drones, robots, vehicles) to derive optimal control policies by interacting with their environment. RL-based controllers have outperformed hand-tuned PID controllers in several benchmarks.

**(iv) Adaptation & Resilience — Static → Evolving**
Online learning allows CPS to adapt to *data drift* — changes in environment, wear, or operating conditions — without manual re-engineering, dramatically extending operational lifespans.

**Three Engineering Examples:**

1. **Autonomous Vehicles (Transportation CPS):** ML models perform real-time perception (pedestrian detection), prediction (trajectory forecasting), and planning (lane-change decisions), enabling SAE Level-4 autonomy.
2. **Smart Grids (Energy CPS):** ML regressors forecast electricity demand and detect grid anomalies, optimising load balancing and preventing cascading blackouts.
3. **Industry 4.0 Manufacturing CPS:** ML-enabled predictive maintenance monitors machine health, predicts failures weeks in advance, and schedules repairs proactively.

In summary, ML transforms CPS from *pre-programmed* machines into *adaptive, intelligent* systems, representing a paradigm shift in how physical engineered systems are designed and operated.

---

**b)** *(7 Marks)* **Compare and contrast traditional programming with Machine Learning using suitable equations and an example.**

**Model Answer:**

**Traditional Programming Paradigm:**
A human engineer supplies the *program logic* and the *input data*; the computer deterministically produces the *output*.

$$
\text{Output} \;=\; f_{\text{hand-coded}}(\text{Input Data})
$$

The function $f_{\text{hand-coded}}$ is explicitly written by the programmer using rules, conditional statements, and algorithms.

**Machine Learning Paradigm:**
The system is supplied with *input–output pairs* (experience) and a *hypothesis class* $\mathcal{H}$. The learning algorithm discovers the optimal function $\hat{f}$.

$$
\hat{f} \;=\; \arg\min_{f \in \mathcal{H}}\ \mathcal{L}\!\bigl( f(x_i),\ y_i \bigr)
$$

where $\mathcal{L}$ is a loss function measuring prediction error.

**Comparative Table:**

| Aspect | Traditional Programming | Machine Learning |
|---|---|---|
| Knowledge Source | Human-coded rules | Patterns in data |
| Output of Process | Predictions / decisions | A learned function / program |
| Generalisation | Limited to programmed cases | Inferred from data |
| Engineering Effort | High in rule-definition | High in data preparation |
| Robustness to Novel Inputs | Poor | Generally good |

**Illustrative Example — Handwritten Digit Recognition:**

- *Traditional Approach:* Engineer writes explicit geometric rules to identify digits (e.g., "if a closed loop exists at the bottom and a vertical line at the top, classify as 9"). Such rules fail for cursive or noisy handwriting.

- *ML Approach:* A Convolutional Neural Network is trained on $60{,}000$ labelled MNIST digit images. The network learns hierarchical features (edges → curves → digit shapes) and achieves $> 99\,\%$ accuracy on unseen test images — vastly outperforming any rule-based system.

Thus, ML inverts the traditional paradigm: instead of writing the program, we let the program *emerge* from data.

---

### Question B — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 / Model Paper]` *(ALTERNATIVE)*

**a)** *(7 Marks)* **Explain Tom Mitchell's definition of Machine Learning in detail. Apply it to the problem of *predictive maintenance in a manufacturing CPS* by explicitly identifying the Task (T), Experience (E), and Performance Measure (P).**

**Model Answer:**

Tom Mitchell's formal definition states:

> *"A computer program is said to learn from experience E with respect to some class of tasks T and performance measure P, if its performance at tasks in T, as measured by P, improves with experience E."*

This definition is foundational because it requires explicit specification of three components, making any ML claim rigorously verifiable.

**Application — Predictive Maintenance in a Manufacturing CPS:**

**1. Task (T):**
The objective is to predict whether a rotating machine (e.g., an industrial motor) will fail within the next $k$ operational days. Formally:

$$
T : x_{\text{sensor}} \;\longmapsto\; y \in \{0, 1\}
$$

where $y = 1$ indicates impending failure (binary classification).

**2. Experience (E):**
The training dataset consists of historical sensor logs and maintenance records:

$$
E \;=\; \Bigl\{ \bigl( x^{(1)}, y^{(1)} \bigr),\ \bigl( x^{(2)}, y^{(2)} \bigr),\ \ldots,\ \bigl( x^{(n)}, y^{(n)} \bigr) \Bigr\}
$$

Each $x^{(i)} \in \mathbb{R}^{m}$ is a feature vector aggregating $m$ sensor readings — temperature, vibration RMS, acoustic emission, current draw — over a sliding window. Each $y^{(i)}$ is the labelled outcome (failure / no failure) from historical records.

**3. Performance Measure (P):**
The model's quality is evaluated using the $F_1$-score on a held-out test set, since failure events are rare and accuracy alone would be misleading:

$$
F_1 \;=\; 2 \cdot \frac{\text{Precision} \cdot \text{Recall}}{\text{Precision} + \text{Recall}}
$$

**How Learning Occurs:**
A classifier (e.g., Random Forest or Gradient Boosted Trees) is trained by minimising a loss function:

$$
\theta^* \;=\; \arg\min_{\theta}\ \mathcal{L}(\theta;\ E)
$$

As $n$ — the number of accumulated historical samples — grows, the classifier's $F_1$-score on $E_{\text{test}}$ improves, satisfying Mitchell's criterion.

**Engineering Impact:** Predictive maintenance ML models typically achieve $F_1$-scores of **0.85–0.95**, reducing unplanned downtime by up to **50 %** and yielding substantial cost savings in industrial CPS.

---

**b)** *(7 Marks)* **"Machine Learning has transformed Cyber-Physical Systems from pre-programmed machines into adaptive, intelligent entities." Critically discuss this statement with reference to four axes of significance.**

**Model Answer:**

This statement captures the *paradigm shift* that ML has brought to CPS engineering. A CPS — by classical definition — combines sensors, computation, networks, and actuators. Pre-ML CPS were *pre-programmed*: every behaviour was a deterministic consequence of engineered rules. Modern ML-enabled CPS are *adaptive* and *intelligent*. This transformation can be examined along **four axes of significance**:

**Axis 1 — From Sensing to Semantic Perception:**
Pre-ML CPS performed *signal processing* — low-pass filtering, FFT-based feature extraction. ML-enabled CPS perform *semantic perception* — recognising *what* the signal means (an approaching pedestrian, a faulty bearing). This shift is enabled by deep learning architectures such as CNNs and Transformers.

**Axis 2 — From Reactive to Predictive Operation:**
Pre-ML CPS respond *after* events (e.g., a threshold-triggered shutdown). ML-enabled CPS *predict* events before they occur. Predictive maintenance models forecast equipment failures weeks in advance, transforming operational economics.

**Axis 3 — From Hand-Tuned Control to Learned Control:**
Classical control theory relies on hand-tuned PID or MPC controllers. ML, especially Reinforcement Learning, enables controllers that are *learned* from interaction. RL controllers have demonstrated superhuman performance in robotic manipulation, drone navigation, and game-playing — domains where hand-coding is infeasible.

**Axis 4 — From Static Deployment to Continuous Adaptation:**
Pre-ML CPS degrade in performance as their environment drifts. ML-enabled CPS *adapt* via online learning, federated learning, and transfer learning — continually updating their internal models from fresh data.

**Critical Reflection:**
Despite its transformative significance, ML in CPS is *not* without limitations. Challenges include:
- **Safety verification** of learned models (regulatory and ethical concerns)
- **Data requirements** (many CPS have sparse failure data)
- **Real-time constraints** (inference latency in safety-critical loops)
- **Explainability** (black-box models are difficult to certify)

Nevertheless, the trajectory is clear — ML is steadily becoming the *core cognitive layer* of CPS, and engineering curricula must reflect this shift. The statement is therefore **substantially correct**, with the caveat that responsible deployment requires complementary advances in safety, robustness, and interpretability.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students LOSE MARKS on this topic:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the Mitchell Triplet (T, E, P):** Many students quote Mitchell's definition but, when asked for an *example*, fail to explicitly label each component. This alone can cost **2–3 marks out of 7**.
> 2. **Confusing Samuel and Mitchell:** Samuel (1959) gave the *informal* definition; Mitchell (1997) gave the *formal* one. Examiners explicitly test this distinction.
> 3. **Generic answers on "Significance":** Avoid vague statements like *"ML is important because it is useful."* Always anchor significance in **concrete CPS applications** — autonomous vehicles, smart grids, Industry 4.0, healthcare CPS, etc.
> 4. **Ignoring the Paradigm Inversion:** When contrasting ML with traditional programming, students often forget that ML *inverts* the data/program roles. This is a **sure-shot 7-mark question** — practice the comparison table.
> 5. **Mathematical Gaps:** Even for definition questions, examiners award partial credit for *any* equation. Always include at least one formal expression, e.g., the loss minimisation formulation or the Mitchell triplet notation.
> 6. **Not Connecting to CPS:** This is a *Machine Learning for CPS* course, not a generic ML course. Any answer that fails to mention *Cyber-Physical Systems*, *sensors*, *actuators*, or *physical-world deployment* will be marked down.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — "Definition and Significance"**

- **Two definitions to memorise verbatim:** Samuel (1959) — *"learn without being explicitly programmed"*; Mitchell (1997) — *"improves at task T with experience E as measured by P"*.
- **Mitchell's triplet:** T (Task) + E (Experience) + P (Performance Measure). All three MUST be specified.
- **Paradigm inversion:** Traditional program = Logic + Data → Output. ML = Input-Output pairs → Learned Program.
- **Key equation:** $\theta^* = \arg\min_{\theta} \mathcal{L}(\theta; E)$ — model parameters are learned by minimising loss.
- **Four axes of ML significance in CPS:** Perception, Prediction, Decision/Control, Adaptation.
- **Three canonical CPS examples:** Autonomous vehicles, Smart grids, Industry 4.0 manufacturing.
- **Three primary ML task types:** Classification, Regression, Clustering.
- **Historical anchors:** Samuel (1959), Mitchell (1997), Deep Learning (2006/2012), CPS integration (2015+).
- **Performance metrics to know:** Accuracy, $F_1$-Score, MSE, Cumulative Reward (RL).
- **Concept of generalisation:** Performance on *unseen* data is the true measure of ML success, not training accuracy.
- **Generalisation gap:** $\vert E_{\text{test}} - E_{\text{train}} \vert$ — a small gap indicates a well-generalised model.
- **CPS-specific concerns:** Real-time latency, safety certification, data sparsity for rare failure events, model interpretability.
- **Always connect ML to CPS in your answers:** This course is *Machine Learning for CPS* — never answer generically.
- **Edge ML trend:** Modern CPS increasingly deploy ML inference at the *edge* (on-device) rather than in the cloud, to meet latency and privacy constraints.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types of Machine Learning: Supervised
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT635 - MACHINE LEARNING FOR CPS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Types of Machine Learning: Supervised

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

**Supervised Learning** is a fundamental paradigm of Machine Learning (ML) in which an artificial intelligence agent learns a *mapping function* $f: \mathcal{X} \rightarrow \mathcal{Y}$ from a labeled training dataset $\mathcal{D} = \{(\mathbf{x}_i, y_i)\}_{i=1}^{N}$, where each input vector $\mathbf{x}_i \in \mathbb{R}^d$ is explicitly paired with a corresponding target output $y_i$.

The objective is to learn a hypothesis $h_{\theta}(\mathbf{x})$ parameterized by a weight vector $\boldsymbol{\theta}$ such that the predicted output $\hat{y} = h_{\theta}(\mathbf{x})$ minimizes a predefined **loss function** $\mathcal{L}(\hat{y}, y)$ over the dataset. Once trained, the model is expected to **generalize** to previously unseen inputs drawn from the same underlying data distribution $P(\mathbf{x}, y)$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Definition:** Supervised learning is the learning task of inferring a function from **labeled training data** consisting of a set of *training examples*, where each example is a pair consisting of an input object and a desired output value (a supervisory signal).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition

Imagine you are teaching a small child to recognize fruits.

- You show the child an apple and say: *"This is an apple."*
- You show a banana and say: *"This is a banana."*
- You show many examples — mango, grapes, orange — and **always** tell the child the correct name.

After seeing hundreds of such examples, the child builds a mental model. Now, when you show a fruit the child has **never seen before**, it can correctly identify it as "apple" or "banana" based on learned features (color, shape, size).

**That is exactly Supervised Learning.**
- The **fruit** = input feature vector $\mathbf{x}$
- The **name you tell** = label $y$ (the "supervisor")
- The **child's brain** = the model $h_{\theta}(\mathbf{x})$
- The **process of learning** = minimizing prediction errors

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU Students:** The word *"supervised"* comes from the idea that the learning algorithm is given the *correct answer* (the label) for every training example, just as a student works under the supervision of a teacher who corrects mistakes.

### 1.3 Critical Distinctions in the Supervised Framework

| Term | Meaning | Notation |
|---|---|---|
| **Feature Vector** | The measurable input variables describing an instance | $\mathbf{x} = (x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_d)$ |
| **Label / Target** | The ground-truth output provided by the supervisor | $y$ |
| **Hypothesis** | The learned function mapping inputs to predictions | $h_{\theta}(\mathbf{x})$ |
| **Loss Function** | Numerical penalty for incorrect predictions | $\mathcal{L}(\hat{y}, y)$ |
| **Generalization** | Ability to perform well on unseen test data | Low test error |
| **Training Set** | Labeled data used to fit the model | $\mathcal{D}_{train}$ |
| **Test Set** | Held-out labeled data used to evaluate the model | $\mathcal{D}_{test}$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cybersecurity / CPS Context (PECPT635):** In Cyber-Physical Systems (CPS), supervised learning is used for *intrusion detection* (classifying network traffic as benign/malicious), *fault diagnosis* (predicting sensor failures), and *anomaly classification* (labeling physical system states as normal/abnormal).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Operational Pipeline of Supervised Learning (Step-by-Step Logic)

A supervised learning system follows a deterministic operational pipeline. Understanding each phase is **mandatory** for KTU board questions.

1. **Data Acquisition & Labeling**
   - *Why:* The supervisor must provide the "ground truth." Without labels, the problem becomes unsupervised.
   - *How:* Domain experts annotate raw data (e.g., radiologists label X-ray images as "tumor / no tumor").

2. **Feature Engineering / Representation**
   - *Why:* Raw data (pixels, voltage readings) must be transformed into numerical features.
   - *How:* Extract $d$-dimensional feature vectors $\mathbf{x}_i \in \mathbb{R}^d$ via scaling, encoding, or domain-specific transformations.

3. **Model Selection**
   - *Why:* The hypothesis class $h_{\theta}$ must be chosen (linear, neural network, decision tree, etc.).
   - *How:* Match model complexity to the dataset size and task type (classification vs. regression).

4. **Loss Function Definition**
   - *Why:* Quantifies the "cost" of the model being wrong.
   - *How:* Pick a differentiable loss suited to the task: MSE for regression, Cross-Entropy for classification.

5. **Optimization (Training)**
   - *Why:* Find parameters $\boldsymbol{\theta}^*$ that minimize expected loss.
   - *How:* Iteratively update $\boldsymbol{\theta}$ using **Gradient Descent**:
   
   $$\boldsymbol{\theta}_{t+1} = \boldsymbol{\theta}_t - \eta \nabla_{\boldsymbol{\theta}} \mathcal{L}(\boldsymbol{\theta}_t)$$
   
   where $\eta$ is the **learning rate** (a hyperparameter) and $\nabla_{\boldsymbol{\theta}} \mathcal{L}$ is the gradient of the loss with respect to parameters.

6. **Evaluation & Generalization Testing**
   - *Why:* Training error ≠ Real-world performance. The model must generalize.
   - *How:* Compute metrics (Accuracy, F1, RMSE) on a held-out test set $\mathcal{D}_{test}$.

### 2.2 The Two Principal Sub-Tasks of Supervised Learning

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield:** Supervised learning splits into exactly **two** canonical problem types. Examiners frequently ask students to differentiate them.

#### A) Classification (Discrete Output)
- The label $y$ belongs to a **finite, discrete** set of classes: $y \in \{1, 2, \ldots, K\}$.
- **Binary Classification:** $K=2$ (e.g., spam vs. not-spam).
- **Multi-class Classification:** $K>2$ (e.g., digit recognition 0-9).
- The model outputs a **probability distribution** over classes, typically via the softmax function:

$$P(y = k \mid \mathbf{x}; \boldsymbol{\theta}) = \frac{e^{\mathbf{w}_k^{\top} \mathbf{x} + b_k}}{\sum_{j=1}^{K} e^{\mathbf{w}_j^{\top} \mathbf{x} + b_j}}$$

#### B) Regression (Continuous Output)
- The label $y$ is a **continuous real-valued** quantity: $y \in \mathbb{R}$.
- Example: predicting house price, temperature, or sensor voltage.
- The simplest model is **Linear Regression**:

$$\hat{y} = h_{\boldsymbol{\theta}}(\mathbf{x}) = \boldsymbol{\theta}^{\top} \mathbf{x} + b = \sum_{j=1}^{d} \theta_j x_j + b$$

where $b$ is the bias term and $\theta_j$ are the feature weights.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table** — these equations appear almost every year in KTU board exams for ML/CPS courses.

| Concept | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Hypothesis (Linear) | $h_{\boldsymbol{\theta}}(\mathbf{x}) = \boldsymbol{\theta}^{\top}\mathbf{x} + b$ | $\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^d$, $\hat{y} \in \mathbb{R}$ |
| Mean Squared Error (MSE) | $J(\boldsymbol{\theta}) = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(h_{\boldsymbol{\theta}}(\mathbf{x}_i) - y_i)^2$ | Unit: square of target unit |
| Mean Absolute Error (MAE) | $\text{MAE} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} \vert h_{\boldsymbol{\theta}}(\mathbf{x}_i) - y_i \vert$ | More robust to outliers |
| Root Mean Squared Error | $\text{RMSE} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(h_{\boldsymbol{\theta}}(\mathbf{x}_i) - y_i)^2}$ | Same unit as target $y$ |
| Binary Cross-Entropy Loss | $\mathcal{L} = -\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}\left[y_i \log(\hat{y}_i) + (1-y_i)\log(1-\hat{y}_i)\right]$ | Used in logistic regression |
| Categorical Cross-Entropy | $\mathcal{L} = -\sum_{i=1}^{N}\sum_{k=1}^{K} y_{i,k}\log(\hat{y}_{i,k})$ | Multi-class extension |
| Sigmoid (Logistic) Function | $\sigma(z) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-z}}$ | Maps $\mathbb{R} \to (0,1)$ |
| Gradient Descent Update | $\boldsymbol{\theta}_{t+1} = \boldsymbol{\theta}_t - \eta \nabla_{\boldsymbol{\theta}} J(\boldsymbol{\theta}_t)$ | $\eta$ = learning rate |
| Accuracy | $\text{Acc} = \frac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN}$ | Range: $[0, 1]$ |
| Precision | $\text{Prec} = \frac{TP}{TP + FP}$ | "Of predicted positives, how many correct?" |
| Recall (Sensitivity) | $\text{Rec} = \frac{TP}{TP + FN}$ | "Of actual positives, how many found?" |
| F1-Score | $F_1 = 2 \cdot \frac{\text{Prec} \cdot \text{Rec}}{\text{Prec} + \text{Rec}}$ | Harmonic mean of P and R |
| Bias-Variance Tradeoff | $\mathbb{E}[(y - \hat{f})^2] = \text{Bias}^2 + \text{Variance} + \sigma^2$ | $\sigma^2$ = irreducible noise |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** In CPS security, Precision-Recall curves are favored over accuracy when the dataset is *imbalanced* (e.g., 99.9% benign traffic vs. 0.1% attacks). High accuracy on imbalanced data is meaningless — a model predicting "always benign" achieves 99.9% accuracy while detecting zero attacks.

### 2.4 Why Supervised Learning? (The "Why" Behind the Method)

- **Theoretically grounded:** Based on Vapnik–Chervonenkis (VC) theory and Statistical Learning Theory.
- **High accuracy:** When sufficient labeled data exists, supervised models outperform alternatives.
- **Interpretable outputs:** Each prediction has a measurable confidence and a known ground truth to compare against.
- **Broad applicability:** Used in medical diagnosis, autonomous driving, CPS fault detection, financial forecasting, and natural language processing.

**Key Limitation:** Requires **expensive, expert-labeled data**. In CPS scenarios, labeling may require physical fault injection experiments which are costly or hazardous.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Closed-Form Solution for Linear Regression (Normal Equation)

This is a **favorite KTU board derivation.** We derive the optimal parameters $\boldsymbol{\theta}^*$ that minimize MSE without using gradient descent.

**Step 1 — Define the problem in matrix form.**

Let the training data consist of $N$ samples with $d$ features each. We construct the design matrix $\mathbf{X} \in \mathbb{R}^{N \times (d+1)}$ by augmenting each row with a leading 1 for the bias term:

$$
\mathbf{X} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & x_{1,1} & x_{1,2} & \cdots & x_{1,d} \\ 1 & x_{2,1} & x_{2,2} & \cdots & x_{2,d} \\ \vdots & \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\ 1 & x_{N,1} & x_{N,2} & \cdots & x_{N,d} \end{bmatrix}, \quad \mathbf{y} = \begin{bmatrix} y_1 \\ y_2 \\ \vdots \\ y_N \end{bmatrix}, \quad \boldsymbol{\theta} = \begin{bmatrix} b \\ \theta_1 \\ \vdots \\ \theta_d \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 2 — Express predictions in matrix form.**

The vector of all predictions on the training set is:

$$\hat{\mathbf{y}} = \mathbf{X} \boldsymbol{\theta}$$

**Step 3 — Express the MSE cost function in matrix form.**

$$J(\boldsymbol{\theta}) = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left( \hat{y}_i - y_i \right)^2 = \frac{1}{N} (\hat{\mathbf{y}} - \mathbf{y})^{\top} (\hat{\mathbf{y}} - \mathbf{y})$$

Substituting $\hat{\mathbf{y}} = \mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\theta}$:

$$J(\boldsymbol{\theta}) = \frac{1}{N} (\mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\theta} - \mathbf{y})^{\top} (\mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\theta} - \mathbf{y})$$

**Step 4 — Expand the quadratic form.**

$$
J(\boldsymbol{\theta}) = \frac{1}{N} \left[ \boldsymbol{\theta}^{\top}\mathbf{X}^{\top}\mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\theta} - \boldsymbol{\theta}^{\top}\mathbf{X}^{\top}\mathbf{y} - \mathbf{y}^{\top}\mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\theta} + \mathbf{y}^{\top}\mathbf{y} \right]
$$

Since $\boldsymbol{\theta}^{\top}\mathbf{X}^{\top}\mathbf{y}$ is a scalar, it equals its transpose $\mathbf{y}^{\top}\mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\theta}$. Combining:

$$J(\boldsymbol{\theta}) = \frac{1}{N} \left[ \boldsymbol{\theta}^{\top}\mathbf{X}^{\top}\mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\theta} - 2\,\boldsymbol{\theta}^{\top}\mathbf{X}^{\top}\mathbf{y} + \mathbf{y}^{\top}\mathbf{y} \right]$$

**Step 5 — Take the gradient with respect to $\boldsymbol{\theta}$ and set it to zero.**

Using the matrix calculus identities $\nabla_{\boldsymbol{\theta}}(\boldsymbol{\theta}^{\top}\mathbf{A}\boldsymbol{\theta}) = 2\mathbf{A}\boldsymbol{\theta}$ and $\nabla_{\boldsymbol{\theta}}(\mathbf{b}^{\top}\boldsymbol{\theta}) = \mathbf{b}$:

$$\nabla_{\boldsymbol{\theta}} J(\boldsymbol{\theta}) = \frac{1}{N} \left[ 2\mathbf{X}^{\top}\mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\theta} - 2\mathbf{X}^{\top}\mathbf{y} \right] = \frac{2}{N} \left[ \mathbf{X}^{\top}\mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\theta} - \mathbf{X}^{\top}\mathbf{y} \right]$$

**Step 6 — Solve for the optimum by setting gradient to zero.**

$$
\frac{2}{N} \left[ \mathbf{X}^{\top}\mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\theta}^* - \mathbf{X}^{\top}\mathbf{y} \right] = \mathbf{0}
$$

$$
\mathbf{X}^{\top}\mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\theta}^* = \mathbf{X}^{\top}\mathbf{y}
$$

$$
\boxed{\boldsymbol{\theta}^* = \left( \mathbf{X}^{\top}\mathbf{X} \right)^{-1} \mathbf{X}^{\top}\mathbf{y}}
$$

This is the celebrated **Normal Equation**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Note:** The matrix $\mathbf{X}^{\dagger} = (\mathbf{X}^{\top}\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{X}^{\top}$ is called the **Moore–Penrose pseudo-inverse** of $\mathbf{X}$. Students frequently lose marks by writing only "inverse of X" — be precise: it's $(\mathbf{X}^{\top}\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{X}^{\top}$, not $\mathbf{X}^{-1}$, because $\mathbf{X}$ is generally non-square.

### 3.2 Analytical Decision Matrix: Classification vs. Regression

| Criterion | Classification | Regression |
|---|---|---|
| Output type | Discrete labels $y \in \{1,\ldots,K\}$ | Continuous real numbers $y \in \mathbb{R}$ |
| Decision boundary | Required (hyperplane / curve) | Not applicable |
| Standard loss | Cross-Entropy | MSE / MAE |
| Evaluation metric | Accuracy, F1, AUC-ROC | RMSE, MAE, $R^2$ |
| Output activation | Softmax / Sigmoid | Identity (linear) |
| Hypothesis form | $P(y \mid \mathbf{x}; \boldsymbol{\theta})$ | $h_{\boldsymbol{\theta}}(\mathbf{x}) = \boldsymbol{\theta}^{\top}\mathbf{x}$ |
| Example algorithm | Logistic Regression, SVM, k-NN | Linear Regression, Random Forest Regression |
| CPS use case | Attack / No-Attack classification | Predicting rotor temperature |

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (For KTU Exam Practice)

**Problem:** Given two training points $(\mathbf{x}_1 = 1, y_1 = 2)$ and $(\mathbf{x}_2 = 2, y_2 = 4)$, fit a linear regression $h(x) = \theta x$ (no bias).

**Step 1 — Build the design matrix (without bias):**

$$
\mathbf{X} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 2 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \mathbf{y} = \begin{bmatrix} 2 \\ 4 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute $\mathbf{X}^{\top}\mathbf{X}$:**

$$
\mathbf{X}^{\top}\mathbf{X} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 2 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 2 \end{bmatrix} = 1 \cdot 1 + 2 \cdot 2 = 5
$$

**Step 3 — Compute $\mathbf{X}^{\top}\mathbf{y}$:**

$$
\mathbf{X}^{\top}\mathbf{y} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 2 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 2 \\ 4 \end{bmatrix} = 1 \cdot 2 + 2 \cdot 4 = 10
$$

**Step 4 — Apply the normal equation:**

$$
\theta^* = (\mathbf{X}^{\top}\mathbf{X})^{-1} \mathbf{X}^{\top}\mathbf{y} = \frac{1}{5} \cdot 10 = 2
$$

**Result:** $h(x) = 2x$. Verify: $h(1) = 2$ ✓, $h(2) = 4$ ✓. Loss is exactly zero — the data is perfectly linear.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 High-Level Supervised Learning Workflow

The following Mermaid diagram captures the complete supervised learning pipeline — the *exact* sequence examiners expect you to reproduce in 14-mark questions.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Unlabeled Data X] --> B[Feature Engineering]
    B --> C[Labeled Dataset Dtrain]
    C --> D[Choose Hypothesis Class h theta]
    D --> E[Define Loss Function L]
    E --> F[Optimization via Gradient Descent]
    F --> G[Trained Model h star]
    G --> H[Test on Held Out Dtest]
    H --> I{Generalize Well?}
    I -->|Yes| J[Deploy for CPS Inference]
    I -->|No| K[Hyperparameter Tuning]
    K --> D
    J --> L[Predict on New Input x new]
    L --> M[Output y hat]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The loop `K → D` represents *iterative model refinement* — a concept KTU examiners love to test under "bias-variance tradeoff" or "model selection" topics.

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Supervised Learning in a CPS Security Pipeline

The diagram below illustrates how supervised learning is integrated into a Cyber-Physical System for *real-time intrusion detection* — directly aligned with course code **PECPT635**.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[CPS Sensors] --> A2[Preprocessing Module]
    A2 --> A3[Feature Extractor]
    A3 --> A4[Supervised Classifier h theta]
    A4 --> A5{Threat Level}
    A5 -->|Benign| A6[Normal Operation]
    A5 -->|Anomaly| A7[Alert and Mitigation]
    A4 -.trained on.-> B1[Labeled Attack Dataset]
    B1 --> B2[Training Engine]
    B2 -.updates.-> A4
    B2 --> B3[Performance Monitor F1 and ROC]
    B3 -.feedback.-> B2
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CPS-Specific Insight:** The dashed lines represent *offline training* flows, while solid lines are *online inference* paths. In a real CPS, the inference path must operate in *real time* (often sub-millisecond latency), while training is performed *offline* on accumulated labeled logs. KTU board questions frequently test this separation.

### 4.3 Concept Map: Supervised Learning Family of Algorithms

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    SL[Supervised Learning]
    SL --> CLS[Classification]
    SL --> REG[Regression]
    CLS --> LGC[Logistic Regression]
    CLS --> SVM[Support Vector Machine]
    CLS --> DTC[Decision Tree Classifier]
    CLS --> KNN[k Nearest Neighbors]
    CLS --> NN[Neural Networks]
    REG --> LNR[Linear Regression]
    REG --> PTR[Polynomial Regression]
    REG --> DTR[Decision Tree Regressor]
    REG --> RFR[Random Forest Regression]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** When asked *"List supervised learning algorithms,"* present them grouped under Classification/Regression as shown above. This structured presentation earns full marks versus a flat list.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Question]**

**Q1. Define Supervised Learning. Differentiate between classification and regression with one example each.** `[3 marks]`

**Model Answer:**

Supervised learning is a machine learning paradigm where an algorithm learns a mapping from inputs to outputs using a dataset where each example is paired with a correct label (the "supervisor's signal"). The goal is to learn a function $h_{\theta}(\mathbf{x})$ that predicts the correct output for unseen inputs.

**Difference:**

| Aspect | Classification | Regression |
|---|---|---|
| Output | Discrete label $y \in \{1,\ldots,K\}$ | Continuous real number $y \in \mathbb{R}$ |
| Example | Email spam detection (spam / not-spam) | Predicting house price in lakhs |

The classification output is categorical, whereas the regression output is a measurable quantity on a continuous scale.

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Question]**

**Q2. State the bias-variance tradeoff. Why is it important in supervised learning?** `[3 marks]`

**Model Answer:**

The bias-variance tradeoff decomposes the expected prediction error of a model into three components:

$$\mathbb{E}\!\left[(y - \hat{f}(\mathbf{x}))^2\right] = \text{Bias}^2(\hat{f}) + \text{Variance}(\hat{f}) + \sigma^2$$

- **Bias²** — error from oversimplified assumptions (underfitting).
- **Variance** — error from sensitivity to training data fluctuations (overfitting).
- **$\sigma^2$** — irreducible noise in the data.

**Importance:** It guides model complexity selection. Increasing model complexity reduces bias but increases variance, and vice versa. A good supervised learning model balances both to minimize generalization error on unseen data — a critical requirement for CPS applications where deployment-time data differs from training data.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 / Module 1 Choice Set]**

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a) Explain the supervised learning workflow with a neat block diagram. List any four supervised learning algorithms.** `[7 marks]`

**Model Answer:**

The supervised learning workflow consists of the following sequential stages:

1. **Data Collection & Labeling:** Domain experts annotate a dataset $\mathcal{D} = \{(\mathbf{x}_i, y_i)\}_{i=1}^{N}$ where each $\mathbf{x}_i$ is a feature vector and $y_i$ is the correct label (the supervisor's signal).

2. **Data Preprocessing:** Handle missing values, normalize features (e.g., z-score: $x' = (x - \mu)/\sigma$), and split data into training $\mathcal{D}_{train}$ and test $\mathcal{D}_{test}$ sets (commonly 80:20).

3. **Model Selection:** Choose the hypothesis class $h_{\boldsymbol{\theta}}$ — e.g., linear model, decision tree, neural network.

4. **Loss Function Definition:** Quantify prediction error. For regression, use **MSE**:
   
   $$J(\boldsymbol{\theta}) = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(h_{\boldsymbol{\theta}}(\mathbf{x}_i) - y_i)^2$$
   
   For classification, use **Cross-Entropy**.

5. **Training (Optimization):** Iteratively update parameters using gradient descent:
   
   $$\boldsymbol{\theta}_{t+1} = \boldsymbol{\theta}_t - \eta \nabla_{\boldsymbol{\theta}} J(\boldsymbol{\theta}_t)$$

6. **Evaluation:** Test the trained model $h_{\boldsymbol{\theta}^*}$ on $\mathcal{D}_{test}$ using metrics such as accuracy, F1-score, or RMSE.

7. **Deployment:** Use the trained model for inference on real-world CPS inputs.

**Block Diagram:** (Refer to the workflow diagram in SECTION 4.1 of these notes — examiners award full marks only if the diagram is included.)

**Four Supervised Learning Algorithms:**
1. Linear Regression
2. Logistic Regression
3. Decision Tree Classifier
4. k-Nearest Neighbors (k-NN)

---

**(b) Derive the normal equation for linear regression. Discuss one limitation of the closed-form solution.** `[7 marks]`

**Model Answer:**

**Derivation:** (Refer to the complete derivation in SECTION 3.1 of these notes.)

Starting from the matrix-form MSE cost:

$$J(\boldsymbol{\theta}) = \frac{1}{N}(\mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\theta} - \mathbf{y})^{\top}(\mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\theta} - \mathbf{y})$$

Expanding:

$$J(\boldsymbol{\theta}) = \frac{1}{N}\left[\boldsymbol{\theta}^{\top}\mathbf{X}^{\top}\mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\theta} - 2\boldsymbol{\theta}^{\top}\mathbf{X}^{\top}\mathbf{y} + \mathbf{y}^{\top}\mathbf{y}\right]$$

Setting $\nabla_{\boldsymbol{\theta}} J(\boldsymbol{\theta}) = \mathbf{0}$:

$$\frac{2}{N}\left[\mathbf{X}^{\top}\mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\theta} - \mathbf{X}^{\top}\mathbf{y}\right] = \mathbf{0}$$

Solving for $\boldsymbol{\theta}$:

$$
\boxed{\boldsymbol{\theta}^* = (\mathbf{X}^{\top}\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{X}^{\top}\mathbf{y}}
$$

**Limitation:**
- **Computational cost:** Inverting $\mathbf{X}^{\top}\mathbf{X} \in \mathbb{R}^{(d+1)\times(d+1)}$ costs $\mathcal{O}(d^3)$ in time. When $d$ is large (e.g., image features with $d > 10^4$), the closed-form solution becomes infeasible. In such cases, iterative methods like **gradient descent** are preferred.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** A very common mistake students make is writing $\boldsymbol{\theta}^* = \mathbf{X}^{-1}\mathbf{y}$. **This is WRONG** because the design matrix $\mathbf{X}$ is rectangular ($N \times d$, with $N \neq d$) and therefore not invertible. The correct pseudo-inverse expression is $(\mathbf{X}^{\top}\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{X}^{\top}$. **Losing 2 marks** for this error is common in KTU valuation.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a) Explain the difference between classification and regression in supervised learning. For each, give a suitable loss function and one real-world application in Cyber-Physical Systems (CPS).** `[7 marks]`

**Model Answer:**

**Classification** deals with predicting a *discrete categorical label*, while **Regression** deals with predicting a *continuous real-valued quantity*.

| Feature | Classification | Regression |
|---|---|---|
| Output $y$ | $y \in \{1, 2, \ldots, K\}$ | $y \in \mathbb{R}$ |
| Loss Function | Cross-Entropy | Mean Squared Error (MSE) |
| Evaluation | Accuracy, F1, AUC-ROC | RMSE, MAE, $R^2$ |
| CPS Application | Intrusion detection in industrial IoT (Benign / Malicious traffic) | Predicting motor bearing temperature in a smart factory |

**Cross-Entropy Loss (Binary):**

$$\mathcal{L}_{CE} = -\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}\left[y_i \log(\hat{y}_i) + (1-y_i)\log(1-\hat{y}_i)\right]$$

**Mean Squared Error:**

$$\mathcal{L}_{MSE} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(h_{\boldsymbol{\theta}}(\mathbf{x}_i) - y_i)^2$$

**CPS Application Detail:** In a smart-grid CPS, classification is used to detect whether a sudden voltage drop is a *line fault* or a *scheduled load surge* (discrete decision). Regression is used to forecast *next-hour power demand* from historical consumption and weather data (continuous prediction).

---

**(b) Consider a training set with two points: $(\mathbf{x}_1 = 1, y_1 = 1)$ and $(\mathbf{x}_2 = 2, y_2 = 3)$. Using the normal equation, find the parameters of the linear regression model $h(x) = \theta x$ (no bias). Verify by computing MSE.** `[7 marks]`

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Form the design matrix and target vector:**

$$
\mathbf{X} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 2 \end{bmatrix}, \qquad \mathbf{y} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 3 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute $\mathbf{X}^{\top}\mathbf{X}$:**

$$
\mathbf{X}^{\top}\mathbf{X} = (1)(1) + (2)(2) = 1 + 4 = 5
$$

**Step 3 — Compute $\mathbf{X}^{\top}\mathbf{y}$:**

$$
\mathbf{X}^{\top}\mathbf{y} = (1)(1) + (2)(3) = 1 + 6 = 7
$$

**Step 4 — Apply the normal equation:**

$$
\theta^* = (\mathbf{X}^{\top}\mathbf{X})^{-1} \mathbf{X}^{\top}\mathbf{y} = \frac{1}{5} \cdot 7 = 1.4
$$

**Step 5 — Verify with MSE:**

The fitted model is $h(x) = 1.4\,x$.

- $h(1) = 1.4$, error = $1.4 - 1 = 0.4$, squared = $0.16$
- $h(2) = 2.8$, error = $2.8 - 3 = -0.2$, squared = $0.04$

$$
\text{MSE} = \frac{1}{2}(0.16 + 0.04) = \frac{0.20}{2} = 0.10
$$

**Result:** $\theta^* = 1.4$ with training MSE = **0.10**. The model has learned a slope of 1.4, deviating from the ideal slope of 2 because the data points $(1,1)$ and $(2,3)$ are not perfectly collinear with the origin (and we forced $b=0$).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> 1. **Forgetting to show intermediate steps** (i.e., writing the final $\theta^*$ directly) costs 2 marks. Always show $\mathbf{X}^{\top}\mathbf{X}$, $\mathbf{X}^{\top}\mathbf{y}$, and the inversion step explicitly.
> 2. **MSE unit:** If the target is in volts, MSE is in volts² and RMSE is in volts. Examiners may deduct 1 mark for not specifying units.
> 3. **For numerical problems, write the formula first, then substitute, then compute.** This mirrors KTU's step-marking scheme.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for KTU Board Exams**

- ✅ **Supervised Learning** = learning from *labeled* data $\mathcal{D} = \{(\mathbf{x}_i, y_i)\}$.
- ✅ The *supervisor* provides the correct output for every training example.
- ✅ The two sub-tasks are **Classification** (discrete $y$) and **Regression** (continuous $y$).
- ✅ The hypothesis is a parameterized function $h_{\boldsymbol{\theta}}(\mathbf{x})$.
- ✅ The **objective** of training is to minimize a loss function $J(\boldsymbol{\theta})$ over the dataset.
- ✅ **MSE** is the standard regression loss; **Cross-Entropy** is the standard classification loss.
- ✅ The **Normal Equation** is $\boldsymbol{\theta}^* = (\mathbf{X}^{\top}\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{X}^{\top}\mathbf{y}$ — remember the pseudo-inverse form.
- ✅ **Gradient Descent** iteratively updates: $\boldsymbol{\theta}_{t+1} = \boldsymbol{\theta}_t - \eta \nabla_{\boldsymbol{\theta}} J(\boldsymbol{\theta}_t)$.
- ✅ The learning rate $\eta$ is a **hyperparameter** that controls step size.
- ✅ Evaluation metrics for classification: **Accuracy, Precision, Recall, F1-score**.
- ✅ Evaluation metrics for regression: **MSE, RMSE, MAE, $R^2$**.
- ✅ The **Bias-Variance Tradeoff** governs generalization: high bias → underfitting, high variance → overfitting.
- ✅ In CPS, supervised learning is widely used for **intrusion detection, fault diagnosis, and predictive maintenance**.
- ✅ Key limitation: requires **expensive labeled data**; not suitable when labels are unavailable (use unsupervised or reinforcement learning instead).
- ✅ The 80:20 **train-test split** is the standard convention for evaluating supervised models.
- ✅ Always remember to **normalize features** before applying gradient descent to ensure stable convergence.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Unsupervised
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT635 - MACHINE LEARNING FOR CPS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Unsupervised

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Unsupervised Learning** is a paradigm of Machine Learning (ML) in which an algorithm is trained on a dataset consisting of input vectors $\mathbf{X} = \{\mathbf{x}_1, \mathbf{x}_2, \dots, \mathbf{x}_n\}$ **without any corresponding target labels** $y_i$. The objective is to discover hidden structure, latent representations, groupings, or density distributions inherent within the data.

Mathematically, an unsupervised learner approximates an unknown function:

$$f: \mathcal{X} \rightarrow \mathcal{Y}$$

where $\mathcal{Y}$ represents an **inferred structure** (clusters, manifolds, lower-dimensional embeddings, or probability densities) rather than a known ground-truth label space.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Definition Snapshot:** *"Unsupervised learning is the training of a model using information that is neither classified nor labeled, allowing the algorithm to act on that data without guidance, thereby discovering inherent patterns and structures."*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Toddler in a Toy Store" Analogy:**
> Imagine a toddler (the algorithm) placed in a massive toy store with **no labels, no price tags, and no parent pointing things out**. The child naturally begins to **group toys by similarity** — cars with cars, dolls with dolls, soft toys in one corner, action figures in another. The toddler has *no teacher* saying "this is a car" or "that is a doll." The grouping emerges purely from observing inherent features (wheels, size, color, texture).
>
> This is precisely how **Unsupervised Learning** operates. The "toys" are your data points $\mathbf{x}_i$, and the "groups" are the clusters or patterns the algorithm discovers autonomously.

### 1.3 Key Characteristics

| Characteristic | Description |
|---|---|
| **No Label Supervision** | Dataset $\mathcal{D} = \{\mathbf{x}_i\}_{i=1}^{N}$; target variable $y$ is absent. |
| **Goal** | Discovery of intrinsic data structure: clusters, manifolds, anomalies. |
| **Evaluation** | Difficult — no ground truth; uses internal metrics like **Silhouette Score, Davies-Bouldin Index, Reconstruction Error**. |
| **Exploratory Nature** | Used for EDA (Exploratory Data Analysis) and hypothesis generation. |
| **Computational Role** | Often a **preprocessing step** (e.g., feature extraction) before supervised tasks. |

> [!TIP]
> **CPS Context (Cyber-Physical Systems):** In PECPT635, unsupervised learning is pivotal for **anomaly detection in sensor streams** (e.g., detecting faulty IoT sensors in a smart grid without needing labeled fault data) and **state-space compression** for control systems.

### 1.4 Supervised vs. Unsupervised — A Comparative Snapshot

| Property | Supervised Learning | Unsupervised Learning |
|---|---|---|
| **Training Data** | $(\mathbf{x}_i, y_i)$ pairs | $\mathbf{x}_i$ only |
| **Objective** | Learn $P(y \mid \mathbf{x})$ or a decision boundary | Learn $P(\mathbf{x})$ or hidden structure |
| **Feedback Signal** | Loss computed against true label $y$ | Intrinsic objective (compactness, reconstruction) |
| **Examples** | Classification, Regression | Clustering, Dimensionality Reduction, Density Estimation |
| **Evaluation** | Accuracy, Precision, Recall, MSE | Silhouette, Inertia, Log-Likelihood |
| **Data Requirement** | **Labeled** (expensive) | **Unlabeled** (abundant, cheap) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Taxonomy of Unsupervised Learning Tasks

Unsupervised learning decomposes into three principal task families, all of which are **high-yield for KTU board examinations**:

1. **Clustering** — Partitioning data into $K$ disjoint groups based on similarity.
2. **Dimensionality Reduction** — Projecting high-dimensional data $\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^d$ into $\mathbf{z} \in \mathbb{R}^k$ with $k \ll d$ while preserving information.
3. **Density Estimation** — Estimating the underlying probability distribution $P(\mathbf{x})$ from which samples are drawn.

### 2.2 Mathematical Foundation: The Clustering Objective

The most common clustering objective is the **Within-Cluster Sum of Squares (WCSS)** minimization:

$$J(C, \mu) = \sum_{i=1}^{K} \sum_{\mathbf{x} \in C_i} \lVert \mathbf{x} - \mu_i \rVert^2_2$$

where:
- $K$ = number of clusters
- $C_i$ = set of points assigned to cluster $i$
- $\mu_i$ = centroid of cluster $i$, defined as $\mu_i = \frac{1}{\vert C_i \vert} \sum_{\mathbf{x} \in C_i} \mathbf{x}$

**Geometric Intuition:** The algorithm places centroids such that the total squared Euclidean distance from every point to its assigned centroid is minimized. This is the basis of the famous **K-Means algorithm**.

### 2.3 Distance Metrics — The "Ruler" of Similarity

| Metric | Formula | Use Case | KTU Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Euclidean ($L_2$)** | $d(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y}) = \sqrt{\sum_{j=1}^{d}(x_j - y_j)^2}$ | Continuous, isotropic features | ⭐⭐⭐ Most Tested |
| **Manhattan ($L_1$)** | $d(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y}) = \sum_{j=1}^{d} \vert x_j - y_j \vert$ | High-dimensional sparse data | ⭐⭐ |
| **Cosine** | $d(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y}) = 1 - \frac{\mathbf{x} \cdot \mathbf{y}}{\lVert \mathbf{x} \rVert \lVert \mathbf{y} \rVert}$ | Text/document vectors | ⭐⭐ |
| **Mahalanobis** | $d(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y}) = \sqrt{(\mathbf{x}-\mathbf{y})^T \Sigma^{-1} (\mathbf{x}-\mathbf{y})}$ | Correlated features | ⭐ |

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates **every equation** a KTU 2024 student must memorize for this topic. **No vertical pipes are used inside table cells** to preserve rendering integrity.

| Concept | Formula | Variable Definitions | Domain |
|---|---|---|---|
| **K-Means Objective** | $J = \sum_{i=1}^{K} \sum_{\mathbf{x} \in C_i} \lVert \mathbf{x} - \mu_i \rVert^2$ | $K$ clusters, centroid $\mu_i$ | Clustering |
| **Centroid Update** | $\mu_i = \frac{1}{\vert C_i \vert} \sum_{\mathbf{x} \in C_i} \mathbf{x}$ | Mean of cluster $i$ | Clustering |
| **Silhouette Coefficient** | $s(i) = \frac{b(i) - a(i)}{\max\{a(i), b(i)\}}$ | $a(i)$ = avg intra-cluster distance; $b(i)$ = min avg distance to other clusters | Evaluation |
| **PCA Reconstruction** | $\hat{\mathbf{x}} = \mathbf{W} \mathbf{W}^T (\mathbf{x} - \bar{\mathbf{x}}) + \bar{\mathbf{x}}$ | $\mathbf{W}$ = projection matrix | Dim. Reduction |
| **PCA Objective** | $\max_{\mathbf{W}} \text{tr}(\mathbf{W}^T \mathbf{S} \mathbf{W})$ s.t. $\mathbf{W}^T \mathbf{W} = \mathbf{I}$ | $\mathbf{S}$ = covariance matrix | Dim. Reduction |
| **Covariance Matrix** | $\mathbf{S} = \frac{1}{n-1} \sum_{i=1}^{n} (\mathbf{x}_i - \bar{\mathbf{x}})(\mathbf{x}_i - \bar{\mathbf{x}})^T$ | $\bar{\mathbf{x}}$ = sample mean | Dim. Reduction |
| **Explained Variance** | $\text{EV}_k = \frac{\lambda_k}{\sum_{j=1}^{d} \lambda_j}$ | $\lambda_k$ = $k$-th eigenvalue | Dim. Reduction |
| **Gaussian Density** | $P(\mathbf{x}) = \frac{1}{(2\pi)^{d/2} \vert \Sigma \vert^{1/2}} \exp\!\left(-\frac{1}{2}(\mathbf{x}-\mu)^T \Sigma^{-1} (\mathbf{x}-\mu)\right)$ | $\mu$, $\Sigma$ = mean, covariance | Density Estimation |
| **EM Log-Likelihood** | $\ell(\theta) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \log \sum_{z_i} P(\mathbf{x}_i, z_i \mid \theta)$ | $z_i$ = latent variable | Mixture Models |

### 2.5 Why Unsupervised Learning Matters in Engineering

> [!TIP]
> **Industrial Utility:**
> - **Smart Manufacturing (CPS):** Identifying operational modes of a CNC machine without labeled fault data.
> - **Healthcare CPS:** Clustering patient vitals to discover unknown disease subtypes.
> - **Autonomous Vehicles:** Discovering road segments with similar driving behavior for trajectory prediction.
> - **Network Security:** Unsupervised anomaly detection for zero-day cyberattacks in IoT networks.

### 2.6 The Curse of Dimensionality — A Critical Caveat

As dimensionality $d$ grows, the **volume of the feature space increases exponentially**, causing data to become sparse. The ratio of distances between nearest and farthest neighbors converges to 1, rendering distance-based methods (K-Means, KNN) **ineffective**. This motivates **Dimensionality Reduction** as a necessary preprocessing step.

$$\lim_{d \to \infty} \frac{\text{dist}_{\max} - \text{dist}_{\min}}{\text{dist}_{\min}} \to 0$$

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: K-Means Convergence via Lloyd's Algorithm

The K-Means algorithm is guaranteed to **converge in finite steps** because the objective $J$ is monotonically non-increasing. Let us derive this rigorously.

**Step 1 — Define the Objective Functional**

We seek to minimize the total intra-cluster variance:

$$J(\mathcal{C}, \mathcal{M}) = \sum_{i=1}^{K} \sum_{\mathbf{x}_j \in C_i} \lVert \mathbf{x}_j - \mu_i \rVert_2^2$$

where $\mathcal{C} = \{C_1, \dots, C_K\}$ is the cluster assignment partition and $\mathcal{M} = \{\mu_1, \dots, \mu_K\}$ is the centroid set.

**Step 2 — Optimization over Assignments (Fixed $\mathcal{M}$)**

For a fixed centroid configuration, each point $\mathbf{x}_j$ is assigned to its **nearest centroid** (argmin over $i$):

$$C_i^{(t)} = \left\{ \mathbf{x}_j : \lVert \mathbf{x}_j - \mu_i^{(t)} \rVert_2^2 \leq \lVert \mathbf{x}_j - \mu_l^{(t)} \rVert_2^2, \forall l \right\}$$

This step **minimizes** $J$ because any reassignment strictly reduces (or maintains) the squared distance.

**Step 3 — Optimization over Centroids (Fixed $\mathcal{C}$)**

For a fixed assignment, the optimal centroid is the **mean of its cluster members**. We prove this by setting the partial derivative of $J$ with respect to $\mu_i$ to zero:

$$\frac{\partial J}{\partial \mu_i} = \frac{\partial}{\partial \mu_i} \sum_{\mathbf{x}_j \in C_i} (\mathbf{x}_j - \mu_i)^T (\mathbf{x}_j - \mu_i) = -2 \sum_{\mathbf{x}_j \in C_i} (\mathbf{x}_j - \mu_i)$$

Setting the derivative to zero:

$$-2 \sum_{\mathbf{x}_j \in C_i} (\mathbf{x}_j - \mu_i) = 0 \implies \sum_{\mathbf{x}_j \in C_i} \mathbf{x}_j = \vert C_i \vert \cdot \mu_i$$

Therefore:

$$\mu_i^{(t+1)} = \frac{1}{\vert C_i \vert} \sum_{\mathbf{x}_j \in C_i^{(t)}} \mathbf{x}_j$$

**Step 4 — Convergence Guarantee**

Since $J^{(t+1)} \leq J^{(t)}$ at every iteration, and $J$ is bounded below by $0$, the algorithm **must converge** in a finite number of steps. However, convergence is to a **local minimum**, not necessarily the global minimum.

---

### 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: Principal Component Analysis (PCA)

**Problem Setup:** Given centered data matrix $\mathbf{X} \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times d}$ (rows are samples, columns are features) with zero mean, find an orthogonal projection onto a lower-dimensional subspace $\mathbb{R}^k$ that **maximizes the projected variance**.

**Step 1 — Construct the Covariance Matrix**

$$\mathbf{S} = \frac{1}{n-1} \mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{X}$$

This $d \times d$ symmetric positive semi-definite matrix encodes feature co-variation.

**Step 2 — Formulate the Variance Maximization Problem**

The variance of the data projected onto a unit direction $\mathbf{w} \in \mathbb{R}^d$ is:

$$\sigma^2_{\mathbf{w}} = \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{S} \mathbf{w}$$

We seek:

$$\mathbf{w}^* = \arg\max_{\mathbf{w}} \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{S} \mathbf{w} \quad \text{subject to} \quad \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{w} = 1$$

**Step 3 — Apply the Method of Lagrange Multipliers**

Construct the Lagrangian:

$$\mathcal{L}(\mathbf{w}, \lambda) = \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{S} \mathbf{w} - \lambda(\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{w} - 1)$$

**Step 4 — Take the Gradient and Set to Zero**

$$\nabla_{\mathbf{w}} \mathcal{L} = 2 \mathbf{S} \mathbf{w} - 2 \lambda \mathbf{w} = 0$$

This yields the celebrated **eigenvalue equation**:

$$\mathbf{S} \mathbf{w} = \lambda \mathbf{w}$$

**Step 5 — Interpret the Result**

The optimal projection direction $\mathbf{w}_1$ is the **eigenvector corresponding to the largest eigenvalue** $\lambda_1$ of $\mathbf{S}$. The maximum projected variance equals $\lambda_1$:

$$\mathbf{w}_1^T \mathbf{S} \mathbf{w}_1 = \lambda_1$$

**Step 6 — Extend to $k$ Components**

Subsequent principal components $\mathbf{w}_2, \mathbf{w}_3, \dots, \mathbf{w}_k$ are the eigenvectors of $\mathbf{S}$ corresponding to the next $k-1$ largest eigenvalues, subject to orthonormality $\mathbf{w}_i^T \mathbf{w}_j = \delta_{ij}$.

**Step 7 — Reconstruction**

The low-dimensional embedding is $\mathbf{z}_i = \mathbf{W}^T \mathbf{x}_i$ where $\mathbf{W} = [\mathbf{w}_1, \dots, \mathbf{w}_k] \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times k}$.

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example: K-Means on 4 Points

Consider four 2D points: $P_1 = (1,1), P_2 = (1,2), P_3 = (8,8), P_4 = (9,9)$. Let $K = 2$.

**Iteration 1:**
- Initialize centroids: $\mu_1 = (1,1), \mu_2 = (2,1)$.
- Assign $P_1$: $d(P_1, \mu_1) = 0, d(P_1, \mu_2) = 1$. Assign to $C_1$.
- Assign $P_2$: $d(P_2, \mu_1) = 1, d(P_2, \mu_2) = 1$. Tie — assign to $C_1$.
- Assign $P_3$: $d(P_3, \mu_1) = \sqrt{98}, d(P_3, \mu_2) = \sqrt{61}$. Assign to $C_2$.
- Assign $P_4$: $d(P_4, \mu_1) = \sqrt{128}, d(P_4, \mu_2) = \sqrt{73}$. Assign to $C_2$.
- Update: $\mu_1 = ((1+1)/2, (1+2)/2) = (1, 1.5)$; $\mu_2 = ((8+9)/2, (8+9)/2) = (8.5, 8.5)$.

**Iteration 2:**
- Reassign $P_2$: $d(P_2, \mu_1) = 0.5, d(P_2, \mu_2) = 9.19$. Stays in $C_1$.
- All other points remain. **Convergence achieved.**

Final clusters: $C_1 = \{P_1, P_2\}$, $C_2 = \{P_3, P_4\}$.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Architecture: Unsupervised Learning Pipeline for CPS Sensor Data

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[CPS Sensor Array] --> B[Raw Multivariate Data X]
    B --> C[Preprocessing]
    C --> C1[Normalization Z-score]
    C --> C2[Missing Value Imputation]
    C --> C3[Noise Filtering]
    C1 --> D[Unsupervised Engine]
    C2 --> D
    C3 --> D
    D --> D1[Clustering Module]
    D --> D2[Dim Reduction Module]
    D --> D3[Density Estimation Module]
    D1 --> E[Operational Mode Labels]
    D2 --> F[Compressed State Vector]
    D3 --> G[Anomaly Scores]
    E --> H[Downstream Control Policy]
    F --> H
    G --> H
    H --> I[Actuator Commands CPS]
```

### 4.2 K-Means Algorithm Flow Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start] --> B[Initialize K centroids randomly]
    B --> C[Assignment Step]
    C --> C1[Assign each point to nearest centroid]
    C1 --> D[Update Step]
    D --> D1[Recompute centroids as cluster means]
    D1 --> E{Convergence Check}
    E -->|Centroids unchanged| F[Output Final Clusters]
    E -->|Centroids shifted| C
    F --> G[End]
```

### 4.3 PCA Conceptual Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Original d-dimensional space] --> B[Compute Covariance Matrix S]
    B --> C[Eigendecomposition S = W Lambda W transpose]
    C --> D[Select top k eigenvectors]
    D --> E[Project X onto W to obtain Z]
    E --> F[Reconstruction X hat from Z]
    F --> G[Minimize Reconstruction Error]
```

### 4.4 Taxonomy of Unsupervised Algorithms

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Unsupervised Learning] --> B[Clustering]
    A --> C[Dimensionality Reduction]
    A --> D[Density Estimation]
    A --> E[Association Rule Mining]
    B --> B1[K Means]
    B --> B2[Hierarchical Agglomerative]
    B --> B3[DBSCAN]
    B --> B4[Gaussian Mixture Models]
    C --> C1[PCA]
    C --> C2[t SNE]
    C --> C3[Autoencoders]
    C --> C4[ICA]
    D --> D1[Kernel Density Estimation]
    D --> D2[Expectation Maximization]
    E --> E1[Apriori Algorithm]
    E --> E2[FP Growth]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024] Define unsupervised learning. How does it differ from supervised learning?**

**Model Answer:**
