<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]`

**Q:** Differentiate between Supervised, Unsupervised, and Reinforcement Learning with one CPS example for each.

**Model Answer:**

| Learning Type | Data Requirement | Goal | CPS Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| Supervised | Labeled $(x, y)$ pairs | Learn $f: \mathcal{X} \rightarrow \mathcal{Y}$ | Predicting motor temperature from labeled sensor data |
| Unsupervised | Unlabeled $\{x\}$ | Discover hidden structure / clusters | Grouping IoT devices by traffic behavior |
| Reinforcement | Reward signal $r$ | Maximize cumulative reward | A drone learning to avoid obstacles |

**Conclusion**: Supervised learning requires explicit labels, unsupervised finds structure in raw data, and reinforcement learning learns through environmental feedback — making it ideal for sequential CPS control tasks. **(3 marks)**

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]`

**Q:** Explain the **bias-variance tradeoff** in the context of choosing an ML algorithm for CPS.

**Model Answer:**

The **bias-variance tradeoff** balances two error sources:

- **High Bias (Underfitting)**: The model is too simple — e.g., fitting a linear regressor to nonlinear sensor data. **CPS Risk**: Misses critical faults.
- **High Variance (Overfitting)**: The model memorizes training noise — e.g., a deep tree on 50 sensor readings. **CPS Risk**: Fails on unseen conditions.

**Mathematical decomposition of expected prediction error**:

$$\mathbb{E}\left[ (y - \hat{f}(x))^{2} \right] = \text{Bias}^{2}(\hat{f}(x)) + \text{Variance}(\hat{f}(x)) + \sigma^{2}$$

where $\sigma^{2}$ is the irreducible noise. In CPS, the **safety-critical nature** demands careful bias-variance balancing — typically favoring slightly higher bias to ensure robust generalization. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall** ⚠️
> Students commonly lose marks on this question by:
> 1. **Skipping the cost function derivation** — KTU requires *every algebraic step*, not just the final formula.
> 2. **Confusing sigmoid with softmax** — logistic regression uses sigmoid only.
> 3. **Writing algorithm names without justification** — you must explain *why* each algorithm fits the CPS use case.
> 4. **Ignoring units and ranges** — always state whether a quantity is in $[0,1]$ or $\mathbb{R}^{+}$.
> 5. **Not labelling diagrams** — KTU examiners deduct 1–2 marks for unlabelled Mermaid/hand-drawn diagrams.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model]`

**(a)** Derive the gradient descent update rule for **Linear Regression** with the Mean Squared Error cost function. Clearly state the hypothesis, cost function, partial derivative, and final update equation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a real-world CPS example, explain how **Logistic Regression** can be used for binary classification. Derive the **sigmoid function** and write the **cross-entropy loss** equation. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1: Hypothesis**
$$h_{\theta}(x) = \theta^{T} x$$

**Step 2: Cost Function**
$$J(\theta) = \frac{1}{2m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left( h_{\theta}(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)} \right)^{2}$$

**Step 3: Partial Derivative**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial J(\theta)}{\partial \theta_{j}}
&= \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left( h_{\theta}(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)} \right) x_{j}^{(i)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Update Rule**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\theta_{j} &:= \theta_{j} - \alpha \cdot \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left( h_{\theta}(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)} \right) x_{j}^{(i)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**CPS Application**: Predicting the next-hour power demand in a smart grid from historical load, temperature, and time-of-day features. **(7 marks — full derivation)**

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1: CPS Use Case**
In a **wearable heart monitor**, we classify ECG signals as *Normal* ($y=0$) or *Arrhythmia* ($y=1$).

**Step 2: Sigmoid Function Derivation**
We want a function mapping $\mathbb{R} \rightarrow [0,1]$. Take the **logistic function**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma(z) &= \frac{e^{z}}{e^{z} + e^{0}} = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-z}}
\end{aligned}
$$

Properties: $\sigma(0) = 0.5$, $\sigma(\infty) = 1$, $\sigma(-\infty) = 0$.

**Step 3: Hypothesis for Logistic Regression**
$$h_{\theta}(x) = \sigma(\theta^{T} x) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-\theta^{T} x}}$$

This gives $P(y=1 \vert x; \theta) = h_{\theta}(x)$ and $P(y=0 \vert x; \theta) = 1 - h_{\theta}(x)$.

**Step 4: Cross-Entropy Loss**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L(\theta) &= -\frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left[ y^{(i)} \log(h_{\theta}(x^{(i)})) + (1 - y^{(i)}) \log(1 - h_{\theta}(x^{(i)})) \right]
\end{aligned}
$$

This loss penalizes confident wrong predictions heavily. **(7 marks)**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]`

**(a)** Explain the **K-Means clustering algorithm** with its objective function. Show how cluster assignments and centroids are updated iteratively. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **Q-Learning algorithm** for a CPS. Write the **Bellman optimality equation** and the **Q-value update rule**. Explain with an example (e.g., a robot learning to navigate a grid). **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Algorithm Steps of K-Means:**

1. **Initialize** $k$ centroids $\mu_{1}, \mu_{2}, \dots, \mu_{k}$ randomly.
2. **Repeat until convergence**:
   - **Assignment step**: For each $x^{(i)}$, set
   $$c^{(i)} := \arg\min_{j} \Vert x^{(i)} - \mu_{j} \Vert^{2}$$
   - **Update step**: For each centroid $\mu_{j}$, compute the new mean:
   $$\mu_{j} := \frac{1}{m_{j}} \sum_{i: c^{(i)} = j} x^{(i)}$$
3. **Output**: Final cluster assignments $\{c^{(i)}\}$.

**Objective Function (WCSS):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
J(c, \mu) = \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \Vert x^{(i)} - \mu_{c^{(i)}} \Vert^{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**CPS Application**: Clustering sensor nodes in a smart building based on temperature and humidity profiles to detect HVAC anomalies. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Markov Decision Process (MDP) Tuple:**
$$(\mathcal{S}, \mathcal{A}, P, R, \gamma)$$

**Bellman Optimality Equation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V^{*}(s) = \max_{a \in \mathcal{A}} \left[ R(s, a) + \gamma \sum_{s' \in \mathcal{S}} P(s' \vert s, a) V^{*}(s') \right]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Q-Learning Update Rule:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q(s, a) \leftarrow Q(s, a) + \alpha \left[ r + \gamma \max_{a'} Q(s', a') - Q(s, a) \right]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Example — Robot Grid Navigation:**

- **States** $\mathcal{S}$: cells $\{1, 2, \dots, 16\}$ in a $4 \times 4$ grid.
- **Actions** $\mathcal{A}$: {Up, Down, Left, Right}.
- **Reward** $R$: $+10$ at goal cell, $-1$ per step, $-100$ for hitting obstacle.
- **Discount factor** $\gamma = 0.9$, **Learning rate** $\alpha = 0.1$.

After sufficient episodes, the robot learns the optimal path: maximum $Q$-value route to the goal. The CPS controller then executes $\pi^{*}(s) = \arg\max_{a} Q(s, a)$. **(7 marks)**

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Module 1**

- **Three paradigms** to memorize: **Supervised**, **Unsupervised**, **Reinforcement**.
- **Linear Regression** uses **MSE** cost and **gradient descent** for parameter updates.
- **Logistic Regression** uses the **sigmoid** $\sigma(z) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-z}}$ and **cross-entropy** loss.
- **Decision Trees** split using **Gini** ($1 - \sum p_{i}^{2}$) or **Entropy** ($-\sum p_{i} \log_{2} p_{i}$).
- **k-NN** is **lazy** — no training; prediction is by majority vote of nearest $k$ points.
- **SVM** maximizes the margin; solve $\min \frac{1}{2}\Vert w \Vert^{2}$ subject to $y_{i}(w^{T}x_{i} + b) \geq 1$.
- **K-Means** minimizes **WCSS**; converges to a local minimum (not always global).
- **PCA** finds eigenvectors of the **covariance matrix** $\Sigma v = \lambda v$.
- **MDP** tuple is $(\mathcal{S}, \mathcal{A}, P, R, \gamma)$.
- **Bellman Optimality** governs value functions in RL.
- **Q-Learning** is **model-free**, **off-policy**, and uses the **max operator** in updates.
- Always **state units and ranges** (e.g., $\sigma \in [0,1]$, $\gamma \in [0,1]$).
- Always **justify the algorithm choice** in CPS context — never just list the formula.
- Always **label diagrams and axes** — examiners penalize unlabelled figures.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: The Role of Machine Learning in CPS
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECPT635 - MACHINE LEARNING FOR CPS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** The Role of Machine Learning in CPS # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition A **Cyber-Physical System (CPS)** is an engineered system-of-systems that seamlessly integrates computation, communication, and physical processes through sensors, actuators, embedded processors, and network infrastructure. The **Role of Machine Learning (ML) in CPS** refers to the systematic application of data-driven, statistical learning algorithms to enable **perception, prediction, decision-making, and adaptive control** within the cyber layer of these integrated systems. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** *Machine Learning empowers CPS to transcend deterministic, rule-based behaviour by introducing probabilistic reasoning, statistical inference, and adaptive intelligence, thereby allowing the system to operate reliably under uncertainty, variability, and dynamic environmental conditions.* The convergence of ML and CPS is foundational to **Industry 4.0**, **Internet of Things (IoT)**, **autonomous systems**, and **smart infrastructure** — all of which form core application domains under the KTU 2024 Professional Elective framework. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Self-Driving Car" Imagine a **human driver learning to drive**: | Human Driver Component | CPS Equivalent | ML Role | |---|---|---| | Eyes & Ears (perception) | **Sensors** (LiDAR, Camera, GPS) | Computer Vision, Sensor Fusion | | Brain (decisions) | **Embedded Processor / Controller** | Reinforcement Learning, Decision Trees | | Memory (experience) | **Cloud / Edge Database** | Supervised Learning from historical data | | Reflexes (reactions) | **Actuators** (Brakes, Steering, Throttle) | Real-time ML Control Loops | | Practice over time | **Continuous Deployment** | Online / Incremental Learning | Just as a driver improves with experience, a CPS improves its performance by **learning from data streams** generated by sensors and feedback loops. Without ML, the car would behave like a rigid robot following pre-coded rules; with ML, it becomes an **adaptive intelligent agent** capable of handling novel traffic scenarios. ## 1.3 Core Functional Pillars of ML in CPS > [!NOTE] > **The Five Functional Pillars (KTU Board Favourite):** > 1. **Perception** — Extracting meaningful information from raw sensor data. > 2. **Prediction** — Forecasting future states (e.g., equipment failure, traffic flow). > 3. **Decision-Making** — Selecting optimal actions from learned policies. > 4. **Control** — Translating decisions into physical actuator commands. > 5. **Anomaly Detection** — Identifying deviations indicating faults or attacks. ## 1.4 Defining Metrics & Standards - **Latency Budget:** Typically **<10 ms** for safety-critical CPS (autonomous vehicles, medical robotics). - **Reliability Target:** **99.999%** (Five Nines) uptime for industrial CPS. - **Learning Convergence:** Measured in **epochs** and **loss-function value**. - **Accuracy Metric:** Classification **Accuracy**, **Precision**, **Recall**, **F1-Score**; Regression **MSE / RMSE**. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The CPS Architecture — A Layered View A CPS architecture consists of **three tightly-coupled layers**, with ML operating as the "cognitive bridge": ### Layer 1 — Physical Layer Contains the **physical processes** (mechanical, thermal, electrical, chemical) and **actuators** that influence the real world. Sensors continuously sample physical state variables. ### Layer 2 — Cyber Layer (ML Resides Here) Hosts the **computation, communication, and ML inference engines**. This layer performs: - Data acquisition & pre-processing - Feature engineering - Model training & inference - Decision logic execution ### Layer 3 — Communication Layer Provides **bidirectional data flow** between physical and cyber layers using protocols such as **MQTT**, **OPC-UA**, **DDS**, or **5G URLLC**. ## 2.2 The "Why" Behind ML Integration A traditional CPS uses **model-based control** (e.g., PID controllers, state-space models), which requires: - A precise mathematical model of the physical process. - Known parameters and linear behaviour. - Deterministic environmental conditions. When systems become **non-linear, high-dimensional, time-varying, or uncertain**, model-based control fails. **ML solves this** by: 1. **Learning complex non-linear mappings** directly from data. 2. **Generalising to unseen scenarios** through statistical inference. 3. **Continuously adapting** to environmental drift via online learning. ## 2.3 Categorisation of ML Techniques Applied to CPS | ML Category | CPS Application Example | Typical Algorithm | |---|---|---| | **Supervised Learning** | Fault classification in motors | SVM, Random Forest, CNN | | **Unsupervised Learning** | Sensor clustering & pattern discovery | K-Means, DBSCAN, Autoencoders | | **Reinforcement Learning** | Adaptive cruise control, robot navigation | Q-Learning, DDPG, PPO | | **Deep Learning** | Image-based object detection | CNN, RNN, LSTM, Transformers | | **Federated Learning** | Privacy-preserving multi-vehicle learning | FedAvg, FedProx | ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Essential Equations for Board Examinations:** | # | Concept | Formula | Description | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | General Learning Objective | $\min_{\theta} \; \mathbb{E}_{(x,y) \sim \mathcal{D}} \; \mathcal{L}\big(f_{\theta}(x), y\big)$ | Minimise expected loss over data distribution $\mathcal{D}$ | | 2 | Mean Squared Error (Regression) | $\text{MSE} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2$ | Average squared prediction error | | 3 | Root Mean Squared Error | $\text{RMSE} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2}$ | Same units as target variable | | 4 | Classification Accuracy | $\text{Accuracy} = \frac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN}$ | Fraction of correct predictions | | 5 | Precision | $\text{Precision} = \frac{TP}{TP + FP}$ | Quality of positive predictions | | 6 | Recall (Sensitivity) | $\text{Recall} = \frac{TP}{TP + FN}$ | Coverage of actual positives | | 7 | F1-Score | $F_1 = 2 \cdot \frac{\text{Precision} \cdot \text{Recall}}{\text{Precision} + \text{Recall}}$ | Harmonic mean of P and R | | 8 | Gradient Descent Update | $\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \eta \nabla_{\theta} \mathcal{L}(\theta_t)$ | Iterative parameter optimisation | | 9 | Bellman Equation (RL) | $Q^{\pi}(s,a) = \mathbb{E}\big[r + \gamma \max_{a'} Q^{\pi}(s',a')\big]$ | Value of state-action pair | | 10 | Sensor Fusion (Kalman) | $\hat{x}_{t \vert t} = \hat{x}_{t \vert t-1} + K_t (z_t - H \hat{x}_{t \vert t-1})$ | Optimal state estimate fusion | | 11 | CPS Response Time | $T_{\text{response}} = T_{\text{sense}} + T_{\text{compute}} + T_{\text{actuate}}$ | End-to-end latency constraint | | 12 | Generalisation Gap | $\text{Gap} = \vert E_{\text{test}} - E_{\text{train}} \vert$ | Overfitting indicator | > [!NOTE] > **KTU Tip:** The Gradient Descent update (Eq. 8) and the CPS Response Time decomposition (Eq. 11) are **guaranteed 7-marker questions** in any ESE paper. Memorise the symbol meanings: $\theta$ = parameters, $\eta$ = learning rate, $\nabla$ = gradient operator, $\gamma$ = discount factor. ## 2.5 Engineering Real-World Utility | Domain | CPS-ML Use Case | Industry Impact | |---|---|---| | **Automotive** | Autonomous driving (Tesla Autopilot) | Reduced accidents, optimised routing | | **Healthcare** | Closed-loop insulin pumps, robotic surgery | Personalised treatment, micro-precision | | **Smart Grid** | Load forecasting, demand response | Renewable integration, blackout prevention | | **Manufacturing** | Predictive maintenance on CNC machines | 30–50% downtime reduction | | **Aerospace** | Flight control with adaptive ML | Fuel savings, fault tolerance | | **Agriculture** | Autonomous tractors, drone-based crop monitoring | Precision farming, yield optimisation | # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Deriving the Closed-Form Linear Regression Solution Used in CPS Sensor Calibration In many CPS applications, we use **linear regression** to map sensor readings to calibrated physical units (e.g., raw ADC voltage to temperature in °C). Below is the exhaustive derivation of the **Normal Equation**. ### Step 1: Problem Setup Given $N$ sensor-input samples $x_i$ and corresponding physical-output samples $y_i$, find parameters $\theta_0$ (bias) and $\theta_1$ (slope) such that: $$\hat{y}_i = \theta_0 + \theta_1 x_i$$ ### Step 2: Define the Loss Function The Mean Squared Error (MSE) loss is chosen: $$\mathcal{L}(\theta_0, \theta_1) = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2 = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} (y_i - \theta_0 - \theta_1 x_i)^2$$ ### Step 3: Compute Partial Derivatives We minimise $\mathcal{L}$ by setting its partial derivatives to zero: $$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \theta_0} = -\frac{2}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} (y_i - \theta_0 - \theta_1 x_i) = 0$$ $$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \theta_1} = -\frac{2}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i (y_i - \theta_0 - \theta_1 x_i) = 0$$ ### Step 4: Simplify the First Equation Expanding the first derivative equation: $$\begin{aligned} \sum_{i=1}^{N} (y_i - \theta_0 - \theta_1 x_i) &= 0 \\ \sum_{i=1}^{N} y_i - N\theta_0 - \theta_1 \sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i &= 0 \\ N\theta_0 &= \sum_{i=1}^{N} y_i - \theta_1 \sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i \\ \theta_0 &= \bar{y} - \theta_1 \bar{x} \end{aligned}$$ where $\bar{y} = \frac{1}{N}\sum y_i$ and $\bar{x} = \frac{1}{N}\sum x_i$. ### Step 5: Substitute Into the Second Equation Plugging $\theta_0$ into the second partial derivative equation: $$\begin{aligned} \sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i \big(y_i - (\bar{y} - \theta_1 \bar{x}) - \theta_1 x_i\big) &= 0 \\ \sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i (y_i - \bar{y}) - \theta_1 \sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i (x_i - \bar{x}) &= 0 \\ \theta_1 &= \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i (y_i - \bar{y})}{\sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i (x_i - \bar{x})} \end{aligned}$$ ### Step 6: Compact Closed-Form (Normal Equation) The above can be rewritten using covariance and variance: $$\theta_1 = \frac{\text{Cov}(x, y)}{\text{Var}(x)}, \qquad \theta_0 = \bar{y} - \theta_1 \bar{x}$$ In matrix form for the general $d$-dimensional case: $$\theta = (X^{\top} X)^{-1} X^{\top} y$$ This is the **Normal Equation**, valid when $X^{\top}X$ is invertible. For high-dimensional CPS data with millions of samples (e.g., vibration sensors), gradient descent (Eq. 8 in formula sheet) is preferred due to computational cost. ## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Style) > A temperature sensor outputs voltage $x$ (in V), and the calibrated temperature $y$ (in °C) is recorded 5 times: $(x, y) = (1, 25), (2, 30), (3, 35), (4, 40), (5, 45)$. > Compute the linear calibration parameters. **Solution:** Compute means: $\bar{x} = 3$, $\bar{y} = 35$. Compute numerator: $$\sum x_i (y_i - \bar{y}) = (1)(25-35) + (2)(30-35) + (3)(35-35) + (4)(40-35) + (5)(45-35) = -10 - 10 + 0 + 20 + 50 = 50$$ Compute denominator: $$\sum x_i (x_i - \bar{x}) = (1)(-2) + (2)(-1) + (3)(0) + (4)(1) + (5)(2) = -2 - 2 + 0 + 4 + 10 = 10$$ Therefore: $$\theta_1 = \frac{50}{10} = 5, \qquad \theta_0 = 35 - 5 \times 3 = 20$$ Calibration equation: $$\boxed{\; \hat{y} = 20 + 5x \;}$$ **Interpretation:** A 1-volt increase in sensor output corresponds to a **5 °C** rise in physical temperature. ## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — ML vs Traditional CPS Control | Dimension | Model-Based Control (Pre-ML) | ML-Enabled CPS | |---|---|---| | **System Knowledge** | Requires exact mathematical model | Learns model from data | | **Adaptability** | Static, requires manual retuning | Continuous online adaptation | | **Non-linearity Handling** | Limited (linearisation required) | Native handling via deep nets | | **Scalability** | Poor with high-dim sensors | Excellent (CNNs, Transformers) | | **Real-time Latency** | Microseconds (analytical) | Milliseconds (inference bound) | | **Interpretability** | High (closed-form equations) | Low — "black box" concern | | **Data Dependency** | Low | Very high | | **Fault Tolerance** | Redundant sensors needed | Anomaly detection inherent | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Functional Architecture: ML-Driven CPS Closed Loop The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the **end-to-end functional flow** of an ML-enabled CPS, mapping how data moves from the physical world through the cyber layer and back. ```mermaid flowchart TB A[Physical World Process] -->|Raw Signals| B[Sensor Array] B -->|Analog / Digital Data| C[Signal Conditioning and ADC] C -->|Cleaned Data Stream| D[Edge Gateway and Preprocessor] D -->|Feature Vectors| E[ML Inference Engine] F[Trained ML Model Repository] -->|Load Weights| E E -->|Predictions and Decisions| G[Decision and Policy Module] G -->|Control Commands| H[Actuator Interface] H -->|Physical Action| A E -->|Anomaly Alerts| I[Monitoring Dashboard] G -->|Telemetry Data| J[Cloud Storage] J -->|Retraining Data| K[ML Training Pipeline] K -->|Updated Model| F I -->|Human Override| G ``` **Reading the Diagram:** - The **outer loop** (A → B → C → D → E → G → H → A) represents the **real-time inference loop**. - The **inner loop** (E → J → K → F → E) represents the **offline/online learning loop** that periodically improves the model. - **I** provides human-in-the-loop supervision for safety-critical events. ## 4.2 Block Diagram: Hierarchical ML Roles in CPS ```mermaid flowchart LR P1[Perception Layer
CNN, Object Detection] --> P2[Cognition Layer
RNN, LSTMs, Transformers] P2 --> P3[Decision Layer
RL, Planning, MDPs] P3 --> P4[Control Layer
Adaptive PID, MPC, ML Controllers] P4 --> P5[Actuation Layer
Motors, Valves, Relays] P5 --> P6[Physical Environment] P6 -->|Feedback| P1 subgraph DataPlane P1 P2 P3 end subgraph ControlPlane P4 P5 end ``` ## 4.3 Use-Case Block Diagram: Predictive Maintenance CPS ```mermaid flowchart TD M1[Vibration Sensor on Motor] --> M2[Edge Data Logger] M3[Temperature Sensor] --> M2 M4[Current Sensor] --> M2 M2 --> M5{Feature Extraction Window 1s} M5 --> M6[FFT and Statistical Features] M6 --> M7[Trained Random Forest Classifier] M7 -->|Healthy| M8[Continue Operation] M7 -->|Warning| M9[Schedule Maintenance] M7 -->|Fault Predicted| M10[Emergency Shutdown] M7 --> M11[Dashboard with RUL Estimate] M9 --> M11 M10 --> M11 ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 Marks) ### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model QP]` **Define Cyber-Physical System (CPS). List any three roles of Machine Learning in CPS.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** > **Definition (1 mark):** A Cyber-Physical System (CPS) is an integrated system combining computation, communication, and physical processes, where embedded computers monitor and control the physical world through sensors and actuators. > > **Three Roles of ML (3 × 1 mark each):** > 1. **Perception & Sensor Fusion** — ML models (e.g., CNNs) extract meaningful information from raw multi-modal sensor streams (camera, LiDAR, IMU). > 2. **Predictive Analytics** — ML algorithms forecast future system states such as equipment failures, enabling predictive maintenance. > 3. **Adaptive Decision-Making** — Reinforcement Learning agents learn optimal control policies in dynamic, uncertain environments (e.g., autonomous driving). ### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model QP]` **Differentiate between model-based control and ML-based control in CPS. Mention one limitation of each.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Aspect | Model-Based Control | ML-Based Control | |---|---|---| | Foundation | Mathematical model of plant | Learns from data | | Best For | Linear, well-known systems | Complex, non-linear systems | | Limitation 1 | Poor with uncertain / time-varying parameters (1 mark) | Requires large labelled datasets (1 mark) | | Limitation 2 | Manual retuning needed | Black-box interpretability issues (1 mark) | --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice, 14 Marks) ### **Question A — Option 1 (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]` **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the layered architecture of a Cyber-Physical System with a neat block diagram. Discuss how Machine Learning integrates into each layer. **(b) [7 Marks]** With suitable examples, describe the role of supervised, unsupervised, and reinforcement learning in CPS applications. #### Model Solution for (a): The **3-layer CPS architecture** consists of: 1. **Physical Layer (1 mark):** Contains sensors (e.g., temperature, pressure, vibration) and actuators (e.g., motors, valves). It interfaces with the real-world physical process. 2. **Communication Layer (1 mark):** Provides bidirectional data transmission using wired (CAN, Ethernet) or wireless (Wi-Fi, 5G, LoRa) protocols with bounded latency. 3. **Cyber Layer (1 mark):** Hosts computation, storage, and the **ML inference engine**. **ML Integration Per Layer (4 marks):** - In the **Physical Layer**, ML supports smart sensors that perform on-device inference (TinyML). - In the **Communication Layer**, ML optimises network traffic (e.g., reinforcement learning for spectrum allocation). - In the **Cyber Layer**, ML performs perception, prediction, and decision-making. - **Cross-layer integration:** A closed feedback loop exists where ML outputs inform actuator commands, and new sensor data triggers model retraining. **Diagram:** Refer to Section 4.1 Mermaid diagram (2 marks for neat labelled diagram). #### Model Solution for (b): **Supervised Learning in CPS (2.5 marks):** - Uses labelled input-output pairs $(x, y)$ to learn mappings. - **Example:** Training a CNN on labelled images of defective vs. non-defective products for **visual quality inspection** in a smart factory CPS. **Unsupervised Learning in CPS (2.5 marks):** - Discovers hidden structure in unlabelled data. - **Example:** Using **K-Means clustering** on IoT sensor readings to discover anomalous operating modes in a wind turbine without prior fault labels. **Reinforcement Learning in CPS (2 marks):** - Agent learns optimal policy $\pi(a \mid s)$ by interacting with the environment and receiving rewards. - **Example:** **Deep Q-Network (DQN)** for autonomous drone navigation, where the drone learns to avoid obstacles by maximising cumulative reward. --- ### **Question B — Option 2 / Internal Choice (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Adapted]` **(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the closed-form Normal Equation for simple linear regression used in CPS sensor calibration. Explain its significance with a numerical example. **(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss the role of ML in predictive maintenance for industrial CPS. Include suitable algorithms, the data pipeline, and explain how Remaining Useful Life (RUL) is estimated. #### Model Solution for (a): Refer to the **complete derivation in Section 3.1** of these notes. **Key Equations to Present (3 marks):** $$\hat{y}_i = \theta_0 + \theta_1 x_i$$ $$\mathcal{L} = \frac{1}{N} \sum (y_i - \theta_0 - \theta_1 x_i)^2$$ $$\theta_1 = \frac{\sum x_i (y_i - \bar{y})}{\sum x_i (x_i - \bar{x})}, \qquad \theta_0 = \bar{y} - \theta_1 \bar{x}$$ **Numerical Example (3 marks):** Use the sensor calibration worked example from Section 3.2, showing step-by-step computation leading to $\hat{y} = 20 + 5x$. **Significance (1 mark):** Provides a **deterministic, closed-form** mapping from raw sensor voltage to calibrated physical quantity, enabling accurate CPS measurements without iterative optimisation. #### Model Solution for (b): **Predictive Maintenance (PdM) Overview (1 mark):** PdM uses ML to forecast equipment failures **before** they occur, reducing unplanned downtime by up to **50%** and maintenance costs by **10–25%**. **Data Pipeline (3 marks):** 1. **Acquisition:** Sensors (vibration, temperature, acoustic emission, current) sample at 1 kHz–100 kHz. 2. **Pre-processing:** Noise filtering, normalisation, sliding-window segmentation. 3. **Feature Engineering:** Extract statistical (mean, RMS, kurtosis), spectral (FFT peaks), and time-frequency (wavelet) features. 4. **Model Training:** Train on historical failure data. **Algorithms (2 marks):** - **Random Forest / XGBoost** for fault classification. - **LSTM / GRU networks** for time-series RUL prediction. - **Autoencoders** for unsupervised anomaly detection. **RUL Estimation (1 mark):** RUL = Predicted time until a health indicator (HI) crosses a failure threshold: $$\text{RUL}(t) = \arg\min_{t' > t} \; \big\{ H(t') \leq H_{\text{threshold}} \big\}$$ --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in This Topic:** > 1. **Conflating CPS with IoT:** IoT is the *connectivity* layer; CPS is the *integration* of computation with physical processes. Students often lose 2 marks for using the terms interchangeably. > 2. **Forgetting the "Closed Loop":** Any answer about ML in CPS *must* mention the feedback loop (sensor → ML → actuator → physical → sensor). Answers describing only one-way processing lose marks. > 3. **Missing Quantitative Example:** In derivation questions, omitting a numerical substitution (e.g., plugging values into the Normal Equation) forfeits 2 marks. > 4. **No Equation for "Predictive Maintenance":** Examiners expect at least one formula (RUL, HI threshold, or feature extraction equation). Verbal-only answers lose 3 marks. > 5. **Vague Algorithm Names:** Saying "deep learning" without naming the architecture (CNN, LSTM, DQN) is penalised. Always be specific. > 6. **Latency Ignorance:** Failing to mention real-time constraints (sub-10 ms for safety-critical CPS) costs 1 mark in design-oriented questions. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — "The Role of ML in CPS"** - ✅ **CPS = Computation + Communication + Physical Processes**, tightly integrated via feedback loops. - ✅ **ML enables 5 pillars:** Perception, Prediction, Decision, Control, Anomaly Detection. - ✅ **3-layer architecture:** Physical Layer → Communication Layer → Cyber Layer (ML resides here). - ✅ **ML Categories:** Supervised (labelled), Unsupervised (unlabelled), Reinforcement (reward-based), Deep (neural networks), Federated (privacy-preserving). - ✅ **Closed-loop flow:** Sensor → Data → ML Inference → Decision → Actuator → Physical Process → (back to Sensor). - ✅ **Latency budget:** <10 ms for safety-critical CPS (autonomous vehicles, medical devices). - ✅ **Key Formulas to Memorise:** - MSE / RMSE for regression evaluation. - Accuracy / Precision / Recall / F1 for classification. - Gradient Descent update: $\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \eta \nabla \mathcal{L}(\theta_t)$. - Bellman Equation for RL value estimation. - Normal Equation for linear regression: $\theta = (X^{\top}X)^{-1} X^{\top} y$. - ✅ **Real-world Applications:** Autonomous vehicles, smart grids, predictive maintenance, medical CPS, aerospace flight control, precision agriculture. - ✅ **ML vs Model-Based Control:** ML wins in non-linear, uncertain, high-dimensional settings; model-based wins in deterministic, low-latency settings. - ✅ **Standard Exam Keywords to Use:** *Closed-loop, Real-time inference, Sensor fusion, Adaptive control, Online learning, Edge AI, Digital twin.* - ✅ **Board Tip:** Always draw a **neat labelled block diagram** — it guarantees 2–3 marks even if the text is incomplete.

topics.title: Classification Algorithms - Decision Trees
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT635 - MACHINE LEARNING FOR CPS
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Classification Algorithms - Decision Trees

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Decision Tree (DT):** A non-parametric, supervised learning algorithm used for **classification** (and regression) that predicts the value of a target variable by learning simple decision rules inferred from the data features. It models decisions and their possible consequences as a tree-like structure of nodes and branches.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context (Course: *Machine Learning for Cyber-Physical Systems*), a decision tree classifier is treated as a **white-box, interpretable ML model** widely deployed for **fault detection, intrusion classification, sensor-event categorization, and anomaly tagging** in CPS pipelines where explainability of decisions is mandatory for safety certification.

### Structural Vocabulary

| Term | Mathematical Notation | Role |
|---|---|---|
| **Root Node** | $r$ | Top-most node, represents the entire training dataset $S$ |
| **Internal Node** | $n_i$ | Represents a test on attribute $A_j$ |
| **Branch / Edge** | $e_k$ | Outcome of the test (e.g., $A_j = v$) |
| **Leaf Node** | $L_m$ | Carries the class label $c \in C = \{c_1, c_2, \dots, c_k\}$ |
| **Splitting Criterion** | $\Phi(A_j)$ | Measure (Entropy / Gini / Gain Ratio) used to pick the best attribute |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Doctor's Diagnosis Flowchart"

Imagine visiting a doctor with a fever. The doctor does not run every possible test at once. Instead, she follows a **decision flowchart**:

1. **Question 1:** "Do you have a cough?" → If **YES**, go left; if **NO**, go right.
2. **Question 2 (left branch):** "Is your throat red?" → If **YES**, diagnosis = **Strep Throat** (leaf). If **NO**, go further.
3. **Question 3:** "Have you travelled recently?" → etc.

This flowchart is precisely a **Decision Tree**. The doctor has effectively built a classifier from past patient records — choosing, at each node, the **single question that best separates sick vs. healthy patients**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** In CPS (Cyber-Physical Systems), decision trees function identically — they ask sequential questions about sensor readings (temperature, vibration, packet delay) until a fault class (Normal, Degraded, Critical) is reached at a leaf.

---

## 1.3 Why Decision Trees in CPS?

| CPS Requirement | How Decision Trees Satisfy It |
|---|---|
| **Real-time inference** on edge devices | Tree traversal is $O(\text{depth})$, no matrix math |
| **Explainability** for safety audits | Each decision is a traceable IF–THEN rule |
| **Low memory footprint** | Store only thresholds, not full model weights |
| **Handles heterogeneous sensors** | Mix of categorical & numerical features |
| **Robust to missing readings** | Surrogate splits / default branches |

---

## 1.4 Top-Down Inductive Learning — The Core Philosophy

> [!NOTE]
> Decision trees are built using a **Greedy Top-Down Recursive Partitioning** strategy (formally Hunt's Algorithm, 1966). At each step, the algorithm picks the **locally best attribute** to split on — never reconsidering earlier splits.

This "myopic" choice makes training extremely fast but is the very reason **pruning** becomes necessary later.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Hunt's Algorithm — Foundation of All Modern DT Variants

**Hunt's Algorithm (1966)** recursively partitions $S$ until every leaf is "pure" (single class). Modern variants (ID3, C4.5, CART) only differ in **how they choose the splitting attribute**.

### Operational Logic Steps

1. **Step 1 — Initialization:** Let all training tuples $S = \{(x_1, y_1), \dots, (x_n, y_n)\}$ reside at the root.
2. **Step 2 — Purity Check:** If all tuples in $S$ share class $c_k$, declare it a leaf with label $c_k$.
3. **Step 3 — Attribute Selection:** Otherwise, choose an attribute $A^*$ that **best separates** the tuples according to a chosen criterion $\Phi$.
4. **Step 4 — Branching:** Create one branch per distinct value of $A^*$; partition $S$ into subsets $S_v$.
5. **Step 5 — Recursion:** Recursively apply Steps 2–4 on each $S_v$.
6. **Step 6 — Stop Criterion:** Halt when (a) subset is pure, (b) no remaining attributes, (c) $\Phi$ gain falls below threshold $\epsilon$, or (d) tree depth exceeds $d_{\max}$.

---

## 2.2 Attribute Selection Measures — The Three Pillars

### 2.2.1 Entropy & Information Gain (ID3 — Quinlan, 1986)

**Entropy** measures the **impurity / disorder** of a sample set $S$ with respect to class distribution.

$$H(S) = -\sum_{i=1}^{k} p_i \, \log_2(p_i)$$

where $p_i$ is the proportion of tuples in $S$ belonging to class $c_i$. Range: $0 \le H(S) \le \log_2(k)$.

**Information Gain (IG)** is the expected reduction in entropy caused by partitioning $S$ on attribute $A$:

$$IG(S, A) = H(S) - \sum_{v \in \text{Values}(A)} \frac{\vert S_v \vert}{\vert S \vert} \, H(S_v)$$

**Decision rule:** Choose the attribute with **maximum** $IG(S, A)$.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall — Bias of ID3:** Information Gain is biased toward attributes with **many distinct values** (e.g., "Date", "Sensor_ID"). An attribute with a unique value per tuple achieves $IG = H(S)$ but is useless. This is why **Gain Ratio** was introduced.

---

### 2.2.2 Gain Ratio (C4.5 — Quinlan, 1993)

$$GR(S, A) = \frac{IG(S, A)}{SI(S, A)}$$

where the **Split Information** $SI$ penalizes attributes with many branches:

$$SI(S, A) = -\sum_{v \in \text{Values}(A)} \frac{\vert S_v \vert}{\vert S \vert} \log_2 \!\left( \frac{\vert S_v \vert}{\vert S \vert} \right)$$

**Decision rule:** Choose attribute with **maximum** $GR(S, A)$.

---

### 2.2.3 Gini Index (CART — Breiman et al., 1984)

**Gini impurity** measures the probability of misclassifying a randomly chosen tuple:

$$\text{Gini}(S) = 1 - \sum_{i=1}^{k} p_i^2$$

Range: $0$ (pure) to $1 - 1/k$ (maximally impure).

For a split on attribute $A$:

$$\text{Gini}_{\text{split}}(S, A) = \sum_{v \in \text{Values}(A)} \frac{\vert S_v \vert}{\vert S \vert} \, \text{Gini}(S_v)$$

**Decision rule:** Choose attribute that **minimizes** $\text{Gini}_{\text{split}}$ (equivalently, maximizes the Gini *gain*: $\text{Gini}(S) - \text{Gini}_{\text{split}}(S, A)$).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CART specifics:** Produces only **binary splits**, supports both classification (Gini) and regression (variance reduction), and uses **cost-complexity pruning**.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| Formula | Expression | Range / Boundary Condition | Used In |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Entropy** | $H(S) = -\sum p_i \log_2 p_i$ | $0 \le H \le \log_2 k$; $H = 0 \Leftrightarrow$ pure | ID3 |
| **Information Gain** | $IG(S,A) = H(S) - \sum_{v} \frac{\vert S_v\vert}{\vert S\vert} H(S_v)$ | $0 \le IG \le H(S)$ | ID3 |
| **Split Information** | $SI(S,A) = -\sum_{v} \frac{\vert S_v\vert}{\vert S\vert} \log_2 \frac{\vert S_v\vert}{\vert S\vert}$ | $0 \le SI \le \log_2(\#\text{values})$ | C4.5 |
| **Gain Ratio** | $GR(S,A) = IG(S,A) \,/\, SI(S,A)$ | Normalizes IG against branch count | C4.5 |
| **Gini Index** | $\text{Gini}(S) = 1 - \sum p_i^2$ | $0 \le \text{Gini} \le 1 - 1/k$ | CART |
| **Gini Split** | $\text{Gini}_{\text{split}}(S,A) = \sum_{v} \frac{\vert S_v\vert}{\vert S\vert} \text{Gini}(S_v)$ | Lower = better split | CART |
| **Classification Error** | $E(S) = 1 - \max_i p_i$ | $0 \le E \le 1 - 1/k$ | All |
| **Pruning Penalty** | $R_\alpha(T) = R(T) + \alpha \cdot \vert \text{leaves}(T) \vert$ | Controls tree complexity | CART |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Boundary:** $H(S) = 0$ **iff** all tuples in $S$ belong to a single class. Examiners frequently ask: *"Compute entropy of a perfectly pure node."* The answer is always **0 bits**.

---

## 2.4 Engineering Utility in Cyber-Physical Systems

| CPS Subsystem | DT Application | Benefit |
|---|---|---|
| **Industrial IoT** | Fault classification from vibration + temperature | Real-time root-cause on edge MCU |
| **Smart Grid** | Predicting outage vs. transient vs. normal | Interpretable rules for regulators |
| **Autonomous Vehicles** | Decision on obstacle class (Pedestrian / Cyclist / Sign) | Verifiable safety rules |
| **Healthcare CPS** | Sepsis early-warning from wearable streams | Auditable by clinicians |
| **Network CPS** | Intrusion vs. benign traffic from packet headers | Tree exported as IF–THEN rules for firewalls |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Numerical Example — Full ID3 Computation (KTU Pattern)

### Dataset (CPS-style: classifying Motor Status)

| Day | Temp (°C) | Vibration (mm/s) | Motor Status |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | High | High | **Fault** |
| 2 | High | Low | **Normal** |
| 3 | Normal | High | **Fault** |
| 4 | Normal | Normal | **Normal** |
| 5 | Normal | Low | **Normal** |
| 6 | High | Normal | **Fault** |

Total tuples $\vert S \vert = 6$. Class counts: Fault = 3, Normal = 3.

> **Goal:** Build the decision tree using ID3 (Information Gain) with attributes $A_1 = \text{Temp}$, $A_2 = \text{Vibration}$.

---

### Step A — Compute Root Entropy $H(S)$

Class probabilities:
$$p_{\text{Fault}} = \frac{3}{6} = 0.5, \qquad p_{\text{Normal}} = \frac{3}{6} = 0.5$$

$$H(S) = -\sum_{i} p_i \log_2 p_i$$

$$H(S) = -\bigl(0.5 \log_2 0.5 + 0.5 \log_2 0.5\bigr)$$

$$H(S) = -\bigl(0.5 \cdot (-1) + 0.5 \cdot (-1)\bigr) = 1.0 \text{ bit}$$

> **Conversion logic:** $\log_2(0.5) = -1$; substituting yields $H = +1$ bit. Maximum entropy for a binary class distribution.

---

### Step B — Compute $IG(S, \text{Temp})$

**Partition on Temp:**

| Temp = High | Count | Fault | Normal |
|---|---|---|---|
| $S_{High}$ | 3 | 2 | 1 |

| Temp = Normal | Count | Fault | Normal |
|---|---|---|---|
| $S_{Normal}$ | 3 | 1 | 2 |

**Entropy of $S_{High}$:**

$$H(S_{High}) = -\left( \tfrac{2}{3} \log_2 \tfrac{2}{3} + \tfrac{1}{3} \log_2 \tfrac{1}{3} \right)$$

$$\log_2(2/3) = \log_2 2 - \log_2 3 = 1 - 1.585 = -0.585$$

$$\log_2(1/3) = -\log_2 3 = -1.585$$

$$H(S_{High}) = -\bigl(\tfrac{2}{3}(-0.585) + \tfrac{1}{3}(-1.585)\bigr)$$

$$= -\bigl(-0.390 - 0.528\bigr) = 0.918 \text{ bits}$$

**Entropy of $S_{Normal}$:** (by symmetry, identical class distribution $1/3, 2/3$)

$$H(S_{Normal}) = 0.918 \text{ bits}$$

**Weighted sum:**

$$\sum_v \frac{\vert S_v \vert}{\vert S \vert} H(S_v) = \tfrac{3}{6}(0.918) + \tfrac{3}{6}(0.918) = 0.918$$

**Information Gain:**

$$IG(S, \text{Temp}) = H(S) - 0.918 = 1.000 - 0.918 = 0.082 \text{ bits}$$

---

### Step C — Compute $IG(S, \text{Vibration})$

**Partition on Vibration:**

| Vibration | Count | Fault | Normal |
|---|---|---|---|
| High | 2 | 2 | 0 |
| Low | 2 | 0 | 2 |
| Normal | 2 | 1 | 1 |

**Entropy $S_{High}$** (pure — only Fault):

$$H(S_{High}) = -(1 \log_2 1 + 0) = 0 \text{ bits}$$

**Entropy $S_{Low}$** (pure — only Normal):

$$H(S_{Low}) = 0 \text{ bits}$$

**Entropy $S_{Normal}$** (mixed 1 Fault, 1 Normal):

$$H(S_{Normal}) = -(0.5 \log_2 0.5 + 0.5 \log_2 0.5) = 1.0 \text{ bit}$$

**Weighted sum:**

$$\sum_v \frac{\vert S_v \vert}{\vert S \vert} H(S_v) = \tfrac{2}{6}(0) + \tfrac{2}{6}(0) + \tfrac{2}{6}(1.0) = 0.333$$

**Information Gain:**

$$IG(S, \text{Vibration}) = 1.000 - 0.333 = 0.667 \text{ bits}$$

---

### Step D — Attribute Selection

$$IG(S, \text{Vibration}) = 0.667 > IG(S, \text{Temp}) = 0.082$$

> **Root decision:** Split on **Vibration**.

---

### Step E — Recurse on $S_{Normal}$ (Vibration = Normal)

Tuples with Vibration = Normal: Day 4 (Normal), Day 6 (Fault). One tuple each, classes differ → still impure.

Available attribute: **Temp**.

**Partition:**

| Temp = High | Count | Fault | Normal |
|---|---|---|---|
| | 1 | 1 | 0 |

| Temp = Normal | Count | Fault | Normal |
|---|---|---|---|
| | 1 | 0 | 1 |

Both leaves are **pure** → recursion halts.

---

### Step F — Final Tree Structure

```
                [ Vibration? ]
                /    |     \
            High    Low   Normal
             |       |       |
          Fault   Normal   [Temp?]
                            /    \
                         High  Normal
                          |      |
                       Fault  Normal
```

**Analytical Summary Matrix:**

| Attribute | $H(S_v)$ weighted | $IG(S,A)$ bits | Selected? |
|---|---|---|---|
| Temp | 0.918 | 0.082 | No |
| Vibration | 0.333 | **0.667** | **Yes (Root)** |

---

## 3.2 Gain Ratio Recomputation (Same Data)

**SplitInfo for Vibration:**

$$SI(S, \text{Vibration}) = -\left[ \tfrac{2}{6}\log_2 \tfrac{2}{6} + \tfrac{2}{6}\log_2 \tfrac{2}{6} + \tfrac{2}{6}\log_2 \tfrac{2}{6} \right]$$

$$\log_2(1/3) = -1.585$$

$$SI = -3 \cdot \tfrac{1}{3}(-1.585) = 1.585 \text{ bits}$$

$$GR(S, \text{Vibration}) = \frac{0.667}{1.585} = 0.421$$

**SplitInfo for Temp:**

$$SI(S, \text{Temp}) = -\left[ \tfrac{3}{6}\log_2 \tfrac{3}{6} + \tfrac{3}{6}\log_2 \tfrac{3}{6} \right] = 1.000 \text{ bit}$$

$$GR(S, \text{Temp}) = \frac{0.082}{1.000} = 0.082$$

**Decision:** Vibration still wins ($0.421 > 0.082$), confirming ID3's choice.

---

## 3.3 Gini Index Verification (CART-Style)

$$\text{Gini}(S) = 1 - (0.5)^2 - (0.5)^2 = 1 - 0.25 - 0.25 = 0.500$$

**Gini split on Vibration:**

$$\text{Gini}_{\text{split}} = \tfrac{2}{6}(0) + \tfrac{2}{6}(0) + \tfrac{2}{6}(1 - (0.5)^2 - (0.5)^2)$$

$$= 0 + 0 + \tfrac{2}{6}(0.5) = 0.167$$

**Gini gain** $= 0.500 - 0.167 = 0.333$.

CART would again select **Vibration** at the root, validating cross-criterion consistency on this dataset.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — ID3 vs C4.5 vs CART

| Property | ID3 | C4.5 | CART |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Selection Criterion** | Information Gain | Gain Ratio | Gini Index |
| **Split Type** | Multi-way | Multi-way | **Binary only** |
| **Handles Missing Values?** | No | Yes | Yes |
| **Handles Continuous Attr?** | No | Yes (threshold) | Yes (threshold) |
| **Pruning** | None built-in | Error-based | Cost-complexity |
| **Output** | Classification only | Classification only | Classification + Regression |
| **KTU Typical Marks** | 3–4 | 7 (full numerical) | 7 (formula derivation) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Decision Tree Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Training Data S with features and class labels]) --> Init[Initialize Root Node R with all tuples]
    Init --> PurCheck{Is subset S pure? single class}
    PurCheck -- YES --> LeafPure[Declare Leaf Node assign majority class label]
    PurCheck -- NO --> SelCrit[Compute Selection Measure for every attribute A in remaining set]
    SelCrit --> SelCritKind{Selection Measure Type}
    SelCritKind -- ID3 --> IG[Information Gain IG S A equals H S minus weighted sum of H Sv]
    SelCritKind -- C45 --> GR[Gain Ratio GR S A equals IG divided by SplitInfo]
    SelCritKind -- CART --> GI[Gini Gain equals Gini S minus weighted Gini split]
    IG --> Compare
    GR --> Compare
    GI --> Compare
    Compare[Pick attribute A star with best score maximum IG or GR or minimum Gini]
    Compare --> Branch[Partition S into subsets Sv one per attribute value]
    Branch --> StopCheck{Stop Conditions Met}
    StopCheck -- YES --> LeafStop[Create Leaf with majority class]
    StopCheck -- NO --> PurCheck
    LeafPure --> Done([Return Constructed Decision Tree])
    LeafStop --> Done
```

## 4.2 Decision Tree Traversal & Classification Path

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Query([New CPS Sensor Sample x]) --> RootN{Root Node Test on A1}
    RootN -- Outcome v1 --> N1{Internal Node Test on A2}
    RootN -- Outcome v2 --> N2{Internal Node Test on A3}
    RootN -- Outcome v3 --> LeafA[Leaf Class c1]
    N1 -- Outcome v1a --> LeafB[Leaf Class c2]
    N1 -- Outcome v1b --> LeafC[Leaf Class c3]
    N2 -- Outcome v2a --> LeafD[Leaf Class c1]
    N2 -- Outcome v2b --> LeafE[Leaf Class c3]
    LeafA --> Pred([Final Predicted Class Label])
    LeafB --> Pred
    LeafC --> Pred
    LeafD --> Pred
    LeafE --> Pred
```

## 4.3 Pruning Workflow (Reduced Error + Cost-Complexity)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    FullT([Fully Grown Tree Tmax overfits training data]) --> Eval[Evaluate on Validation Set or Compute Cost Complexity R alpha of T]
    Eval --> Decision{Replace subtree with leaf if error or R alpha does not increase}
    Decision -- YES --> Prune[Collapse subtree into single leaf node with majority class]
    Decision -- NO --> Keep[Retain subtree]
    Prune --> Iterate[Repeat until no further improvement]
    Keep --> Iterate
    Iterate --> FinalT([Pruned Optimal Tree Topt])
```

## 4.4 Pre-Pruning vs Post-Pruning Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PrePrune[Pre Pruning Early Stopping]
        P1[During tree growth] --> P2{Halt test threshold chi squared gain below epsilon or depth greater dmax}
        P2 -- YES --> P3[Stop splitting declare leaf]
    end
    subgraph PostPrune[Post Pruning]
        Q1[After tree fully grown] --> Q2[Generate pruned candidates by collapsing subtrees]
        Q2 --> Q3{Select pruned tree with lowest validation error}
        Q3 --> Q4[Final Generalised Tree]
    end
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**"Define the term *Information Gain* in the context of a Decision Tree classifier. Why is it called a *greedy* measure?"**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Information Gain $IG(S, A)$ of attribute $A$ relative to training set $S$ is the **expected reduction in entropy** caused by partitioning $S$ on $A$:

$$IG(S, A) = H(S) - \sum_{v \in \text{Values}(A)} \frac{\vert S_v \vert}{\vert S \vert} H(S_v)$$

It is called **greedy** because at each internal node the algorithm **locally maximizes** $IG$ without considering whether this choice leads to the globally optimal tree deeper down. The decision is irrevocable — backtracking is not performed. *(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for formula, 1 mark for greedy justification.)*

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**"Distinguish between *pre-pruning* and *post-pruning* in decision trees. Which is computationally cheaper and why?"**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Pre-Pruning (Early Stopping) | Post-Pruning (Reduced-Error / Cost-Complexity) |
|---|---|---|
| **When applied** | During tree growth | After full tree is built |
| **Mechanism** | Halt recursion if $IG < \epsilon$ or depth $> d_{\max}$ | Replace subtrees with leaves if validation error drops |
| **Risk** | Under-fitting (stops too early) | Computationally heavier but safer |
| **Computational cost** | **Cheaper** (avoids growing many subtrees) | Expensive (must fully grow then test every subtree) |

**Cheaper method: Pre-pruning**, because it never builds branches that are immediately discarded. *(1 mark pre-pruning def, 1 mark post-pruning def, 1 mark cost comparison.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 2]**

> **(a)** Explain the **ID3 algorithm** for decision tree induction with its mathematical foundation. Derive the entropy and information gain equations from first principles. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Given the CPS dataset below classifying **Motor Status** based on `Temperature` and `Vibration`, build the decision tree using **Information Gain**. Show every computation explicitly. **(7 marks)**

| Day | Temp | Vibration | Status |
|---|---|---|---|
| D1 | Hot | High | Fault |
| D2 | Hot | Low | Normal |
| D3 | Cool | High | Fault |
| D4 | Cool | Low | Normal |
| D5 | Cool | High | Fault |
| D6 | Hot | Low | Normal |

---

### Model Solution — Question A(a) — ID3 Foundations (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Definition of ID3 (1 mark):**
Iterative Dichotomiser 3 (Quinlan, 1986) is a greedy top-down algorithm that recursively partitions the dataset using the attribute with the highest Information Gain until pure leaves are obtained.

**Step 2 — Entropy Derivation (3 marks):**

Entropy is grounded in Shannon's information theory. The information content of an event with probability $p$ is $-\log_2 p$ bits. For a discrete distribution over $k$ classes, the **expected information** (entropy) is:

$$H(S) = \sum_{i=1}^{k} p_i \cdot (-\log_2 p_i) = -\sum_{i=1}^{k} p_i \log_2 p_i$$

**Step 3 — Information Gain Derivation (3 marks):**
After splitting on attribute $A$ with values $v$, the expected entropy of the children is the weighted average:

$$H_{\text{after}}(S \mid A) = \sum_{v} \frac{\vert S_v \vert}{\vert S \vert} H(S_v)$$

The **reduction** is the Information Gain:

$$IG(S, A) = H(S) - H_{\text{after}}(S \mid A)$$

ID3 selects $A^* = \arg\max_{A} IG(S, A)$.

---

### Model Solution — Question A(b) — Numerical Build (7 marks)

$\vert S \vert = 6$, Fault = 3, Normal = 3.

**Root entropy:**

$$H(S) = -\left(\tfrac{3}{6}\log_2 \tfrac{3}{6} + \tfrac{3}{6}\log_2 \tfrac{3}{6}\right) = 1.0 \text{ bit}$$

**Split on Temperature:**

| Temp | Count | Fault | Normal | $H(S_v)$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Hot | 3 | 2 | 1 | $-(\tfrac{2}{3}\log_2\tfrac{2}{3} + \tfrac{1}{3}\log_2\tfrac{1}{3}) = 0.918$ |
| Cool | 3 | 1 | 2 | $0.918$ |

$$IG(S, \text{Temp}) = 1.0 - \tfrac{3}{6}(0.918) - \tfrac{3}{6}(0.918) = 1.0 - 0.918 = 0.082 \text{ bits}$$

**Split on Vibration:**

| Vibration | Count | Fault | Normal | $H(S_v)$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| High | 3 | 3 | 0 | $0$ (pure) |
| Low | 3 | 0 | 3 | $0$ (pure) |

$$IG(S, \text{Vibration}) = 1.0 - \tfrac{3}{6}(0) - \tfrac{3}{6}(0) = 1.0 \text{ bit}$$

**Selection:** Vibration wins ($1.0 > 0.082$). Root = Vibration. Both children are pure → **tree complete in one level**.

```
            [ Vibration? ]
            /            \
         High            Low
          |               |
        Fault          Normal
```

**(1 mark for entropy, 2 marks for Temp IG, 2 marks for Vibration IG, 1 mark for tree, 1 mark for final tree diagram.)**

---

### Question B — Alternative Choice (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 2]**

> **(a)** Explain the **Gini Index** used in CART. How does it differ from Information Gain? Discuss its advantages and limitations. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Compute the **Gini Index** for the following dataset split and decide which attribute produces the purer partition. **(7 marks)**

| Attribute | Subset | Class A | Class B |
|---|---|---|---|
| $A_1$ | $S_1$ | 40 | 10 |
| $A_1$ | $S_2$ | 5 | 45 |
| $A_2$ | $T_1$ | 30 | 20 |
| $A_2$ | $T_2$ | 20 | 30 |

---

### Model Solution — Question B(a) — Gini Index Theory (7 marks)

**Definition (2 marks):** Gini Index at node $S$ measures the probability of incorrect classification if a label is randomly drawn according to the class distribution:

$$\text{Gini}(S) = 1 - \sum_{i=1}^{k} p_i^2$$

**Difference from IG (2 marks):**

| Aspect | Information Gain | Gini Index |
|---|---|---|
| Basis | Information theory (entropy) | Statistical variance |
| Computation | Requires $\log$ operations | Only squares — faster |
| Sensitivity | More sensitive to probability changes | Slightly less nuanced |
| Use | ID3, C4.5 | CART |

**Advantages (1 mark):** Computationally cheaper; no logarithm required; works well for binary classification.
**Limitations (2 marks):** Biased toward multi-class partitions; doesn't favour rarer pure classes as strongly as entropy-based criteria; creates only binary trees in CART (information loss for categorical attributes with many values).

---

### Model Solution — Question B(b) — Gini Computation (7 marks)

**Gini for Attribute $A_1$:**

$$\text{Gini}(S_1) = 1 - (40/50)^2 - (10/50)^2 = 1 - 0.64 - 0.04 = 0.32$$

$$\text{Gini}(S_2) = 1 - (5/50)^2 - (45/50)^2 = 1 - 0.01 - 0.81 = 0.18$$

Weighted Gini split:

$$\text{Gini}_{\text{split}}(A_1) = \tfrac{50}{100}(0.32) + \tfrac{50}{100}(0.18) = 0.16 + 0.09 = 0.25$$

**Gini for Attribute $A_2$:**

$$\text{Gini}(T_1) = 1 - (30/50)^2 - (20/50)^2 = 1 - 0.36 - 0.16 = 0.48$$

$$\text{Gini}(T_2) = 1 - (20/50)^2 - (30/50)^2 = 1 - 0.16 - 0.36 = 0.48$$

Weighted Gini split:

$$\text{Gini}_{\text{split}}(A_2) = \tfrac{50}{100}(0.48) + \tfrac{50}{100}(0.48) = 0.48$$

**Decision (1 mark):** $A_1$ chosen since $\text{Gini}_{\text{split}}(A_1) = 0.25 < 0.48 = \text{Gini}_{\text{split}}(A_2)$. $A_1$ produces the **purer partition**.

**(1 mark per Gini calculation × 4, 1 mark weighted average × 2, 1 mark decision.)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Forgetting $\log_2$ base:** Examiners will deduct 1 mark if you write $\ln$ or natural log; ID3 specifically uses $\log_2$.
> 2. **Skipping weighted averaging:** Marks lost if $H(S_v)$ values are listed but not multiplied by $\vert S_v\vert/\vert S\vert$.
> 3. **Confusing IG direction:** IG is **maximized**; Gini is **minimized**. Writing the wrong direction loses 1 mark.
> 4. **Forgetting pure-node entropy = 0:** Whenever a child has tuples of only one class, write $H = 0$ explicitly — don't skip the term.
> 5. **Ignoring pruning:** For 14-mark questions, examiners expect a one-line note on overfitting / pruning; omission loses 1 mark.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Must Remember for KTU 2024 Exam**

- **Definition:** A Decision Tree is a hierarchical, rule-based classifier built by **greedy recursive partitioning**.
- **Three selection criteria:** Information Gain (ID3), Gain Ratio (C4.5), Gini Index (CART).
- **Core formulas:** $H(S) = -\sum p_i \log_2 p_i$; $IG = H(S) - \sum \frac{\vert S_v\vert}{\vert S\vert} H(S_v)$; $GR = IG / SI$; $\text{Gini} = 1 - \sum p_i^2$.
- **Pure node boundary condition:** $H = 0$ and $\text{Gini} = 0$ iff all tuples share the same class.
- **ID3 bias:** Prefers high-cardinality attributes → use **Gain Ratio** (C4.5) to correct.
- **CART binary restriction:** Always produces two-way splits.
- **Overfitting guard:** Apply **pre-pruning** (early stopping) or **post-pruning** (cost-complexity $R_\alpha(T) = R(T) + \alpha \cdot \vert \text{leaves}\vert$).
- **Stopping conditions:** purity reached, no remaining attributes, $IG < \epsilon$, depth $> d_{\max}$, tuple count $< n_{\min}$.
- **CPS relevance:** Real-time inference, white-box rules, edge-deployable, mixed-type sensor data, missing-value tolerant.
- **Common exam trick:** Provide a numerical dataset where two attributes yield **same IG** — explain tie-breaking using **Gain Ratio** (penalty term $SI$).
- **Always state units:** Entropy is in **bits** when base 2 is used.
- **Final answer check:** Decision rule → choose $A^*$ with **highest** $IG$ / $GR$, or **lowest** Gini.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Support Vector Machines
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT635 - MACHINE LEARNING FOR CPS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Support Vector Machines

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Support Vector Machines (SVM) — Core Definition & Intuition

## 1. Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Support Vector Machine (SVM)** is a **supervised learning algorithm** developed within the framework of **Statistical Learning Theory (Vapnik–Chervonenkis theory)**. It is primarily used for **binary classification**, and by extension, multi-class classification and regression (Support Vector Regression, SVR). The fundamental objective of an SVM is to find an **optimal separating hyperplane** that maximally separates data points belonging to different classes in a feature space, by maximizing the **margin** — the perpendicular distance between the hyperplane and the nearest training samples (called **support vectors**).

In the KTU 2024 syllabus context (Cyber-Physical Systems), SVMs are deployed for **fault detection in CPS**, **intrusion detection in IoT networks**, **anomaly classification in sensor streams**, and **pattern recognition** tasks where decision boundaries must be both **robust** and **geometrically optimal**.

## 2. Intuitive Overview — The "Widest Street" Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — Two Groups, One Street:**
> Imagine two rival groups of students standing on a football field. You, as the principal, must draw a **straight line** between them. But where exactly? A naive choice might be a line that just barely separates the two groups, but if a new student from either group arrives, they might end up on the wrong side of the line.
>
> The **SVM solution**: instead of drawing *any* separating line, draw the line that is **equidistant** from both groups and creates the **widest possible street (margin)** between them. The students standing closest to this street are called the **support vectors** — they alone define the entire boundary. All other students are irrelevant to the final decision line.

This ensures **maximum generalization** — the widest street is most robust against noise, outliers, and new incoming data points.

## 3. Core Terminology (Syllabus-Highlighted Vocabulary)

| Term | Mathematical Symbol | Plain-English Meaning |
|------|--------------------|-----------------------|
| **Hyperplane** | $\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x} + b = 0$ | The decision boundary that separates classes |
| **Weight Vector** | $\mathbf{w}$ | Normal vector perpendicular to the hyperplane |
| **Bias** | $b$ | Offset of the hyperplane from origin |
| **Support Vector** | $\mathbf{x}_i$ with $\alpha_i > 0$ | Training points lying exactly on the margin boundary |
| **Margin** | $\gamma = \dfrac{2}{\Vert\mathbf{w}\Vert}$ | Perpendicular distance between the two margin planes |
| **Slack Variable** | $\xi_i$ | Allowable margin violation in soft-margin SVM |
| **Penalty Parameter** | $C$ | Trade-off controller between margin width and misclassification |
| **Kernel Function** | $K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j)$ | Implicit mapping into higher-dimensional space |

> [!TIP]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** For KTU examinations, you are expected to know the **difference between hard-margin (linearly separable) SVM, soft-margin (linearly non-separable) SVM, and kernel-based non-linear SVM**. Each of these will be analyzed in detail in the following sections.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 1. Geometric Foundations of Linear SVM

### 1.1 The Separating Hyperplane

For a binary classification problem with training set $\{(\mathbf{x}_i, y_i)\}_{i=1}^{N}$ where $\mathbf{x}_i \in \mathbb{R}^d$ and $y_i \in \{-1, +1\}$, the **decision hyperplane** is defined as:

$$
\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x} + b = 0
$$

- Any point with $\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x} + b > 0$ is classified as **+1**
- Any point with $\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x} + b < 0$ is classified as **−1**

### 1.2 Margin Geometry

The **margin** $\gamma$ is the perpendicular distance from the nearest data point to the hyperplane. For the canonical (normalized) hyperplane, where support vectors satisfy $\vert \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x}_i + b \vert = 1$, the margin is:

$$
\gamma = \frac{2}{\Vert\mathbf{w}\Vert}
$$

**Why this matters:** Maximizing $\gamma$ is *equivalent* to minimizing $\Vert\mathbf{w}\Vert^2$. This is the geometric insight that converts a geometric problem into a convex optimization problem.

### 1.3 Classification Confidence

The signed quantity $y_i(\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x}_i + b)$ is called the **functional margin**. Correct classification requires $y_i(\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x}_i + b) > 0$. For SVM, we enforce the stronger condition:

$$
y_i(\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x}_i + b) \geq 1 \quad \forall i \in \{1, 2, \dots, N\}
$$

## 2. Hard-Margin SVM — The Optimization Problem

### 2.1 Primal Problem Formulation

**Objective:** Minimize $\Vert\mathbf{w}\Vert$ subject to correct classification of all training points.

$$
\min_{\mathbf{w}, b} \quad \frac{1}{2} \Vert\mathbf{w}\Vert^2
$$

$$
\text{subject to} \quad y_i (\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x}_i + b) \geq 1, \quad i = 1, 2, \dots, N
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The factor $\frac{1}{2}$ is included for mathematical convenience (makes the derivative of $\Vert\mathbf{w}\Vert^2$ yield $\mathbf{w}$ directly). It does not change the location of the optimum.

### 2.2 Why $\frac{1}{2} \Vert\mathbf{w}\Vert^2$ and not $\Vert\mathbf{w}\Vert$?

- **Convexity:** $\Vert\mathbf{w}\Vert^2$ is a strictly convex quadratic function, guaranteeing a **unique global optimum**.
- **Differentiability:** Smooth gradient enables efficient optimization via Quadratic Programming (QP).
- **Standard form:** Aligns with canonical QP solvers used in industrial SVM libraries (e.g., LIBSVM, SMO).

## 3. Soft-Margin SVM — Handling Non-Separable Data

When classes are **not linearly separable** (which is the realistic CPS scenario due to sensor noise), slack variables $\xi_i \geq 0$ are introduced:

$$
\min_{\mathbf{w}, b, \xi} \quad \frac{1}{2} \Vert\mathbf{w}\Vert^2 + C \sum_{i=1}^{N} \xi_i
$$

$$
\text{subject to} \quad y_i (\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x}_i + b) \geq 1 - \xi_i, \quad \xi_i \geq 0
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Penalty Parameter $C$:**
> - **Large $C$** → Heavy penalty on misclassification → Narrow margin, fewer violations (risk of overfitting).
> - **Small $C$** → Lenient penalty → Wider margin, more violations allowed (risk of underfitting).
> - $C = \infty$ recovers the **hard-margin** case.

## 4. The Kernel Trick — Non-Linear Classification

When a linear boundary cannot separate classes in the original input space, data is implicitly mapped into a higher-dimensional **feature space** $\mathcal{F}$ via a mapping $\phi: \mathbb{R}^d \rightarrow \mathcal{F}$. The kernel function computes the inner product **without explicitly computing** $\phi$:

$$
K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j) = \langle \phi(\mathbf{x}_i), \phi(\mathbf{x}_j) \rangle
$$

This is the **"Kernel Trick"** — it avoids the **curse of dimensionality** by computing inner products efficiently in the original space.

## 5. KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!TIP]
> **Bookmark this table** — these formulas are examinable in both Part A (3-mark) and Part B (14-mark) questions.

| # | Concept | Formula | Notes |
|---|---------|---------|-------|
| 1 | **Decision Hyperplane** | $\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x} + b = 0$ | Defines the separator |
| 2 | **Margin Width** | $\gamma = \dfrac{2}{\Vert\mathbf{w}\Vert}$ | $\Vert\mathbf{w}\Vert = \sqrt{\sum_j w_j^2}$ |
| 3 | **Hard-Margin Primal** | $\min \tfrac{1}{2}\Vert\mathbf{w}\Vert^2$ s.t. $y_i(\mathbf{w}^T\mathbf{x}_i + b) \geq 1$ | Linearly separable case |
| 4 | **Soft-Margin Primal** | $\min \tfrac{1}{2}\Vert\mathbf{w}\Vert^2 + C\sum_i \xi_i$ s.t. $y_i(\mathbf{w}^T\mathbf{x}_i + b) \geq 1 - \xi_i$ | Non-separable case |
| 5 | **Lagrangian** | $\mathcal{L} = \tfrac{1}{2}\Vert\mathbf{w}\Vert^2 - \sum_i \alpha_i[y_i(\mathbf{w}^T\mathbf{x}_i + b) - 1]$ | $\alpha_i \geq 0$ |
| 6 | **Dual Problem** | $\max_{\alpha} \sum_i \alpha_i - \tfrac{1}{2}\sum_i\sum_j \alpha_i \alpha_j y_i y_j \mathbf{x}_i^T \mathbf{x}_j$ | s.t. $\sum_i \alpha_i y_i = 0$, $\alpha_i \geq 0$ |
| 7 | **Decision Function** | $f(\mathbf{x}) = \text{sgn}\!\left(\sum_i \alpha_i y_i K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}) + b\right)$ | At inference |
| 8 | **Linear Kernel** | $K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j) = \mathbf{x}_i^T \mathbf{x}_j$ | Equivalent to no mapping |
| 9 | **Polynomial Kernel** | $K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j) = (\gamma \mathbf{x}_i^T \mathbf{x}_j + r)^d$ | Degree $d$, coef $r$ |
| 10 | **RBF (Gaussian) Kernel** | $K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j) = \exp\!\left(-\gamma \Vert\mathbf{x}_i - \mathbf{x}_j\Vert^2\right)$ | Most popular non-linear kernel |
| 11 | **Sigmoid Kernel** | $K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j) = \tanh(\gamma \mathbf{x}_i^T \mathbf{x}_j + r)$ | Not strictly valid (Mercer) |
| 12 | **KKT Complementarity** | $\alpha_i [y_i(\mathbf{w}^T\mathbf{x}_i + b) - 1] = 0$ | Identifies support vectors |

## 6. Real-World Utility in Engineering (CPS-Focused)

- **Fault Diagnosis in Industrial CPS:** SVM classifies vibration signatures from rotating machinery into "healthy" / "faulty" categories.
- **Intrusion Detection in IoT:** Linear SVM separates normal network traffic from malicious patterns.
- **Medical CPS:** Classifies ECG/EEG signals for arrhythmia detection.
- **Autonomous Vehicles:** Kernel SVM identifies pedestrians, traffic signs, and obstacles.
- **Smart Grid:** Detects anomalous power consumption patterns from smart-meter data streams.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 1. Derivation 1 — From Geometric Margin to Optimization Problem

**Given:** Two parallel margin hyperplanes passing through the support vectors of each class:
$\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x} + b = +1$ (positive support) and $\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x} + b = -1$ (negative support).

**Step 1:** The perpendicular distance from any point $\mathbf{x}_0$ to the hyperplane $\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x} + b = 0$ is given by:

$$
d = \frac{\vert\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x}_0 + b\vert}{\Vert\mathbf{w}\Vert}
$$

**Step 2:** The distance from the positive support hyperplane $\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x} + b = +1$ to the decision hyperplane $\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x} + b = 0$ is computed by substituting $\mathbf{x}_0$ lying on the positive hyperplane (where $\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x}_0 + b = +1$):

$$
d_+ = \frac{1}{\Vert\mathbf{w}\Vert}
$$

**Step 3:** By symmetry, the distance from the negative support hyperplane to the decision hyperplane is identical:

$$
d_- = \frac{1}{\Vert\mathbf{w}\Vert}
$$

**Step 4:** The total margin $\gamma$ is the sum of both distances:

$$
\gamma = d_+ + d_- = \frac{1}{\Vert\mathbf{w}\Vert} + \frac{1}{\Vert\mathbf{w}\Vert} = \frac{2}{\Vert\mathbf{w}\Vert}
$$

**Step 5:** Maximizing the margin $\gamma$ is mathematically equivalent to minimizing its reciprocal, and squaring removes the square root for convexity:

$$
\max_{\mathbf{w}, b} \frac{2}{\Vert\mathbf{w}\Vert} \equiv \min_{\mathbf{w}, b} \frac{1}{2}\Vert\mathbf{w}\Vert^2
$$

This is the **canonical SVM objective** — geometrically motivated but algebraically tractable.

## 2. Derivation 2 — Primal to Dual Conversion via Lagrangian

**Step 1:** Construct the **Lagrangian function** by introducing non-negative multipliers $\alpha_i \geq 0$ for each inequality constraint:

$$
\mathcal{L}(\mathbf{w}, b, \boldsymbol{\alpha}) = \frac{1}{2}\Vert\mathbf{w}\Vert^2 - \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i \left[y_i(\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x}_i + b) - 1\right]
$$

**Step 2:** Apply the **stationarity conditions** by setting partial derivatives to zero (the Lagrangian must be minimized with respect to $\mathbf{w}$ and $b$, maximized with respect to $\alpha$).

Differentiate with respect to $\mathbf{w}$:

$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \mathbf{w}} = \mathbf{w} - \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i y_i \mathbf{x}_i = 0
$$

Solving:

$$
\mathbf{w} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i y_i \mathbf{x}_i
$$

This is the **support vector expansion** — $\mathbf{w}$ is expressed as a linear combination of training points.

**Step 3:** Differentiate with respect to $b$:

$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial b} = -\sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i y_i = 0
$$

Solving:

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i y_i = 0
$$

This is the **equality constraint** of the dual problem.

**Step 4:** Substitute the expressions for $\mathbf{w}$ back into the Lagrangian to eliminate the primal variables and obtain the **Wolfe dual problem**:

$$
\max_{\boldsymbol{\alpha}} \quad \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i - \frac{1}{2}\sum_{i=1}^{N}\sum_{j=1}^{N} \alpha_i \alpha_j y_i y_j (\mathbf{x}_i^T \mathbf{x}_j)
$$

subject to:

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i y_i = 0, \quad \alpha_i \geq 0 \quad \forall i
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why the Dual?** The dual formulation has two major advantages:
> 1. The data appears **only as inner products** $\mathbf{x}_i^T \mathbf{x}_j$, enabling the **kernel trick**.
> 2. The number of variables equals $N$ (number of samples), not $d$ (feature dimension), which is computationally advantageous when $d \gg N$ (common in genomics, text mining).

## 3. Derivation 3 — Kernel Trick and the Feature Map

**Given:** A non-linearly separable dataset in $\mathbb{R}^d$. Map to a higher-dimensional feature space $\mathcal{F}$ via $\phi: \mathbb{R}^d \rightarrow \mathcal{F}$ such that data becomes linearly separable in $\mathcal{F}$.

**Step 1:** The dual objective in feature space becomes:

$$
\max_{\boldsymbol{\alpha}} \sum_i \alpha_i - \frac{1}{2}\sum_i\sum_j \alpha_i \alpha_j y_i y_j \, \phi(\mathbf{x}_i)^T \phi(\mathbf{x}_j)
$$

**Step 2:** Define the **kernel function** as the inner product in feature space:

$$
K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j) = \phi(\mathbf{x}_i)^T \phi(\mathbf{x}_j)
$$

**Step 3:** Substitute into the dual:

$$
\max_{\boldsymbol{\alpha}} \sum_i \alpha_i - \frac{1}{2}\sum_i\sum_j \alpha_i \alpha_j y_i y_j \, K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j)
$$

**Step 4:** The decision function for a new point $\mathbf{x}$ becomes:

$$
f(\mathbf{x}) = \text{sgn}\!\left(\sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i y_i K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}) + b\right)
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Insight:** $\phi(\mathbf{x})$ is **never explicitly computed**. We only need $K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j)$. This is the "trick" — infinite-dimensional mappings (like the RBF kernel) become feasible because we never need coordinates in $\mathcal{F}$.

## 4. Derivation 4 — Mercer's Condition (Valid Kernels)

A function $K(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{z})$ is a valid kernel (i.e., there exists a feature map $\phi$ such that $K = \phi(\mathbf{x})^T \phi(\mathbf{z})$) if and only if $K$ is **symmetric** and **positive semi-definite**, meaning for any finite set $\{\mathbf{x}_1, \dots, \mathbf{x}_N\}$ the **Gram matrix** $G$ is positive semi-definite:

$$
G_{ij} = K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j)
$$

$$
\mathbf{c}^T G \mathbf{c} \geq 0 \quad \forall \mathbf{c} \in \mathbb{R}^N
$$

> [!TIP]
> For **KTU exams**, the standard kernel examples (linear, polynomial, RBF) are guaranteed to satisfy Mercer's condition. The sigmoid kernel is **not** strictly Mercer-compliant for all parameters.

## 5. KKT Conditions — Identifying Support Vectors

The Karush-Kuhn-Tucker (KKT) conditions for the hard-margin SVM primal are:

$$
\alpha_i \geq 0
$$

$$
y_i(\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x}_i + b) - 1 \geq 0
$$

$$
\alpha_i [y_i(\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x}_i + b) - 1] = 0 \quad \text{(complementarity)}
$$

**Implications:**
- If $\alpha_i = 0$: point $\mathbf{x}_i$ is **not a support vector** (lies far from margin or is correctly classified with slack).
- If $\alpha_i > 0$: point $\mathbf{x}_i$ **is a support vector** (lies exactly on the margin).

## 6. Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Property | Hard-Margin SVM | Soft-Margin SVM | Kernel SVM |
|----------|-----------------|-----------------|------------|
| **Data Requirement** | Linearly separable | Allows overlap/misclassification | Non-linearly separable |
| **Slack $\xi_i$** | Not allowed | $\xi_i \geq 0$ permitted | Optional |
| **Parameter $C$** | Not used (effectively $\infty$) | Controls trade-off | Controls trade-off |
| **Feature Space** | Original $\mathbb{R}^d$ | Original $\mathbb{R}^d$ | Mapped to $\mathcal{F}$ via $\phi$ |
| **Inner Product** | $\mathbf{x}_i^T \mathbf{x}_j$ | $\mathbf{x}_i^T \mathbf{x}_j$ | $K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j)$ |
| **KKT Conditions** | $\alpha_i \geq 0$, strict | $\alpha_i \geq 0$, $\xi_i \geq 0$ | Same with $K$ substituted |
| **CPS Use Case** | Ideal sensor data | Noisy sensor streams | Complex fault patterns |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For KTU 2024 Scheme, Part A questions test **definition-level understanding** and **conceptual clarity**. Answers must be precise and within **60–80 words**. Avoid unnecessary elaboration.

### Question 1. [KTU University Exam — Model Question]

> Define a **Support Vector** in the context of SVM. Why are support vectors sufficient to define the optimal hyperplane?

**Model Answer:**

A support vector is a training data point $\mathbf{x}_i$ for which the corresponding Lagrange multiplier $\alpha_i > 0$ in the dual formulation. By the **KKT complementarity condition**, only points lying exactly on the margin hyperplanes (where $y_i(\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x}_i + b) = 1$) become support vectors.

Support vectors are sufficient because the optimal weight vector $\mathbf{w}$ is expressed as $\mathbf{w} = \sum_i \alpha_i y_i \mathbf{x}_i$. Since $\alpha_i = 0$ for all non-support vectors, these points do not contribute to $\mathbf{w}$, and removing them leaves the decision boundary unchanged.

---

### Question 2. [KTU University Exam — Model Question]

> What is the **kernel trick** in SVM? List any **two** commonly used kernel functions.

**Model Answer:**

The kernel trick is a mathematical technique that allows SVM to operate in a high-dimensional feature space $\mathcal{F}$ without explicitly computing the coordinates of data points in $\mathcal{F}$. Instead, it replaces the inner product $\phi(\mathbf{x}_i)^T \phi(\mathbf{x}_j)$ with a kernel function $K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j)$ computed directly in the original input space.

Two commonly used kernels are:
1. **Polynomial Kernel:** $K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j) = (\gamma \mathbf{x}_i^T \mathbf{x}_j + r)^d$
2. **Radial Basis Function (RBF) Kernel:** $K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j) = \exp(-\gamma \Vert\mathbf{x}_i - \mathbf{x}_j\Vert^2)$

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!TIP]
> KTU 2024 Scheme Part B questions in ESE carry **14 marks** and almost always have **internal choice** (you must answer either OR). Each part typically has **two sub-parts of 7 marks each**. Time allocation: **25–30 minutes** per question.

### Question A. [KTU University Exam — Model Question, 14 Marks]

**A.** *(a)* Derive the **primal optimization problem** for a hard-margin SVM from first principles, starting from the geometric definition of the margin. Clearly state all assumptions. *(7 marks)*

*(b)* Explain the role of **slack variables** and the penalty parameter **$C$** in soft-margin SVM. Write the soft-margin primal formulation and discuss how varying $C$ affects the decision boundary. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution for A(a) — Hard-Margin Derivation:

**Step 1 — Define the hyperplane:** For binary classification, let the decision boundary be:

$$
\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x} + b = 0
$$

**Step 2 — Define margin planes:** The two parallel hyperplanes through the support vectors are:

$$
\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x} + b = +1 \quad \text{and} \quad \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x} + b = -1
$$

**Step 3 — Compute margin:** The perpendicular distance between these two planes is:

$$
\gamma = \frac{2}{\Vert\mathbf{w}\Vert}
$$

**Step 4 — Maximize margin:** Maximizing $\gamma$ is equivalent to minimizing $\Vert\mathbf{w}\Vert^2$. Including the $\tfrac{1}{2}$ factor for algebraic convenience:

$$
\min_{\mathbf{w}, b} \quad \frac{1}{2}\Vert\mathbf{w}\Vert^2
$$

**Step 5 — Add classification constraints:** Every training point must lie on the correct side:

$$
y_i(\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x}_i + b) \geq 1 \quad \forall i
$$

**Step 6 — Final primal:**

$$
\min_{\mathbf{w}, b} \quad \frac{1}{2}\Vert\mathbf{w}\Vert^2 \quad \text{s.t.} \quad y_i(\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x}_i + b) \geq 1
$$

#### Model Solution for A(b) — Soft-Margin SVM:

**Step 1 — Recognize non-separability:** Real CPS data often overlaps due to noise. Hard-margin SVM has no feasible solution in such cases.
