<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of t-SNE

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**t-Distributed Stochastic Neighbor Embedding (t-SNE)** is a nonlinear dimensionality reduction technique introduced by Laurens van der Maaten and Geoffrey Hinton in 2008. It is specifically designed for the **visualization of high-dimensional datasets** by mapping them onto a low-dimensional space (typically 2D or 3D), while preserving the **local structure** (neighborhood relationships) of the data.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *t-SNE is a probabilistic, manifold-learning-based technique that converts pairwise similarities between high-dimensional data points into joint probability distributions, then minimizes the Kullback–Leibler (KL) divergence between the high-dimensional and low-dimensional distributions using a gradient descent method, employing a Student's t-distribution in the low-dimensional space to overcome the crowding problem.*

**Key Terminology Decoded:**
- **Stochastic** — Uses random probability distributions rather than deterministic distances.
- **Neighbor Embedding** — Preserves the identity of each point's nearest neighbors.
- **t-Distributed** — Uses the heavy-tailed **Student's t-distribution** (with 1 degree of freedom, equivalent to a Cauchy distribution) in the low-dimensional map.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Galaxy Cartographer"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition — The Star Map Analogy:**
> Imagine you have a 3D photo of the **Milky Way galaxy** with millions of stars. You want to draw it on a flat 2D piece of paper (your notebook) without losing the information about *which stars are close to which*. If you simply project it, stars that were near each other might overlap or get scattered. **t-SNE is the smart "cartographer"** that carefully rearranges the stars on your 2D paper so that stars which were neighbors in the 3D galaxy *remain neighbors* on the paper — while also trying to ensure stars that were far apart *stay far apart*. The "t-distribution" part is what allows the cartographer to spread out distant stars generously (because it has a long tail), preventing them from being crammed into the center.

## 1.3 Where t-SNE is Used in Engineering (Cyber-Physical Systems Context)

- **Anomaly detection** in IoT sensor stream data.
- **Visualization of high-dimensional feature spaces** extracted from CPS time-series (e.g., vibration, temperature, pressure sensors).
- **Clustering analysis** of operating modes in industrial machinery.
- **Dimensionality reduction** before classification in fault-diagnosis pipelines.

> [!TIP]
> **Mandatory Highlight:** t-SNE is primarily a **visualization tool** — not a general-purpose dimensionality reduction method for downstream supervised tasks. For supervised learning pipelines, PCA or UMAP are often preferred.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two Phases of t-SNE

t-SNE operates in two distinct probabilistic phases:

### Phase 1 — High-Dimensional Similarity (Input Space)

For every pair of high-dimensional data points $\mathbf{x}_i$ and $\mathbf{x}_j$, t-SNE computes the conditional probability $p_{j \mid i}$ that $\mathbf{x}_i$ would pick $\mathbf{x}_j$ as its **neighbor**, assuming neighbors are picked proportionally to a **Gaussian (Normal) distribution** centered at $\mathbf{x}_i$.

The bandwidth $\sigma_i$ of this Gaussian is **perplexity-adaptive** (set via binary search) so that each point has a balanced effective number of neighbors.

### Phase 2 — Low-Dimensional Similarity (Map Space)

In the low-dimensional map, t-SNE places each point as $\mathbf{y}_i \in \mathbb{R}^d$ (with $d = 2$ typically). Similarity between map points uses a **Student's t-distribution with 1 degree of freedom** (Cauchy distribution), which has a much heavier tail than the Gaussian.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Table 2.1 — Master Formula Sheet for t-SNE**

| # | Concept | Formula | Purpose / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Conditional probability (high-dim) | $p_{j \mid i} = \dfrac{\exp\!\left(-\,\dfrac{\Vert \mathbf{x}_i - \mathbf{x}_j \Vert^{2}}{2\sigma_i^{2}}\right)}{\sum_{k \neq i} \exp\!\left(-\,\dfrac{\Vert \mathbf{x}_i - \mathbf{x}_k \Vert^{2}}{2\sigma_i^{2}}\right)}$ | Probability $i$ chooses $j$ as neighbor |
| 2 | Joint probability (high-dim) | $p_{ij} = \dfrac{p_{j \mid i} + p_{i \mid j}}{2N}$ | **Symmetrized**; ensures $p_{ij} = p_{ji}$ |
| 3 | Perplexity constraint | $\text{Perp}(P_i) = 2^{H(P_i)}$, where $H(P_i) = -\sum_j p_{j \mid i} \log_2 p_{j \mid i}$ | User-set hyperparameter (typically **5–50**) |
| 4 | Low-dim similarity (Student-t, dof = 1) | $q_{ij} = \dfrac{\left(1 + \Vert \mathbf{y}_i - \mathbf{y}_j \Vert^{2}\right)^{-1}}{\sum_{k \neq l}\left(1 + \Vert \mathbf{y}_k - \mathbf{y}_l \Vert^{2}\right)^{-1}}$ | Heavy-tailed; tackles crowding |
| 5 | Cost function (KL divergence) | $C = KL(P \Vert Q) = \sum_{i \neq j} p_{ij} \log\!\left(\dfrac{p_{ij}}{q_{ij}}\right)$ | Measures mismatch between distributions |
| 6 | Gradient of cost | $\dfrac{\partial C}{\partial \mathbf{y}_i} = 4 \sum_j \left(p_{ij} - q_{ij}\right)\left(\mathbf{y}_i - \mathbf{y}_j\right)\left(1 + \Vert \mathbf{y}_i - \mathbf{y}_j \Vert^{2}\right)^{-1}$ | Used in gradient descent |
| 7 | Update rule | $\mathcal{Y}^{(t+1)} = \mathcal{Y}^{(t)} + \eta \,\dfrac{\partial C}{\partial \mathcal{Y}} + \alpha(t)\,\Delta \mathcal{Y}^{(t-1)}$ | With momentum $\alpha(t)$ and learning rate $\eta$ |

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Notation Rule:** Use `\Vert` (or `\mid`) in LaTeX for norm bars — **never** raw `\vert` or `|` — to avoid markdown table breakage.

## 2.3 The "Why" Behind Each Step

- **Why Gaussian in high-dim?** Because local neighborhoods in the original space are dense and approximately Euclidean-normally distributed.
- **Why Student's t in low-dim?** The volume of a 2D sphere grows slower than a 10D sphere, so moderate-distance points in high-D would map to uncomfortably close points in 2D (the **crowding problem**). The heavy tail of t-distribution lets moderately distant map points repel each other strongly.
- **Why KL divergence and not MSE?** KL is asymmetric — it heavily penalizes placing *nearby* points far apart, while tolerating placing *distant* points close together. This perfectly matches the goal of preserving **local** structure.

## 2.4 Practical Engineering Utility

In CPS analytics, t-SNE visualizes:
- **Vibration signatures** from rotating machinery (motor, turbine).
- **Network traffic clusters** for intrusion detection in IoT gateways.
- **Sensor-fusion feature maps** before feeding into LSTM or CNN classifiers.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the High-Dimensional Conditional Probability $p_{j \mid i}$

We wish to assign a probability that point $\mathbf{x}_i$ picks point $\mathbf{x}_j$ as its neighbor, based on a Gaussian centered at $\mathbf{x}_i$.

$$
p_{j \mid i} = \frac{\exp\!\left(-\dfrac{\Vert \mathbf{x}_i - \mathbf{x}_j \Vert^{2}}{2\sigma_i^{2}}\right)}{\sum_{k \neq i} \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{\Vert \mathbf{x}_i - \mathbf{x}_k \Vert^{2}}{2\sigma_i^{2}}\right)}
$$

### Step-by-Step Reasoning

**Step 1:** Begin with a Gaussian kernel (un-normalized similarity):

$$
w_{j \mid i} = \exp\!\left(-\frac{\Vert \mathbf{x}_i - \mathbf{x}_j \Vert^{2}}{2\sigma_i^{2}}\right)
$$

> *Logic:* When $\mathbf{x}_j$ is near $\mathbf{x}_i$, the squared distance is small, so the exponent is close to 0, and $w \approx 1$. When far, distance is large, exponent $\to -\infty$, so $w \to 0$.

**Step 2:** Normalize so that probabilities sum to 1 over all $j \neq i$:

$$
\sum_{j \neq i} p_{j \mid i} = 1
$$

**Step 3:** Set $p_{i \mid i} = 0$ (a point cannot be its own neighbor):

$$
p_{j \mid i} = \frac{w_{j \mid i}}{\sum_{k \neq i} w_{k \mid i}}, \quad p_{i \mid i} = 0
$$

## 3.2 Derivation of the Symmetric Joint Probability $p_{ij}$

The conditional $p_{j \mid i}$ is **asymmetric** — that is, $p_{j \mid i} \neq p_{i \mid j}$. We symmetrize using:

$$
p_{ij} = \frac{p_{j \mid i} + p_{i \mid j}}{2N}
$$

### Step-by-Step Reasoning

**Step 1:** For an outlier point $\mathbf{x}_i$ that has no close neighbors, all $p_{j \mid i}$ become extremely tiny, making $p_{ij}$ negligibly small. This causes the outlier to have an excessively strong attractive force in the map.

**Step 2:** Symmetrization fixes this: even an outlier has *some* probability mass contributed *to* it by its (few) actual neighbors, stabilizing the optimization.

**Step 3:** The factor $1/2N$ ensures that $\sum_{i,j} p_{ij} = 1$, making it a valid joint probability distribution.

## 3.3 Perplexity — Choosing $\sigma_i$

The perplexity of a discrete distribution $P_i$ is defined as:

$$
\text{Perp}(P_i) = 2^{H(P_i)}
$$

where the Shannon entropy is:

$$
H(P_i) = -\sum_{j \neq i} p_{j \mid i} \log_{2} p_{j \mid i}
$$

### Step-by-Step Reasoning

**Step 1:** Fix a target perplexity $\text{Perp}_{\text{user}}$ (e.g., 30).

**Step 2:** Perform a **binary search** over $\sigma_i$ to find the value such that $\text{Perp}(P_i)$ equals $\text{Perp}_{\text{user}}$.

**Step 3:** Larger $\sigma_i$ → broader Gaussian → smoother distribution → higher perplexity.

## 3.4 Derivation of the Low-Dimensional Similarity $q_{ij}$

We define $q_{ij}$ using the **Student's t-distribution with 1 degree of freedom** (Cauchy):

$$
q_{ij} = \frac{\left(1 + \Vert \mathbf{y}_i - \mathbf{y}_j \Vert^{2}\right)^{-1}}{\sum_{k \neq l}\left(1 + \Vert \mathbf{y}_k - \mathbf{y}_l \Vert^{2}\right)^{-1}}
$$

with $q_{ii} = 0$.

### Step-by-Step Reasoning

**Step 1:** Recall the standard Cauchy PDF form is $\propto (1 + x^{2})^{-1}$.

**Step 2:** Substitute $x = \Vert \mathbf{y}_i - \mathbf{y}_j \Vert$ to obtain a symmetric kernel.

**Step 3:** Because the denominator involves $\sum_{k \neq l}$ (not $\sum_{k \neq i}$), $q_{ij}$ is naturally **symmetric**.

**Step 4:** The heavy tail $(1 + d^{2})^{-1}$ decays much slower than the Gaussian $\exp(-d^{2}/2)$, allowing moderately distant map points to retain meaningful repulsion.

## 3.5 Cost Function — Kullback–Leibler Divergence

$$
C \;=\; KL(P \Vert Q) \;=\; \sum_{i \neq j} p_{ij} \log\!\left(\frac{p_{ij}}{q_{ij}}\right)
$$

> *Logic:* If $p_{ij}$ is large (close pair in high-D) and $q_{ij}$ is small (the map separates them), the cost is *high*. Conversely, if $p_{ij}$ is small and $q_{ij}$ is large, the cost is *low*. This **asymmetry** preserves local neighborhoods preferentially.

## 3.6 Gradient Descent Update

Differentiating $C$ with respect to $\mathbf{y}_i$ (using the chain rule on $q_{ij}$):

$$
\frac{\partial C}{\partial \mathbf{y}_i} \;=\; 4 \sum_{j} \bigl(p_{ij} - q_{ij}\bigr)\bigl(\mathbf{y}_i - \mathbf{y}_j\bigr)\bigl(1 + \Vert \mathbf{y}_i - \mathbf{y}_j \Vert^{2}\bigr)^{-1}
$$

### Step-by-Step Reasoning

**Step 1:** Interpret $(p_{ij} - q_{ij})$ as a **residual force**:
- If $p_{ij} > q_{ij}$ → too far in the map → **attractive force** pulling $\mathbf{y}_i$ toward $\mathbf{y}_j$.
- If $p_{ij} < q_{ij}$ → too close in the map → **repulsive force** pushing $\mathbf{y}_i$ away from $\mathbf{y}_j$.

**Step 2:** The factor $\bigl(1 + \Vert \mathbf{y}_i - \mathbf{y}_j \Vert^{2}\bigr)^{-1}$ inverts the pairwise distance — large when close, small when far.

**Step 3:** Combine with momentum:

$$
\mathcal{Y}^{(t+1)} \;=\; \mathcal{Y}^{(t)} + \eta \,\frac{\partial C}{\partial \mathcal{Y}} + \alpha(t)\,\Delta \mathcal{Y}^{(t-1)}
$$

> *Logic:* Momentum helps escape shallow local minima in the early phase and stabilizes convergence later (early exaggeration trick).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Algorithmic Flow Diagram of t-SNE

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Start: Input high-dimensional dataset X = x1 to xN"] --> B["Step 1: Compute pairwise Euclidean distances d(xi, xj)"]
    B --> C["Step 2: For each xi, run binary search on sigma_i to match target perplexity"]
    C --> D["Step 3: Compute conditional probabilities p j|i using Gaussian kernel"]
    D --> E["Step 4: Symmetrize to obtain joint probability p_ij = (p j|i + p i|j) divided by 2N"]
    E --> F["Step 5: Initialize low-dimensional map Y with small random values"]
    F --> G["Step 6: Compute low-dim similarity q_ij using Student t-distribution"]
    G --> H["Step 7: Compute cost C = KL divergence between P and Q"]
    H --> I{"Step 8: Converged or max iterations reached?"}
    I -->|"No"| J["Step 9: Compute gradient dC/dY and update Y with momentum"]
    J --> G
    I -->|"Yes"| K["Step 10: Output 2D map Y for visualization"]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT["Input Layer"]
        X["High-Dimensional Data X in R to the D"]
    end
    subgraph PROC1["High-Dim Similarity Engine"]
        G["Gaussian Kernel with Perplexity-Based Sigma_i"]
        S["Symmetrizer producing Joint P"]
    end
    subgraph PROC2["Low-Dim Map Engine"]
        T["Student-t Kernel on Map Points Y"]
        Q["Joint Distribution Q in R to the d"]
    end
    subgraph OPT["Optimization Core"]
        KL["KL Divergence Calculator: C = sum p_ij log p_ij over q_ij"]
        GD["Gradient Descent with Momentum"]
    end
    subgraph OUT["Output"]
        Y["Low-Dim Embedding Y in R 2 or R 3 for Visualization"]
    end
    X --> G --> S --> KL
    X --> T --> Q --> KL
    KL --> GD --> Y
    GD -.feedback.-> T
```

## 4.3 Conceptual Comparison: Gaussian vs Student-t Kernel Behavior

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Two points at moderate distance in high-D"] --> B{"Which kernel is used?"}
    B -->|"High-D: Gaussian"| C["Strong attraction since Gaussian decays fast"]
    B -->|"Low-D: Student-t (heavy tail)"| D["Moderate repulsion since tail decays slowly"]
    C --> E["Allows distant map points to spread apart solving crowding"]
    D --> E
```

## 4.4 Hyperparameter Influence Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P["Perplexity: 5 to 50"] --> O1["Controls effective neighbor count"]
    P --> O2["Affects sigma_i bandwidth"]
    LR1["Learning Rate eta"] --> O3["Step size in gradient descent"]
    LR1 --> O4["Too high: divergence; Too low: slow"]
    MOM["Momentum alpha"] --> O5["Smooths updates"]
    EE["Early Exaggeration Factor"] --> O6["Multiplies P by large constant in first 50 to 100 iterations"]
    EE --> O7["Forces wide initial separation of clusters"]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model]`
**Define t-SNE. Why is the Student t-distribution used in the low-dimensional space instead of a Gaussian?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Definition (1.5 marks):** t-SNE (t-Distributed Stochastic Neighbor Embedding) is a nonlinear dimensionality reduction technique that models pairwise similarities between high-dimensional data points as conditional probabilities using a Gaussian distribution, then learns a low-dimensional embedding by minimizing the KL divergence between the high-D and low-D probability distributions, with the low-D distribution modeled using a Student's t-distribution.
- **Reason for t-distribution (1.5 marks):** The Student's t-distribution has a heavier tail than the Gaussian, which allows moderately distant points in the low-D map to remain sufficiently separated. This mitigates the **crowding problem**, where points that are moderately distant in high-D get crammed too closely in low-D due to the insufficient "area" available around each point.

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]`
**What is perplexity in t-SNE? How is $\sigma_i$ determined from it?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Perplexity definition (1.5 marks):** Perplexity is a user-defined hyperparameter (typically 5–50) that smoothly approximates the effective number of neighbors for each point. Mathematically, it equals $2^{H(P_i)}$ where $H(P_i)$ is the Shannon entropy of the conditional distribution $P_i$.
- **Determination of $\sigma_i$ (1.5 marks):** For each data point $\mathbf{x}_i$, a binary search is performed over $\sigma_i$ such that the perplexity of the resulting conditional distribution $P_i$ matches the user-defined target perplexity.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, 14 Marks]`

**(a)** Derive the conditional probability $p_{j \mid i}$ used by t-SNE to model high-dimensional similarities. Explain how the bandwidth $\sigma_i$ is chosen using perplexity. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Explain the KL divergence cost function used in t-SNE. Derive the gradient update rule with momentum and discuss the role of early exaggeration. **[7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution for Part (a):

**Step 1 (2 marks):** Define the un-normalized similarity using a Gaussian kernel centered at $\mathbf{x}_i$:

$$
w_{j \mid i} = \exp\!\left(-\frac{\Vert \mathbf{x}_i - \mathbf{x}_j \Vert^{2}}{2\sigma_i^{2}}\right)
$$

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Normalize so that $\sum_{j \neq i} p_{j \mid i} = 1$ and set $p_{i \mid i} = 0$:

$$
p_{j \mid i} = \frac{\exp\!\left(-\,\dfrac{\Vert \mathbf{x}_i - \mathbf{x}_j \Vert^{2}}{2\sigma_i^{2}}\right)}{\sum_{k \neq i} \exp\!\left(-\,\dfrac{\Vert \mathbf{x}_i - \mathbf{x}_k \Vert^{2}}{2\sigma_i^{2}}\right)}
$$

**Step 3 (1.5 marks):** Perplexity is defined as $\text{Perp}(P_i) = 2^{H(P_i)}$, with $H(P_i) = -\sum_j p_{j \mid i} \log_2 p_{j \mid i}$. A user-specified perplexity (5–50) is set.

**Step 4 (1.5 marks):** A binary search is run over $\sigma_i$ until $\text{Perp}(P_i)$ matches the user-set perplexity, ensuring each point has a balanced effective neighborhood size.

#### Model Solution for Part (b):

**Step 1 (2 marks):** KL divergence measures mismatch between two distributions:

$$
C = KL(P \Vert Q) = \sum_{i \neq j} p_{ij} \log\!\left(\frac{p_{ij}}{q_{ij}}\right)
$$

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Differentiating with respect to $\mathbf{y}_i$ yields the gradient:

$$
\frac{\partial C}{\partial \mathbf{y}_i} = 4 \sum_{j} (p_{ij} - q_{ij})(\mathbf{y}_i - \mathbf{y}_j)(1 + \Vert \mathbf{y}_i - \mathbf{y}_j \Vert^{2})^{-1}
$$

**Step 3 (1.5 marks):** The update rule with momentum:

$$
\mathcal{Y}^{(t+1)} = \mathcal{Y}^{(t)} + \eta \,\frac{\partial C}{\partial \mathcal{Y}} + \alpha(t)\,\Delta \mathcal{Y}^{(t-1)}
$$

**Step 4 (1.5 marks):** Early exaggeration multiplies all $p_{ij}$ by a large constant (e.g., 12) during the first 50–100 iterations. This artificially inflates attractive forces, causing clusters to separate widely initially; then the multiplier is set back to 1 for fine-grained local refinement.

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, 14 Marks — Alternative Choice]`

**(a)** Explain the crowding problem in dimensionality reduction. How does t-SNE's use of the Student's t-distribution resolve it? **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Describe the complete t-SNE algorithm with its key phases: high-D similarity computation, low-D similarity computation, cost function formulation, and optimization. Use appropriate equations. **[7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution for Part (a):

**Step 1 (2 marks):** When high-D data is mapped to 2D/3D, the available "volume" around each point is drastically smaller. Even moderate distances in high-D become unattainable in low-D.

**Step 2 (2 marks):** As a result, points that should be separated end up clustered tightly in the center, distorting the local structure — this is the **crowding problem**.

**Step 3 (1.5 marks):** t-SNE uses the Student's t-distribution (Cauchy) in the low-D space with kernel $(1 + \Vert \mathbf{y}_i - \mathbf{y}_j \Vert^{2})^{-1}$. Its heavy tail decays slowly, so moderately distant points retain a relatively large $q_{ij}$.

**Step 4 (1.5 marks):** This means that even pairs that are not very close in low-D still repel each other strongly, allowing clusters to spread out and preserving the local geometry of the original manifold.

#### Model Solution for Part (b):

**Phase 1 — High-D Similarity (2 marks):** Compute $p_{j \mid i}$ using Gaussian kernel with perplexity-tuned $\sigma_i$, then symmetrize:

$$
p_{ij} = \frac{p_{j \mid i} + p_{i \mid j}}{2N}
$$

**Phase 2 — Low-D Similarity (2 marks):** Initialize $\mathbf{y}_i$ randomly. Compute $q_{ij}$ using Student's t-distribution:

$$
q_{ij} = \frac{(1 + \Vert \mathbf{y}_i - \mathbf{y}_j \Vert^{2})^{-1}}{\sum_{k \neq l}(1 + \Vert \mathbf{y}_k - \mathbf{y}_l \Vert^{2})^{-1}}
$$

**Phase 3 — Cost Function (1.5 marks):**

$$
C = \sum_{i \neq j} p_{ij} \log\!\left(\frac{p_{ij}}{q_{ij}}\right)
$$

**Phase 4 — Optimization (1.5 marks):** Apply gradient descent with momentum and early exaggeration:

$$
\mathcal{Y}^{(t+1)} = \mathcal{Y}^{(t)} + \eta \,\frac{\partial C}{\partial \mathcal{Y}} + \alpha(t)\,\Delta \mathcal{Y}^{(t-1)}
$$

Run for typically 1000 iterations until convergence.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Forgetting to set $p_{ii} = 0$** — the diagonal terms must be explicitly zeroed.
> 2. **Writing symmetric $p_{j \mid i} = p_{i \mid j}$** in the conditional step — they are *asymmetric*; only after symmetrization does $p_{ij} = p_{ji}$.
> 3. **Using Gaussian instead of Student's t in low-D** — examiners deduct heavily for this since the whole "t" in t-SNE is justified by the heavy tail.
> 4. **Skipping the gradient derivation** — for 7-mark questions, KTU expects the explicit formula with all four factors visible.
> 5. **Confusing perplexity with number of clusters** — perplexity is not the number of clusters, but a soft measure of effective neighbors.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — t-SNE**

- ✅ t-SNE = **t-Distributed Stochastic Neighbor Embedding** — primarily a **visualization** technique for high-D data.
- ✅ Phase 1 uses a **Gaussian** kernel in high-D; Phase 2 uses a **Student's t-distribution (dof = 1)** in low-D.
- ✅ Perplexity is a **smooth measure of effective neighbors**; $\sigma_i$ is set per point via binary search.
- ✅ The joint probability $p_{ij}$ is **symmetrized** to fix outliers.
- ✅ Cost function is the **KL divergence** $KL(P \Vert Q)$ — asymmetric, favors preserving local structure.
- ✅ Gradient has the elegant force interpretation: $(p_{ij} - q_{ij})$ acts as attractive or repulsive residual.
- ✅ **Crowding problem** is solved by the heavy tail of the Student's t-distribution.
- ✅ Optimization uses **gradient descent with momentum** and **early exaggeration**.
- ✅ t-SNE is **non-deterministic** (random initialization) and computationally **expensive** ($\mathcal{O}(N^{2})$).
- ✅ In CPS engineering, t-SNE helps visualize high-D sensor signatures for anomaly and fault diagnosis.
- ✅ **Do not use t-SNE embeddings as direct input to downstream supervised learners** — they are not stable across runs.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fundamentals of RL
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT635 - MACHINE LEARNING FOR CPS
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Fundamentals of RL

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

**Reinforcement Learning (RL)** is a goal-directed computational framework within the broader paradigm of **Machine Learning for Cyber-Physical Systems (CPS)** in which an autonomous **agent** learns an optimal **policy** of sequential decision-making by interacting with a stochastic **environment**, perceiving its **state**, executing **actions**, and receiving scalar **reward** signals. The agent's objective is to maximize the expected cumulative **return** over time, formalized through the **Markov Decision Process (MDP)** mathematical scaffold.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board Examiner Verbatim Expectation):**
> *"Reinforcement Learning is a machine learning paradigm concerned with how an agent ought to take actions in an environment so as to maximize the notion of cumulative reward. The agent is not told which actions to take, but instead must discover which actions yield the most reward by trying them out — a trial-and-error search combined with delayed reward."*

## 1.2 The Three Pillars Distinguishing RL

RL sits at the intersection of three major disciplines. Understanding these pillars is critical for KTU answers:

| Pillar | Description | Connection to CPS |
|---|---|---|
| **Supervised Learning Trait** | Uses labeled information (rewards) | Robot receives feedback signals |
| **Unsupervised Learning Trait** | Discovers structure without explicit instruction | Self-exploration of state space |
| **Optimal Control Trait** | Maximizes cumulative objective under dynamics | Trajectory planning in autonomous vehicles |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: Teaching a Pet Dog New Tricks**
> Imagine you are training a puppy to fetch a ball. You do not write a program telling the puppy *exactly* which muscle to contract at each millisecond. Instead:
> - **Agent** → The puppy (the decision-maker)
> - **Environment** → The room, the ball, the floor, gravity
> - **State (s)** → Where the puppy and ball are right now
> - **Action (a)** → Move paw, run, bite, sit
> - **Reward (r)** → Treat when it brings the ball, scolding when it chews the sofa
> - **Policy (π)** → The puppy's internal "strategy" (e.g., "when I see the ball, run towards it")
> - **Return (G)** → Total treats earned over the entire play session
>
> Over hundreds of repetitions, the puppy's policy improves — **not by being told, but by experiencing consequences of its actions.** This is the essence of RL.

## 1.4 Why RL is Crucial for Cyber-Physical Systems (CPS)

In CPS (autonomous drones, smart grids, robotic manipulators, ADAS), it is often **impossible to write explicit control laws** for every contingency. RL provides a data-driven mechanism for systems to *self-tune* their behavior in continuous, high-dimensional, real-time physical environments.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Standard Metric (Bold for KTU):** The **discount factor** $\gamma \in [0, 1]$ — usually set between **0.9 and 0.99** in practice. It is the **single most important hyperparameter** governing the trade-off between *immediate* and *long-term* rewards.

---

## 1.5 Core Terminology Snapshot

- **Agent** — The learner / decision-maker.
- **Environment** — Everything outside the agent that it cannot control arbitrarily.
- **State ($s_t$)** — A complete description of the environment at time $t$ (Markovian).
- **Action ($a_t$)** — The choice the agent makes from the action space $\mathcal{A}$.
- **Reward ($r_t$)** — A scalar feedback signal; the *only* metric of success.
- **Policy ($\pi$)** — The agent's behavior function mapping states to actions.
- **Return ($G_t$)** — Cumulative discounted reward from time $t$ onward.
- **Episode** — A finite sequence of interactions terminating at a terminal state.
- **Trajectory / Rollout** — The full sequence of (s, a, r) tuples generated under a policy.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Markov Decision Process (MDP) — The Backbone of RL

The entire mathematical machinery of finite RL rests on the **Markov Decision Process** tuple. Every KTU question on RL fundamentals is built on this.

**MDP Tuple:** $\mathcal{M} = \langle \mathcal{S}, \mathcal{A}, P, R, \gamma \rangle$

### Component-wise Breakdown

1. **State Space $\mathcal{S}$** — Set of all possible states. Can be **discrete** (gridworld) or **continuous** (joint angles of a robot arm). $\mathcal{S}$ may be finite or infinite.
2. **Action Space $\mathcal{A}$** — Set of all valid actions. Discrete (Left/Right) or continuous (torque values in Nm).
3. **Transition Probability Function $P$** — Defined formally as:

$$P(s' \mid s, a) = \Pr\{S_{t+1} = s' \mid S_t = s, A_t = a\}$$

This satisfies the **stochasticity axiom**: $\sum_{s' \in \mathcal{S}} P(s' \mid s, a) = 1$ for all $s, a$.

4. **Reward Function $R$** — $R(s, a, s')$ or $R(s, a)$ or $R(s')$, depending on formulation. In KTU, the most common is the **$R(s, a, s')$** form:

$$r_{t+1} = R(S_t, A_t, S_{t+1})$$

5. **Discount Factor $\gamma \in [0, 1)$** — Reduces the present value of distant rewards.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why $\gamma < 1$ matters (KTU Hot Topic):**
> - In **continuing tasks** (no terminal state), $\gamma < 1$ is **mathematically required** to prevent the return $G_t$ from diverging to infinity.
> - $\gamma \to 0$ → agent is *myopic* (only cares about immediate reward).
> - $\gamma \to 1$ → agent is *far-sighted* (future rewards weighted almost equally).

## 2.2 The Markov Property (Critical for KTU)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (The Markov Property):**
> A state $S_t$ is *Markovian* if and only if the future is conditionally independent of the past given the present:
> $$\Pr\{S_{t+1} = s' \mid S_t = s, A_t = a\} = \Pr\{S_{t+1} = s' \mid S_0, A_0, S_1, A_1, \ldots, S_t, A_t\}$$
> In plain English: **"The state contains all relevant history."** A chess board position is Markovian; a single photograph without prior moves is not.

## 2.3 Policy — The Agent's Strategy

A policy $\pi$ is a distribution over actions given states:

$$\pi(a \mid s) = \Pr\{A_t = a \mid S_t = s\}$$

Two flavors:
- **Deterministic Policy:** $a = \pi(s)$. Same action every time in a given state.
- **Stochastic Policy:** Returns a probability distribution. Often needed in adversarial / partial-information settings.

## 2.4 Value Functions — The Heart of RL Evaluation

### State-Value Function $V^\pi(s)$

$$V^\pi(s) = \mathbb{E}_\pi \!\left[ G_t \mid S_t = s \right] = \mathbb{E}_\pi \!\left[ \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^k R_{t+k+1} \,\Big|\, S_t = s \right]$$

> *"Expected return when starting in state $s$ and following policy $\pi$ thereafter."*

### Action-Value Function $Q^\pi(s, a)$

$$Q^\pi(s, a) = \mathbb{E}_\pi \!\left[ G_t \mid S_t = s, A_t = a \right]$$

> *"Expected return when starting in $s$, taking action $a$, then following $\pi$."*

**Relation between $V$ and $Q$:**

$$V^\pi(s) = \sum_{a \in \mathcal{A}} \pi(a \mid s) \, Q^\pi(s, a)$$

## 2.5 The Bellman Equation (Board Examiner Gold Mine)

### 2.5.1 Bellman Expectation Equation for $V^\pi$

$$V^\pi(s) = \sum_{a} \pi(a \mid s) \sum_{s', r} p(s', r \mid s, a) \left[ r + \gamma \, V^\pi(s') \right]$$

### 2.5.2 Bellman Expectation Equation for $Q^\pi$

$$Q^\pi(s, a) = \sum_{s', r} p(s', r \mid s, a) \left[ r + \gamma \sum_{a'} \pi(a' \mid s') \, Q^\pi(s', a') \right]$$

### 2.5.3 Bellman Optimality Equation

$$V^*(s) = \max_{a} \sum_{s', r} p(s', r \mid s, a) \left[ r + \gamma \, V^*(s') \right]$$

$$Q^*(s, a) = \sum_{s', r} p(s', r \mid s, a) \left[ r + \gamma \max_{a'} Q^*(s', a') \right]$$

## 2.6 The Exploration–Exploitation Trade-off

> [!NOTE]
> **The Fundamental Dilemma (KTU Favorite 2-Mark Question):**
> - **Exploitation** → Use current best-known action to gain reward.
> - **Exploration** → Try a new action to discover potentially better rewards.
> - **Pure exploitation** → may get stuck in local optima.
> - **Pure exploration** → never capitalizes on learning.
>
> **Common strategies:** $\epsilon$-greedy, Softmax (Boltzmann), Upper Confidence Bound (UCB), Thompson Sampling.

## 2.7 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (Print This!)

| # | Concept | Formula | Notation / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Return (Cumulative Discounted Reward) | $G_t = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^k R_{t+k+1}$ | Scalar, dimensionless |
| 2 | Discount Factor | $\gamma \in [0, 1)$ | Dimensionless |
| 3 | State Transition Probability | $P(s' \mid s, a)$ | Probability $\in [0,1]$ |
| 4 | Stochastic Policy | $\pi(a \mid s)$ | Probability $\in [0,1]$ |
| 5 | State-Value Function | $V^\pi(s) = \mathbb{E}_\pi[G_t \mid S_t = s]$ | Scalar |
| 6 | Action-Value Function | $Q^\pi(s, a) = \mathbb{E}_\pi[G_t \mid S_t = s, A_t = a]$ | Scalar |
| 7 | Bellman Expectation (V) | $V^\pi(s) = \sum_a \pi(a \mid s) \sum_{s'} P(s' \mid s, a)[R + \gamma V^\pi(s')]$ | Scalar |
| 8 | Bellman Expectation (Q) | $Q^\pi(s, a) = \sum_{s'} P(s' \mid s, a)[R + \gamma \sum_{a'} \pi(a' \mid s') Q^\pi(s', a')]$ | Scalar |
| 9 | Bellman Optimality (V) | $V^*(s) = \max_a \sum_{s'} P(s' \mid s, a)[R + \gamma V^*(s')]$ | Scalar |
| 10 | Bellman Optimality (Q) | $Q^*(s, a) = \sum_{s'} P(s' \mid s, a)[R + \gamma \max_{a'} Q^*(s', a')]$ | Scalar |
| 11 | Average Reward (undiscounted) | $\rho^\pi = \lim_{T\to\infty} \tfrac{1}{T} \mathbb{E}[\sum_{t=0}^{T-1} R_{t+1}]$ | Scalar |
| 12 | Optimal Policy from Q* | $\pi^*(a \mid s) = 1$ if $a = \arg\max_a Q^*(s, a)$ else 0 | Deterministic |

## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Autonomous Vehicles (CPS):** RL learns lane-change and overtaking policies where hand-coded rules fail.
- **Robotic Manipulation:** Deep RL (e.g., DDPG, SAC) trains robot arms in simulation (Sim2Real transfer).
- **Energy Grid Management:** RL schedules loads in smart grids to minimize cost and peak demand.
- **HVAC Control:** RL tunes building climate control with $\sim$20% energy savings reported in literature.
- **Inventory & Supply Chain:** RL treats reorder decisions as sequential actions under stochastic demand.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From Return to Bellman Equation (Complete, No Steps Skipped)

This derivation is **routinely asked as a 7-mark question** in KTU Module 3. Every transition is shown.

### Step 1: Definition of Return

By definition, the return starting at time $t$ is the sum of discounted future rewards:

$$G_t \equiv R_{t+1} + \gamma R_{t+2} + \gamma^2 R_{t+3} + \ldots = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^k R_{t+k+1}$$

### Step 2: Recursive Decomposition of $G_t$

Split the sum into the first term and the rest:

$$G_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^k R_{t+k+2}$$

Recognize that the remaining sum is exactly $G_{t+1}$:

$$G_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma \, G_{t+1}$$

**This is the celebrated recursive return relation.**

### Step 3: Introduce the State-Value Function

$$V^\pi(s) = \mathbb{E}_\pi[G_t \mid S_t = s]$$

Substitute the recursion from Step 2:

$$V^\pi(s) = \mathbb{E}_\pi\!\left[ R_{t+1} + \gamma G_{t+1} \,\Big|\, S_t = s \right]$$

### Step 4: Apply Linearity of Expectation

$$V^\pi(s) = \mathbb{E}_\pi\!\left[ R_{t+1} \mid S_t = s \right] + \gamma \, \mathbb{E}_\pi\!\left[ G_{t+1} \mid S_t = s \right]$$

### Step 5: Condition on Next Action and Next State

Use the **law of total expectation** by conditioning on $A_t = a$ and $S_{t+1} = s'$:

$$V^\pi(s) = \sum_{a} \pi(a \mid s) \sum_{s', r} p(s', r \mid s, a) \left[ r + \gamma \, \mathbb{E}_\pi[G_{t+1} \mid S_{t+1} = s'] \right]$$

### Step 6: Recognize the Inner Expectation as $V^\pi(s')$

By definition, $\mathbb{E}_\pi[G_{t+1} \mid S_{t+1} = s'] = V^\pi(s')$. Substitute:

$$V^\pi(s) = \sum_{a} \pi(a \mid s) \sum_{s', r} p(s', r \mid s, a) \left[ r + \gamma \, V^\pi(s') \right]$$

**This is the Bellman Expectation Equation for $V^\pi$.** $\blacksquare$

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiners' Tip:** Always state the "law of total expectation" by name when applying Step 5 — it earns you 1 bonus mark and demonstrates conceptual depth.

## 3.2 Derivation: Optimal Policy Extraction from $Q^*$

### Given
You have computed the optimal action-value function $Q^*(s, a)$ for all $(s, a)$.

### Goal
Derive the optimal deterministic policy $\pi^*$.

### Proof

By definition of optimality, the optimal policy maximizes expected return:

$$\pi^* = \arg\max_\pi \, \mathbb{E}_\pi[G_t \mid S_t = s]$$

From the Bellman Optimality equation:

$$V^*(s) = \max_{a} Q^*(s, a)$$

Since $Q^*(s, a)$ already encodes the *expected return of taking action $a$ in state $s$ and behaving optimally thereafter*, the optimal policy simply **picks the action with the highest $Q^*$-value**:

$$\pi^*(a \mid s) = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } a = \arg\max_{a'} Q^*(s, a') \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

This gives a **deterministic** optimal policy. $\blacksquare$

## 3.3 Analytical Worked Example (For Practice)

**Problem:** A 2-state MDP. $S = \{s_1, s_2\}$, $A = \{a_1, a_2\}$. With policy $\pi$ such that $\pi(a_1 \mid s_1) = 0.7$, $\pi(a_2 \mid s_1) = 0.3$, $\pi(a_1 \mid s_2) = 0.5$, $\pi(a_2 \mid s_2) = 0.5$. Transition table:

| $s$ | $a$ | $s'$ | $P(s' \mid s, a)$ | $R(s, a, s')$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $s_1$ | $a_1$ | $s_1$ | 0.4 | 5 |
| $s_1$ | $a_1$ | $s_2$ | 0.6 | 10 |
| $s_1$ | $a_2$ | $s_1$ | 1.0 | 0 |
| $s_2$ | $a_1$ | $s_2$ | 1.0 | 2 |
| $s_2$ | $a_2$ | $s_1$ | 1.0 | -1 |

Let $\gamma = 0.9$. Compute $V^\pi(s_1)$ using the Bellman equation (assume $V^\pi(s_2) = 5$ for illustration).

### Solution

Apply the Bellman expectation equation:

$$V^\pi(s_1) = \sum_{a} \pi(a \mid s_1) \sum_{s'} P(s' \mid s_1, a) \left[ R(s_1, a, s') + \gamma V^\pi(s') \right]$$

**Branch $a = a_1$ (weight 0.7):**

$$0.7 \times \left[ 0.4 (5 + 0.9 \cdot V(s_1)) + 0.6 (10 + 0.9 \cdot V(s_2)) \right]$$

$$= 0.7 \times \left[ 0.4 (5 + 0.9 V(s_1)) + 0.6 (10 + 0.9 \cdot 5) \right]$$

$$= 0.7 \times \left[ 0.4 (5 + 0.9 V(s_1)) + 0.6 (14.5) \right]$$

$$= 0.7 \times \left[ 2 + 0.36 V(s_1) + 8.7 \right] = 0.7 \times \left[ 10.7 + 0.36 V(s_1) \right]$$

$$= 7.49 + 0.252 \, V(s_1)$$

**Branch $a = a_2$ (weight 0.3):**

$$0.3 \times \left[ 1.0 \cdot (0 + 0.9 V(s_1)) \right] = 0.3 \times 0.9 V(s_1) = 0.27 V(s_1)$$

**Sum:**

$$V^\pi(s_1) = 7.49 + 0.252 V(s_1) + 0.27 V(s_1)$$

$$V^\pi(s_1) - 0.522 V(s_1) = 7.49$$

$$0.478 V(s_1) = 7.49 \quad\Rightarrow\quad V^\pi(s_1) \approx 15.67$$

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Policy Types

| Feature | Value-Based RL | Policy-Based RL | Actor–Critic (Hybrid) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Learns | $V$ or $Q$ | $\pi(a \mid s; \theta)$ directly | Both $V$ and $\pi$ |
| Action Space | Best for discrete | Handles continuous naturally | Both |
| Convergence | Tabular: guaranteed | High variance, local optima | Balanced |
| Examples | Q-Learning, SARSA, DQN | REINFORCE, PPO, TRPO | A2C, A3C, DDPG, SAC |
| KTU 14-Mark Depth | Often 7-marker | Often 7-marker | Full 14-marker |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Agent–Environment Interaction Loop (Core RL Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    agentNode[AGENT]
    policyNode[POLICY PI]
    valueNode[VALUE FUNCTION V OR Q]
    envNode[ENVIRONMENT]
    stateNode[STATE S]
    rewardNode[REWARD R]
    actNode[ACTION A]
    transNode[TRANSITION P S GIVEN S A]
    newStateNode[NEXT STATE S PRIME]

    agentNode -->|executes action| actNode
    actNode -->|applied to| envNode
    envNode -->|samples next state from| transNode
    transNode -->|yields| newStateNode
    envNode -->|emits| rewardNode
    newStateNode -->|perceived by agent| stateNode
    stateNode -->|input to| policyNode
    policyNode -->|combines with| valueNode
    valueNode -->|guides improvement of| policyNode
    rewardNode -->|feeds back to update| valueNode
```

**Reading the loop:** At time $t$, the agent sees $S_t$, picks $A_t$ via $\pi$, the environment returns $R_{t+1}$ and transitions to $S_{t+1}$. The cycle continues until termination.

## 4.2 Hierarchy of RL Components (Knowledge Graph)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    root[RL FRAMEWORK]
    root --> mdp[MDP TUPLE S A P R GAMMA]
    root --> agent[AGENT DECISION MAKER]
    root --> env[ENVIRONMENT STOCHASTIC DYNAMICS]
    root --> obj[OBJECTIVE MAXIMIZE EXPECTED RETURN]

    mdp --> state[STATE SPACE S]
    mdp --> action[ACTION SPACE A]
    mdp --> trans[TRANSITION P S PRIME GIVEN S A]
    mdp --> reward[REWARD R S A S PRIME]
    mdp --> gamma[DISCOUNT FACTOR GAMMA IN ZERO ONE]

    agent --> pol[POLICY PI A GIVEN S]
    agent --> vf[VALUE FUNCTIONS V AND Q]
    vf --> vexp[V PI EXPECTED RETURN]
    vf --> qexp[Q PI ACTION VALUE]
    vf --> vopt[V STAR OPTIMAL]
    vf --> qopt[Q STAR OPTIMAL]

    obj --> bell[BELLMAN EQUATIONS]
    bell --> bexp[EXPECTATION BELLMAN]
    bell --> bopt[OPTIMALITY BELLMAN]
```

## 4.3 Classification of RL Algorithms (Tree)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    rlAlg[RL ALGORITHMS]
    rlAlg --> modelBased[MODEL BASED]
    rlAlg --> modelFree[MODEL FREE]

    modelBased --> dyna[DYNA Q]
    modelBased --> alphaZero[ALPHA ZERO STYLE]
    modelBased --> pilco[PILCO]

    modelFree --> mbPolicy[POLICY OPTIMIZATION]
    modelFree --> mbValue[VALUE BASED]
    modelFree --> mbHybrid[ACTOR CRITIC HYBRID]

    mbPolicy --> reinforce[REINFORCE]
    mbPolicy --> ppo[PPO]
    mbPolicy --> trpo[TRPO]

    mbValue --> qlearn[Q LEARNING]
    mbValue --> sarsa[SARSA]
    mbValue --> dqn[DQN]

    mbHybrid --> a2c[A2C A3C]
    mbHybrid --> ddpg[DDPG]
    mbHybrid --> sac[SAC]
```

## 4.4 The Exploration–Exploitation Decision Process

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start[AT STATE S T]
    start --> gen[RANDOM NUMBER R IN ZERO ONE]
    gen -->|"r LESS THAN EPSILON"| explore[EXPLORE CHOOSE RANDOM ACTION]
    gen -->|"r GREATER THAN EPSILON"| exploit[EXPLOIT CHOOSE ARGMAX Q]
    explore --> exec[EXECUTE ACTION A T]
    exploit --> exec
    exec --> obs[OBSERVE R T PLUS 1 AND S T PLUS 1]
    obs --> update[UPDATE Q OR V ESTIMATE]
    update --> tplus1[MOVE TO T PLUS 1]
    tplus1 --> start
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read this in exam:** $\epsilon$-greedy is the *default* baseline for KTU numerical questions. Always cite that $\epsilon$ is **annealed** (decayed) over training — high early (explore), low later (exploit).

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define Reinforcement Learning. List any four key differences between RL and Supervised Learning.**

**Model Answer (Examiner's Key):**

**Definition:** Reinforcement Learning is a machine learning paradigm in which an agent learns to make sequential decisions by interacting with an environment to maximize a scalar cumulative reward signal, without being given explicit input-output pairs.

**Four Key Differences:**

| Aspect | Supervised Learning | Reinforcement Learning |
|---|---|---|
| Feedback type | Correct labels provided | Scalar reward signal |
| Data | IID, fixed dataset | Sequential, correlated trajectories |
| Decision horizon | Single prediction | Long sequence of actions |
| Objective | Minimize prediction error | Maximize cumulative reward |

> *(3 marks: 1 for definition, 2 for tabulated comparison.)*

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Explain the Exploration–Exploitation dilemma in RL with an example.**

**Model Answer:**

The exploration–exploitation dilemma arises because an RL agent must simultaneously:
- **Exploit** the best-known action to gain immediate reward, and
- **Explore** new actions to potentially discover better long-term rewards.

**Example:** In a restaurant recommendation system, always recommending the top-rated restaurant (exploitation) prevents discovering a new restaurant that the user might love (exploration). Pure exploitation leads to suboptimal policies; pure exploration wastes accumulated knowledge. The trade-off is typically managed using strategies like $\epsilon$-greedy, Upper Confidence Bound, or Boltzmann exploration.

---

## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Option 1
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 | Modified for 2024 Scheme]`

#### (a) Explain the Markov Decision Process (MDP) framework in detail. State the Bellman Optimality Equation for $V^*(s)$ and discuss its significance. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

An **MDP** is a mathematical framework for modeling sequential decision-making under stochasticity, formally defined by the tuple $\mathcal{M} = \langle \mathcal{S}, \mathcal{A}, P, R, \gamma \rangle$:

1. **State Space $\mathcal{S}$:** Set of all possible environment configurations. The **Markov property** holds: the future is independent of the past given the present state.
2. **Action Space $\mathcal{A}$:** Set of all actions available to the agent in each state.
3. **Transition Function $P(s' \mid s, a)$:** Probability of moving from state $s$ to $s'$ under action $a$. Satisfies $\sum_{s'} P(s' \mid s, a) = 1$.
4. **Reward Function $R(s, a, s')$:** Immediate scalar feedback upon transition.
5. **Discount Factor $\gamma \in [0, 1)$:** Encodes preference for immediate vs. delayed rewards.

**Bellman Optimality Equation:**

$$V^*(s) = \max_{a \in \mathcal{A}} \sum_{s' \in \mathcal{S}} P(s' \mid s, a) \left[ R(s, a, s') + \gamma \, V^*(s') \right]$$

**Significance:**
- It expresses that the **optimal value** of a state equals the **best expected one-step reward plus the discounted optimal future value**.
- It is a **system of non-linear equations** solvable by dynamic programming (Value Iteration, Policy Iteration) for tabular MDPs.
- It defines the **principle of optimality** (Bellman, 1957): optimal policies are composed of optimal sub-policies.
- It guarantees the existence of a **stationary deterministic optimal policy** in finite MDPs.

> *(7 marks breakdown: 2 for MDP components, 2 for equation, 2 for significance, 1 for terminology.)*

#### (b) A 3-state MDP has $\mathcal{S} = \{s_1, s_2, s_3\}$, $s_3$ is terminal. From $s_1$, action $a_1$ leads to $s_2$ with reward +1, and $a_2$ leads to $s_3$ with reward 0. From $s_2$, action $a_1$ leads to $s_1$ with reward +2, and $a_2$ leads to $s_3$ with reward 0. Take $\gamma = 0.5$. Compute the optimal policy and optimal value function using one step of Value Iteration. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Initialize $V_0(s) = 0$ for all $s$.**

**Step 2 — One Bellman Optimality Backup:**

**For $s_1$:**
- Action $a_1$: $R + \gamma V_0(s_2) = 1 + 0.5 \cdot 0 = 1$
- Action $a_2$: $R + \gamma V_0(s_3) = 0 + 0 = 0$
- $V_1(s_1) = \max(1, 0) = 1$ → optimal action: $a_1$

**For $s_2$:**
- Action $a_1$: $R + \gamma V_0(s_1) = 2 + 0.5 \cdot 0 = 2$
- Action $a_2$: $R + \gamma V_0(s_3) = 0 + 0 = 0$
- $V_1(s_2) = \max(2, 0) = 2$ → optimal action: $a_1$

**For $s_3$ (terminal):** $V_1(s_3) = 0$

**Step 3 — Optimal Policy after one iteration:**

$$\pi^*(s_1) = a_1, \quad \pi^*(s_2) = a_1, \quad V_1(s_1) = 1, \quad V_1(s_2) = 2$$

**Convergence Note:** As iterations proceed, $V_k \to V^*$ and $\pi$ stabilizes. Because the policy at every state already maximizes the backup, this policy remains optimal across further iterations (verify by second backup: $V_2(s_1) = 1 + 0.5 \cdot 2 = 2$, $V_2(s_2) = 2 + 0.5 \cdot 2 = 3$, etc.).

> *(7 marks breakdown: 2 for initialization, 3 for backup computation, 1 for policy, 1 for convergence comment.)*

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Option 2
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024 | Modified for 2024 Scheme]`

#### (a) Define the action-value function $Q^\pi(s, a)$ and derive the Bellman Expectation Equation for $Q^\pi$. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Definition:** The action-value function $Q^\pi(s, a)$ is the expected return when starting in state $s$, taking action $a$, and thereafter following policy $\pi$:

$$Q^\pi(s, a) \equiv \mathbb{E}_\pi \!\left[ G_t \mid S_t = s, A_t = a \right] = \mathbb{E}_\pi \!\left[ \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^k R_{t+k+1} \,\Big|\, S_t = s, A_t = a \right]$$

**Derivation:**

**Step 1 — Use the recursive return:** $G_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma G_{t+1}$

$$Q^\pi(s, a) = \mathbb{E}_\pi\!\left[ R_{t+1} + \gamma G_{t+1} \mid S_t = s, A_t = a \right]$$

**Step 2 — Condition on next state and reward:**

$$Q^\pi(s, a) = \sum_{s', r} p(s', r \mid s, a) \left[ r + \gamma \, \mathbb{E}_\pi[G_{t+1} \mid S_{t+1} = s'] \right]$$

**Step 3 — Expand inner expectation over next action and use $Q^\pi$ definition:**

$$\mathbb{E}_\pi[G_{t+1} \mid S_{t+1} = s'] = \sum_{a'} \pi(a' \mid s') \, Q^\pi(s', a')$$

**Step 4 — Final equation:**

$$Q^\pi(s, a) = \sum_{s', r} p(s', r \mid s, a) \left[ r + \gamma \sum_{a'} \pi(a' \mid s') \, Q^\pi(s', a') \right]$$

> *(7 marks breakdown: 2 for definition, 5 for step-by-step derivation.)*

#### (b) Compare and contrast model-based and model-free RL. Give two examples of algorithms in each category. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

| Aspect | Model-Based RL | Model-Free RL |
|---|---|---|
| Environment model | Learns or is given $P(s' \mid s, a)$ and $R$ | Does not learn the model |
| Sample efficiency | High — uses model for planning | Low — needs many real interactions |
| Computation | Higher (planning) | Lower |
| Real-world CPS use | Simulators (MuJoCo, CARLA) | Direct hardware deployment |
| Robustness | Brittle if model is wrong | More robust to model errors |

**Model-Based Examples:**
1. **Dyna-Q** — Combines real experience with model-generated rollouts.
2. **AlphaZero** — Uses a learned model in the form of an MCTS tree.

**Model-Free Examples:**
1. **Q-Learning (off-policy, value-based)** — Learns $Q(s, a)$ via Bellman updates.
2. **REINFORCE (on-policy, policy-based)** — Directly updates policy parameters via gradient ascent on expected return.

**Hybrid Example (Bonus):** **DDPG (Deep Deterministic Policy Gradient)** combines learned Q-function (model-free) with a learned dynamics model for sample efficiency.

> *(7 marks breakdown: 3 for comparison table, 2 for model-based examples, 2 for model-free examples.)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in RL Fundamentals:**
> 1. **Confusing $V$ with $Q$** — $V^\pi(s)$ is a *state-only* expectation; $Q^\pi(s, a)$ is a *state-action* expectation. Mixing them up costs 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting the $\gamma$ in the recursion** — Always include the discount factor; examiners deduct 1 mark per missing $\gamma$.
> 3. **Markov Property mis-statement** — Don't say "the state contains all past information." Say: *"The future is conditionally independent of the past given the present state."* Examiners reward the precise formulation.
> 4. **In MDP tables: not summing $P$ to 1** — Always verify $\sum_{s'} P(s' \mid s, a) = 1$. If your table doesn't sum, you'll lose 1 mark for the transition probabilities.
> 5. **Mixing up Bellman Expectation vs Optimality** — Expectation uses $\sum_a \pi(a \mid s) \cdot (\ldots)$, Optimality uses $\max_a (\ldots)$. This is a 3-mark killer.
> 6. **Deterministic vs stochastic policy** — In KTU answers, **always** state which type you are deriving; silent assumptions are penalized.
> 7. **"Discount factor must equal 1"** — This is wrong for infinite-horizon continuing tasks. State that $\gamma < 1$ ensures convergence of the geometric series.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ⭐ **MDP Tuple:** $\langle \mathcal{S}, \mathcal{A}, P, R, \gamma \rangle$ — memorize verbatim.
- ⭐ **Markov Property** = "future $\perp$ past $\mid$ present state."
- ⭐ **Return** $G_t = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^k R_{t+k+1}$ — **recursive form** $G_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma G_{t+1}$ is a must-derive.
- ⭐ **State-Value $V^\pi(s)$** = expected return from $s$ under $\pi$.
- ⭐ **Action-Value $Q^\pi(s, a)$** = expected return from $s, a$ under $\pi$.
- ⭐ **Relation:** $V^\pi(s) = \sum_a \pi(a \mid s) Q^\pi(s, a)$.
- ⭐ **Bellman Expectation** uses $\sum_a \pi(a \mid s) \cdot (\ldots)$.
- ⭐ **Bellman Optimality** uses $\max_a (\ldots)$.
- ⭐ **Optimal policy extraction:** $\pi^*(s) = \arg\max_a Q^*(s, a)$.
- ⭐ **Exploration–Exploitation** — every RL problem has it; default tool is $\epsilon$-greedy.
- ⭐ **$\gamma \in [0, 1)$** — closer to 0 = myopic; closer to 1 = far-sighted.
- ⭐ **Model-Based** uses $P$ and $R$ (sample efficient, brittle).
- ⭐ **Model-Free** learns directly from samples (sample hungry, robust).
- ⭐ **Episodic tasks** have terminal states; **continuing tasks** do not.
- ⭐ **Stationary optimal policy** exists for finite MDPs.
- ⭐ **For KTU numerical:** always re-verify $\sum_{s'} P(s' \mid s, a) = 1$ before plugging into Bellman.
- ⭐ **Sample** the definition of $V^*$ and $Q^*$ in every numerical — it carries 1 free mark.
- ⭐ **Mention the "principle of optimality"** when writing Bellman — it demonstrates conceptual depth.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Key Concepts: States
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECPT635 - MACHINE LEARNING FOR CPS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Key Concepts: States # SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In the context of **Cyber-Physical Systems (CPS)** and **Machine Learning for CPS**, a **state** is formally defined as the minimum set of internal variables of a dynamical system that, together with the future inputs and the system dynamics, completely determines the future behaviour of the system. Mathematically, a state is represented as a vector: $$ x(t) \in \mathbb{R}^{n} $$ where $n$ denotes the **order** of the system (also called the *system dimension*). The collection of all possible values that $x(t)$ can assume forms the **state space** $\mathcal{X} \subseteq \mathbb{R}^{n}$. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** A *state* is a unique configuration of a system at a given instant that captures all information required to predict the system's future evolution, assuming full knowledge of future inputs and the system model. No other variable outside the state vector can add predictive information. ## 1.2 Component Terminology | Term | Meaning | Notation | |---|---|---| | **State Variable** | Scalar component of the state vector | $x_i(t)$ | | **State Vector** | Column vector of all state variables | $x(t) = [x_1(t), x_2(t), \ldots, x_n(t)]^{T}$ | | **State Space** | Geometric space spanned by all state vectors | $\mathcal{X} \subseteq \mathbb{R}^{n}$ | | **State Trajectory** | Path traced by $x(t)$ in state space as $t$ evolves | $\gamma : \mathbb{R} \rightarrow \mathcal{X}$ | | **Initial State** | State at $t = t_0$ | $x(t_0)$ | ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Analogy — The Car Dashboard:** > Imagine you are driving a car on a highway. To fully describe *what the car is doing right now* and *predict what it will do next second*, you need only a handful of numbers: > > 1. **Current position** (where it is on the road) > 2. **Current velocity** (how fast it is moving) > 3. **Current acceleration** (how rapidly velocity is changing) > > These three numbers, taken together, form the **state** of the car. Notice that the *colour of the car*, *radio volume*, or *fuel brand* do **not** belong to the state because they do not influence future motion. The state is the **minimal sufficient summary** of the past needed to forecast the future. This same principle applies to a CPS — whether it is an autonomous drone, a smart grid, a robotic arm, or an industrial IoT process controller. ## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics - **State dimension (n):** Number of independent state variables, dimensionless, typically $\mathbf{2 \leq n \leq 1000}$ for industrial CPS. - **Sampling period ($T_s$):** Discrete-time interval between consecutive state updates; common values are $\mathbf{1\,\text{ms} \leq T_s \leq 1\,\text{s}}$ for embedded CPS. - **State estimation covariance ($P$):** $n \times n$ symmetric positive-definite matrix quantifying uncertainty; units depend on state dimension. ## 1.5 Why "States" Matter in ML for CPS In Machine Learning for CPS, the state is the **bridge between the physical world and the learning algorithm**. Almost every ML technique used in CPS — Kalman filtering, reinforcement learning, recurrent neural networks (RNNs), system identification — operates on, estimates, or learns from the **state**. Without a rigorous definition of state, neither classical estimation nor modern deep learning models can be applied to physical systems in a theoretically sound way. # SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Properties That Define a Valid State For a variable (or collection of variables) to qualify as a state, it must satisfy the **Markov property** (memorylessness). The following bullet list captures the rigorous conditions: - **Markov Property (Memoryless Condition):** The future state depends *only* on the present state and the *future inputs*, never on the past history. Formally: $$ P(x_{t+1} \mid x_t, x_{t-1}, \ldots, x_0, u_t) = P(x_{t+1} \mid x_t, u_t) $$ - **Why this matters:** It guarantees that the state is a *sufficient statistic* of history. Any older information is either encoded in $x_t$ or is irrelevant. - **How to verify:** If adding past values $x_{t-k}$ for $k \geq 1$ changes the prediction of $x_{t+1}$ beyond what $x_t$ already provides, then $x_t$ is **not** a valid Markovian state — additional variables must be appended to the state vector. - **Minimality (Non-Redundancy):** No state variable should be expressible as a function of the others. Redundant variables inflate $n$ and degrade numerical conditioning of estimators. - **Completeness:** The state must capture *all* internal dynamics. If some internal energy (e.g., thermal capacitance voltage) is ignored, predictions will diverge from reality. ## 2.2 Continuous-Time vs. Discrete-Time States | Aspect | Continuous-Time State | Discrete-Time State | |---|---|---| | Notation | $x(t) \in \mathbb{R}^n$ | $x_k \in \mathbb{R}^n$, where $k \in \mathbb{Z}$ | | Evolution Law | $\dot{x}(t) = f(x(t), u(t), t)$ | $x_{k+1} = f(x_k, u_k, k)$ | | Use in CPS | Analog physical plants | Sampled/embedded implementations | | ML Link | Physics-based simulation | Recurrent NN training, RL episodes | ## 2.3 Categorisation of States in CPS The concept of "state" appears in multiple layers of a CPS. KTU examiners frequently test this classification: 1. **Physical State** — Actual continuous-time condition of the physical plant (e.g., temperature, pressure, angular velocity). 2. **Cyber / Estimated State** — Digital reconstruction computed by observers or ML models running on embedded processors. 3. **Latent State** — Hidden variables not directly measurable but inferred (e.g., battery State-of-Health, SoH). 4. **Belief State** — In stochastic systems, a probability distribution over states rather than a single vector; central to POMDPs and Bayesian filters. ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > The following table is the **cheat sheet** you must memorise. Every formula below is **directly examinable** under KTU 2024 PECPT635. | # | Concept | Equation | Remarks | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | State vector definition | $x(t) = [x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n]^{T}$ | $n$ = system order | | 2 | State-space ODE (LTI) | $\dot{x}(t) = A x(t) + B u(t)$ | $A \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$ | | 3 | Measurement equation | $y(t) = C x(t) + D u(t)$ | $C \in \mathbb{R}^{p \times n}$ | | 4 | Discrete-time update | $x_{k+1} = A_d x_k + B_d u_k$ | $A_d = e^{A T_s}$ | | 5 | Markov property | $P(x_{k+1}\vert x_k,u_k) = P(x_{k+1}\vert x_k,\ldots,x_0,u_k)$ | Memoryless | | 6 | State transition matrix | $\Phi(t) = e^{A t}$ | Solution of homogeneous ODE | | 7 | Observability rank condition | $\text{rank}(\mathcal{O}) = n$ | $\mathcal{O} = [C^{T}, (CA)^{T}, \ldots, (CA^{n-1})^{T}]^{T}$ | | 8 | Controllability rank condition | $\text{rank}(\mathcal{C}) = n$ | $\mathcal{C} = [B, AB, \ldots, A^{n-1}B]$ | | 9 | Lyapunov stability | $A^{T} P + P A = -Q$ | $P \succ 0$, $Q \succ 0$ | | 10 | Kalman state update | $\hat{x}_{k\mid k} = \hat{x}_{k\mid k-1} + K_k (y_k - C\hat{x}_{k\mid k-1})$ | $K_k = P_{k\mid k-1} C^{T} (C P_{k\mid k-1} C^{T} + R)^{-1}$ | > ⚠️ **Markdown safeguard applied:** All vertical-bar-like symbols inside equations are rendered using `\vert` to avoid breaking the table layout. ## 2.5 Real-World Utility of the State Concept - **Autonomous Vehicles:** The state vector typically contains position $(x, y)$, heading $\theta$, velocity $v$, and yaw-rate $\dot{\theta}$. The self-driving stack estimates this state via sensor fusion (camera + LiDAR + GPS). - **Smart Grids:** State estimation (weighted least-squares) reconstructs voltages at every bus — a state vector of dimension equal to the number of buses. - **Industrial Robotics:** Joint positions and velocities form the state; model-predictive control (MPC) rolls out trajectories over a horizon in state space. - **Healthcare CPS:** Patient state (heart rate, blood pressure, glucose, oxygen saturation) is tracked in real time to drive closed-loop drug delivery. - **Reinforcement Learning:** The state $s_t$ is the input to the policy $\pi(a \mid s)$; learning the optimal $\pi$ is impossible without a well-defined state representation. ## 2.6 Connection to Machine Learning In modern ML-for-CPS pipelines, the state is often a **learned representation**: $$ x_t^{\text{latent}} = f_{\theta}(x_{t-1}^{\text{latent}}, u_t) $$ where $f_{\theta}$ is a neural network parametrised by $\theta$. Architectures such as **LSTMs, GRUs, Neural ODEs, and State-Space Models (S4, Mamba)** explicitly learn state transition functions from data. The mathematical notion of "state" thus remains identical to the classical control definition — only the *method* of obtaining the state changes. # SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Solution of the Continuous-Time LTI State Equation **Goal:** Starting from $\dot{x}(t) = A x(t) + B u(t)$, derive the closed-form state trajectory. **Step 1 — Rewrite as a first-order linear ODE.** Treat $A x(t)$ as a "damping" term and $B u(t)$ as a forcing term. $$ \dot{x}(t) - A x(t) = B u(t) $$ **Step 2 — Introduce the integrating factor.** The matrix integrating factor is $\Phi(t) = e^{-A t}$. Multiply both sides: $$ e^{-A t} \dot{x}(t) - e^{-A t} A x(t) = e^{-A t} B u(t) $$ **Step 3 — Recognise the left-hand side as an exact derivative.** $$ \frac{d}{dt}\left[e^{-A t} x(t)\right] = e^{-A t} B u(t) $$ This step uses the identity $\frac{d}{dt} e^{-A t} = -A e^{-A t} = -e^{-A t} A$ (valid because $A$ commutes with its exponential). **Step 4 — Integrate both sides from $t_0$ to $t$.** $$ e^{-A t} x(t) - e^{-A t_0} x(t_0) = \int_{t_0}^{t} e^{-A \tau} B u(\tau) \, d\tau $$ **Step 5 — Multiply both sides by $e^{A t}$.** $$ x(t) = e^{A (t - t_0)} x(t_0) + \int_{t_0}^{t} e^{A (t - \tau)} B u(\tau) \, d\tau $$ **Step 6 — Identify the terms.** $$ x(t) = \Phi(t - t_0)\, x(t_0) + \int_{t_0}^{t} \Phi(t - \tau) B u(\tau)\, d\tau $$ where $\Phi(t - t_0) = e^{A(t - t_0)}$ is the **state transition matrix**. > **Interpretation:** The state at time $t$ is the sum of (a) the freely evolved initial condition and (b) the accumulated contribution of all past inputs, each propagated forward via the same transition matrix. This is the cornerstone result used in observer design, Kalman filtering, and trajectory optimisation. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Discretisation of the Continuous State Equation **Goal:** Convert $\dot{x}(t) = A x(t) + B u(t)$ into the discrete update $x_{k+1} = A_d x_k + B_d u_k$ assuming a Zero-Order Hold (ZOH) on $u(t)$ over interval $[k T_s, (k+1) T_s)$. **Step 1 — Apply the continuous solution with $t_0 = k T_s$, $t = (k+1) T_s$.** $$ x((k+1)T_s) = e^{A T_s} x(k T_s) + \int_{k T_s}^{(k+1) T_s} e^{A((k+1) T_s - \tau)} B u(\tau)\, d\tau $$ **Step 2 — Apply ZOH assumption $u(\tau) = u(k T_s)$ for all $\tau$ in the interval.** $$ x((k+1)T_s) = e^{A T_s} x(k T_s) + \left[\int_{0}^{T_s} e^{A \eta} B\, d\eta\right] u(k T_s) $$ where the substitution $\eta = (k+1)T_s - \tau$ has been used. **Step 3 — Define the discrete matrices.** $$ A_d \equiv e^{A T_s}, \qquad B_d \equiv \int_{0}^{T_s} e^{A \eta} B\, d\eta $$ **Step 4 — Final compact form.** $$ x_{k+1} = A_d x_k + B_d u_k $$ This is the **discrete-time state update** used in every embedded CPS implementation and in nearly every RL environment. --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Observability Matrix for a Single-Output LTI System **Goal:** Show how repeated differentiation of $y(t) = C x(t)$ produces a linear system in $x(t)$ and the inputs. **Step 1 — Differentiate $y(t)$.** $$ \dot{y}(t) = C \dot{x}(t) = C A x(t) + C B u(t) $$ **Step 2 — Differentiate again.** $$ \ddot{y}(t) = C A^{2} x(t) + C A B u(t) + C B \dot{u}(t) $$ **Step 3 — Pattern after $n-1$ differentiations.** $$ y^{(n-1)}(t) = C A^{n-1} x(t) + \text{(terms involving } u \text{ and its derivatives)} $$ **Step 4 — Stack the equations into a single linear system.** $$ \begin{aligned} \begin{bmatrix} y(t) \\ \dot{y}(t) \\ \ddot{y}(t) \\ \vdots \\ y^{(n-1)}(t) \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} C \\ C A \\ C A^{2} \\ \vdots \\ C A^{n-1} \end{bmatrix} x(t) + \text{input-dependent terms} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5 — Recognise the observability matrix.** $$ \mathcal{O} = \begin{bmatrix} C \\ C A \\ C A^{2} \\ \vdots \\ C A^{n-1} \end{bmatrix} $$ **Step 6 — Observability condition.** If $\text{rank}(\mathcal{O}) = n$, then $x(t)$ can be uniquely recovered from a sufficiently rich measurement history — the system is **observable**. --- ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparing State Concepts Across CPS Layers | Layer | State Type | Mathematical Form | Typical ML Method | Update Frequency | |---|---|---|---|---| | Physical plant | Continuous physical state | $\dot{x} = f(x, u)$ | Physics-informed NN | Continuous | | Sensor front-end | Sampled measurement | $y_k = h(x_k) + v_k$ | Denoising autoencoder | $f_s = 1/T_s$ | | Edge estimator | Cyber state estimate | $\hat{x}_{k\mid k}$ | Kalman / Particle filter | Synchronous | | Cloud analytics | Latent representation | $z_t = \phi_{\theta}(y_{1:t})$ | LSTM / Transformer | Asynchronous | | Decision layer | Belief / policy state | $b(s)$ or $V(s)$ | RL value function | Episode-level | > [!NOTE] > The word "state" is reused across layers but always obeys the **Markov property within its own layer**. Recognising which layer a question targets is a frequent KTU exam discriminator. --- ## 3.5 Derivation 4 — Stability via Lyapunov's Direct Method (Discrete-Time) **Goal:** Show how a Lyapunov function $V(x_k) = x_k^{T} P x_k$ leads to a stability test for the autonomous discrete system $x_{k+1} = A_d x_k$. **Step 1 — Compute the change in $V$.** $$ \Delta V = V(x_{k+1}) - V(x_k) = x_{k+1}^{T} P x_{k+1} - x_k^{T} P x_k $$ **Step 2 — Substitute $x_{k+1} = A_d x_k$.** $$ \Delta V = x_k^{T} A_d^{T} P A_d x_k - x_k^{T} P x_k $$ **Step 3 — Factor.** $$ \Delta V = x_k^{T} \left( A_d^{T} P A_d - P \right) x_k $$ **Step 4 — Stability condition.** For asymptotic stability, we require $\Delta V < 0$ for all $x_k \neq 0$. Hence we need: $$ A_d^{T} P A_d - P \prec 0 $$ Equivalently, there must exist a symmetric positive-definite matrix $P \succ 0$ satisfying the **Discrete Lyapunov Equation** above. This is the discrete analogue of the continuous result listed in the formula sheet. # SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Concept Map — States in CPS (Mermaid Mind-Map) ```mermaid mindmap root((State
Concepts in CPS)) Definition State Vector x(t) State Space X Markov Property Minimality Types Continuous Time State Discrete Time State Physical State Cyber State Latent State Belief State Representations State Space Equations Transfer Function Neural Latent Embedding POMDP Belief Properties Observability Controllability Stability Identifiability ML Connection Kalman Filter Recurrent Neural Networks Reinforcement Learning State Neural ODEs State Space Models S4 Engineering Use Autonomous Vehicles Smart Grid Estimation Industrial Robotics Healthcare CPS ``` ## 4.2 Block Diagram — State Estimation Pipeline (Mermaid Flow) ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeA[Physical Plant]:::plant --> nodeB[Sensor Array] nodeB --> nodeC[Noisy Measurement y_k] nodeC --> nodeD[Predict Step
x_hat_k_minus] nodeE[Previous Estimate x_hat_k_minus_1] --> nodeD nodeD --> nodeF[Innovation
y_k - H x_hat_k_minus] nodeF --> nodeG[Kalman Gain K_k] nodeG --> nodeH[Update Step
x_hat_k = x_hat_k_minus + K_k innovation] nodeH --> nodeI[Cyber State Estimate x_hat_k] nodeI --> nodeJ[ML Module / Controller] nodeJ --> nodeK[Control Action u_k] nodeK --> nodeA nodeI --> nodeE classDef plant fill:#e1f5fe,stroke:#01579b,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Reading the diagram:** The loop shows how the *physical state* (inside `Physical Plant`) is sensed, corrupted by noise, and reconstructed as a *cyber state estimate* $\hat{x}_k$. This estimate is then consumed by the ML module (anomaly detector, RL policy, MPC optimiser) whose output closes the loop via the actuator. The dashed bottom edge represents the recurrence of $\hat{x}_k$ feeding back into the predictor for the next timestep. ## 4.3 State-Transition Flow in an RL-Embedded CPS (Mermaid Sequence) ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant Env as Physical Environment participant Sensor as Sensor Stack participant Agent as RL Agent / NN Policy participant Actuator as Actuator Env->>Sensor: Emit physical state s_t Sensor->>Agent: Observation o_t (possibly partial) Agent->>Agent: Encode into belief state b_t Agent->>Actuator: Action a_t = pi(b_t) Actuator->>Env: Apply control u_t Env->>Env: Transition to s_{t+1} Env->>Sensor: Emit s_{t+1} ``` ## 4.4 Comparative Block Architecture — Classical Observer vs. ML Estimator ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph ClassicPath["Classical Kalman / Luenberger Observer"] direction TB nodeA1[Plant Model A, B, C] --> nodeA2[Predict x_dot = Ax + Bu] nodeA2 --> nodeA3[Correct with y_k] nodeA3 --> nodeA4[Estimated state x_hat] end subgraph MLPath["Machine-Learning State Estimator"] direction TB nodeB1[Training Data y_1 ... y_N] --> nodeB2[Neural Network f_theta] nodeB2 --> nodeB3[Latent state z_t] nodeB3 --> nodeB4[Output layer: x_hat or y_hat] end nodeA4 --> nodeC{Fusion or Selection} nodeB4 --> nodeC nodeC --> nodeD[Downstream CPS Decision Module] ``` > [!NOTE] > This block-level architecture is the typical fallback KTU examiners expect when a topic is too abstract for a physical diagram. It captures **function**, not geometry, but conveys the engineering truth that ML estimators and classical observers serve the same role — *state reconstruction* — through different mathematical machinery. # SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, PECPT635]` **Define the term "state" of a cyber-physical system. State and briefly justify the Markov property that a valid state must satisfy.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** A *state* of a CPS is the minimal set of internal variables that, together with the future inputs and the system dynamics, completely determines the future behaviour of the system. Mathematically, the state at time $t$ is a vector $x(t) \in \mathbb{R}^n$ whose components are called state variables. The **Markov property** requires that the conditional probability of the next state depends only on the current state and the current input, and not on the history: $$ P(x_{k+1} \mid x_k, u_k) = P(x_{k+1} \mid x_k, x_{k-1}, \ldots, x_0, u_k) $$ **Justification (1 mark):** This memorylessness guarantees that $x_k$ is a sufficient statistic of all past information, which is essential for both classical observers (e.g., Kalman filter) and modern ML models (e.g., RNNs, state-space models) to make optimal predictions without storing the entire history. --- ### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, PECPT635]` **Distinguish between *physical state*, *cyber state*, and *latent state* in a CPS. Give one example of each.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Type | Definition | Example | |---|---|---| | Physical state | True continuous-time condition of the physical plant | Temperature $T(t)$ of a chemical reactor | | Cyber state | Digital reconstruction computed by observers/ML on embedded hardware | Estimate $\hat{T}_k$ produced by a Kalman filter running on an ARM Cortex-M4 | | Latent state | Hidden internal variable not directly measurable but inferred | State-of-Health (SoH) of a lithium-ion battery | Each differs in observability: physical states are *ground truth* but may be inaccessible; cyber states are *computable approximations*; latent states are *inferred variables* that often require ML to estimate. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) ### Question A `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper]` **(a)** For a single-input single-output linear time-invariant (LTI) system described by: $$ \dot{x}(t) = A x(t) + B u(t), \qquad y(t) = C x(t) $$ with $$ A = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -2 & -3 \end{bmatrix}, \quad B = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad C = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix} $$ **(i)** Derive the state transition matrix $\Phi(t) = e^{A t}$. **(4 marks)** **(ii)** Compute the discrete-time matrices $A_d$ and $B_d$ for sampling period $T_s = 0.1$ s, assuming a Zero-Order Hold on the input. **(3 marks)** **(b)** Test the system for **observability** and **controllability**. State the conditions used and interpret the result. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution — Question A **(a)(i) Derivation of $\Phi(t) = e^{A t}$ — 4 marks** **Step 1 — Compute eigenvalues of $A$.** Characteristic polynomial: $$ \det(\lambda I - A) = \det\!\begin{bmatrix} \lambda & -1 \\ 2 & \lambda + 3 \end{bmatrix} = \lambda(\lambda + 3) + 2 = \lambda^{2} + 3 \lambda + 2 $$ Roots: $\lambda_1 = -1$, $\lambda_2 = -2$. **Step 2 — Use the spectral form of the matrix exponential.** $$ e^{A t} = e^{-t} E_1 + e^{-2t} E_2 $$ where the projection matrices $E_1, E_2$ are obtained via: $$ E_i = \prod_{j \neq i} \frac{A - \lambda_j I}{\lambda_i - \lambda_j} $$ **Step 3 — Compute $E_1$:** $$ E_1 = \frac{A - (-2) I}{(-1) - (-2)} = \frac{A + 2 I}{1} = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 1 \\ -2 & -1 \end{bmatrix} $$ **Step 4 — Compute $E_2$:** $$ E_2 = \frac{A - (-1) I}{(-2) - (-1)} = \frac{A + I}{-1} = \begin{bmatrix} -1 & -1 \\ 2 & 2 \end{bmatrix} $$ **Step 5 — Combine:** $$ \Phi(t) = e^{A t} = e^{-t}\!\begin{bmatrix} 2 & 1 \\ -2 & -1 \end{bmatrix} + e^{-2t}\!\begin{bmatrix} -1 & -1 \\ 2 & 2 \end{bmatrix} $$ $$ \Phi(t) = \begin{bmatrix} 2 e^{-t} - e^{-2t} & e^{-t} - e^{-2t} \\ -2 e^{-t} + 2 e^{-2t} & -e^{-t} + 2 e^{-2t} \end{bmatrix} $$ **(a)(ii) Discrete-time matrices at $T_s = 0.1$ s — 3 marks** $$ A_d = \Phi(T_s) = \begin{bmatrix} 2 e^{-0.1} - e^{-0.2} & e^{-0.1} - e^{-0.2} \\ -2 e^{-0.1} + 2 e^{-0.2} & -e^{-0.1} + 2 e^{-0.2} \end{bmatrix} $$ Numerically: $$ A_d \approx \begin{bmatrix} 1.8130 - 0.8187 & 0.9048 - 0.8187 \\ -1.8130 + 1.6374 & -0.9048 + 1.6374 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.9943 & 0.0861 \\ -0.1756 & 0.7326 \end{bmatrix} $$ For $B_d$, apply $B_d = \int_{0}^{T_s} e^{A \eta} B\, d\eta$: $$ B_d = \int_{0}^{0.1} \Phi(\eta)\, d\eta \cdot B $$ Integrating each entry of $\Phi(\eta) B$ and evaluating at $\eta = 0.1$: $$ B_d \approx \begin{bmatrix} 0.0043 \\ 0.0861 \end{bmatrix} $$ (Students may also use $B_d \approx T_s B$ as a first-order Euler approximation for verification.) **(b) Observability and Controllability test — 7 marks** **Step 1 — Controllability matrix $\mathcal{C}$.** $$ \mathcal{C} = [\,B \;\; AB\,] = \left[\, \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} \;\; \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ -3 \end{bmatrix} \,\right] = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ 1 & -3 \end{bmatrix} $$ Determinant: $$ \det(\mathcal{C}) = (0)(-3) - (1)(1) = -1 \neq 0 $$ Hence $\text{rank}(\mathcal{C}) = 2 = n$ → **system is controllable**. **Step 2 — Observability matrix $\mathcal{O}$.** $$ \mathcal{O} = \begin{bmatrix} C \\ CA \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} $$ Determinant = $1 \neq 0$ → $\text{rank}(\mathcal{O}) = 2 = n$ → **system is observable**. **Interpretation (2 marks):** Because the system is both controllable and observable, a state-feedback controller can place the closed-loop poles arbitrarily, and a Luenberger observer can reconstruct $x(t)$ from $y(t)$ alone. This makes the system a *minimal realisation*, suitable for full ML-driven controller design. --- ### Question B (Alternative Choice) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper]` **(a)** Explain the **Markov property** of a CPS state with a suitable mathematical statement. Discuss, with an example, why the *state* (not the *output*) must satisfy this property for an RL agent to learn an optimal policy. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Consider the discrete-time system $x_{k+1} = A_d x_k + B_d u_k$ with $$ A_d = \begin{bmatrix} 0.9 & 0.1 \\ 0 & 0.8 \end{bmatrix}, \qquad B_d = \begin{bmatrix} 0.05 \\ 0.20 \end{bmatrix} $$ Starting from $x_0 = [1, 0]^{T}$ and applying $u_k = -K x_k$ with $K = [1,\; 2]$, simulate the state trajectory for $k = 0, 1, 2, 3$ and verify whether the closed-loop system is stable. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution — Question B **(a) Markov Property and RL — 7 marks** **Mathematical statement (3 marks):** A sequence of states $\{x_0, x_1, x_2, \ldots\}$ is said to satisfy the Markov property if, for all $k$: $$ P(x_{k+1} \in S \mid x_k, x_{k-1}, \ldots, x_0) = P(x_{k+1} \in S \mid x_k) $$ for any measurable set $S \subseteq \mathcal{X}$. In a CPS with control inputs, the property is extended to: $$ P(x_{k+1} \mid x_k, u_k) = P(x_{k+1} \mid x_k, x_{k-1}, \ldots, x_0, u_k, u_{k-1}, \ldots, u_0) $$ **Why this matters in RL (3 marks):** In reinforcement learning, the policy $\pi(a \mid s)$ conditions only on the current state $s_k$. The Bellman optimality equation $$ V^{*}(s) = \max_{a}\!\left[ R(s,a) + \gamma \sum_{s'} P(s' \mid s, a)\, V^{*}(s') \right] $$ is well-posed *only* if $P(s' \mid s, a)$ depends solely on the current $(s, a)$. If the Markov property fails — e.g., if the agent observes only $y_k$ while $x_k$ has internal hidden momentum — the agent will mis-learn and performance will collapse. **Example (1 mark):** A quadrotor whose RL agent observes only position $p_k$ but ignores velocity $v_k$ violates Markov; the same $p_k$ can correspond to vastly different futures depending on whether the quadrotor is rising or falling. Including $v_k$ in the state restores Markov and dramatically improves learning. **(b) Closed-loop simulation — 7 marks** **Closed-loop update rule:** $$ x_{k+1} = (A_d - B_d K)\, x_k $$ **Step 1 — Compute $A_{\text{cl}} = A_d - B_d K$.** $$ B_d K = \begin{bmatrix} 0.05 \\ 0.20 \end{bmatrix} [\,1 \;\; 2\,] = \begin{bmatrix} 0.05 & 0.10 \\ 0.20 & 0.40 \end{bmatrix} $$ $$ A_{\text{cl}} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.9 - 0.05 & 0.1 - 0.10 \\ 0 - 0.20 & 0.8 - 0.40 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.85 & 0 \\ -0.20 & 0.40 \end{bmatrix} $$ **Step 2 — Iterative simulation.** | $k$ | $x_k^{(1)}$ | $x_k^{(2)}$ | |---|---|---| | 0 | 1.0000 | 0.0000 | | 1 | $0.85 \cdot 1 + 0 \cdot 0 = 0.8500$ | $-0.20 \cdot 1 + 0.40 \cdot 0 = -0.2000$ | | 2 | $0.85 \cdot 0.85 = 0.7225$ | $-0.20 \cdot 0.85 + 0.40 \cdot (-0.20) = -0.1700 - 0.0800 = -0.2500$ | | 3 | $0.85 \cdot 0.7225 = 0.6141$ | $-0.20 \cdot 0.7225 + 0.40 \cdot (-0.25) = -0.1445 - 0.1000 = -0.2445$ | **Step 3 — Stability check.** Eigenvalues of $A_{\text{cl}}$: triangular matrix, so eigenvalues are the diagonal entries $0.85$ and $0.40$. Both satisfy $\vert \lambda \vert < 1$, hence the closed-loop system is **asymptotically stable**. This is consistent with the observed trajectory decay: $\vert x_k^{(1)} \vert$ decays from $1 \rightarrow 0.85 \rightarrow 0.72 \rightarrow 0.61$, and $\vert x_k^{(2)} \vert$ remains bounded and decaying. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common marks-losing mistakes observed in PECPT635 valuation:** > > 1. **Confusing state with output.** Students frequently write $y(t)$ when the question asks for $x(t)$. Remember: state is internal, output is measured. > 2. **Forgetting the Markov condition.** A full-marks definition of state *must* include the memorylessness property.
