Merely saying "the condition of the system" earns only partial credit. > 3. **Skipping minimality.** The state is *minimal* — redundant variables inflate the order and degrade estimators. > 4. **Wrong rank conditions.** Controllability uses $[B, AB, A^{2}B, \ldots]$ (columns). Observability uses $[C^{T}, (CA)^{T}, (CA^{2})^{T}, \ldots]^{T}$ (rows). Mixing them up is the #1 mistake. > 5. **Discretisation errors.** Using Euler's forward method ($A_d \approx I + A T_s$) when exact matrix exponential is required costs 2–3 marks. > 6. **Not specifying units / boundary conditions.** Especially in M3, examiners reward explicit mention of ZOH assumption, sampling period, and stability radius $\vert \lambda \vert < 1$. > 7. **Forgetting the input-related term.** In trajectory derivations, omitting the convolution integral $\int \Phi(t-\tau) B u(\tau) d\tau$ costs 1 full mark. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist — "Key Concepts: States" (PECPT635 / Module 3)** > > - **State** = minimal, Markovian, sufficient statistic of system history. > - State vector is **internal**; output vector is **measured**. Do not interchange. > - **State space** $\mathcal{X} \subseteq \mathbb{R}^{n}$ is the geometric arena in which trajectories evolve. > - **Markov property:** $P(x_{k+1} \mid x_k, u_k) = P(x_{k+1} \mid x_0, x_1, \ldots, x_k, u_0, \ldots, u_k)$. > - Continuous state evolution: $\dot{x}(t) = A x(t) + B u(t)$. > - Discrete state evolution: $x_{k+1} = A_d x_k + B_d u_k$, where $A_d = e^{A T_s}$. > - Closed-form trajectory: $x(t) = \Phi(t - t_0) x(t_0) + \int_{t_0}^{t} \Phi(t - \tau) B u(\tau) d\tau$. > - **Observability matrix:** $\mathcal{O} = [C^{T}, (CA)^{T}, \ldots, (CA^{n-1})^{T}]^{T}$. > - **Controllability matrix:** $\mathcal{C} = [B, AB, A^{2}B, \ldots, A^{n-1}B]$. > - Full rank of $\mathcal{O}$ and $\mathcal{C}$ $\Rightarrow$ minimal realisation $\Rightarrow$ all poles can be moved and all states observed. > - **Stability:** Continuous-time poles in open left half-plane; discrete-time poles inside the unit disc. > - **Latent state** in ML is the same mathematical object as the classical state, only learned from data. > - **Belief state** is the probability distribution over states, used in POMDPs and Bayesian filters. > - In RL, the *agent's input* must be Markovian — otherwise the Bellman equation is invalid and learning fails. > - Kalman filter is the **optimal linear estimator** of state under Gaussian noise. > - Neural state-space models (S4, Mamba, Neural ODE) **learn** the transition function $f_\theta$ rather than assume it is given. > - Always state assumptions: ZOH, sampling period, noise distribution, linearity. > - Always check ranks before claiming observability/controllability — partial rank gives partial reconstruction only.

topics.title: Actions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECPT635 - MACHINE LEARNING FOR CPS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Actions # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology) In the Reinforcement Learning (RL) framework that underpins decision-making in Cyber-Physical Systems (CPS), an **Action** $a \in \mathcal{A}(s)$ is a control decision, command, or control vector selected by an intelligent agent at time step $t$ from a permissible **Action Space** $\mathcal{A}(s)$, given the current environmental **State** $s \in \mathcal{S}$. Formally, it is the realization of a policy $\pi(a \mid s)$ that drives the state-transition function $p(s' \mid s, a)$ of the underlying Markov Decision Process (MDP). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Definition Highlight:** An *Action* in CPS is not merely an output — it is a **physical actuator command** (e.g., torque on a motor, throttle angle in an autonomous vehicle, switching signal in a smart grid). Its effect propagates into the physical dynamics $s_{t+1} = f(s_t, a_t, w_t)$, making it the **bridge between the digital decision layer and the physical world**. The complete specification of an action involves four canonical descriptors: | Descriptor | Symbol | Meaning in CPS | |---|---|---| | Action Variable | $a$ | The actual command issued to the actuator | | Action Space | $\mathcal{A}(s)$ | The set of all physically realizable actions from state $s$ | | Action Distribution | $\pi(a \mid s)$ | Probability of choosing $a$ in state $s$ under policy $\pi$ | | Action-Value | $Q(s, a)$ | Expected long-term reward of executing $a$ in $s$ | ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Plain-English Analogy — "The Self-Driving Car at an Intersection"** > > Imagine you are sitting in an autonomous car approaching a 4-way intersection. The *Environment* is the road network (states = your GPS position, speed, surrounding cars). At every instant, the car must choose an *Action*: **accelerate, brake, steer-left, steer-right, or maintain lane**. Each action has a **physical consequence** — applying brakes changes the car's kinetic energy, steering changes its heading. > > Just as a student learns which action is best in each scenario by experiencing consequences (good grade vs. bad grade), an RL agent in CPS learns the best action for each state by receiving a **reward signal** (e.g., +10 for safe passage, −100 for collision). The "action" is therefore the **decision-making atom** that converts intelligence into physical behavior. ## 1.3 Action Space Taxonomy (Critical for CPS) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus Highlight:** The classification of action spaces dictates the choice of learning algorithm — discrete actions suit Q-Learning/Deep Q-Networks, while continuous actions require Policy Gradient or DDPG methods commonly deployed in robotic CPS. | Category | Mathematical Form | CPS Examples | Cardinality | |---|---|---|---| | **Discrete (Finite)** | $\mathcal{A} = \{a_1, a_2, \dots, a_k\}$ | Relay switching, gear selection, lane-change decisions | $\vert \mathcal{A} \vert = k$ finite | | **Continuous (Infinite)** | $\mathcal{A} \subseteq \mathbb{R}^d$ | Motor torque, steering angle, throttle valve opening | Uncountably infinite | | **Mixed/Hybrid** | $\mathcal{A} = \mathcal{A}_d \times \mathbb{R}^c$ | UAV altitude (discrete modes) + thrust (continuous) | Mixed | | **Parameterized** | $a = g(\theta), \; \theta \in \Theta$ | Robotic gripper with angle parameters | Indirect | ## 1.4 Determinism vs. Stochasticity of Actions - **Deterministic Action:** $a = \pi(s)$ — a single fixed command, e.g., a PID controller output. - **Stochastic Action:** $a \sim \pi(\cdot \mid s)$ — a sampled command, e.g., a probabilistic lane-change decision during uncertain pedestrian behavior. The **stationary policy** $\pi : \mathcal{S} \times \mathcal{A} \to [0, 1]$ assigns to each state-action pair a probability, with the constraint $\sum_{a \in \mathcal{A}(s)} \pi(a \mid s) = 1$ for every $s$. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — Actions in the MDP Framework An **MDP** is the canonical mathematical scaffold for RL-driven CPS. It is the 5-tuple $(\mathcal{S}, \mathcal{A}, P, R, \gamma)$, where the action $a \in \mathcal{A}$ sits at the heart of every transition: - **State Space $\mathcal{S}$**: All observable configurations of the CPS. - **Action Space $\mathcal{A}$**: The control alphabet the agent can emit. - **Transition Kernel $P(s' \mid s, a)$**: Probability of reaching $s'$ after action $a$ in $s$. - **Reward Function $R(s, a, s')$**: Scalar feedback received. - **Discount Factor $\gamma \in [0, 1)$**: Weighs future vs. immediate rewards. ### 2.1.1 Why Actions Matter — The "Why" Behind the Concept > [!NOTE] > **The "Why":** In CPS, actions are the **only tangible interface** between the learning algorithm and the physical plant. A misclassified image (perception error) can be tolerated by software; a **wrong actuator command** can cause physical destruction (motor burnout, vehicle collision). Hence the rigorous treatment of actions — including their spaces, selection policies, and value estimates — is non-negotiable. ## 2.2 The Action-Value Function $Q(s, a)$ — Definition and Significance The **Action-Value Function** (or Q-function) quantifies the long-term expected return when action $a$ is taken in state $s$ and policy $\pi$ is followed thereafter: $$Q_{\pi}(s, a) \;=\; \mathbb{E}_{\pi}\!\left[\, G_t \;\middle|\; S_t = s,\; A_t = a \,\right]$$ where the **discounted return** $G_t$ is: $$G_t \;=\; R_{t+1} \;+\; \gamma R_{t+2} \;+\; \gamma^{2} R_{t+3} \;+\; \dots \;=\; \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^{k}\, R_{t+k+1}$$ ## 2.3 The Bellman Optimality Equation for Actions The recursive decomposition of $Q^*$ (the optimal action-value) is: $$Q^{*}(s, a) \;=\; \sum_{s',\, r}\, p(s', r \mid s, a)\!\left[\, r \;+\; \gamma \,\max_{a'} Q^{*}(s', a')\,\right]$$ The **optimal policy** at any state is then obtained by **action selection** via: $$\pi^{*}(s) \;=\; \arg\max_{a \in \mathcal{A}(s)} Q^{*}(s, a)$$ ## 2.4 Action Selection Strategies (Exploration–Exploitation Trade-off) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 High-Yield Section:** Examiners consistently test the three canonical action-selection rules. Memorize the formulas, the trade-off they encode, and the parameter meanings. ### 2.4.1 $\varepsilon$-Greedy (Greedy with Probabilistic Exploration) $$\pi(a \mid s) \;=\; \begin{cases} 1 - \varepsilon + \dfrac{\varepsilon}{\vert \mathcal{A}(s) \vert} & \text{if } a = \arg\max_{a'} Q(s, a') \\[10pt] \dfrac{\varepsilon}{\vert \mathcal{A}(s) \vert} & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$ - $\varepsilon$ is the **exploration rate** (typically annealed from 1.0 $\to$ 0.01). - Ensures every action is sampled infinitely often if state visits are infinite (Glimm–Ripley condition). ### 2.4.2 Softmax / Boltzmann Exploration $$\pi(a \mid s) \;=\; \frac{\exp\!\left(Q(s, a)\, /\, \tau\right)}{\sum_{a' \in \mathcal{A}(s)} \exp\!\left(Q(s, a')\, /\, \tau\right)}$$ - $\tau$ is the **temperature** parameter. High $\tau \Rightarrow$ near-uniform (exploratory); $\tau \to 0 \Rightarrow$ greedy. - Sophisticated alternative to $\varepsilon$-greedy as it weights exploration by relative Q-values. ### 2.4.3 Upper Confidence Bound (UCB) $$A_{t} \;=\; \arg\max_{a \in \mathcal{A}} \left[\, Q_t(a) \;+\; c\,\sqrt{\frac{\ln t}{N_t(a)}}\,\right]$$ - $N_t(a)$ = number of times action $a$ has been selected up to step $t$. - $c$ = confidence-scaling constant controlling optimism under uncertainty. ## 2.5 Continuous Action Spaces — Reparameterization For continuous $\mathcal{A} \subseteq \mathbb{R}^d$, the action is drawn from a Gaussian policy: $$a \sim \mathcal{N}\!\left(\mu_{\theta}(s),\; \Sigma_{\theta}(s)\right)$$ with policy parameters $\theta$ updated by REINFORCE / Actor-Critic gradients. ## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > **Examiner-Approved Quick Reference Table** — *Reproduce in rough work for rapid recall during ESE.* | Concept | Formula | Variable Meaning | Engineering Use | |---|---|---|---| | Return | $G_t = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^{k} R_{t+k+1}$ | $R$ reward, $\gamma$ discount | Long-term reward aggregation | | Action-Value | $Q_{\pi}(s, a) = \mathbb{E}_{\pi}[G_t \mid s, a]$ | Expected return after $(s, a)$ | Evaluate each actuator command | | Bellman Optimality | $Q^{*}(s, a) = \sum_{s',r} p(s',r \mid s, a)\left[r + \gamma \max_{a'} Q^{*}(s', a')\right]$ | Self-consistent optimal Q | Solve for best actuator policy | | Optimal Action | $\pi^{*}(s) = \arg\max_{a} Q^{*}(s, a)$ | Greedy choice over Q-values | Real-time CPS control | | $\varepsilon$-Greedy | $\pi(a \mid s) = 1 - \varepsilon + \varepsilon / \vert \mathcal{A} \vert$ (greedy) | $\varepsilon$ exploration rate | Discrete switching decisions | | Softmax Policy | $\pi(a \mid s) \propto \exp\!\left(Q(s, a) / \tau\right)$ | $\tau$ temperature | Probability-weighted exploration | | UCB Action | $A_t = \arg\max_{a}\left[Q_t(a) + c\sqrt{\ln t / N_t(a)}\right]$ | $N_t(a)$ visit count | Bandit-style CPS control | | Gaussian Continuous | $a \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu_{\theta}(s), \Sigma_{\theta}(s))$ | $\theta$ policy net weights | Robotic actuator commands | | TD Update on Action | $Q(s, a) \leftarrow Q(s, a) + \alpha\!\left[r + \gamma \max_{a'} Q(s', a') - Q(s, a)\right]$ | $\alpha$ learning rate | Online Q-Learning in CPS | | Policy Gradient | $\nabla_{\theta} J(\theta) = \mathbb{E}_{\pi}\!\left[\nabla_{\theta} \log \pi_{\theta}(a \mid s)\, Q(s, a)\right]$ | $J$ objective return | Direct actor optimization | ## 2.7 Real-World Utility in Engineering Fields | Domain | Action Example | Learning Requirement | |---|---|---| | Autonomous Vehicles | Steering, throttle, brake | Continuous-action DDPG | | Smart Grid | Load-shedding switch | Discrete Q-Learning | | Industrial Robotics | Joint torques | Policy-gradient SAC | | HVAC Control | Damper position | Continuous PPO | | Autonomous Drones | Rotor RPM | Continuous DDPG + safety shields | # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Bellman Equation for the Action-Value Function ### Goal Derive the recursive Bellman equation for $Q_{\pi}(s, a)$ starting from first principles. This derivation is a **favorite 7-mark sub-question** in KTU Module-3 examinations. ### Step-by-Step Deduction **Step 1 — Definition of $Q_{\pi}(s, a)$** Start from the formal definition. The action-value at time $t$ under policy $\pi$ is the conditional expectation of the return: $$Q_{\pi}(s, a) \;=\; \mathbb{E}_{\pi}\!\left[\, G_t \,\middle|\, S_t = s,\; A_t = a\,\right]$$ **Step 2 — Expand the Return $G_t$** Decompose $G_t$ into the immediate reward $R_{t+1}$ and the discounted remainder $\gamma G_{t+1}$: $$Q_{\pi}(s, a) \;=\; \mathbb{E}_{\pi}\!\left[\, R_{t+1} \;+\; \gamma\, G_{t+1} \,\middle|\, S_t = s,\; A_t = a\,\right]$$ **Step 3 — Apply Linearity of Expectation** Distribute the conditional expectation across the additive terms: $$Q_{\pi}(s, a) \;=\; \mathbb{E}_{\pi}\!\left[\, R_{t+1} \,\middle|\, S_t = s,\; A_t = a\,\right] \;+\; \gamma\, \mathbb{E}_{\pi}\!\left[\, G_{t+1} \,\middle|\, S_t = s,\; A_t = a\,\right]$$ **Step 4 — Marginalize Over the Next State $s'$** The first term depends only on $(s, a)$ and the transition kernel $p(s' \mid s, a)$. Let $r(s, a, s')$ denote the expected immediate reward for the transition $(s, a) \to s'$: $$\mathbb{E}_{\pi}\!\left[\, R_{t+1} \,\middle|\, s, a\,\right] \;=\; \sum_{s'} p(s' \mid s, a)\, r(s, a, s')$$ **Step 5 — Marginalize the Future Return Over $s'$ and $a'$** The second term — the expected future return — must sum over the next-state distribution and the next action drawn from $\pi$: $$\mathbb{E}_{\pi}\!\left[\, G_{t+1} \,\middle|\, s, a\,\right] \;=\; \sum_{s'} p(s' \mid s, a) \sum_{a'} \pi(a' \mid s')\, \mathbb{E}_{\pi}\!\left[\, G_{t+1} \,\middle|\, s', a'\,\right]$$ Recognize that $\mathbb{E}_{\pi}[G_{t+1} \mid s', a'] = Q_{\pi}(s', a')$, yielding the recursion: $$Q_{\pi}(s, a) \;=\; \sum_{s',\, r} p(s', r \mid s, a)\!\left[\, r \;+\; \gamma \sum_{a'} \pi(a' \mid s')\, Q_{\pi}(s', a')\,\right]$$ **Step 6 — Specialize to the Optimal Policy** When the inner sum uses the **maximum** over $a'$ (greedy in Q-values), we obtain the **Bellman Optimality Equation for Actions**: $$Q^{*}(s, a) \;=\; \sum_{s',\, r} p(s', r \mid s, a)\!\left[\, r \;+\; \gamma\, \max_{a'} Q^{*}(s', a')\,\right]$$ > [!NOTE] > **Conversion Logic Recap:** Step 4 introduces the law of total probability; Step 5 uses the Markov property to factor the conditional expectation; Step 6 swaps the expectation of the policy for a maximization, which is the defining transition from a *policy-evaluation* equation to an *optimality* equation. ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — $\varepsilon$-Greedy Action Selection Probability ### Goal Show that the $\varepsilon$-greedy rule assigns probability $1 - \varepsilon + \varepsilon/\vert \mathcal{A} \vert$ to the greedy action and $\varepsilon/\vert \mathcal{A} \vert$ to every other action, with the sum of probabilities equal to 1. ### Deduction **Step 1 — Allocate the Exploration Budget** The agent flips a biased coin: with probability $\varepsilon$, it explores; with probability $1 - \varepsilon$, it exploits. When exploring, it samples uniformly from all $\vert \mathcal{A} \vert$ actions. **Step 2 — Compute Probability of the Greedy Action $a^{*}$** The greedy action $a^{*} = \arg\max_{a} Q(s, a)$ receives probability mass from two sources: 1. Direct exploitation: $(1 - \varepsilon)$ when $a^{*}$ is selected. 2. Random exploration: $\varepsilon \cdot \frac{1}{\vert \mathcal{A} \vert}$ when the uniform draw happens to land on $a^{*}$. Summing both contributions: $$P(a = a^{*} \mid s) \;=\; (1 - \varepsilon) \;+\; \frac{\varepsilon}{\vert \mathcal{A} \vert} \;=\; 1 - \varepsilon + \frac{\varepsilon}{\vert \mathcal{A} \vert}$$ **Step 3 — Compute Probability of Any Non-Greedy Action $a \neq a^{*}$** A non-greedy action can only be selected during the exploration branch (probability $\varepsilon$) and only when it is the uniform draw (probability $1 / \vert \mathcal{A} \vert$): $$P(a \mid s) \;=\; \frac{\varepsilon}{\vert \mathcal{A} \vert}, \quad \forall\, a \neq a^{*}$$ **Step 4 — Verify the Probability Sum** The sum across all actions in $\mathcal{A}$ must be 1: $$\sum_{a \in \mathcal{A}} \pi(a \mid s) \;=\; \left(1 - \varepsilon + \frac{\varepsilon}{\vert \mathcal{A} \vert}\right) + (\vert \mathcal{A} \vert - 1)\cdot \frac{\varepsilon}{\vert \mathcal{A} \vert}$$ $$=\; 1 - \varepsilon + \frac{\varepsilon}{\vert \mathcal{A} \vert} + \frac{(\vert \mathcal{A} \vert - 1)\,\varepsilon}{\vert \mathcal{A} \vert}$$ $$=\; 1 - \varepsilon + \varepsilon \cdot \frac{1 + \vert \mathcal{A} \vert - 1}{\vert \mathcal{A} \vert} \;=\; 1 - \varepsilon + \varepsilon \;=\; 1 \quad \checkmark$$ ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Softmax Action Selection as a Limit of $\varepsilon$-Greedy ### Goal Demonstrate that the softmax/Boltzmann rule is a *smooth* generalization of the $\varepsilon$-greedy rule and degenerates to greedy as $\tau \to 0$. ### Deduction **Step 1 — Write the Softmax Policy** $$\pi_{\tau}(a \mid s) \;=\; \frac{\exp\!\left(Q(s, a)\, /\, \tau\right)}{\sum_{a'} \exp\!\left(Q(s, a')\, /\, \tau\right)}$$ **Step 2 — Take the Low-Temperature Limit $\tau \to 0^{+}$** As $\tau \to 0$, the exponential of $Q/\tau$ grows fastest for the action with the largest $Q$. Using L'Hôpital-style dominance arguments, the largest term in the numerator and denominator dominates: $$\lim_{\tau \to 0^{+}} \pi_{\tau}(a \mid s) \;=\; \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } a = \arg\max_{a'} Q(s, a') \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$ which is precisely the **pure greedy policy**. **Step 3 — Take the High-Temperature Limit $\tau \to \infty$** As $\tau \to \infty$, all $Q / \tau \to 0$, hence $\exp(Q/\tau) \to \exp(0) = 1$. The policy collapses to uniform: $$\lim_{\tau \to \infty} \pi_{\tau}(a \mid s) \;=\; \frac{1}{\vert \mathcal{A}(s) \vert} \quad \forall\, a \in \mathcal{A}(s)$$ > [!NOTE] > **Conversion Logic Recap:** Temperature $\tau$ plays the role of an *annealing knob*. Sweeping $\tau$ from large (random) to small (greedy) implements a **soft curriculum of exploration**, mirroring the $\varepsilon$-decay schedule of $\varepsilon$-greedy — but with action-proportional weighting. ## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Temporal-Difference Update for Action Values (Q-Learning) ### Goal Show how a sampled experience tuple $(s, a, r, s')$ updates $Q(s, a)$ toward the optimal value. ### Deduction **Step 1 — Sample-Based Estimate of the TD Target** The Bellman optimality equation's expectation is replaced by a single sample: $$\text{TD Target:} \quad y_t \;=\; r \;+\; \gamma\, \max_{a'} Q(s', a')$$ **Step 2 — Temporal-Difference Error** The deviation between the bootstrap target and the current estimate is the **TD error** $\delta_t$: $$\delta_t \;=\; y_t \;-\; Q(s, a) \;=\; r \;+\; \gamma\, \max_{a'} Q(s', a') \;-\; Q(s, a)$$ **Step 3 — Stochastic Gradient Step with Learning Rate $\alpha$** Q-Learning performs an incremental update that minimizes the squared TD error $\delta_t^{2}$: $$Q(s, a) \;\leftarrow\; Q(s, a) \;+\; \alpha\, \delta_t \;=\; Q(s, a) \;+\; \alpha\!\left[\, r \;+\; \gamma\, \max_{a'} Q(s', a') \;-\; Q(s, a)\,\right]$$ **Step 4 — Convergence Statement** Under standard stochastic-approximation assumptions (every $(s, a)$ visited infinitely often, learning-rate schedule satisfying $\sum \alpha_t = \infty$, $\sum \alpha_t^{2} < \infty$), $Q(s, a) \to Q^{*}(s, a)$ almost surely (Watkins & Dayan, 1992). # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Figure 1 — The Agent–Environment Loop with Action Emphasis > [!NOTE] > The diagram below illustrates how an *Action* $a_t$ emitted by the agent propagates through the CPS environment, causing a state transition and producing a reward. This is the canonical RL feedback loop, with physical-plant emphasis suited to CPS modules. ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph AGENT["INTELLIGENT AGENT (Digital Decision Layer)"] policyNode["Policy pi
State to Action Mapping"] qnode["Q-Value Estimator
Action Value Function"] selNode["Action Selector
epsilon-greedy or softmax"] end subgraph PLANT["PHYSICAL PLANT / CPS ENVIRONMENT"] dyn["Dynamics f
s t+1 equals f of s t, a t, w t"] sensor["Sensor Suite
Observes Physical Variables"] reward["Reward Calculator
r t+1 equals R of s t, a t"] end s_t["State s t
Sensor Reading Vector"] a_t["Action a t
Actuator Command Vector"] s_next["Next State s t+1"] r_next["Reward r t+1"] sensor --> s_t s_t --> policyNode policyNode --> selNode qnode --> selNode selNode --> a_t a_t --> dyn a_t --> reward dyn --> s_next sensor -. observes .-> dyn reward --> r_next r_next --> qnode s_next --> policyNode style AGENT fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#0d47a1 style PLANT fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100 style a_t fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,stroke-width:3px ``` ## 4.2 Figure 2 — Taxonomy of Action Spaces in CPS Applications ```mermaid flowchart TD actionRoot["Action Space A"] disc["DISCRETE ACTIONS
A equals a1, a2, ..., ak"] cont["CONTINUOUS ACTIONS
A subset of R power d"] hybrid["MIXED HYBRID ACTIONS"] param["PARAMETERIZED ACTIONS
a equals g of theta"] disc --> d1["Smart Grid Relay
ON or OFF"] disc --> d2["Gear Selection
1, 2, 3, 4, 5, R"] disc --> d3["Lane Change
LEFT or RIGHT or STAY"] cont --> c1["Motor Torque
tau in minus 10 to 10 Nm"] cont --> c2["Steering Angle
phi in minus 30 to 30 deg"] cont --> c3["Throttle Valve
theta in 0 to 1"] hybrid --> h1["UAV Mode plus Thrust
HOVER plus R plus continuous"] hybrid --> h2["Robot Arm
Grip OPEN or CLOSE plus joint angles"] param --> p1["Robotic Gripper
Finger angles theta 1, theta 2"] param --> p2["Soft Actuator
Curvature parameter kappa"] style actionRoot fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#0d47a1,stroke-width:2px style disc fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20 style cont fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#e65100 style hybrid fill:#f8bbd0,stroke:#880e4f style param fill:#d1c4e9,stroke:#311b92 ``` ## 4.3 Figure 3 — Block Diagram of Action-Selection Mechanism ```mermaid flowchart TD sInput["State s"] qTable["Q-Table or Q-Network
Q of s, a"] explore["Exploration Mechanism
Random number r in 0 to 1"] decide{"r less than epsilon?"} exploit["Exploit Branch
a star equals arg max Q"] random["Explore Branch
Uniform sample from A"] output["Selected Action a"] sInput --> qTable qTable --> decide explore --> decide decide -->|YES| random decide -->|NO| exploit random --> output exploit --> output style sInput fill:#e1f5fe,stroke:#01579b style qTable fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17 style decide fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c,stroke-width:2px style output fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,stroke-width:3px ``` ## 4.4 Figure 4 — Exploration vs. Exploitation Trade-off Map ```mermaid flowchart LR A["Pure Exploration
epsilon equals 1 or tau large"] B["Balanced
epsilon equals 0 point 1 or tau medium"] C["Pure Exploitation
epsilon equals 0 or tau small"] A -->|Decrease epsilon or tau| B B -->|Further annealing| C A -.- A_DESC["High discovery
Low reward in short term
Gathers state-action knowledge"] B -.- B_DESC["Optimal for long-run learning
Avoids premature convergence"] C -.- C_DESC["Maximizes immediate reward
Risk of local optima
No further learning"] style A fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#b71c1c style B fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20 style C fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#0d47a1 ``` ## 4.5 Figure 5 — Action Influence on the CPS Closed-Loop Dynamics ```mermaid flowchart LR ctrl["Controller
RL Agent"] cmd["Action a t
Digital Command"] dac["DAC
Digital to Analog"] act["Actuator
Motor / Valve / Switch"] plant["Physical Plant
Dynamics s t+1 equals f of s t, a t"] sens["Sensor
Transducer"] adc["ADC
Analog to Digital"] obs["Observation s t
State Estimate"] ctrl --> cmd --> dac --> act --> plant plant --> sens --> adc --> obs --> ctrl style ctrl fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#0d47a1 style act fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#e65100 style plant fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c,stroke-width:3px style sens fill:#d1c4e9,stroke:#311b92 style cmd fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20 ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Define an "Action" in the context of Reinforcement Learning for Cyber-Physical Systems. Differentiate between discrete and continuous action spaces with one CPS example each.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** An **Action** $a \in \mathcal{A}(s)$ is a control command issued by an RL agent at time $t$, given the current state $s$, to influence the CPS environment via its actuator interface. (1 mark) - **Discrete Action Space:** A finite set $\mathcal{A} = \{a_1, a_2, \dots, a_k\}$. *Example:* ON/OFF switching of a smart-grid relay (1 mark). - **Continuous Action Space:** An interval $\mathcal{A} \subseteq \mathbb{R}^d$. *Example:* Continuous torque command $\tau \in [-10, 10]$ Nm applied to a robotic arm joint (1 mark). ### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **What is the role of the $\varepsilon$-greedy policy in action selection? State the formula for the probability of selecting a non-greedy action.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** The $\varepsilon$-greedy policy resolves the **exploration–exploitation dilemma** by selecting the currently best-known action with probability $1 - \varepsilon$, and exploring uniformly at random with probability $\varepsilon$. (2 marks) The probability of selecting any non-greedy action $a \neq a^{*}$ is: $$\pi(a \mid s) \;=\; \frac{\varepsilon}{\vert \mathcal{A}(s) \vert} \quad \text{(1 mark)}$$ --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) ### Question B-A `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 3]` **(a)** Derive the Bellman Optimality Equation for the action-value function $Q^{*}(s, a)$ starting from its definition. State clearly the meaning of every symbol. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Consider an autonomous vehicle CPS with the following simplified MDP: - States: $s_1$ (highway), $s_2$ (city), $s_3$ (destination) - Actions: $a_1$ (accelerate), $a_2$ (brake), $a_3$ (turn) - Transition probabilities $p(s' \mid s, a)$ and reward function $R(s, a)$ are given in the table: | $(s, a)$ | $s'$ | $p(s' \mid s, a)$ | $R(s, a, s')$ | |---|---|---|---| | $(s_1, a_1)$ | $s_1$ | 0.3 | +1 | | $(s_1, a_1)$ | $s_3$ | 0.7 | +10 | | $(s_1, a_2)$ | $s_2$ | 1.0 | +2 | | $(s_2, a_3)$ | $s_3$ | 1.0 | +5 | Compute $Q^{*}(s_1, a_1)$ assuming $\gamma = 0.9$ and that subsequent actions will be chosen optimally. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution **Part (a) — Derivation of Bellman Optimality for $Q^{*}(s, a)$** **(7 Marks Model Answer — Step-by-Step)** **Step 1:** Begin with the formal definition of the action-value function under policy $\pi$: $$Q_{\pi}(s, a) \;=\; \mathbb{E}_{\pi}\!\left[\, G_t \,\middle|\, S_t = s,\; A_t = a\,\right]$$ **Step 2:** Decompose the return $G_t$ into the immediate reward and the discounted continuation: $$G_t \;=\; R_{t+1} \;+\; \gamma\, G_{t+1}$$ **Step 3:** Substitute into the expectation and apply linearity: $$Q_{\pi}(s, a) \;=\; \mathbb{E}_{\pi}\!\left[\, R_{t+1} \,\middle|\, s, a\,\right] \;+\; \gamma\, \mathbb{E}_{\pi}\!\left[\, G_{t+1} \,\middle|\, s, a\,\right]$$ **Step 4:** Marginalize the first expectation over the next state $s'$: $$\mathbb{E}_{\pi}\!\left[\, R_{t+1} \,\middle|\, s, a\,\right] \;=\; \sum_{s',\, r} p(s', r \mid s, a)\cdot r$$ **Step 5:** For the second expectation, apply the Markov property and marginalize over $s'$ and the next action $a'$ drawn from policy $\pi$: $$\mathbb{E}_{\pi}\!\left[\, G_{t+1} \,\middle|\, s, a\,\right] \;=\; \sum_{s'} p(s' \mid s, a) \sum_{a'} \pi(a' \mid s')\, Q_{\pi}(s', a')$$ **Step 6:** Combine to obtain the Bellman **expectation** equation: $$Q_{\pi}(s, a) \;=\; \sum_{s',\, r} p(s', r \mid s, a)\!\left[\, r \;+\; \gamma \sum_{a'} \pi(a' \mid s')\, Q_{\pi}(s', a')\,\right]$$ **Step 7:** The optimal action-value $Q^{*}$ corresponds to the policy that maximizes the inner sum — replace the policy expectation with a maximization: $$Q^{*}(s, a) \;=\; \sum_{s',\, r} p(s', r \mid s, a)\!\left[\, r \;+\; \gamma\, \max_{a'} Q^{*}(s', a')\,\right]$$ **Symbol Glossary:** - $Q^{*}(s, a)$ = optimal expected return when taking action $a$ in state $s$ - $p(s', r \mid s, a)$ = joint probability of transitioning to $s'$ with reward $r$ after action $a$ in state $s$ - $\gamma$ = discount factor for future rewards - $\max_{a'} Q^{*}(s', a')$ = value of the best next action from $s'$ **Part (b) — Numerical Computation of $Q^{*}(s_1, a_1)$** **Step 1:** Identify the transitions triggered by $(s_1, a_1)$: - Transition 1: $s_1 \xrightarrow{a_1} s_1$, with $p = 0.3$, reward $r = +1$. - Transition 2: $s_1 \xrightarrow{a_1} s_3$, with $p = 0.7$, reward $r = +10$. **Step 2:** Apply the Bellman Optimality Equation directly: $$Q^{*}(s_1, a_1) \;=\; \sum_{s'} p(s' \mid s_1, a_1)\!\left[\, r(s_1, a_1, s') \;+\; \gamma\, \max_{a'} Q^{*}(s', a')\,\right]$$ **Step 3:** Substitute the two transitions. Assume the vehicle has reached terminal $s_3$ or trivially optimal continuation, so $\max_{a'} Q^{*}(s_3, a') = 0$ (absorbing terminal state), and $\max_{a'} Q^{*}(s_1, a') = 0$ for the self-loop (no further improvement beyond this evaluation): $$Q^{*}(s_1, a_1) \;=\; 0.3 \cdot \left[\, 1 + 0.9 \cdot \max_{a'} Q^{*}(s_1, a')\,\right] \;+\; 0.7 \cdot \left[\, 10 + 0.9 \cdot \max_{a'} Q^{*}(s_3, a')\,\right]$$ **Step 4:** Substitute $\max_{a'} Q^{*}(s_3, a') = 0$ and $\max_{a'} Q^{*}(s_1, a') = 0$ (initial Bellman evaluation without bootstrapping beyond known data): $$Q^{*}(s_1, a_1) \;=\; 0.3 \cdot \left[\, 1 + 0\,\right] \;+\; 0.7 \cdot \left[\, 10 + 0\,\right]$$ **Step 5:** Compute numerically: $$Q^{*}(s_1, a_1) \;=\; 0.3 \cdot 1 \;+\; 0.7 \cdot 10 \;=\; 0.3 \;+\; 7.0 \;=\; 7.3$$ **Final Answer:** $\boxed{Q^{*}(s_1, a_1) = 7.3}$ --- ### Question B-B `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 3]` **(a)** Explain in detail the three canonical action-selection strategies: $\varepsilon$-greedy, Softmax (Boltzmann), and Upper Confidence Bound (UCB). Provide the governing equation of each and discuss how each balances exploration vs. exploitation. **(7 marks)** **(b)** An industrial robot arm performs a pick-and-place task with three discrete actions: $a_1$ (reach), $a_2$ (grasp), $a_3$ (release). The current Q-table estimate at state $s$ is: | State | $Q(s, a_1)$ | $Q(s, a_2)$ | $Q(s, a_3)$ | |---|---|---|---| | $s$ | 4.0 | 6.5 | 5.2 | Using $\varepsilon = 0.2$ and $\tau = 1.0$: - (i) Determine the action selected by the $\varepsilon$-greedy policy. **(2 marks)** - (ii) Compute the Softmax probabilities for all three actions. **(3 marks)** - (iii) If action $a_2$ has been tried $N_t(a_2) = 8$ times in $t = 100$ steps and $c = 2$, compute the UCB score for $a_2$ (assume $Q_t(a_2) = 6.5$). **(2 marks)** #### Model Solution **Part (a) — Three Action-Selection Strategies** **Strategy 1: $\varepsilon$-Greedy** (2 marks) - **Idea:** With probability $1 - \varepsilon$ pick the greedy action $\arg\max_a Q(s, a)$; with probability $\varepsilon$ sample uniformly. - **Equation:** $$\pi(a \mid s) \;=\; \begin{cases} 1 - \varepsilon + \frac{\varepsilon}{\vert \mathcal{A} \vert} & \text{if } a = a^{*} \\ \frac{\varepsilon}{\vert \mathcal{A} \vert} & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$ - **Trade-off:** Simple but treats all non-greedy actions as equal — wasteful when some actions are clearly inferior. **Strategy 2: Softmax / Boltzmann** (2 marks) - **Idea:** Probabilities proportional to exponentiated Q-values, scaled by temperature $\tau$. - **Equation:** $$\pi(a \mid s) \;=\; \frac{\exp\!\left(Q(s, a)\, /\, \tau\right)}{\sum_{a'} \exp\!\left(Q(s, a')\, /\, \tau\right)}$$ - **Trade-off:** Sophisticated — assigns higher exploration mass to promising actions. $\tau$ anneals from high (random) to low (greedy). **Strategy 3: Upper Confidence Bound (UCB)** (2 marks) - **Idea:** Add an *optimism bonus* to under-tried actions, encouraging principled exploration. - **Equation:** $$A_t \;=\; \arg\max_{a} \left[\, Q_t(a) \;+\; c\,\sqrt{\frac{\ln t}{N_t(a)}}\,\right]$$ - **Trade-off:** The $1/\sqrt{N_t(a)}$ term guarantees no action is starved — even actions with mediocre Q-values are tried enough to be confident in their rejection. **Conclusion (1 mark):** All three balance exploration and exploitation but differ in *how* they allocate exploration probability. UCB has the strongest regret guarantees (Lai–Robbins bound), Softmax is gradient-friendly, and $\varepsilon$-greedy is the simplest and most widely deployed baseline in CPS. **Part (b) — Numerical Computations** **(i) $\varepsilon$-Greedy Action Selection (2 marks)** The greedy action is $a^{*} = \arg\max_a Q(s, a) = a_2$ since $Q(s, a_2) = 6.5$ is the maximum. - With probability $1 - \varepsilon = 0.8$, action $a_2$ is chosen. - With probability $\varepsilon = 0.2$, a uniform random draw picks $a_1$, $a_2$, or $a_3$. - **Most probable selected action:** $a_2$ (the greedy choice). **(ii) Softmax Probabilities (3 marks)** Compute the exponentials: $$\exp(Q/\tau) \;=\; \exp(4.0/1.0) \;=\; e^{4.0} \;\approx\; 54.598$$ $$\exp(Q/\tau) \;=\; \exp(6.5/1.0) \;=\; e^{6.5} \;\approx\; 665.142$$ $$\exp(Q/\tau) \;=\; \exp(5.2/1.0) \;=\; e^{5.2} \;\approx\; 181.272$$ Sum of exponentials: $$\Sigma \;=\; 54.598 + 665.142 + 181.272 \;=\; 901.012$$ Probabilities: $$\pi(a_1 \mid s) \;=\; \frac{54.598}{901.012} \;\approx\; 0.0606$$ $$\pi(a_2 \mid s) \;=\; \frac{665.142}{901.012} \;\approx\; 0.7382$$ $$\pi(a_3 \mid s) \;=\; \frac{181.272}{901.012} \;\approx\; 0.2012$$ **Sanity check:** $0.0606 + 0.7382 + 0.2012 = 1.0000$ ✓ **(iii) UCB Score for $a_2$ (2 marks)** Apply the UCB formula with $Q_t(a_2) = 6.5$, $N_t(a_2) = 8$, $t = 100$, $c = 2$: $$\text{UCB}_t(a_2) \;=\; Q_t(a_2) \;+\; c\,\sqrt{\frac{\ln t}{N_t(a_2)}} \;=\; 6.5 \;+\; 2 \cdot \sqrt{\frac{\ln 100}{8}}$$ Compute $\ln 100 \approx 4.605$: $$\text{UCB}_t(a_2) \;=\; 6.5 \;+\; 2 \cdot \sqrt{\frac{4.605}{8}} \;=\; 6.5 \;+\; 2 \cdot \sqrt{0.5756} \;=\; 6.5 \;+\; 2 \cdot 0.7587$$ $$\text{UCB}_t(a_2) \;=\; 6.5 \;+\; 1.5174 \;=\; \boxed{8.017}$$ --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in Module-3 "Actions" Questions** > > 1. **Bellman Confusion:** Students frequently write the Bellman equation for the *state-value* $V(s)$ instead of the *action-value* $Q(s, a)$. The presence of the inner $\max_{a'}$ makes it explicitly $Q$-based. Writing $V(s)$ here costs **3 marks**. > > 2. **Missing Symbol Definitions:** Examiners award **1 dedicated mark** for the symbol glossary. Students who skip definitions of $\gamma$, $p$, $r$ lose this even if the derivation is algebraically correct. > > 3. **Probability Sum Failure:** When asked to derive $\varepsilon$-greedy probabilities, students often forget the *additional* $\varepsilon / \vert \mathcal{A} \vert$ term on the greedy action — only writing $(1 - \varepsilon)$. The correct greedy probability is $1 - \varepsilon + \varepsilon / \vert \mathcal{A} \vert$. This is a **2-mark pitfall**. > > 4. **Mixing Up Continuous vs. Discrete:** For continuous-action CPS problems, students sometimes apply argmax over an infinite set, which is undefined. The correct formulation is a policy $\pi_{\theta}(a \mid s)$ parameterized as a Gaussian. Examiners deduct **2 marks** for this confusion. > > 5. **UCB Log Base:** UCB requires the *natural logarithm* $\ln$, not $\log_{10}$. Wrong base loses **1 mark** in numerical problems. > > 6. **TD Update Direction:** The TD update increases $Q(s, a)$ toward the bootstrap target. Students sometimes invert the sign, producing divergence. Memorize: $Q \leftarrow Q + \alpha (\text{target} - Q)$, **never** $Q \leftarrow Q - \alpha (\text{target} - Q)$. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **High-Density Revision Checklist — Print This Before the Exam** - [x] An **Action** $a \in \mathcal{A}(s)$ is the agent's control command that interfaces the digital policy layer with the **physical actuator** of the CPS. - [x] The **action space** $\mathcal{A}(s)$ may be **discrete** (finite set), **continuous** ($\subseteq \mathbb{R}^d$), **hybrid**, or **parameterized** — each requires a different learning algorithm. - [x] The **policy** $\pi(a \mid s)$ is the action-selection distribution; for it to be valid, $\sum_{a} \pi(a \mid s) = 1$ for all $s$. - [x] The **action-value** $Q_{\pi}(s, a)$ is the expected discounted return after executing $a$ in $s$. - [x] **Bellman Optimality Equation for $Q$:** $Q^{*}(s, a) = \sum_{s', r} p(s', r \mid s, a)\left[r + \gamma \max_{a'} Q^{*}(s', a')\right]$ — **the most important formula of Module 3.** - [x] **$\varepsilon$-Greedy:** Greedy with prob $1 - \varepsilon + \varepsilon / \vert \mathcal{A} \vert$; uniform random with prob $\varepsilon / \vert \mathcal{A} \vert$. - [x] **Softmax:** $\pi(a \mid s) \propto \exp(Q(s, a) / \tau)$; degenerates to greedy as $\tau \to 0$. - [x] **UCB:** Adds optimism bonus $c \sqrt{\ln t / N_t(a)}$; has strongest regret guarantees. - [x] **TD Update for Actions:** $Q(s, a) \leftarrow Q(s, a) + \alpha [r + \gamma \max_{a'} Q(s', a') - Q(s, a)]$ — converges to $Q^{*}$ under Robbins–Monro conditions. - [x] **Continuous actions** are sampled from $\mathcal{N}(\mu_{\theta}(s), \Sigma_{\theta}(s))$ and optimized via **policy gradients**: $\nabla_{\theta} J(\theta) = \mathbb{E}[\nabla_{\theta} \log \pi_{\theta}(a \mid s) Q(s, a)]$. - [x] **Exploration–Exploitation Dilemma:** Central trade-off; never ignore it in design questions — examiners look for explicit discussion. - [x] **CPS-Specific Concern:** Action latency, actuator saturation, and safety constraints (shielding) are essential real-world overlays on the theoretical action-selection framework. - [x] **Common Pitfall:** Forgetting that in CPS, the action must respect **physical constraints** (e.g., torque limits, slew-rate bounds) — a theoretical optimum may be physically infeasible. - [x] **Memorize Three Things:** (1) Bellman Optimality for $Q$, (2) $\varepsilon$-greedy formula with both terms, (3) Softmax probability computation.

topics.title: Rewards
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT635 - MACHINE LEARNING FOR CPS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Rewards

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of **Machine Learning for Cyber-Physical Systems (CPS)**, a **Reward** is a scalar numerical feedback signal $R_t \in \mathbb{R}$ that an intelligent agent receives from the environment at each time step $t$ as a consequence of executing an action $A_t$ in a state $S_t$ and transitioning to a new state $S_{t+1}$. The reward is the formal mechanism by which the *purpose* or *objective* of the task is communicated to the learning agent.

Mathematically, the **Reward Function** $R$ is defined as:

$$R : S \times A \times S \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$$

where $R(s, a, s') = \mathbb{E}[R_{t+1} \mid S_t = s, A_t = a, S_{t+1} = s']$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Highlight:** A reward is NOT a label (as in supervised learning). It is a *consequence-based scalar evaluation* emitted by the environment (or a model of the CPS), and the agent's sole objective is to maximize the cumulative stream of these scalars over time.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are teaching a **robot vacuum cleaner** (the agent) to navigate your house (the CPS environment). Every time the robot successfully docks at the charging station, you press a "good job" buzzer (+10 points). Every time it bumps into a wall, you press a "bad job" buzzer (-5 points). Every second of battery drain earns -1 point.

These buzzers are the **rewards**. The robot doesn't know the floor plan. It doesn't get a "map" of correct paths. It simply learns from the stream of positive and negative buzzers that *eventually arrive* as a result of its choices. Over thousands of attempts, it figures out a policy of behavior that maximizes the total of all buzzers combined.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Three Pillars of Reward Design in CPS:**
> 1. **Scalar Value** — A single real number (not a vector, not a class).
> 2. **Delayed Consequence** — Rewards can arrive many steps after the action that caused them (the *credit assignment problem*).
> 3. **Task Encoding** — The reward function **IS** the formal specification of the task. Mis-specifying it is called the *reward hacking* or *Goodhart's Law* failure mode.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Constants

The critical hyper-parameter associated with rewards is the **discount factor** $\gamma$:

- **$\gamma \in [0, 1]$** — dimensionless scalar controlling the present value of future rewards.
- **$\gamma = 0$** → agent is *myopic* (cares only about immediate reward).
- **$\gamma = 1$** → agent is *far-sighted* (treats future and present rewards equally).
- **Standard KTU/CPS convention:** $\gamma \in [0.9, 0.99]$.

The **Return** $G_t$ (cumulative discounted reward) is the metric that *actually* gets maximized:

$$G_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma R_{t+2} + \gamma^2 R_{t+3} + \ldots = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^k R_{t+k+1}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Why discounting matters in CPS:** In control loops for physical systems, distant future rewards are inherently uncertain (actuator wear, environmental drift). Discounting ensures **convergence** of the value function to a finite limit.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Operational Role of Rewards in the RL-CPS Loop

The interaction cycle of an RL agent inside a CPS is fundamentally structured around rewards:

1. **Perception** — Sensors in the CPS observe the physical state $S_t$ (e.g., temperature, velocity, voltage).
2. **Decision** — The policy $\pi(a \mid s)$ selects an action $A_t$ that maps to actuator commands.
3. **Actuation** — Actuators modify the physical world, causing a state transition to $S_{t+1}$.
4. **Reward Emission** — The environment (or a reward-modeling module) emits $R_{t+1}$ based on the new state and action.
5. **Learning Update** — The agent updates its value estimates using the tuple $(S_t, A_t, R_{t+1}, S_{t+1})$.

## 2.2 Taxonomy of Reward Types (KTU High-Yield Classification)

| **Reward Type** | **Mathematical Form** | **CPS Use-Case Example** | **Pros & Cons** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Dense Reward** | $R_{t+1}$ emitted at *every* step | Lane-keeping in autonomous vehicles: small +0.1 every frame the car stays centered | + Faster learning. − Expensive to specify, can lead to reward hacking. |
| **Sparse Reward** | $R_{t+1} = 0$ for most $t$, nonzero only at goal | Robotic arm reaching a target: +100 only on grasp, 0 otherwise | + Simple to define. − Credit assignment nightmare. |
| **Positive Reward** | $R_{t+1} > 0$ for desired behavior | Energy-efficient HVAC: +1 per kWh saved | Reinforces desired actions. |
| **Negative Reward (Penalty/Cost)** | $R_{t+1} < 0$ for undesired behavior | Collision in drone swarm: -1000 per crash | Discourages unsafe actions. |
| **Shaped Reward** | $R' = R + F(s, a, s')$ with shaping potential $\Phi$ | Distance-to-goal bonus in navigation | + Accelerates learning. − Must use *potential-based* shaping to preserve optimal policy. |
| **Intrinsic Reward** | $R^{int} = f(\text{novelty}, \text{curiosity})$ | Exploration bonus in unknown CPS environments | + Solves sparse reward. − Can cause distraction (the "noisy TV" problem). |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following equations are the **must-know reward mathematics** for the KTU 2024 ESE on this module. All units of reward are **dimensionless** (or system-specific utility units).

| **#** | **Concept** | **Equation** | **Boundary / Domain** |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Reward Function | $R(s,a,s') = \mathbb{E}[R_{t+1} \mid S_t=s, A_t=a, S_{t+1}=s']$ | $R: S \times A \times S \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$ |
| 2 | Return (Cumulative Reward) | $G_t = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^k R_{t+k+1}$ | Finite iff $\gamma < 1$ and $R$ bounded |
| 3 | Discounted Return (Recursive) | $G_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma G_{t+1}$ | $\gamma \in [0, 1)$ |
| 4 | State-Value Function | $v_\pi(s) = \mathbb{E}_\pi[G_t \mid S_t = s]$ | Equals expected return from $s$ under $\pi$ |
| 5 | Action-Value Function (Q) | $q_\pi(s,a) = \mathbb{E}_\pi[G_t \mid S_t=s, A_t=a]$ | Foundation of Q-learning |
| 6 | Expected Step Reward | $\bar{r}(s,a) = \sum_{s'} P(s' \mid s,a) \, R(s,a,s')$ | Sum over reachable $s'$ |
| 7 | Potential-Based Shaping | $F(s,a,s') = \gamma \Phi(s') - \Phi(s)$ | $\Phi: S \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$ is any potential |
| 8 | Optimality of Shaping | $\pi^{*\prime} = \pi^{*}$ under $R'$ | Holds **iff** $F$ is potential-based (Ng et al., 1999) |
| 9 | Average Reward Setting | $r(\pi) = \lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{1}{T} \mathbb{E}\left[\sum_{t=0}^{T} R_{t+1}\right]$ | Used in continuing (non-episodic) CPS tasks |
| 10 | Reward Variance Bound | $\text{Var}(G_t) \leq \frac{R_{\max}^2}{(1-\gamma)^2}$ | Assumes $\vert R_{t+1} \vert \leq R_{\max}$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Pitfall:** In tables and equations, the *vertical bar* (conditional probability) is rendered as `\mid` or `\vert`, never as a raw `\|` pipe, to prevent markdown table parsing failures.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

In CPS applications, rewards are the **single point of contact between human intent and autonomous behavior**. They are used in:

- **Autonomous Driving** — Reward for staying in lane, penalty for harsh braking.
- **Smart Grid Control** — Reward for load balancing, penalty for frequency deviation.
- **Robotic Manipulation** — Reward for successful grasp, penalty for torque saturation.
- **Industrial Process Control** — Reward for yield maximization, penalty for off-spec product.

> [!TIP]
> The *art* of CPS reward engineering is balancing **alignment** (the reward actually captures the designer's true intent) with **learnability** (the reward signal is informative enough for sample-efficient policy improvement).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — From One-Step Reward to Discounted Return

We begin from the definition of the return as a sum of future rewards, and show that it admits a *recursive* (Bellman-style) form. This is the cornerstone identity used in nearly every RL algorithm.

**Starting Point:** The undiscounted cumulative reward to horizon $T$ is

$$G_t^{(T)} = R_{t+1} + R_{t+2} + R_{t+3} + \ldots + R_{t+T}$$

**Step 1: Factor out the first reward.**

The first term in the sum is $R_{t+1}$, and the remainder is identical in form to $G_{t+1}^{(T-1)}$:

$$G_t^{(T)} = R_{t+1} + \left( R_{t+2} + R_{t+3} + \ldots + R_{t+T} \right)$$

$$G_t^{(T)} = R_{t+1} + G_{t+1}^{(T-1)}$$

**Step 2: Introduce the discount factor $\gamma \in [0, 1)$ on each future term.**

The discounted return assigns geometrically decreasing weight to distant rewards:

$$G_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma R_{t+2} + \gamma^2 R_{t+3} + \gamma^3 R_{t+4} + \ldots$$

$$G_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma \left( R_{t+2} + \gamma R_{t+3} + \gamma^2 R_{t+4} + \ldots \right)$$

**Step 3: Recognize the bracketed expression as $\gamma G_{t+1}$.**

The inner sum is precisely $\gamma G_{t+1}$ (note the leading $\gamma$ already factored out):

$$G_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma \, G_{t+1}$$

**Step 4: Verify convergence.**

Since $\vert R_{t+1} \vert \leq R_{\max}$ (bounded reward assumption) and $\gamma < 1$, the geometric series $\sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^k$ converges to $\frac{1}{1-\gamma}$. Hence:

$$\vert G_t \vert \leq R_{\max} \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^k = \frac{R_{\max}}{1 - \gamma}$$

This bound justifies the finiteness of the value function in discounted settings — a critical KTU exam point.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters:** The recursive form $G_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma G_{t+1}$ is the *seed* from which the Bellman optimality equation and the TD(0) learning update are derived. Every modern RL algorithm (Q-learning, SARSA, Actor-Critic, PPO) traces its lineage back to this identity.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Expected Reward Under a Stochastic Policy

We derive the expected *one-step* reward when the agent follows a stochastic policy $\pi(a \mid s)$ and the environment has stochastic dynamics $P(s' \mid s, a)$.

**Starting Point:** The expected value of $R_{t+1}$ conditioned on being in state $s$ and following $\pi$:

$$\bar{r}_\pi(s) = \mathbb{E}_\pi [R_{t+1} \mid S_t = s]$$

**Step 1: Apply the law of total expectation, marginalizing over actions.**

$$\bar{r}_\pi(s) = \sum_{a \in \mathcal{A}} \pi(a \mid s) \, \mathbb{E}[R_{t+1} \mid S_t = s, A_t = a]$$

**Step 2: Further marginalize over next-state $s'$ using the dynamics.**

The inner expectation, conditioned on $(s, a)$, is an expectation over $s' \sim P(\cdot \mid s, a)$:

$$\mathbb{E}[R_{t+1} \mid S_t = s, A_t = a] = \sum_{s' \in \mathcal{S}} P(s' \mid s, a) \, R(s, a, s')$$

**Step 3: Combine both marginalizations.**

$$\bar{r}_\pi(s) = \sum_{a \in \mathcal{A}} \pi(a \mid s) \sum_{s' \in \mathcal{S}} P(s' \mid s, a) \, R(s, a, s')$$

**Step 4: Rewrite in compact tensor notation.**

$$\bar{r}_\pi(s) = \sum_{a, s'} \pi(a \mid s) \, P(s' \mid s, a) \, R(s, a, s')$$

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Insight:** In CPS, $\bar{r}_\pi(s)$ is what a *digital twin* can pre-compute offline to accelerate online policy evaluation, since the system dynamics $P$ are often known from first-principles physical models.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Potential-Based Reward Shaping (Ng, Harada, Russell, 1999)

We derive the *only* safe form of reward shaping that provably preserves the optimal policy. This is a **favourite KTU 14-mark question** topic.

**Problem Statement:** Given an original reward $R(s, a, s')$, we want to add a shaping term $F(s, a, s')$ to accelerate learning, while guaranteeing that the optimal policy under $R' = R + F$ is identical to the optimal policy under $R$.

**Step 1: Define the candidate shaping function as a difference of potentials.**

Let $\Phi : \mathcal{S} \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$ be any real-valued function on states. Define:

$$F(s, a, s') = \gamma \, \Phi(s') - \Phi(s)$$

**Step 2: Compute the new Q-function under the shaped reward.**

The shaped Q-function is:

$$q^{*'}_\pi(s, a) = \mathbb{E}_\pi \left[ \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^k (R_{t+k+1} + F_{t+k+1}) \,\middle|\, S_t = s, A_t = a \right]$$

**Step 3: Substitute the shaping definition and telescope.**

$$q^{*'}_\pi(s, a) = \mathbb{E}_\pi \left[ \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^k R_{t+k+1} \,\middle|\, S_t=s, A_t=a \right] + \mathbb{E}_\pi \left[ \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^k (\gamma \Phi(S_{t+k+1}) - \Phi(S_{t+k})) \right]$$

The first term is the original $q^*_\pi(s, a)$. The second term telescopes:

$$\sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^k \gamma \Phi(S_{t+k+1}) = \sum_{k=1}^{\infty} \gamma^k \Phi(S_{t+k})$$

$$\sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^k \Phi(S_{t+k}) = \Phi(S_t) + \sum_{k=1}^{\infty} \gamma^k \Phi(S_{t+k})$$

Subtracting:

$$\text{Second term} = -\Phi(S_t) = -\Phi(s)$$

**Step 4: Conclude the policy invariance.**

$$q^{*'}_\pi(s, a) = q^*_\pi(s, a) - \Phi(s)$$

Since $\Phi(s)$ does not depend on the action $a$, the argmax over actions is unchanged:

$$\arg\max_a q^{*'}_\pi(s, a) = \arg\max_a q^*_\pi(s, a)$$

$$\boxed{\pi^{*\prime} = \pi^{*}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Theorem (Ng et al.):** Potential-based reward shaping using $F(s,a,s') = \gamma\Phi(s') - \Phi(s)$ is **necessary and sufficient** to guarantee policy invariance while accelerating convergence. Any other shaping form risks producing a *different* optimal policy.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Reward Formulations

| **Property** | **Dense Reward** | **Sparse Reward** | **Shaped Reward** | **Average Reward** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Information density per step** | High | Very low | High | Medium |
| **Sample efficiency** | High | Low | High | Medium |
| **Risk of reward hacking** | High | Low | Medium-High | Medium |
| **Spec complexity** | High | Low | Medium | Medium |
| **Best CPS application** | Continuous control (motors, HVAC) | Goal-reaching (manipulation) | Navigation (drones) | Continuing tasks (process control) |
| **Optimality guarantee** | Direct | Direct | Only if potential-based | Requires differential value |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Reward Flow in an RL-Controlled CPS

The following block diagram traces the *closed-loop* flow of rewards through a generic reinforcement learning controller embedded in a cyber-physical system.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CPSEnv["Physical Environment CPS"]
        Plant["Physical Plant Sensors and Actuators"]
    end
    St["State St observed"]
    At["Action At selected"]
    Rwd["Reward Rt+1 emitted"]
    Stp1["Next State St+1"]
    subgraph Agent["RL Agent Software"]
        Pol["Policy pi"]
        ValFn["Value Function v or q"]
        Upd["Learning Update Rule"]
    end
    St --> Pol
    Pol --> At
    At --> Plant
    Plant --> Stp1
    Stp1 --> Rwd
    Rwd --> ValFn
    At --> ValFn
    ValFn --> Upd
    Upd --> Pol
    classDef env fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0d6efd,color:#000;
    classDef agent fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#198754,color:#000;
    classDef sig fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#fd7e14,color:#000;
    class St,At,Stp1,Rwd sig;
    class Pol,ValFn,Upd agent;
    class Plant env;
```

**Reading Guide:**
- The **blue cluster** is the physical world (sensors sample $S_t$, actuators apply $A_t$).
- The **green cluster** is the RL software (policy, value function, learning rule).
- The **yellow nodes** are the *reward-bearing signals* — these are the only quantities that carry task information from the physical world back to the learner.

    Intrinsic["Intrinsic reward curiosity"]
    Avg["Average reward continuing"]

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper Style]**

**Q1.** *Define the term "reward" in a reinforcement learning framework for cyber-physical systems. How does it differ from a "label" in supervised learning?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **reward** $R_{t+1}$ is a scalar numerical feedback signal emitted by the environment at time $t+1$ in response to the agent executing action $A_t$ in state $S_t$. It is generated by the *reward function* $R : S \times A \times S \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$.

It differs from a supervised **label** in three ways:
1. **Sourcing** — Labels are provided by an external oracle/ground-truth dataset; rewards are emitted by the environment (or a learned model) as a *consequence of action*.
2. **Timing** — Labels are typically available immediately and synchronously; rewards may be **delayed** (the credit assignment problem).
3. **Optimality** — Labels encode the *correct answer*; rewards encode *how good* an outcome was, and do not prescribe a unique action.

> *(Examiner's note: Award full 3 marks only if all three differences are explicitly stated.)*

**Q2.** *State the recursive form of the discounted return and explain the role of the discount factor $\gamma$ in CPS control loops.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The recursive form of the discounted return is:

$$G_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma \, G_{t+1}, \quad \gamma \in [0, 1)$$

The discount factor $\gamma$ plays three roles in CPS control:
1. **Mathematical** — Ensures $G_t$ remains bounded (by $\frac{R_{\max}}{1-\gamma}$) so that value-function estimates converge.
2. **Statistical** — Encodes the increasing uncertainty of distant future rewards (actuator wear, model drift).
3. **Behavioral** — Controls the agent's planning horizon: $\gamma \to 0$ gives myopic behavior, $\gamma \to 1$ gives far-sighted behavior.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Adapted Style]**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Derive the recursive form of the discounted return $G_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma G_{t+1}$ starting from its definition as an infinite geometric series. Clearly state the bounded-reward and $\gamma < 1$ assumptions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With the help of a block diagram, describe the closed-loop flow of a reward signal in a reinforcement learning controller embedded inside a cyber-physical system. Identify the entities that emit, process, and consume the reward. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Solution:**

**Step 1 — Definition:** The discounted return is the sum of all future rewards, each scaled by a power of the discount factor:

$$G_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma R_{t+2} + \gamma^2 R_{t+3} + \ldots = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^k R_{t+k+1}$$

**Step 2 — Factor the first term:** We separate the $k=0$ term:

$$G_t = R_{t+1} + \sum_{k=1}^{\infty} \gamma^k R_{t+k+1}$$

**Step 3 — Re-index the sum:** Let $j = k - 1$, so $k = j+1$ and $R_{t+k+1} = R_{t+j+2}$:

$$\sum_{k=1}^{\infty} \gamma^k R_{t+k+1} = \sum_{j=0}^{\infty} \gamma^{j+1} R_{t+j+2} = \gamma \sum_{j=0}^{\infty} \gamma^{j} R_{(t+1)+j+1}$$

**Step 4 — Recognize the inner sum as $\gamma G_{t+1}$:**

The inner sum is precisely $G_{t+1}$, so:

$$G_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma \, G_{t+1}$$

**Step 5 — Justify convergence (assumptions):**
- **Assumption 1 (Bounded reward):** $\vert R_{t} \vert \leq R_{\max}$ for all $t$.
- **Assumption 2 (Discount factor):** $\gamma \in [0, 1)$.

Then the infinite geometric series $\sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^k = \frac{1}{1-\gamma}$ converges, and:

$$\vert G_t \vert \leq \frac{R_{\max}}{1-\gamma} < \infty \quad \blacksquare$$

**(b) Solution:**

**Block diagram (textual description for written exam):**

```
+---------------+       +-----------+       +-------------------+
|  Environment  |------>|  Agent    |------>|  Physical Plant   |
|  (CPS World)  |  St   |  Policy π |  At   |  (Sensors/Actuators)|
+---------------+       +-----------+       +-------------------+
        ^                                            |
        |                                            v
        |            +--------------------+   +----------------+
        +------------|  Reward Function   |<--|  Next State    |
        |  Rt+1      |  R(s, a, s')       |   |  St+1          |
        |            +--------------------+   +----------------+
        |                     ^
        |                     |
        |            +--------------------+
        +-----------|  Value Function &   |
          Q-value   |  Learning Update    |
                     +--------------------+
```

**Flow description (marking scheme aligned):**
- **Emitter of reward:** The environment (or a digital twin / reward model) computes $R_{t+1} = R(S_t, A_t, S_{t+1})$.
- **Consumer of reward:** The value function $v_\pi(s)$ and the learning-update rule (e.g., TD(0), Q-learning) which adjusts the policy $\pi$.
- **Loop closure:** The updated policy emits a new action, which re-enters the physical plant, completing the RL-CPS closed loop.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** *(Alternative Choice)*

**(a)** Explain *potential-based reward shaping*. State and prove the policy-invariance theorem for a shaping function of the form $F(s, a, s') = \gamma \Phi(s') - \Phi(s)$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A robotic arm CPS receives a reward of $+10$ upon grasping a target object, $-1$ for every second of operation, and $0$ otherwise. The episode length is 20 steps and the discount factor is $\gamma = 0.95$. Compute the expected return $G_0$ if the grasp succeeds at step $t=15$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Solution:**

**Conceptual Explanation (3 marks):**
Potential-based reward shaping modifies the original reward $R$ with an *additional* term $F$ that is expressed as the difference of a *potential function* evaluated at consecutive states. The potential $\Phi(s)$ encodes "how close" the agent is to the goal. This is mathematically guaranteed *not* to change the optimal policy, while providing a dense learning signal that dramatically accelerates convergence in sparse-reward CPS tasks like robotic grasping.

**Theorem Statement (1 mark):**
*If $F(s, a, s') = \gamma \Phi(s') - \Phi(s)$ for some real-valued function $\Phi: \mathcal{S} \to \mathbb{R}$, then the optimal policy under the shaped reward $R' = R + F$ is identical to the optimal policy under the original reward $R$.*

**Proof (3 marks):**
- The Q-function under $R'$ satisfies (using the recursive return):

$$q^{*'}(s,a) = \mathbb{E}\left[ R + F + \gamma G' \mid s, a \right]$$

- Substituting $F = \gamma\Phi(s') - \Phi(s)$ and expanding the telescoping sum over an infinite horizon:

$$q^{*'}(s,a) = q^*(s,a) + \mathbb{E}\left[ \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^k (\gamma\Phi(s_{k+1}) - \Phi(s_k)) \,\middle|\, s_0=s, a_0=a \right]$$

- The sum telescopes to $-\Phi(s_0) = -\Phi(s)$ (as shown in the detailed derivation in Section 3.3).

- Therefore:

$$q^{*'}(s,a) = q^*(s,a) - \Phi(s)$$

- Since $\Phi(s)$ does not depend on the action $a$, $\arg\max_a q^{*'}(s,a) = \arg\max_a q^*(s,a)$, so $\pi^{*\prime} = \pi^*$. $\blacksquare$

**(b) Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify the reward at each time step.**

The reward structure is:
- $R_t = -1$ for all $t$ (per-second operational cost).
- $R_{15} = +10$ (grasp bonus received at step 15, replacing the $-1$ cost for that step).
- All other $R_t = 0$.

So the full reward sequence starting from $t=0$ is:

$$R_1, R_2, \ldots, R_{15}, \ldots, R_{20} = -1, -1, \ldots, (+10), \ldots, -1$$

**Step 2 — Write the return formula.**

$$G_0 = \sum_{k=0}^{19} \gamma^k R_{k+1}$$

**Step 3 — Separate the sum into a baseline cost and the grasp bonus.**

$$G_0 = \underbrace{\sum_{k=0}^{19} \gamma^k (-1)}_{\text{cost term}} + \underbrace{\sum_{k=0}^{19} \gamma^k \cdot (\text{grasp bonus adjustment at } k=14)}_{\text{bonus term}}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the cost term.**

The cost term is a finite geometric series:

$$C = -\sum_{k=0}^{19} (0.95)^k = -\frac{1 - (0.95)^{20}}{1 - 0.95}$$

**Step 5 — Compute the grasp bonus term.**

At step $t=15$ (which corresponds to $k=14$ in the sum), the reward becomes $+10$ instead of $-1$. The *net adjustment* at $k=14$ is $(+10) - (-1) = +11$. So the bonus term is:

$$B = +11 \cdot (0.95)^{14}$$

**Step 6 — Numerical evaluation.**

For the cost term:

$$(0.95)^{20} \approx 0.3585$$

$$C = -\frac{1 - 0.3585}{0.05} = -\frac{0.6415}{0.05} = -12.83$$

For the bonus term:

$$(0.95)^{14} \approx 0.4877$$

$$B = 11 \times 0.4877 = 5.36$$

**Step 7 — Final answer.**

$$G_0 = C + B = -12.83 + 5.36 = \boxed{-7.47}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Common Mistake #1 (2 marks lost):** Treating the $+10$ as replacing the $-1$ incorrectly — students often write $R_{15} = +10 - 1 = 9$ but then *also* subtract the $-1$ for that step. The net adjustment is $+11$, not $+9$.
> 2. **Common Mistake #2 (2 marks lost):** Using the *episodic* return ($T=20$, no discount) instead of the *discounted* return. The problem explicitly states $\gamma = 0.95$, which is a strong hint to use the discount factor.
> 3. **Common Mistake #3 (1 mark lost):** Forgetting to show the *formula* substitution for $(0.95)^{20}$ and $(0.95)^{14}$. Always write the expression *before* the numerical value.
> 4. **Common Mistake #4 (1 mark lost):** Not stating the bounded-reward and $\gamma < 1$ assumptions in the derivation question. Even if your math is correct, omitting the assumptions loses at least 1 mark.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist**

- **Reward** $R_{t+1} \in \mathbb{R}$ is a *scalar*, emitted by the environment, encoding task success/failure.
- The **Reward Function** $R(s, a, s')$ is a mapping from transitions to real numbers.
- The **Return** $G_t$ is the *objective* being maximized: $G_t = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^k R_{t+k+1}$.
- The **Recursive Return** $G_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma G_{t+1}$ is the *most-tested equation* in this module.
- **Discount factor** $\gamma \in [0, 1)$ ensures convergence and encodes planning horizon.
- **Convergence bound:** $\vert G_t \vert \leq \frac{R_{\max}}{1-\gamma}$ — must be stated explicitly in derivations.
- **Dense vs Sparse:** Dense rewards accelerate learning but are hard to design; sparse rewards are easy to specify but cause credit-assignment issues.
- **Potential-based shaping** $F = \gamma\Phi(s') - \Phi(s)$ is the *only* shaping form that guarantees policy invariance.
- **Reward hacking** (Goodhart's Law) occurs when the agent maximizes the reward but fails the *spirit* of the task — a critical concern in safety-critical CPS.
- **Average reward** setting is preferred for non-episodic continuing CPS tasks (e.g., process control, smart grid).
- **The reward function IS the task specification** — its design is the most consequential decision in any RL-for-CPS deployment.
- **KTU 2024 standard:** Always use `\vert` or `\mid` in markdown tables for absolute value or conditional probability, never a raw `\|` pipe character.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT635 - MACHINE LEARNING FOR CPS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Policy

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In **Reinforcement Learning (RL)** — which forms the computational backbone of decision-making in Cyber-Physical Systems (CPS) — a **Policy** is formally defined as:

> **Policy (π):** A mapping from perceived states of the environment to actions to be taken when in those states. It is the *strategy* or *decision-making rule* that an RL agent follows to interact with its environment.

Mathematically, a policy can be expressed in two canonical forms:

- **Deterministic Policy:** $\pi(s) = a$ — for every state $s$, a specific action $a$ is selected.
- **Stochastic Policy:** $\pi(a \mid s) = P(a \mid s)$ — for every state $s$, a probability distribution over actions $a \in \mathcal{A}(s)$ is specified.

Where:
- $s \in \mathcal{S}$ represents the state in the **state space** $\mathcal{S}$
- $a \in \mathcal{A}$ represents the action in the **action space** $\mathcal{A}$
- $P(a \mid s)$ is the **conditional probability** of taking action $a$ given state $s$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The policy is the *only* component of the RL agent that directly determines behavior. The value functions ($V_\pi$ and $Q_\pi$) are *defined with respect to a given policy*. Hence, policy is the **central abstraction** in RL for CPS decision-making (e.g., autonomous navigation, robotic control, smart grid optimization).

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

### 🚗 Real-World Analogy: The Self-Driving Car's Rulebook

Imagine you are teaching a **self-driving car** to navigate through Kerala's city traffic. The car constantly observes its surroundings (state) — the position of nearby vehicles, traffic signals, pedestrians, road curvature — and must decide what to do (action) — accelerate, brake, turn left, turn right.

The **Policy** is essentially the **complete rulebook or driving philosophy** the car follows.

| Driving Scenario (State) | Action Taken (Rule) | Policy Component |
|:------------------------|:--------------------|:-----------------|
| Green light ahead | Accelerate | $\pi(s_1) = \text{accelerate}$ |
| Pedestrian crossing | Brake | $\pi(s_2) = \text{brake}$ |
| Heavy rain + sharp turn | Slow down + slight left | $\pi(s_3 \mid s_3) = 0.7$ (stochastic) |

A **deterministic** policy is like a *strict* driver who always makes the *same exact choice* in a given situation. A **stochastic** policy is like an *experienced* driver who might occasionally choose a slightly different maneuver (e.g., 70% brake, 30% gentle swerve) because randomness can help in **exploration** of unfamiliar scenarios.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Stochastic Policies Matter in CPS:** In safety-critical Cyber-Physical Systems (autonomous drones, industrial robots), a *purely deterministic* policy may get stuck in suboptimal behaviors. A stochastic policy provides **controlled randomness** that aids in exploration during training and provides **robustness** against sensor noise and environmental uncertainty.

---

## 1.3 Fundamental Classification of Policies

Policies in RL for CPS are broadly classified along two axes:

### Axis 1: Determinism
- **Deterministic Policy** $\pi: \mathcal{S} \rightarrow \mathcal{A}$
- **Stochastic Policy** $\pi: \mathcal{S} \times \mathcal{A} \rightarrow [0, 1]$

### Axis 2: Time Dependence
- **Stationary Policy:** $\pi(a \mid s)$ does not change with time step $t$.
- **Non-Stationary Policy:** $\pi_t(a \mid s)$ explicitly depends on the time step $t$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Focus Point:** In the KTU 2024 scheme, questions on policy predominantly test the distinction between **deterministic vs stochastic**, the role of the **optimal policy** $\pi^*$, and the **policy improvement theorem** — all of which are covered exhaustively in the following sections.

---

## 1.4 The Central Role of Policy in the RL Framework

The policy sits at the *heart* of the RL loop. It is what connects the agent to the environment:

$$
\text{State } s_t \;\xrightarrow{\;\pi\;}\; \text{Action } a_t \;\rightarrow\; \text{Environment} \;\rightarrow\; (r_{t+1},\; s_{t+1})
$$

The **goal of RL** is to find the **optimal policy** $\pi^*$ that maximizes the expected cumulative discounted reward:

$$
\pi^* = \arg\max_{\pi} \; \mathbb{E}_{\tau \sim \pi}\!\left[\sum_{t=0}^{\infty} \gamma^t r_{t+1}\right]
$$

Where:
- $\gamma \in [0, 1)$ is the **discount factor**
- $\tau = (s_0, a_0, r_1, s_1, a_1, \ldots)$ is the **trajectory** sampled under policy $\pi$
- $\mathbb{E}[\cdot]$ denotes **expectation**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 📗 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Mathematical Anatomy of a Policy

A policy $\pi$ is fully characterized by specifying, for every state $s \in \mathcal{S}$, the probability distribution over actions:

$$
\pi(a \mid s) \geq 0 \quad \forall a \in \mathcal{A}(s), \quad \sum_{a \in \mathcal{A}(s)} \pi(a \mid s) = 1
$$

These two conditions (non-negativity and normalization) ensure that $\pi$ is a **valid probability distribution** at every state.

### Key Observations for KTU Examination:

1. **Policy and Markov Property:** If the policy depends *only* on the current state (not on history), it is called a **Markov policy**. Most KTU problems assume Markov policies.
2. **Sufficiency of Stationary Policies:** For Markov Decision Processes (MDPs) with discounted rewards, there *always exists* an **optimal stationary deterministic policy**. This is a fundamental theorem often tested in KTU exams.
3. **Policy Completeness:** A policy uniquely defines an agent's behavior across the *entire* state space.

---

## 2.2 Value Functions Induced by a Policy

A policy $\pi$ induces two crucial **value functions** that quantify "how good" it is to follow $\pi$ from a given state or state-action pair.

### 2.2.1 State-Value Function $V_\pi(s)$

The expected return when starting in state $s$ and following policy $\pi$ thereafter:

$$
V_\pi(s) = \mathbb{E}_{\pi}\!\left[\sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^k r_{t+k+1} \,\Big|\, s_t = s\right]
$$

### 2.2.2 Action-Value Function $Q_\pi(s, a)$

The expected return when taking action $a$ in state $s$ and then following policy $\pi$:

$$
Q_\pi(s, a) = \mathbb{E}_{\pi}\!\left[\sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^k r_{t+k+1} \,\Big|\, s_t = s,\; a_t = a\right]
$$

### 2.2.3 The Bellman Backup Equations (Fundamental for KTU)

These recursive equations form the analytical heart of policy evaluation:

$$
V_\pi(s) = \sum_{a \in \mathcal{A}} \pi(a \mid s) \sum_{s' \in \mathcal{S}} P(s' \mid s, a) \left[ R(s, a, s') + \gamma V_\pi(s') \right]
$$

$$
Q_\pi(s, a) = \sum_{s' \in \mathcal{S}} P(s' \mid s, a) \left[ R(s, a, s') + \gamma \sum_{a' \in \mathcal{A}} \pi(a' \mid s') Q_\pi(s', a') \right]
$$

Where:
- $P(s' \mid s, a)$ is the **state transition probability** of the MDP
- $R(s, a, s')$ is the **expected reward** received

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility in CPS:** In an autonomous drone CPS, $V_\pi(s)$ answers: *"How good is the drone's current hover state under the current flight policy?"* — and $Q_\pi(s, a)$ answers: *"If I bank left now, what cumulative reward can I expect?"*

---

## 2.3 The Optimal Policy

### 2.3.1 Definition

A policy $\pi^*$ is **optimal** if and only if its value function is greater than or equal to that of *all other policies* for *every state*:

$$
V_{\pi^*}(s) \geq V_\pi(s) \quad \forall s \in \mathcal{S}, \; \forall \pi
$$

### 2.3.2 Optimal Action-Value Function

$$
Q^*(s, a) = \max_{\pi} Q_\pi(s, a)
$$

### 2.3.3 Extracting the Optimal Policy from $Q^*$

Once $Q^*$ is known, the optimal policy is simply:

$$
\pi^*(s) = \arg\max_{a \in \mathcal{A}} Q^*(s, a)
$$

This is called the **greedy policy** with respect to $Q^*$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key KTU Theorem (Bellman Optimality):** There always exists a deterministic optimal policy $\pi^*$ for any finite MDP with a discount factor $\gamma < 1$. Moreover, all optimal policies share the same optimal value function $V^*$.

---

## 2.4 Policy Improvement Theorem (⭐ Most Tested Concept)

This theorem is the *cornerstone* of policy-based RL methods and a **frequent 7-14 mark KTU question**.

**Statement:** Let $\pi$ and $\pi'$ be any two deterministic policies such that for all $s \in \mathcal{S}$:

$$
Q_\pi(s, \pi'(s)) \geq V_\pi(s)
$$

Then the policy $\pi'$ must be at least as good as $\pi$. That is:

$$
V_{\pi'}(s) \geq V_\pi(s) \quad \forall s \in \mathcal{S}
$$

**Intuition:** If the action chosen by $\pi'$ in state $s$ yields a higher (or equal) $Q$-value than the current policy $\pi$'s expected value, then *switching to $\pi'$ improves (or maintains) the agent's performance*.

The **strict improvement** occurs if the inequality is strict for *at least one* state.

---
