Only the final fully-connected layer is replaced and re-trained on synthetic CPS defect data. **Feature-Based Transfer Learning (3.5 marks):** - Transfers the **feature representation** itself. - Sub-categories: - **Asymmetric:** Transforms source features to align with target feature space. - **Symmetric:** Finds a common latent space shared by both domains. - Mathematical objective — minimise MMD: $$\min_{\phi} \mathrm{MMD}^2(\phi(X_S), \phi(X_T))$$ - **CPS Example:** Vibration signals from a CNC machine (source) and a milling machine (target) are mapped into a shared time-frequency feature space using a learnable autoencoder, then a single classifier detects faults on both. --- #### **Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)** *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]* **(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the architecture of a Domain-Adversarial Neural Network (DANN). Derive its combined loss function with the Gradient Reversal Layer (GRL). *(7 marks)* **(b)** Discuss the concept of *Negative Transfer*. List any four techniques to mitigate it and explain any two in detail. *(7 marks)* ##### Model Solution for (a): **Step 1 — DANN Architecture (3.5 marks):** Three components share the feature extractor $G_f$: 1. **Feature Extractor** $G_f(x; \theta_f)$ — shared backbone. 2. **Label Predictor** $G_y(\cdot; \theta_y)$ — predicts class labels. 3. **Domain Classifier** $G_d(\cdot; \theta_d)$ — predicts source vs target. A **Gradient Reversal Layer (GRL)** sits between $G_f$ and $G_d$, multiplying the gradient by $-\lambda$ during backpropagation. **Step 2 — Forward Pass Equations (1 mark):** $$f = G_f(x; \theta_f), \quad \hat{y} = G_y(f; \theta_y), \quad \hat{d} = G_d(f; \theta_d)$$ **Step 3 — Combined Loss (1.5 marks):** $$\mathcal{L}(\theta_f, \theta_y, \theta_d) = \frac{1}{n_S}\sum_{i=1}^{n_S}\mathcal{L}_y(\hat{y}_i, y_i) - \frac{\lambda}{n}\sum_{j=1}^{n}\mathcal{L}_d(\hat{d}_j, d_j)$$ The minus sign before domain loss is the result of GRL. **Step 4 — GRL Pseudocode (1 mark):** - Forward: $R_\lambda(x) = x$ - Backward: $\frac{d R_\lambda}{dx} = -\lambda I$ This pushes the feature extractor to produce **domain-invariant** representations. ##### Model Solution for (b): **Negative Transfer (2 marks):** Negative transfer occurs when transfer learning **degrades** the target model's performance compared to training from scratch. Quantified as: $$\Delta_{perf} = \mathrm{Acc}_{TL} - \mathrm{Acc}_{fromScratch} < 0$$ **Four Mitigation Techniques (1 mark each):** 1. **Source Domain Selection** — Choose source closest to target via proxy task or MMD. 2. **Instance Re-weighting** — TrAdaBoost assigns higher weights to source samples similar to target. 3. **Regularisation** — Add $\ell_2$ penalty on how far $\theta_T$ drifts from $\theta_S$. 4. **Multi-source Selection** — Combine multiple source models; choose ensemble weights via validation. **Detailed Explanation of Two (3 marks):** **(i) TrAdaBoost Re-weighting:** Source samples that the target model misclassifies receive **reduced weights** in the next round, while target-domain-similar samples gain weight. This iteratively filters out harmful source knowledge. **(ii) Source Domain Selection via MMD:** Compute pairwise $D_{KL}$ or MMD between candidate sources and the target; select the source with minimum divergence. This ensures the transferred knowledge is genuinely relevant. --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Ways Students Lose Marks on Transfer-Learning Questions:** > 1. **Confusing "domain" and "task"** — They are *separate* concepts. The domain is the *data distribution*; the task is the *learning objective*. Examiners deduct 1 mark per misuse. > 2. **Forgetting to differentiate fine-tuning from feature extraction** — Feature extraction freezes the backbone; fine-tuning unfreezes part of it. Writing only one and not the other loses 2 marks. > 3. **Omitting the Gradient Reversal Layer** in DANN derivations — the GRL is the *defining* innovation of DANN; omitting the negative gradient term is a **3-mark** penalty. > 4. **Not stating the KL/MMD divergence direction** — $D_{KL}(P_S \,\vert\vert\, P_T) \neq D_{KL}(P_T \,\vert\vert\, P_S)$. One-mark deduction for incorrect ordering. > 5. **Writing only definitions without CPS context** — KTU 2024 explicitly emphasises CPS application. Add at least one CPS example per answer. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Definition Recall:** TL = reuse of source-domain knowledge for a related target-domain task. *(1 mark question staple)* - **Necessary Condition:** Either $\mathcal{D}_S \neq \mathcal{D}_T$ or $\mathcal{T}_S \neq \mathcal{T}_T$. - **Three Categories (Pan & Yang, 2014):** Inductive, Transductive, Unsupervised TL. - **Four Strategies:** Instance-based, Feature-based, Parameter-based, Relational-based. - **Two Core Techniques:** Feature Extraction (freeze backbone) and Fine-Tuning (update backbone with smaller $\eta$). - **Domain Adaptation Metric:** MMD and KL divergence quantify the **domain gap**. - **DANN Innovation:** Gradient Reversal Layer creates domain-invariant features. - **Negative Transfer:** TL hurts performance when source–target divergence exceeds threshold $\tau$. - **CPS Relevance:** Scarce labelled sensor data makes TL **mandatory**, not optional, in real deployments. - **Key Pre-trained Models to Memorise:** VGG, ResNet, Inception, BERT, GPT, YOLO, MobileNet. - **Common Pitfall:** Never confuse multi-task learning (joint training) with transfer learning (sequential training). - **Mathematical Identity:** $\mathcal{L}_{DANN} = \mathcal{L}_{task} - \lambda \mathcal{L}_{domain}$ — the **minus** sign is critical and frequently forgotten.

topics.title: Explainable AI
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT635 - MACHINE LEARNING FOR CPS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Explainable AI

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Explainable AI (XAI)** is a set of processes, methods, and frameworks that allow human users to comprehend, interpret, and trust the outputs and decisions produced by machine learning and deep learning models. It bridges the gap between the **"black-box"** nature of high-performing models (like Deep Neural Networks, Gradient Boosting ensembles) and the human need for **transparency, accountability, and causality** in AI-driven decisions.

In the context of **Cyber-Physical Systems (CPS)**, Explainable AI is not merely a desirable feature — it is a **regulatory and safety-critical necessity**. When a Machine Learning model governs a physical actuator (e.g., an autonomous braking system, a medical infusion pump, a power-grid load balancer), stakeholders must be able to trace *why* the model produced a particular decision, to validate safety, debug failures, and comply with legal frameworks like the **EU AI Act (2024)**, **ISO/IEC 23894**, and **IEEE 7001** standards for transparency.

### Key Terminology Distinctions (High-Yield for KTU)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU frequently tests the subtle difference between the following paired terms. Memorize them.

| Term | Definition | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Interpretability** | The ability to understand the model's internal mechanics *by design* (inherent). | Linear Regression, Decision Trees |
| **Explainability** | The ability to *post-hoc* justify the model's outputs when the internal mechanics are opaque. | SHAP values for a Black-Box XGBoost model |
| **Transparency** | The model itself is fully visible — inputs, weights, architecture. | Logistic Regression with visible coefficients |
| **Algorithmic Auditability** | The capacity to trace the algorithm's decision pathway for legal/compliance review. | Logging feature importances in a fraud detector |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Intuition"

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The Specialist Doctor vs. The Black-Box Pill**

Imagine a patient with a chronic disease. There are two doctors:
1. **Doctor A (Black-Box Model)**: Prescribes a highly effective pill. The pill works **98%** of the time, and outperforms all other treatments. But when asked *"Why does this pill work? What does it do inside my body?"*, the doctor simply says, *"Trust the data, the trials confirm efficacy."*
2. **Doctor B (Explainable AI)**: Prescribes the same pill, but draws a complete diagram of the patient's affected organ, points to the molecular pathway the drug targets, lists the biomarkers it modulates, and provides a paper trail of clinical reasoning.

**In a Cyber-Physical System**, Doctor B is the *only acceptable option*. If an autonomous vehicle suddenly brakes on a highway, the engineer must know *why* — was it a misclassified shadow, a sensor fault, or a real obstacle? XAI provides the diagnostic chart.

## 1.3 Why Explainable AI is Critical in CPS

1. **Safety Assurance** — ISO 26262 (automotive) and IEC 61508 (industrial) demand traceability of automated decisions.
2. **Trust Calibration** — Human operators in CPS (e.g., pilots assisted by AI co-pilots) must know when to override the system.
3. **Regulatory Compliance** — GDPR's "Right to Explanation" and the EU AI Act mandate explainability for high-risk AI.
4. **Bias Detection** — XAI exposes hidden feature biases (e.g., a loan-approval CPS unfairly penalizing a specific postal code).
5. **Root Cause Analysis** — When a CPS fails, XAI helps engineers trace the failure to a specific feature, sensor, or edge case.
6. **Human-AI Collaboration** — Explanations allow domain experts to combine their tacit knowledge with ML predictions.

---

## 1.4 The Trade-off Triangle

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A foundational concept in KTU Module 4 is the **Accuracy-Interpretability-Complexity** triangle. You cannot maximize all three simultaneously.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A["High Accuracy
Deep Neural Networks
Gradient Boosting
Ensemble Models"] --> T((Trade-off
Triangle))
    B["High Interpretability
Linear Regression
Decision Trees
Rule-Based Systems"] --> T
    C["Low Complexity
Few Parameters
Linear Boundaries"] --> T
    T --> D["XAI seeks to
recover interpretability
WITHOUT sacrificing accuracy"]
    style T fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    style D fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
```

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Taxonomy of XAI Methods

The KTU 2024 syllabus categorizes Explainable AI methods along **three primary axes**:

### Axis 1: Intrinsic vs. Post-hoc

| Approach | When | Mechanism | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Intrinsic (Ante-hoc)** | Interpretability is built into the model from the start. | The model architecture is self-explanatory. | Linear models, Decision Trees, Attention Mechanisms |
| **Post-hoc** | The model is trained as a black-box; explanation is generated *after* training. | External methods interrogate the trained model. | LIME, SHAP, Grad-CAM, Counterfactuals |

### Axis 2: Model-Specific vs. Model-Agnostic

- **Model-Specific**: Designed for a particular model class (e.g., Grad-CAM works only on CNNs with spatial feature maps).
- **Model-Agnostic**: Can explain any ML model by treating it as a black-box (LIME, SHAP, Partial Dependence Plots).

### Axis 3: Local vs. Global Explanations

- **Local Explanation**: Explains a *single prediction* (e.g., "Why was this loan denied?").
- **Global Explanation**: Explains the *overall model behavior* (e.g., "What features matter most across all predictions?").

> [!NOTE]
> KTU often asks: *"Classify LIME as global/local, intrinsic/post-hoc, model-agnostic/specific."*
> **LIME = Local + Post-hoc + Model-Agnostic** — commit this to memory.

## 2.2 Major XAI Methods — Technical Deep Dive

### 2.2.1 LIME (Local Interpretable Model-agnostic Explanations)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **LIME** was proposed by Ribeiro et al. (2016) and is one of the most-tested XAI methods in KTU.

**Core Idea**: Explain a single prediction of *any* black-box classifier by learning a simple, interpretable surrogate model in the local neighborhood of that prediction.

**Algorithm Steps**:
1. Choose the instance $x$ to be explained.
2. Generate a perturbed dataset $\mathcal{Z} = \{z_1, z_2, ..., z_N\}$ around $x$ by sampling features (e.g., flipping binary features, sampling from a normal distribution for continuous features).
3. Get predictions $f(z_i)$ from the black-box model for each perturbed sample.
4. Assign weights $\pi_x(z_i)$ to each perturbed sample based on its **proximity to $x$** (typically using an exponential kernel):

$$\pi_x(z) = \exp\left(-\frac{D(x, z)^2}{\sigma^2}\right)$$

where $D(x,z)$ is a distance function (Euclidean for continuous, Hamming for categorical) and $\sigma$ is the kernel width.

5. Fit a weighted, interpretable surrogate model $g$ (typically a linear model or shallow decision tree) on $\mathcal{Z}$ that approximates $f$ locally:

$$\xi(x) = \arg\min_{g \in G} \; \mathcal{L}(f, g, \pi_x) + \Omega(g)$$

where:
- $\mathcal{L}(f, g, \pi_x)$ = faithfulness loss (how well $g$ mimics $f$ near $x$)
- $\Omega(g)$ = complexity penalty (keeps $g$ simple, e.g., number of non-zero weights)
- $G$ = the class of interpretable models

**Output**: A list of feature importance weights (the coefficients of the linear surrogate) explaining which features contributed positively/negatively to the prediction for $x$.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake**: LIME does NOT explain the global model — it explains *one prediction* in a *local region*. Do not claim "LIME explains the model."

### 2.2.2 SHAP (SHapley Additive exPlanations)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **SHAP** (Lundberg & Lee, 2017) unifies several XAI methods under a game-theoretic framework grounded in **Shapley values** from cooperative game theory.

**Core Idea**: Each feature is treated as a "player" in a coalition, and the prediction is the "payout." The Shapley value fairly distributes the payout among features.

**Shapley Value Formula**:

$$\phi_j = \sum_{S \subseteq F \setminus \{j\}} \frac{\vert S \vert! \; (\vert F \vert - \vert S \vert - 1)!}{\vert F \vert!} \left[ f_x(S \cup \{j\}) - f_x(S) \right]$$

where:
- $\phi_j$ = Shapley value for feature $j$ (its contribution to the prediction)
- $F$ = the set of all features
- $S$ = a subset of features (a coalition)
- $f_x(S)$ = the model's output when only features in $S$ are known
- The factorial term is the combinatorial weight (number of permutations where $S$ precedes $j$)

**Key Properties of Shapley Values** (the axioms KTU loves to test):

| Property | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Efficiency** | $\sum_j \phi_j = f(x) - \mathbb{E}[f(x)]$ — the sum of Shapley values equals the deviation from the average prediction. |
| **Symmetry** | If two features contribute identically, they receive equal Shapley values. |
| **Dummy** | A feature that doesn't change the prediction gets $\phi_j = 0$. |
| **Additivity** | For an ensemble, Shapley values of components can be summed. |

**SHAP Variants** (Algorithm-Specific Optimizations):

| Variant | Model Type | Speed |
|---|---|---|
| **KernelSHAP** | Model-agnostic (LIME + Shapley) | Slow |
| **TreeSHAP** | Tree-based (XGBoost, LightGBM, RF) | Fast (polynomial) |
| **DeepSHAP** | Deep Neural Networks (backprop-based) | Fast |
| **GradientSHAP** | Models with differentiable outputs | Fast |

### 2.2.3 Grad-CAM (Gradient-weighted Class Activation Mapping)

> [!NOTE]
> **Grad-CAM** is used exclusively for **Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs)** in image-based CPS applications (e.g., visual defect detection in manufacturing).

**Core Idea**: Use the **gradients of a target class** flowing into the final convolutional layer to produce a coarse localization map highlighting which spatial regions of the image are important for the prediction.

**Mathematical Formulation**:

$$L^{c}_{\text{Grad-CAM}} = \text{ReLU}\left(\sum_k \alpha_k^c \; A^k\right)$$

where:
- $A^k$ = the $k$-th feature map of the last convolutional layer
- $\alpha_k^c$ = the global-average-pooled gradient of class score $y^c$ w.r.t. $A^k$:

$$\alpha_k^c = \frac{1}{Z} \sum_i \sum_j \frac{\partial y^c}{\partial A_{ij}^k}$$

- $Z$ = the total number of pixels in the feature map
- ReLU ensures only *positive* influences are kept (negative gradients correspond to features that suppress the class)

**For Fine-Grained Detail**: **Grad-CAM++** modifies the weighting using higher-order gradients; **Guided Grad-CAM** element-wise multiplies Grad-CAM with guided backpropagation for pixel-level resolution.

### 2.2.4 Partial Dependence Plots (PDP)

**Core Idea**: Show the marginal effect of one or two features on the predicted outcome by averaging over all other features.

$$PDP_j(x_j) = \mathbb{E}_{X_{-j}}\left[\hat{f}(x_j, X_{-j})\right] = \int \hat{f}(x_j, X_{-j}) \; dP(X_{-j})$$

**Individual Conditional Expectation (ICE)** complements PDP by showing one line per instance rather than averaging.

### 2.2.5 Counterfactual Explanations

**Core Idea**: Answer the question *"What is the smallest change to the input that would flip the prediction?"*

For a classifier $f$ and an instance $x$ with $f(x) = \text{rejected}$:

$$x^{CF} = \arg\min_{x'} \; d(x, x') \quad \text{subject to} \quad f(x') = \text{approved}$$

where $d(\cdot, \cdot)$ is a distance metric (e.g., $L_1$ or $L_2$ norm). This is critical in CPS for actionable recourse (e.g., "Increase your credit score by 30 points to get approved").

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\pi_x(z) = \exp\left(-\frac{D(x,z)^2}{\sigma^2}\right)$ | LIME proximity weight | Local fidelity weighting |
| 2 | $\xi(x) = \arg\min_{g \in G} \mathcal{L}(f,g,\pi_x) + \Omega(g)$ | LIME explanation | Find interpretable surrogate |
| 3 | $\phi_j = \sum_{S} \frac{\vert S \vert! (\vert F \vert - \vert S \vert - 1)!}{\vert F \vert!} [f_x(S \cup \{j\}) - f_x(S)]$ | SHAP value | Fair feature attribution |
| 4 | $L^{c}_{\text{Grad-CAM}} = \text{ReLU}(\sum_k \alpha_k^c A^k)$ | Grad-CAM heatmap | CNN visual explanation |
| 5 | $\alpha_k^c = \frac{1}{Z} \sum_i \sum_j \frac{\partial y^c}{\partial A_{ij}^k}$ | Grad-CAM weights | Channel importance |
| 6 | $PDP_j(x_j) = \mathbb{E}_{X_{-j}}[\hat{f}(x_j, X_{-j})]$ | Partial Dependence | Global feature effect |
| 7 | $\sum_j \phi_j = f(x) - \mathbb{E}[f(x)]$ | SHAP efficiency axiom | Attribution check |
| 8 | $x^{CF} = \arg\min_{x'} d(x, x')$ s.t. $f(x') = y'$ | Counterfactual | Actionable recourse |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Table Tip**: In the exam, when you write the SHAP formula, never abbreviate. Write the full $\sum_{S \subseteq F \setminus \{j\}}$ set-builder notation. Examiners explicitly look for the **factorial coefficient**.

---

## 2.4 Engineering Utility in CPS — Real-World Mapping

| CPS Domain | XAI Method Used | Engineering Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| Autonomous Vehicles | Grad-CAM, Attention Visualization | Verify the car "sees" pedestrians, not shadows |
| Medical Imaging Devices | SHAP, Counterfactuals | Justify tumor classification to radiologists |
| Smart Grid Load Forecasting | PDP, SHAP | Identify which weather/seasonal variables drive peak demand |
| Industrial Robotics | LIME, Rule Extraction | Explain why a robot arm rejected a workpiece |
| Predictive Maintenance | SHAP, TreeSHAP | Rank sensor contributions to failure predictions |
| IoT Anomaly Detection | Attention-based explanations | Justify flagged network traffic to security analysts |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This section provides the **exhaustive mathematical derivations** that KTU Part B (14-mark) questions frequently demand. Every algebraic step is shown explicitly.

## 3.1 Derivation: Shapley Value Axioms → SHAP

### 3.1.1 The Cooperative Game Setup

Consider a prediction $f(x)$ as a payout from a game played by $F = \{1, 2, ..., M\}$ features. Each feature $j$ earns a payoff $\phi_j$. The total payout equals the value of the grand coalition:

$$\sum_{j=1}^{M} \phi_j = f(x) - \mathbb{E}[f(x)]$$

This is the **Efficiency Axiom**.

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Shapley Derivation

**Step 1** — Define the marginal contribution of feature $j$ to coalition $S$:

$$\Delta_j(S) = f_x(S \cup \{j\}) - f_x(S)$$

where $f_x(S)$ denotes the model's expected output when only features in $S$ are revealed.

**Step 2** — In a random permutation of the $M$ features, feature $j$ can enter the coalition at any position. The number of permutations where the set of features arriving *before* $j$ is exactly $S$ is:

$$N_{\text{perms}}(S, j) = \vert S \vert! \; (M - \vert S \vert - 1)!$$

because we permute the $\vert S \vert$ features already in $S$ (in $\vert S \vert!$ ways) and the $M - \vert S \vert - 1$ features arriving after $j$ (in $(M - \vert S \vert - 1)!$ ways). The position of $j$ is fixed (the $(\vert S \vert + 1)$-th).

**Step 3** — The total number of permutations of $M$ features is $M!$. The Shapley value is the **weighted average** of $\Delta_j(S)$ over all coalitions, where the weight is the probability of encountering $S$ in a random permutation:

$$w(S) = \frac{\vert S \vert! \; (M - \vert S \vert - 1)!}{M!}$$

**Step 4** — Summing over all subsets $S \subseteq F \setminus \{j\}$:

$$\phi_j = \sum_{S \subseteq F \setminus \{j\}} w(S) \cdot \Delta_j(S) = \sum_{S \subseteq F \setminus \{j\}} \frac{\vert S \vert! \; (M - \vert S \vert - 1)!}{M!} \left[ f_x(S \cup \{j\}) - f_x(S) \right]$$

This is the **canonical Shapley value formula** — the one KTU examiners expect on a derivation question.

### 3.1.3 Verification of the Efficiency Axiom

Starting from the Shapley formula, we can prove $\sum_{j=1}^{M} \phi_j = f_x(F) - f_x(\emptyset)$:

$$\sum_{j=1}^{M} \phi_j = \sum_{j=1}^{M} \sum_{S \subseteq F \setminus \{j\}} w(S) \left[ f_x(S \cup \{j\}) - f_x(S) \right]$$

By a telescoping argument (each $f_x(T)$ appears with $+$ sign when some $j \in T \setminus S$ is added, and with $-$ sign when $j$ is removed), all intermediate terms cancel, leaving only:

$$\sum_{j=1}^{M} \phi_j = f_x(F) - f_x(\emptyset) = f(x) - \mathbb{E}[f(x)]$$

This is the **key insight** that makes SHAP **additively faithful**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: LIME's Local Fidelity Objective

### 3.2.1 Problem Setup

Given black-box $f: \mathbb{R}^d \to \mathbb{R}$ and an instance $x$, we seek a simple model $g \in G$ (e.g., linear) that approximates $f$ near $x$.

### 3.2.2 Weighted Loss

The unfaithfulness of $g$ in approximating $f$ in the neighborhood of $x$ is:

$$\mathcal{L}(f, g, \pi_x) = \sum_{z \in \mathcal{Z}} \pi_x(z) \left[ f(z) - g(z') \right]^2$$

where $z' = h(z)$ maps the perturbed sample into the interpretable feature space (e.g., binary indicators for "is word present?" in text, or super-pixel-presence indicators in images).

### 3.2.3 Regularization for Simplicity

We add a complexity penalty $\Omega(g)$ to ensure $g$ remains interpretable (e.g., for a linear model, $\Omega(g) = \lambda \cdot \|w\|_0$ where $\|w\|_0$ is the number of non-zero weights).

### 3.2.4 Final Objective

$$\xi(x) = \arg\min_{g \in G} \; \underbrace{\sum_{z \in \mathcal{Z}} \pi_x(z) [f(z) - g(z')]^2}_{\text{local fidelity}} + \underbrace{\Omega(g)}_{\text{simplicity}}$$

### 3.2.5 Closed-Form for Linear $g$

If $g(z') = w^T z'$, the optimal $w$ has a **closed-form ridge regression solution**:

$$w^* = (Z'^T W Z' + \lambda I)^{-1} Z'^T W \mathbf{f}$$

where:
- $Z'$ is the $N \times d$ matrix of interpretable features
- $W = \text{diag}(\pi_x(z_1), ..., \pi_x(z_N))$
- $\mathbf{f}$ is the vector of black-box predictions
- $\lambda$ is the ridge regularization hyperparameter

This is **computationally cheap** and is the form KTU expects you to mention.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Criterion | LIME | SHAP | Grad-CAM | PDP | Counterfactual |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Scope** | Local | Local (+ Global via aggregation) | Local (image) | Global | Local |
| **Model Dependency** | Agnostic | Agnostic (KernelSHAP) / Specific (TreeSHAP) | Specific (CNN) | Agnostic | Agnostic |
| **Theoretical Guarantee** | Local fidelity | Shapley axioms (efficiency, symmetry, dummy) | Sensitivity to class score | Marginal effect | Distance minimization |
| **Computational Cost** | $O(N \cdot d)$ | $O(2^M)$ exact, faster approximations | $O(1)$ per image (one backward pass) | $O(N \cdot M)$ | Optimization-dependent |
| **Interpretability Form** | Feature weights | Shapley values | Heatmap | Curve | Modified instance |
| **Faithfulness to Model** | Approximate | Mathematically exact (under faithfulness axiom) | Approximate (gradient-based) | Approximate (assumes independence) | Exact (by construction) |

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — SHAP on a 3-Feature Model

> [!NOTE]
> KTU Part B problems often include numerical examples. Walk through this — it is the most common type.

**Problem**: A CPS intrusion detector uses 3 features: $\{X_1, X_2, X_3\}$. The model outputs $f(x) = 0.8$ for a particular instance, with $\mathbb{E}[f(x)] = 0.4$. The model values for all subsets are:

| Subset $S$ | $f_x(S)$ | Subset $S$ | $f_x(S)$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| $\emptyset$ | 0.40 | $\{1\}$ | 0.50 |
| $\{2\}$ | 0.55 | $\{3\}$ | 0.60 |
| $\{1,2\}$ | 0.70 | $\{1,3\}$ | 0.75 |
| $\{2,3\}$ | 0.78 | $\{1,2,3\}$ | 0.80 |

**Step 1**: Compute $\phi_1$ (Shapley for feature 1). The relevant subsets $S \subseteq F \setminus \{1\}$ are $\emptyset, \{2\}, \{3\}, \{2,3\}$.

With $M = 3$:

$$\phi_1 = \frac{0! \cdot 2!}{3!}[f(\{1\}) - f(\emptyset)] + \frac{1! \cdot 1!}{3!}[f(\{1,2\}) - f(\{2\})] + \frac{1! \cdot 1!}{3!}[f(\{1,3\}) - f(\{3\})] + \frac{2! \cdot 0!}{3!}[f(\{1,2,3\}) - f(\{2,3\})]$$

$$\phi_1 = \frac{2}{6}(0.50 - 0.40) + \frac{1}{6}(0.70 - 0.55) + \frac{1}{6}(0.75 - 0.60) + \frac{2}{6}(0.80 - 0.78)$$

$$\phi_1 = \frac{1}{3}(0.10) + \frac{1}{6}(0.15) + \frac{1}{6}(0.15) + \frac{1}{3}(0.02)$$

$$\phi_1 = 0.0333 + 0.0250 + 0.0250 + 0.0067 = 0.0900$$

**Step 2** (by symmetry, KTU expects you to state the others):

$$\phi_2 = \frac{2}{6}(0.55-0.40) + \frac{1}{6}(0.70-0.50) + \frac{1}{6}(0.78-0.60) + \frac{2}{6}(0.80-0.75) = 0.05 + 0.0333 + 0.0300 + 0.0167 = 0.1300$$

$$\phi_3 = \frac{2}{6}(0.60-0.40) + \frac{1}{6}(0.75-0.50) + \frac{1}{6}(0.78-0.55) + \frac{2}{6}(0.80-0.70) = 0.0667 + 0.0417 + 0.0383 + 0.0333 = 0.1800$$

**Step 3** — Verify Efficiency Axiom:

$$\phi_1 + \phi_2 + \phi_3 = 0.09 + 0.13 + 0.18 = 0.40 = f(x) - \mathbb{E}[f(x)] = 0.80 - 0.40 \quad \checkmark$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Tip**: Always verify the Efficiency axiom in numerical problems. Examiners award bonus marks for this self-check.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Architecture: XAI Pipeline in a CPS

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph A["DATA LAYER (CPS Sensors)"]
        S1["Temperature
Sensor"]
        S2["Pressure
Sensor"]
        S3["Vibration
Sensor"]
        S4["Vision
Camera"]
    end

    subgraph B["ML MODEL LAYER (Black-Box)"]
        M1["Deep Neural
Network"]
        M2["XGBoost
Ensemble"]
        M3["CNN
Image Model"]
    end

    subgraph C["XAI EXPLANATION LAYER"]
        X1["SHAP
TreeSHAP"]
        X2["LIME
Local Surrogate"]
        X3["Grad-CAM
Heatmap"]
        X4["Counterfactual
Generator"]
    end

    subgraph D["HUMAN / CPS ACTUATOR INTERFACE"]
        H1["Engineer
Dashboard"]
        H2["Safety
Auditor"]
        H3["Actuator
Override Logic"]
    end

    S1 --> M1
    S2 --> M2
    S3 --> M2
    S4 --> M3
    M1 --> X1
    M2 --> X1
    M2 --> X2
    M3 --> X3
    M1 --> X4
    X1 --> H1
    X2 --> H1
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Q: Differentiate between model interpretability and model explainability. Give one example of each in the context of Cyber-Physical Systems.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks)**:

| Aspect | Interpretability | Explainability |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | The inherent property of a model that allows a human to understand its decision-making process by directly inspecting its structure. | The ability to *post-hoc* justify a model's predictions using external methods when the model is opaque. |
| **When Applied** | At design time (model is interpretable by construction). | After training (a black-box model is explained). |
| **CPS Example** | A Decision Tree predicting pump failure — its branches directly map sensor thresholds to outcomes. | A Deep Neural Network predicting gas-leak severity explained via SHAP values showing that the "pressure spike" feature contributed +0.42 to the alarm. |

*(1 mark — clear definition of each; 1 mark — correct distinction; 1 mark — valid CPS example)*

---

### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Q: What is the difference between LIME and SHAP? Justify why SHAP is theoretically more grounded.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks)**:

- **LIME** approximates the black-box model *locally* with a simple surrogate using proximity-weighted regression. It has **no theoretical guarantee** that feature attributions satisfy fairness axioms.
- **SHAP** is grounded in **Shapley values from cooperative game theory**, which uniquely satisfy the four axioms of **Efficiency, Symmetry, Dummy, and Additivity**. This makes SHAP's attributions **mathematically fair** and **additively consistent** (the sum equals the prediction deviation from the mean).
- Hence, SHAP is considered more theoretically rigorous; LIME is faster and simpler but can produce inconsistent attributions across runs.

*(1 mark — LIME description; 1 mark — SHAP description with axioms; 1 mark — justification of SHAP's rigor)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: Explain the LIME algorithm in detail. How does it generate local explanations for a black-box classifier? Discuss the role of the proximity kernel and the interpretability-fidelity trade-off.**

#### Part (a) — Algorithm Description (7 Marks)

**Model Answer**:

LIME (Local Interpretable Model-agnostic Explanations) explains individual predictions of any black-box classifier by learning a simple, interpretable surrogate model in the local neighborhood of the prediction.

**Step-by-step procedure**:

1. **Input Selection**: Choose the instance $x \in \mathbb{R}^d$ to be explained and the black-box model $f$.

2. **Interpretable Representation**: Convert $x$ into a binary vector $x' \in \{0,1\}^{d'}$ indicating the presence/absence of interpretable features (e.g., word presence for text, super-pixels for images, bin indicators for tabular data).

3. **Perturbation Generation**: Sample $N$ perturbed instances $z'_i \in \{0,1\}^{d'}$ by randomly toggling bits of $x'$.

4. **Map Back to Original Space**: Convert each $z'_i$ back to $z_i \in \mathbb{R}^d$ in the original feature space (e.g., if a binary indicator is "on," use the original value; if "off," use the dataset mean).

5. **Black-Box Prediction**: Compute $f(z_i)$ for each perturbed instance.

6. **Proximity Weighting**: Assign each sample a weight $\pi_x(z_i)$ using an exponential kernel:

$$\pi_x(z_i) = \exp\left(-\frac{D(x, z_i)^2}{\sigma^2}\right)$$

7. **Surrogate Fitting**: Train an interpretable model $g$ (linear regression or shallow tree) on $\{(z'_i, f(z_i), \pi_x(z_i))\}$ by minimizing:

$$\xi(x) = \arg\min_{g \in G} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \pi_x(z_i) [f(z_i) - g(z'_i)]^2 + \Omega(g)$$

8. **Explanation Output**: The non-zero coefficients of $g$ indicate the contribution of each interpretable feature to the prediction of $x$.

*(Breakdown: 1 mark per major step × 7 marks)*

#### Part (b) — Proximity Kernel and Fidelity-Interpretability Trade-off (7 Marks)

**Model Answer**:

**Role of the Proximity Kernel**:
The proximity kernel $\pi_x(z)$ ensures that the surrogate $g$ approximates $f$ **faithfully near $x$** while down-weighting samples far from $x$. The kernel width $\sigma$ controls the *locality*:
- **Small $\sigma$** → Only very close perturbations influence $g$ → High local fidelity but high variance (overfitting).
- **Large $\sigma$** → Distant perturbations also contribute → $g$ becomes more like a global model → Loses local relevance.

For a new point $z$, the Gaussian kernel decays as a function of squared distance $D(x, z)^2$, ensuring samples within distance $\sigma$ contribute meaningfully and samples beyond have exponentially small weight.

**The Interpretability-Fidelity Trade-off**:
The objective $\mathcal{L}(f, g, \pi_x) + \Omega(g)$ embodies a **fundamental tension**:
- **Fidelity term** $\mathcal{L}$: Encourages $g$ to mimic $f$ exactly near $x$. A complex $g$ (e.g., deep tree) has high fidelity but is uninterpretable.
- **Complexity penalty** $\Omega(g)$: Penalizes non-zero weights or tree depth, forcing $g$ to remain simple — but at the cost of fidelity.

**In CPS context**: A doctor using an ML-assisted diagnosis tool must trust the explanation. A faithful-but-incomprehensible model is as useless as an interpretable-but-inaccurate one. LIME navigates this trade-off by allowing the user to specify the *interpretability budget* (e.g., maximum 5 non-zero features in a linear model), which limits $\Omega(g)$ and ensures the explanation is digestible.

**Limitations of LIME**:
1. **Instability**: Different runs produce different explanations due to random perturbation sampling.
2. **Local-Only**: Cannot be used to derive global feature importance reliably.
3. **Definition of Locality**: The choice of distance function $D$ is non-trivial for mixed data types.
4. **No Theoretical Guarantees**: Unlike SHAP, LIME does not satisfy Shapley axioms.

*(Breakdown: 2 marks — kernel role; 2 marks — trade-off formula/explanation; 2 marks — CPS application; 1 mark — limitations)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: Explain the SHAP framework for explainable AI. Derive the Shapley value formula. Apply it to compute feature attributions for a 3-feature model with the given coalition values.**

#### Part (a) — SHAP Framework and Derivation (7 Marks)

**Model Answer**:

**SHAP Framework**:
SHAP (SHapley Additive exPlanations) is a unified XAI framework that attributes a model's prediction to individual features using Shapley values from cooperative game theory. It is **model-agnostic** in its general form (KernelSHAP) and has efficient variants for specific models (TreeSHAP, DeepSHAP).

The key insight is to view the prediction $f(x)$ as a payout from a cooperative game played by $M$ features, and to compute a *fair* distribution of this payout using the Shapley value:

$$\phi_j = \sum_{S \subseteq F \setminus \{j\}} \frac{\vert S \vert! \; (M - \vert S \vert - 1)!}{M!} \left[ f_x(S \cup \{j\}) - f_x(S) \right]$$

**Derivation**:

Start with the marginal contribution of feature $j$ to a coalition $S$:

$$\Delta_j(S) = f_x(S \cup \{j\}) - f_x(S)$$

In a random permutation of the $M$ features, the probability that the set of features arriving *before* $j$ is exactly $S$ is:

$$P(S) = \frac{\vert S \vert! \; (M - \vert S \vert - 1)!}{M!}$$

This is because we permute $\vert S \vert$ features (in $\vert S \vert!$ ways) and $M - \vert S \vert - 1$ features after $j$ (in $(M - \vert S \vert - 1)!$ ways), out of $M!$ total permutations.

The Shapley value is the expected marginal contribution over all subsets:

$$\phi_j = \mathbb{E}_{S}[\Delta_j(S)] = \sum_{S \subseteq F \setminus \{j\}} P(S) \cdot \Delta_j(S)$$

Substituting:

$$\phi_j = \sum_{S \subseteq F \setminus \{j\}} \frac{\vert S \vert! \; (M - \vert S \vert - 1)!}{M!} \left[ f_x(S \cup \{j\}) - f_x(S) \right] \qquad \blacksquare$$

**Shapley Axioms** (the four properties guaranteeing "fair" attribution):
1. **Efficiency**: $\sum_j \phi_j = f(x) - \mathbb{E}[f(x)]$
2. **Symmetry**: Equal contributors receive equal attributions.
3. **Dummy**: Irrelevant features get $\phi_j = 0$.
4. **Additivity**: Attributions of ensemble components sum to the total.

*(Breakdown: 2 marks — SHAP overview; 3 marks — derivation steps; 2 marks — axioms)*

#### Part (b) — Numerical Application (7 Marks)

**Model Answer**:

Consider a CPS anomaly detection model with $M = 3$ features. The model output for a given instance is $f(x) = 0.85$, with the mean prediction $\mathbb{E}[f(x)] = 0.45$. Coalition values:

| Subset $S$ | $f_x(S)$ | Subset $S$ | $f_x(S)$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| $\emptyset$ | 0.45 | $\{1\}$ | 0.58 |
| $\{2\}$ | 0.60 | $\{3\}$ | 0.68 |
| $\{1,2\}$ | 0.78 | $\{1,3\}$ | 0.82 |
| $\{2,3\}$ | 0.85 | $\{1,2,3\}$ | 0.85 |

**Step 1 — Compute $\phi_1$**:
Subsets of $F \setminus \{1\}$: $\emptyset, \{2\}, \{3\}, \{2,3\}$.

$$\phi_1 = \frac{0! \cdot 2!}{3!}(0.58-0.45) + \frac{1! \cdot 1!}{3!}(0.78-0.60) + \frac{1! \cdot 1!}{3!}(0.82-0.68) + \frac{2! \cdot 0!}{3!}(0.85-0.85)$$

$$\phi_1 = \frac{2}{6}(0.13) + \frac{1}{6}(0.18) + \frac{1}{6}(0.14) + \frac{2}{6}(0.00)$$

$$\phi_1 = 0.0433 + 0.0300 + 0.0233 + 0.0000 = 0.0967$$

**Step 2 — Compute $\phi_2$** (subsets of $F \setminus \{2\}$: $\emptyset, \{1\}, \{3\}, \{1,3\}$):

$$\phi_2 = \frac{2}{6}(0.60-0.45) + \frac{1}{6}(0.78-0.58) + \frac{1}{6}(0.85-0.68) + \frac{2}{6}(0.85-0.82)$$

$$\phi_2 = 0.0500 + 0.0333 + 0.0283 + 0.0100 = 0.1217$$

**Step 3 — Compute $\phi_3$** (subsets of $F \setminus \{3\}$: $\emptyset, \{1\}, \{2\}, \{1,2\}$):

$$\phi_3 = \frac{2}{6}(0.68-0.45) + \frac{1}{6}(0.82-0.58) + \frac{1}{6}(0.85-0.60) + \frac{2}{6}(0.85-0.78)$$

$$\phi_3 = 0.0767 + 0.0400 + 0.0417 + 0.0233 = 0.1817$$

**Step 4 — Verify Efficiency Axiom**:

$$\phi_1 + \phi_2 + \phi_3 = 0.0967 + 0.1217 + 0.1817 = 0.4001 \approx 0.40$$

$$f(x) - \mathbb{E}[f(x)] = 0.85 - 0.45 = 0.40 \quad \checkmark$$

**Interpretation**: Feature 3 contributes the most (0.1817), Feature 2 is second (0.1217), and Feature 1 contributes least (0.0967). In a CPS context, this means the third sensor (e.g., vibration) is the dominant indicator of the anomaly.

*(Breakdown: 2 marks — $\phi_1$ computation; 2 marks — $\phi_2$ and $\phi_3$; 2 marks — verification; 1 mark — interpretation)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Forgetting the factorial coefficient** in the Shapley formula (the most common deduction — 1 mark lost).
> 2. **Confusing LIME's scope** — claiming it explains the model globally instead of locally.
> 3. **Writing SHAP axioms incorrectly** — "Symmetry" is not the same as "Add up to total" (that is Efficiency). Memorize all four by name.
> 4. **In numerical problems, omitting the Efficiency verification** — examiners explicitly reward this self-check.
> 5. **Forgetting ReLU in Grad-CAM** — the heatmap must be the ReLU of the weighted sum, not the raw sum.
> 6. **Confusing PDP with ICE** — PDP averages over instances; ICE shows one line per instance.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for Explainable AI (Module 4)**

### Core Definitions
- ✅ **Explainable AI (XAI)** = Methods to interpret and justify ML model decisions, especially in CPS.
- ✅ **Interpretability** = Inherent model transparency (linear, trees).
- ✅ **Explainability** = Post-hoc justification for black-boxes (SHAP, LIME).

### Method Taxonomy
- ✅ **LIME** = **L**ocal, **P**ost-hoc, **M**odel-**A**gnostic — surrogate-based.
- ✅ **SHAP** = Local + Global, based on **Shapley values** with 4 axioms.
- ✅ **Grad-CAM** = Local, CNN-specific, gradient-based heatmap.
- ✅ **PDP** = Global, marginal effect via averaging.
- ✅ **Counterfactuals** = Local, minimal-change instance for recourse.

### Key Formulas (Must Memorize)
- ✅ LIME kernel: $\pi_x(z) = \exp(-D(x,z)^2 / \sigma^2)$
- ✅ LIME objective: $\xi(x) = \arg\min_g \mathcal{L}(f, g, \pi_x) + \Omega(g)$
- ✅ Shapley value: $\phi_j = \sum_S \frac{\vert S \vert! (M - \vert S \vert - 1)!}{M!} [f_x(S \cup \{j\}) - f_x(S)]$
- ✅ Grad-CAM: $L^c = \text{ReLU}(\sum_k \alpha_k^c A^k)$
- ✅ PDP: $PDP_j(x_j) = \mathbb{E}_{X_{-j}}[\hat{f}(x_j, X_{-j})]$

### SHAP Axioms (4 Total — Memorize by Name)
- ✅ **Efficiency** — Sum equals prediction deviation from mean.
- ✅ **Symmetry** — Equal contributors get equal attribution.
- ✅ **Dummy** — Irrelevant features get zero attribution.
- ✅ **Additivity** — Ensemble attributions sum to total.

### CPS-Specific Importance
- ✅ Required for **safety-critical** systems (autonomous vehicles, medical devices).
- ✅ Mandated by **EU AI Act, ISO 26262, GDPR Right to Explanation**.
- ✅ Enables **root-cause analysis** when a CPS fails.
- ✅ Provides **trust calibration** for human-in-the-loop CPS operators.

### Common Pitfalls to Avoid
- ❌ Don't claim LIME gives global explanations.
- ❌ Don't forget the ReLU in Grad-CAM.
- ❌ Don't write SHAP axioms with wrong names.
- ❌ Don't skip the Efficiency verification in numerical problems.
- ❌ Don't confuse PDP averaging with ICE per-instance lines.

---
