topics.title: Future Trends and Challenges: Edge Computing and IoT Integration
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT635 - MACHINE LEARNING FOR CPS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Future Trends and Challenges: Edge Computing and IoT Integration

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Edge Computing** is a distributed computing paradigm that processes data *near the network edge* — i.e., close to the IoT sensors and actuators of a Cyber-Physical System (CPS) — rather than transmitting the entire data stream to a distant cloud data centre. In a CPS context, it forms the **intermediate intelligence layer** between the physical process (sensors/actuators) and the cloud, enabling real-time analytics, low-latency control loops, and bandwidth-efficient ML inference.

**IoT Integration** in CPS refers to the seamless fusion of heterogeneous IoT devices (sensors, actuators, embedded controllers, gateways) with the computational fabric (edge + fog + cloud) such that physical dynamics, communication, and control algorithms operate as a unified, feedback-driven system.

The canonical reference architecture follows the **three-tier IoT-Edge-Cloud continuum**:

| Tier | Layer | Typical Hardware | Latency Class |
|:----:|:------|:-----------------|:-------------:|
| Tier-1 | Device / Sensor / Actuator | MCU, MEMS sensors, motors | **< 1 ms** |
| Tier-2 | Edge / Gateway | Raspberry Pi, Jetson Nano, FPGA | **1 – 10 ms** |
| Tier-3 | Cloud | GPU/TPU clusters, data centres | **50 – 500 ms** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Engineering Metrics (Board Favourites):**
> - **Latency** — end-to-end delay between sensor sample and control actuation (measured in **ms**).
> - **Bandwidth** — uplink/downlink data rate between tiers (measured in **Mbps / Gbps**).
> - **Energy per Inference (EPI)** — joules consumed per ML forward pass on edge hardware.
> - **Model Footprint** — on-device memory required (measured in **MB** or **KB** for TinyML).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Roadside Clinic vs. City Hospital" Model

Imagine a network of villages spread across a district:
- The **village clinic (Edge Node)** treats common ailments (filtering, threshold checks, simple ML inference) immediately at the doorstep.
- The **district hospital (Fog / Gateway)** handles moderately complex cases (multi-sensor fusion, lightweight CNN inference).
- The **specialty city hospital (Cloud)** is consulted only for rare, compute-intensive diagnoses (training large foundation models, long-horizon analytics).

In this analogy, *not every patient needs to travel to the city hospital*. Edge computing applies the same principle to data: **process locally what can be processed locally, and escalate to the cloud only when necessary**. This dramatically reduces *ambulance travel time* (latency), *fuel consumption* (bandwidth), and *patient risk* (safety-critical failures in CPS).

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for Exams — "LAMP"**: **L**atency-critical, **A**utonomous, **M**emory-constrained, **P**ower-limited — the four signatures of an edge workload in CPS.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Operational Logic of Edge–IoT Integration in CPS

A CPS control loop with edge intelligence can be decomposed into the following logical stages:

1. **Physical Sensing Stage** — IoT transducers (temperature, vibration, current, vision) sample the physical process at a rate $f_s$.
2. **Pre-processing Stage** — Noise filtering, outlier rejection, and feature extraction happen *on-device* (Tier-1) to reduce raw data volume.
3. **Edge Inference Stage** — A compressed ML model (e.g., INT8 quantised CNN, decision tree, TinyML network) classifies, regresses, or detects anomalies at the gateway (Tier-2).
4. **Decision & Actuation Stage** — The edge controller issues commands to actuators (motors, valves, brakes) within the real-time deadline $T_{deadline}$.
5. **Cloud Synchronisation Stage** — Telemetry summaries, gradients, and logs are periodically uploaded for long-term learning and model retraining.

The **"Why"** behind this pipeline:
- **Latency collapse**: Wireless round-trip to a distant cloud can exceed **100 ms**, unacceptable for safety-critical CPS (e.g., autonomous braking, robotic surgery).
- **Bandwidth economics**: Streaming 4K camera frames at 30 FPS from 1,000 vehicles would saturate any cellular backhaul; edge filtering reduces uplink traffic by orders of magnitude.
- **Privacy by design**: Raw video/health data never leaves the device, satisfying GDPR and healthcare-data regulations.
- **Resilience**: Edge nodes continue functioning during cloud outages (offline-first architecture).

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following equations constitute the **must-know mathematical core** for any KTU examination question on this topic.

$$
T_{total} \;=\; T_{compute} \;+\; T_{transmit} \;+\; T_{propagate} \;+\; T_{queue}
$$

$$
T_{transmit} \;=\; \frac{D}{R_{link}} \quad \text{[s]} \qquad \text{(Shannon-limited link budget)}
$$

$$
R_{link} \;=\; B \cdot \log_2\!\left(1 + \mathrm{SNR}\right) \quad \text{[bps]} \qquad \text{(Shannon–Hartley capacity)}
$$

$$
E_{compute} \;=\; \alpha \, C \, f_{cpu}^{2} \, V_{dd}^{2} \quad \text{[J]} \qquad \text{(CMOS dynamic-energy model)}
$$

$$
E_{transmit} \;=\; P_{tx} \cdot \frac{D}{R_{link}} \quad \text{[J]}
$$

$$
E_{total} \;=\; \alpha \, C \, f_{cpu}^{2} \, V_{dd}^{2} \;+\; P_{tx} \cdot \frac{D}{R_{link}}
$$

$$
\mathbb{1}_{offload} \;=\; 
\begin{cases}
1, & \text{if } T_{edge} < T_{local} \;\land\; E_{edge} < E_{local} \\
0, & \text{otherwise}
\end{cases}
$$

$$
w_{t+1}^{(global)} \;=\; \sum_{k=1}^{K} \frac{n_k}{N} \, w_{t+1}^{(k)} \quad \text{(Federated Averaging — FedAvg)}
$$

$$
\Delta w \;=\; w_{t+1}^{(global)} - w_{t}^{(global)} \quad \text{(Global update delta)}
$$

| # | Formula (LaTeX) | Engineering Meaning | Variable Glossary |
|:-:|:----------------|:--------------------|:------------------|
| F1 | $T_{total} = T_{comp} + T_{tx} + T_{prop} + T_{queue}$ | End-to-end CPS latency budget | $T$ in **s** or **ms** |
| F2 | $R_{link} = B \cdot \log_2(1 + \mathrm{SNR})$ | Shannon channel capacity | $B$ in **Hz**, SNR dimensionless |
| F3 | $E_{dyn} = \alpha C f_{cpu}^{2} V_{dd}^{2}$ | CMOS dynamic switching energy | $\alpha$ = activity factor |
| F4 | $E_{total} = \alpha C f^{2} V_{dd}^{2} + P_{tx} \cdot D / R_{link}$ | Total energy per inference/decision | Joules |
| F5 | $\mathbb{1}_{offload} = 1$ iff $T_{edge} < T_{local}$ | Binary offloading decision rule | Boolean |
| F6 | $w_{global} = \sum (n_k / N) \, w_k$ | FedAvg model aggregation | $N = \sum n_k$ |
| F7 | $L_{QoS} = w_1 T_{latency} + w_2 E_{energy} + w_3 A_{accuracy}$ | Weighted Quality-of-Service objective | $w_i \geq 0$, $\sum w_i = 1$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Symbol Quirk — Avoid the pipe character in your answer sheet tables.** When writing absolute-value expressions on the board, write $\vert x \vert$ (with `\vert` in LaTeX) rather than `|x|`, because a stray pipe symbol can break KTU's online template parser.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Autonomous Vehicles**: Edge GPUs (NVIDIA Jetson, Mobileye EyeQ) run perception stacks at **< 30 ms** latency; cloud is used only for HD-map updates.
- **Smart Manufacturing (Industry 4.0)**: Edge ML detects bearing faults in CNC spindles via vibration spectrograms; predictions actuate spindle-speed controllers within **< 5 ms**.
- **Healthcare Wearables**: ECG edge inference detects atrial fibrillation locally; only anomaly summaries reach the cardiologist's cloud dashboard, preserving patient privacy.
- **Smart Grid**: Edge PMUs (Phasor Measurement Units) execute ML-based fault classification within sub-cycle windows (**< 16.67 ms** for 60 Hz systems).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation I — Optimum Computation-Offloading Decision

**Problem Setting.** A CPS edge node has a local CPU of frequency $f_L$ (cycles/sec). An ML inference task requires $C$ compute cycles and produces an output payload of $D$ bytes. The wireless uplink to an edge server offers rate $R_u$ (bytes/sec), and the edge server executes at frequency $f_E$. Derive the condition under which offloading is energy-optimal.

**Step 1 — Local execution time.**
The CPU executes $C$ cycles at frequency $f_L$, so the wall-clock time is:

$$
T_{local} \;=\; \frac{C}{f_{L}}
$$

**Step 2 — Local execution energy.**
Using the CMOS dynamic-energy model with activity factor $\alpha$ and supply voltage $V_{dd}$:

$$
E_{local} \;=\; \alpha \, C \, f_{L}^{2} \, V_{dd}^{2}
$$

**Step 3 — Edge execution time.**
The offloading pipeline has three stages: uplink transmission, edge compute, and downlink return of the result payload $D$. We write:

$$
T_{edge} \;=\; T_{up} + T_{compute}^{(E)} + T_{down} \;=\; \frac{D}{R_{u}} + \frac{C}{f_{E}} + \frac{D_{out}}{R_{d}}
$$

For inference workloads where $D_{out} \ll D$ (e.g., classification label = 1 byte vs. image = 1 MB), we approximate $T_{down} \approx 0$, giving:

$$
T_{edge} \;\approx\; \frac{D}{R_{u}} + \frac{C}{f_{E}}
$$

**Step 4 — Edge execution energy.**
The device expends energy *only* on transmission; the edge server's energy is irrelevant to the device's battery:

$$
E_{edge} \;=\; P_{tx} \cdot \frac{D}{R_{u}}
$$

**Step 5 — Offloading decision.**
The device chooses to offload **iff both** latency and energy improve:

$$
\boxed{\;T_{edge} < T_{local} \quad \text{AND} \quad E_{edge} < E_{local}\;}
$$

Substituting the closed-form expressions:

$$
\frac{D}{R_{u}} + \frac{C}{f_{E}} \;<\; \frac{C}{f_{L}} \quad \text{AND} \quad P_{tx} \cdot \frac{D}{R_{u}} \;<\; \alpha \, C \, f_{L}^{2} \, V_{dd}^{2}
$$

**Step 6 — Solving the latency inequality for the bandwidth threshold.**
Rearranging the latency inequality:

$$
\frac{D}{R_{u}} \;<\; \frac{C}{f_{L}} - \frac{C}{f_{E}} \;=\; C \left( \frac{1}{f_{L}} - \frac{1}{f_{E}} \right)
$$

Since $f_E \gg f_L$ in practice, the right-hand side is positive. Inverting (and reversing the inequality because $R_u$ is in the denominator) yields:

$$
\boxed{\;R_{u} \;>\; \frac{D}{C} \cdot \frac{f_{L} \, f_{E}}{f_{E} - f_{L}}\;}
$$

> **Engineering Interpretation.** Offloading is *only* beneficial if the wireless link can sustain a minimum rate $R_u^*$ proportional to the data-to-compute ratio $D/C$. For tiny sensor packets ($D$ small) and compute-heavy ML models ($C$ large), this threshold is easily met; for raw video streams with lightweight inference, it is rarely met — favouring on-device processing.

---

### 3.2 Derivation II — Energy–Latency Pareto Trade-off for Federated Edge Learning

**Setting.** $K$ edge devices collaboratively train a global model via FedAvg. Each device $k$ holds $n_k$ samples; total $N = \sum_{k=1}^{K} n_k$. After $E$ local SGD epochs, the local model update is:

$$
w_{t+1}^{(k)} \;=\; w_t - \eta \, \nabla L_k(w_t)
$$

**Step 1 — Local gradient computation cost.**
Each local epoch requires $n_k$ gradient evaluations. Energy per device:

$$
E_{k}^{(local)} \;=\; E_{comp}^{(k)} \cdot n_k \cdot E
$$

where $E_{comp}^{(k)}$ is energy per sample (dominated by $E_{dyn}$ of F3).

**Step 2 — Communication energy.**
Each round transmits the model delta $\Delta w$ of size $M$ bytes to the aggregator:

$$
E_{k}^{(comm)} \;=\; P_{tx}^{(k)} \cdot \frac{M}{R_{u}^{(k)}}
$$

**Step 3 — Total per-round energy.**
Summing across $K$ devices:

$$
E_{total}^{(round)} \;=\; \sum_{k=1}^{K} \left[\, E_{comp}^{(k)} \, n_k \, E \;+\; P_{tx}^{(k)} \, \frac{M}{R_{u}^{(k)}} \,\right]
$$

**Step 4 — Server-side aggregation.**
The parameter server computes a *weighted average*:

$$
w_{t+1}^{(global)} \;=\; \sum_{k=1}^{K} \frac{n_k}{N} \, w_{t+1}^{(k)}
$$

**Step 5 — Pareto frontier.**
The trade-off is captured by the weighted scalarisation:

$$
\mathcal{L}_{Pareto} \;=\; \lambda \, \frac{E_{total}^{(round)}}{E_{max}} \;+\; (1 - \lambda) \, \frac{T_{round}}{T_{deadline}}
$$

with $\lambda \in [0, 1]$ chosen by the CPS designer.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in KTU answers:** Examiners frequently award full marks only when the student explicitly writes the *aggregation weighted by sample count*, not a plain arithmetic mean. This single-line correctness is a common differentiator.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Cloud vs. Fog vs. Edge vs. TinyML

| Dimension | Cloud Computing | Fog Computing | Edge Computing | TinyML |
|:----------|:---------------:|:-------------:|:--------------:|:------:|
| **Proximity to sensor** | Very far (WAN) | Regional (LAN/MAN) | On-site (PAN) | On-chip |
| **Latency** | 50 – 500 ms | 10 – 50 ms | 1 – 10 ms | **< 1 ms** |
| **Compute Power** | Virtually unlimited | High | Moderate | **< 1 mW** |
| **Model Size** | Foundation models (GB) | Compressed models (MB) | Quantised models (100s KB) | **< 100 KB** |
| **Connectivity Need** | Mandatory | Intermittent OK | Mostly offline-capable | **None / Battery** |
| **Privacy Posture** | Low (data egress) | Medium | High | **Very High** |
| **Typical CPS Use-Case** | Fleet analytics | Plant-wide SCADA | PLC + ML co-processor | Vibration sensor w/ anomaly detector |
| **Failure Blast Radius** | Localised | Regional | Site-wide | **Single device** |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Compilation Safeguards Applied

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All Mermaid node identifiers below are purely **alphanumeric** (e.g., `sensorA`, `edgeNodeB`), and every label is rendered in **plain uppercase alphanumeric text** — no Markdown bold (`**`) or HTML inside double-quoted labels. This guarantees clean compilation in KTU's online rendering pipeline.

### 4.2 Edge-CPS Three-Tier Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Tier1["TIER 1: PHYSICAL AND SENSING LAYER"]
        sensorA["SENSOR UNIT A TEMPERATURE AND VIBRATION"]
        sensorB["SENSOR UNIT B CAMERA AND LIDAR"]
        actuatorA["ACTUATOR MOTOR VALVE BRAKE"]
    end

    subgraph Tier2["TIER 2: EDGE INTELLIGENCE LAYER"]
        preprocess["PREPROCESSING FILTER NORMALISE FE"]
        edgeModel["EDGE ML MODEL QUANTISED CNN OR TINYML"]
        edgeCtrl["EDGE CONTROLLER REAL TIME DECISION"]
        aggregator["FEDAVG AGGREGATOR LOCAL WEIGHTS"]
    end

    subgraph Tier3["TIER 3: CLOUD INTELLIGENCE LAYER"]
        cloudTrain["CLOUD TRAINING GPU TPU CLUSTER"]
        cloudStore["CLOUD STORAGE LONG TERM LOGS"]
        dashBoard["VISUALISATION DASHBOARD OPS TEAM"]
    end

    sensorA --> preprocess
    sensorB --> preprocess
    preprocess --> edgeModel
    edgeModel --> edgeCtrl
    edgeCtrl --> actuatorA
    edgeCtrl --> aggregator
    aggregator -->|UPLINK MODEL DELTA| cloudTrain
    cloudTrain -->|DOWNLOAD UPDATED MODEL| aggregator
    cloudTrain --> cloudStore
    cloudStore --> dashBoard

    classDef tier1Style fill:#FFE5E5,stroke:#B30000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef tier2Style fill:#E5F0FF,stroke:#003D99,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef tier3Style fill:#E5FFE5,stroke:#006600,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    class sensorA,sensorB,actuatorA tier1Style
    class preprocess,edgeModel,edgeCtrl,aggregator tier2Style
    class cloudTrain,cloudStore,dashBoard tier3Style
```

> **Reading the Diagram:** The red band is the *physical* world (Tier-1); the blue band is the *edge brain* where real-time ML happens (Tier-2); the green band is the *cloud brain* for long-horizon learning (Tier-3). Arrows represent data and model-parameter flow. Note the *bidirectional* link between aggregator and cloud training — this is the hallmark of **federated learning**.

### 4.3 Decision Flow for Computation Offloading

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA["NEW INFERENCE TASK ARRIVES AT EDGE NODE"] --> checkDeadline{"IS LATENCY BELOW DEADLINE T"]}
    checkDeadline -->|NO MUST OFFLOAD| txUp["TRANSMIT RAW DATA D TO EDGE SERVER"]
    checkDeadline -->|YES LOCAL POSSIBLE| computeLocal["EXECUTE LOCALLY ON CPU OR NPU"]
    computeLocal --> checkEnergyLocal{"IS ENERGY BELOW BATTERY BUDGET B"]}
    checkEnergyLocal -->|YES| outputLocal["RETURN LOCAL PREDICTION TO ACTUATOR"]
    checkEnergyLocal -->|NO OFFLOAD| txUp
    txUp --> edgeCompute["EDGE SERVER EXECUTES ML MODEL"]
    edgeCompute --> txDown["DOWNLOAD RESULT PAYLOAD"]
    txDown --> outputEdge["RETURN EDGE PREDICTION TO ACTUATOR"]
    outputLocal --> doneA["END INFERENCE LOOP"]
    outputEdge --> doneA
```

> **Engineering Take-away:** The offloading decision is a *bivariate* test on (latency, energy). A naive "always local" or "always cloud" policy is sub-optimal — the diagram encodes the formally derived condition from Section 3.1.

### 4.4 IoT–CPS Integration Reference Stack (Layered View)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L5["LAYER 5 APPLICATION AND DASHBOARDS"]
    L4["LAYER 4 ML AND ANALYTICS EDGE INFERENCE FEDERATED LEARNING"]
    L3["LAYER 3 MIDDLEWARE AND DATA INGESTION MQTT KAFKA OPC UA"]
    L2["LAYER 2 COMMUNICATION 5G WIFI 6 LORA BLETSCHAN"]
    L1["LAYER 1 SENSORS AND ACTUATORS MEMS LIDAR IMU MOTORDRIVERS"]
    L5 --> L4 --> L3 --> L2 --> L1
```

> This stack is the de-facto reference model used in industry (Industrial Internet Consortium, RAMI 4.0). It directly maps to KTU's ML-for-CPS syllabus expectations.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]`**
*Differentiate between Cloud Computing and Edge Computing in the context of Cyber-Physical Systems. Mention any two metrics where Edge Computing outperforms Cloud Computing.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Criterion | Cloud Computing | Edge Computing |
|:----------|:----------------|:---------------|
| Location of compute | Centralised, distant data centre | Distributed, near the sensor/actuator |
| Latency | 50 – 500 ms | **1 – 10 ms** (100× lower) |
| Bandwidth use | High (raw data streamed up) | Low (only summaries uplinked) |
| Privacy | Data leaves premises | Data stays local |
| Failure mode | Single point of failure | Graceful degradation |

**Two metrics where Edge wins: (i) Latency**, **(ii) Bandwidth efficiency / Energy per inference.** *(1 mark for the table, 1 mark per metric justification.)*

---

**Q2. `[KTU University Exam – Jul 2024, Sessional II]`**
*What is TinyML? List two major challenges in deploying machine-learning models on resource-constrained edge devices used in CPS.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **TinyML** is the discipline of running machine-learning inference on **ultra-low-power microcontrollers** (typically < 1 MB RAM, < 1 mW power) — for example, detecting motor bearing faults directly on an ARM Cortex-M0+ attached to the motor housing.

**Two deployment challenges:**
1. **Model compression vs. accuracy trade-off** — aggressive quantisation (FP32 → INT8 → INT4) and pruning shrink model size but degrade accuracy; the edge ML engineer must locate the knee-point of this trade-off.
2. **Memory footprint** — even a "small" CNN may exceed the 256 KB RAM of an MCU; techniques like *layer fusion*, *operator reordering*, and *CMSIS-NN* kernels are required.
3. *(Optional third)* **Power budget** — battery-powered edge nodes must last years; every mJ per inference counts.

*(1 mark for definition + 1 mark each for two challenges.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> - Always draw the **layered/three-tier diagram** with **arrows showing data flow direction** — examiners specifically check this for **2 marks** out of 14.
> - When asked to "explain challenges," students who give only *generic* statements ("security is important") lose **2–3 marks**. Always tie each challenge to a **concrete CPS example** (e.g., adversarial attack on a vision-based robot).
> - For derivation questions, **state the assumption first**, then derive — partial-credit-friendly examiners reward a clearly written assumption line.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Expected Pattern]*

**(a)** Explain the **three-tier architecture** of Edge Computing in IoT-enabled Cyber-Physical Systems with a neat functional block diagram. Discuss the role of each tier with suitable CPS examples.

**(b)** Discuss the **key challenges** of integrating IoT with CPS at the edge, focusing on: *(i) Latency and real-time constraints, (ii) Security and adversarial robustness, (iii) Energy and thermal constraints.* Suggest one mitigation strategy for each.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) — 7 marks**

The three-tier architecture (as already rendered in Section 4.2) consists of:

1. **Tier 1 — Physical/Sensing Layer (2 marks):** Comprises heterogeneous IoT sensors (temperature, vibration, camera, LiDAR) and actuators (motors, valves, brakes). Example: vibration sensor on a CNC spindle.

2. **Tier 2 — Edge Intelligence Layer (3 marks):** Hosts the pre-processing pipeline, the compressed ML inference engine, the real-time controller, and the FedAvg aggregator. Example: NVIDIA Jetson Nano running INT8-quantised YOLO for defect detection on a conveyor line at 25 FPS.

3. **Tier 3 — Cloud Intelligence Layer (2 marks):** Performs heavy training, stores long-horizon telemetry, and serves dashboards. Example: AWS SageMaker retraining a foundation model weekly using aggregated edge gradients.

**(b) — 7 marks**

| # | Challenge | CPS Example | Mitigation Strategy |
|:-:|:----------|:------------|:--------------------|
| (i) | **Real-time latency** (< 10 ms deadline for safety loops) | Autonomous emergency braking must decide within **50 ms** | Use **deterministic edge TPU + model pruning** to keep inference < 5 ms |
| (ii) | **Adversarial security** (adversarial patches fool vision models) | Adversarial sticker on a stop sign fools autonomous-vehicle perception | Apply **adversarial training** + **input-sanitisation filters** at the edge |
| (iii) | **Energy & thermal** (battery + passive cooling only) | Wearable ECG monitor must run 7 days on a 200 mAh battery | Use **event-driven inference** (only wake on irregular heartbeat) + INT4 quantisation |

*(Allocate ~2 marks per challenge row, 1 mark for example, 1 mark for mitigation.)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice*

**(a)** Explain **Federated Learning (FL)** in edge computing for CPS. Derive the **global aggregation formula** with proper notation and discuss how it preserves data privacy.

**(b)** Discuss the following **future trends** in edge–IoT integration for CPS: *(i) TinyML and on-device foundation models, (ii) 5G/6G ultra-reliable low-latency communication (URLLC), (iii) Digital twins at the edge, (iv) Quantum-edge hybrid computing.* For each trend, give one concrete CPS application.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) — 7 marks**

**Federated Learning (FL)** is a distributed ML paradigm where multiple edge devices collaboratively train a shared model **without ever uploading raw local data** to a central server. Only **model updates (gradients or weights)** are transmitted, preserving privacy.

**Derivation of the aggregation formula (3 marks):**

Suppose $K$ edge devices participate. Device $k$ trains on $n_k$ samples; total dataset size $N = \sum_{k=1}^{K} n_k$. After $E$ local SGD epochs, device $k$ reports its updated weights $w_{t+1}^{(k)}$.

The server minimises a *weighted* global loss:

$$
L_{global}(w) \;=\; \sum_{k=1}^{K} \frac{n_k}{N} \, L_k(w)
$$

Taking the gradient and setting it to zero (optimal $w$):

$$
\nabla L_{global}(w^{\star}) \;=\; \sum_{k=1}^{K} \frac{n_k}{N} \, \nabla L_k(w^{\star}) \;=\; 0
$$

The FedAvg update rule (a stochastic approximation) is:

$$
\boxed{\;w_{t+1}^{(global)} \;=\; \sum_{k=1}^{K} \frac{n_k}{N} \, w_{t+1}^{(k)}\;}
$$

**Privacy-preserving properties (2 marks):**
- Raw sensor data (e.g., patient ECG, vehicle telemetry) **never leaves the device**.
- Only model *deltas* $\Delta w = w_{t+1}^{(k)} - w_t$ are uploaded, which can further be protected with **differential privacy noise** or **homomorphic encryption**.
- Aligns with GDPR, HIPAA, and India's DPDP Act 2023.

**Engineering example (2 marks):** Google Gboard next-word prediction across millions of Android phones uses FedAvg; in CPS, Siemens uses federated learning across factory PLCs to learn shared fault signatures without exposing proprietary process data.

---

**(b) — 7 marks**

| # | Future Trend | Concrete CPS Application |
|:-:|:-------------|:-------------------------|
| (i) | **TinyML + on-device foundation models** | An ARM Cortex-M85 running a 50 KB quantised transformer for *anomaly detection* on a wind-turbine gearbox — no cloud needed |
| (ii) | **5G/6G URLLC** | Remote robotic surgery with **< 1 ms** air-interface latency over a private 5G network |
| (iii) | **Edge digital twins** | A real-time digital twin of a city water-distribution network running on municipal edge servers, ingesting SCADA telemetry and predicting pipe bursts 24 h in advance |
| (iv) | **Quantum-edge hybrid** | Quantum kernel estimation running on a *quantum annealer at the edge* to optimise robot-trajectory planning for warehouse automation, while classical edge CPU handles motion control |

*(Allocate ~1.5 marks per trend: 0.5 for definition, 0.5 for CPS example, 0.5 for engineering significance.)*

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Print this and tape it to your wall:**

- **Edge Computing** = compute *near* the sensor, not at a distant cloud; reduces **latency, bandwidth, energy**.
- **Three tiers**: Sensor/Actuator → Edge (ML inference) → Cloud (training & analytics).
- **Key Formulas to Memorise**:
  - $T_{total} = T_{comp} + T_{tx} + T_{prop} + T_{queue}$
  - $E_{dyn} = \alpha C f^{2} V_{dd}^{2}$
  - $w_{global} = \sum (n_k / N) \, w_k$ (FedAvg — *weighted* by sample count)
- **Offloading decision**: offload *iff* $T_{edge} < T_{local}$ **AND** $E_{edge} < E_{local}$.
- **TinyML** = ML on MCUs < 1 mW, < 1 MB RAM; trade-offs are *model size ↔ accuracy*.
- **Major Challenges**: latency, bandwidth, energy, security (adversarial attacks), privacy (GDPR/DPDP), heterogeneity (different MCUs/Gateways).
- **Future Trends**: TinyML, 5G/6G URLLC, Edge Digital Twins, Quantum–Edge Hybrids, Neuromorphic Chips (Loihi, Akida).
- **Exam Pitfalls**:
  1. Always draw the **three-tier diagram** with **directional arrows**.
  2. Never use a **plain arithmetic mean** in FedAvg — examiners deduct marks; always write the *sample-weighted* formula.
  3. When discussing "challenges," **tie each challenge to a CPS example** — generic statements earn 0.
  4. Avoid writing `|x|` in markdown tables during online exams; use $\vert x \vert$ instead.
- **One-line Mantra**: *"Bring intelligence to the data, not data to the intelligence."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Security and Privacy Concerns in CPS
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT635 - MACHINE LEARNING FOR CPS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Security and Privacy Concerns in CPS

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Cyber-Physical System (CPS) Security** is the disciplined, multi-layered engineering paradigm concerned with protecting the confidentiality, integrity, availability, and authenticity of computation, communication, and physical control processes that tightly couple cyber (computational/communication) and physical (sensors, actuators, control loops) components.

**Privacy in CPS** refers to the controlled, lawful, and ethical handling of personally identifiable, behavioral, locational, and operational data generated, transmitted, and processed by sensor-rich CPS, ensuring that individuals, organizations, and infrastructure assets are not exposed to unauthorized inference, profiling, or surveillance.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Anchor:** A CPS is *secure* when it preserves the three classical security triad extended for cyber-physical interactions — **CIA + Physical Safety** (Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability, plus Safety/Authenticity). Privacy is the orthogonal concern of *data subject protection* under sensing, learning, and actuation.

### 1.2 The Four Pillars of CPS Security

| Pillar | Cyber Concern | Physical Concern |
|---|---|---|
| **Confidentiality** | Encrypted data at rest/in transit | Sensor readings hidden from eavesdroppers |
| **Integrity** | Untampered code & ML models | Actuator commands verified against intended set-points |
| **Availability** | Resilient networks, DoS resistance | Real-time control deadlines (e.g., 1 ms in smart grids) |
| **Authenticity/Safety** | Trusted firmware, identity proofs | Verified state-estimation, safe-fail control |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Smart Hospital ICU"

Imagine a hospital **Intensive Care Unit (ICU)** as a CPS:

- **Cyber side:** Heart-rate monitor, infusion pump firmware, the doctor's tablet streaming patient vitals over Wi-Fi.
- **Physical side:** The actual patient, the IV drip, the ventilator.

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "The ICU Patient"**
> - A *security breach* is like a hacker changing the IV drip rate from 30 ml/h to 300 ml/h → **integrity violation with physical harm**.
> - A *privacy breach* is like an attacker reading the patient's HIV status from the network traffic → **confidentiality violation**.
> - A *denial-of-service* is like jamming the Wi-Fi so the monitor goes blank while the patient crashes → **availability violation**.

Just as a hospital uses *access cards (authentication)*, *encrypted medical records (confidentiality)*, and *backup generators (availability)*, a CPS needs a similar layered defense — but at **millisecond speeds** and with **safety-critical consequences**.

## 1.4 Why Security & Privacy in CPS is *Harder* than Classical IT Security

1. **Safety-Critical Real-Time Constraints:** A 200 ms delay in authentication is acceptable for e-mail but fatal in autonomous braking.
2. **Convergence of Attack Surfaces:** Attackers can move from cyber layer → control layer → physical destruction (e.g., Stuxnet centrifuges).
3. **Resource-Constrained Devices:** Many sensors/actuators cannot run heavy cryptography (AES-256) or full ML models.
4. **Long Operational Lifetimes:** A smart-grid sensor may be deployed for 20+ years — far longer than the cryptographic shelf life of today's algorithms.
5. **ML as Both Shield and Sword:** Machine Learning detects attacks but is itself vulnerable to *adversarial perturbations*.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Buzzwords to Memorize:** *Attack Surface*, *Adversarial Machine Learning*, *Differential Privacy*, *Federated Learning*, *Side-Channel Attack*, *Zero-Trust Architecture*, *Intrusion Detection System (IDS)*, *Man-in-the-Middle (MitM)*, *False Data Injection (FDI)*, *Re-identification Attack*.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The CPS Security Threat Model

A **threat model** is a structured description of *who* can attack, *what* they can touch, *how* they attack, and *why*. In CPS, the canonical threat model is the **Dolev–Yao Adversary** extended with **physical/control-layer capabilities**.

### 2.1.1 Adversary Classes (Operational Logic)

- **Step 1 — Passive Eavesdropper:** Reads sensor traffic; violates **confidentiality** and *location privacy*.
- **Step 2 — Active Network Attacker:** Tampers packets in transit; violates **integrity** and *authenticity* (MitM).
- **Step 3 — Insider / Compromised Node:** Sends false sensor readings; violates **integrity** at the data-fusion layer (False Data Injection).
- **Step 4 — Replay Attacker:** Records valid control commands and replays them; bypasses naive freshness checks.
- **Step 5 — Adversarial ML Attacker:** Crafts inputs to fool a deployed ML detector (e.g., evasive malware).
- **Step 6 — Physical / Side-Channel Attacker:** Measures power, EM emanations, or timing; extracts cryptographic keys.

### 2.1.2 The "Why" Behind Each Layer

| Layer | Why it matters in CPS | Real-world incident |
|---|---|---|
| Sensing | Sensor spoofing breaks state-estimation | GPS spoofing of ships (Black Sea, 2017) |
| Communication | Wireless links are broadcast media | Mirai botnet DDoS on Dyn (2016) |
| Computation | Compromised PLCs execute attacker code | Stuxnet (Natanz, 2010) |
| Control | Bad set-points cause physical damage | Ukrainian power-grid hack (2015) |
| Actuation | Motors, valves directly hurt people | Triton/Trisis SIS attack (2017) |

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **All formulas below are high-yield for KTU 2024 ESE — memorize the units, boundary conditions, and interpretation.**

| # | Concept | Formula / Definition | Variables & Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Risk Magnitude** | $R = T \times V \times I$ | $T$ = Threat likelihood (0–1), $V$ = Vulnerability (0–1), $I$ = Impact (qualitative or 0–1) | Foundation of CVSS-style risk scoring |
| 2 | **Shannon Entropy** | $H(X) = -\sum_{i} p_i \log_2 p_i$ | bits | Higher entropy ⇒ more unpredictable ⇒ stronger cryptographic key |
| 3 | **Mutual Information (Privacy Leakage)** | $I(X;Y) = H(X) - H(X \vert Y)$ | bits | Quantifies how much observing $Y$ reveals about $X$ |
| 4 | **Differential Privacy (ε-DP)** | $\Pr[\mathcal{M}(D) \in S] \le e^{\varepsilon}\Pr[\mathcal{M}(D') \in S]$ | $\varepsilon$ = privacy budget (small ⇒ stronger privacy) | $D, D'$ differ in one record |
| 5 | **Laplace Mechanism** | $\mathcal{M}(D) = f(D) + \text{Lap}\!\left(\frac{\Delta f}{\varepsilon}\right)$ | $\Delta f$ = sensitivity (L1) of query $f$ | Adds noise $\sim \text{Laplace}(0, b)$ with $b = \Delta f/\varepsilon$ |
| 6 | **Gaussian Mechanism** | $\mathcal{M}(D) = f(D) + \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2)$ | $\sigma^2$ chosen for $(\varepsilon,\delta)$-DP | Used when $\Delta f$ is L2-norm |
| 7 | **FGSM Adversarial Perturbation** | $x^{\text{adv}} = x + \varepsilon\,\text{sign}\!\left(\nabla_x J(\theta, x, y)\right)$ | $\varepsilon$ = max allowed perturbation | One-step gradient sign attack |
| 8 | **PGD / Iterative Attack** | $x_{t+1} = \Pi_{B_\varepsilon(x)}\!\left(x_t + \alpha\,\text{sign}\!\left(\nabla_x J\right)\right)$ | $\alpha$ = step size, $\Pi$ = projection onto $\ell_\infty$ ball | Stronger multi-step variant |
| 9 | **HMAC Authentication** | $\text{HMAC}(K, m) = H\!\left((K \oplus \text{opad}) \,\|\, H((K \oplus \text{ipad})\| m)\right)$ | $K$ = secret key, $H$ = hash | Lightweight authentication for CPS nodes |
| 10 | **False Data Injection (FDI) Detection Statistic** | $r = \vert y - H\hat{x}\vert_2$ | $y$ = measurement, $H$ = Jacobian | Chi-squared test: $r^2 \sim \chi^2_m$ under no attack |
| 11 | **Authentication Latency Budget** | $t_{\text{auth}} \le t_{\text{control deadline}}$ | seconds / ms | Safety-critical: e.g., 5 ms for vehicle V2X |
| 12 | **K-anonymity** | Every record indistinguishable from at least $k-1$ others | $k$ integer $\ge 1$ | Building block of privacy-preserving data publishing |

> **CRITICAL FORMATTING RULE APPLIED:** The vertical pipe `|` inside math expressions (e.g., $|x|$, $\vert D \vert$) has been replaced with `\vert` or `\mid` to keep markdown table integrity intact.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Smart Grids:** FDI detectors using the $\chi^2$ statistic protect state estimation from malicious meter readings.
- **Autonomous Vehicles:** FGSM-trained adversarial robustness testing ensures that road-sign classifiers cannot be fooled by stickers.
- **Healthcare Wearables:** Differential-privacy federated learning lets hospitals train models across patients without exposing raw ECG data.
- **Industrial IoT:** HMAC-SHA256 tags on every Modbus/TCP frame prevent command injection in SCADA systems.
- **Smart Cities:** $k$-anonymity + DP on transit-card data prevents re-identification of commuters from sparse location traces.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This section is **derivation-heavy** as required by the KTU 2024 OBE framework for PECPT635 (ML for CPS). Every algebraic step is shown explicitly — no logical gaps.

## 3.1 Derivation: Laplace Mechanism Noise Scale for ε-Differential Privacy

We want to add the *minimum* amount of noise that still guarantees **ε-differential privacy** for a query $f: \mathcal{D} \to \mathbb{R}^d$ with finite L1-sensitivity:

$$
\Delta f = \max_{D, D' : d(D,D') = 1} \lVert f(D) - f(D') \rVert_1
$$

**Step 1 — Statement of requirement.**
A randomized mechanism $\mathcal{M}$ satisfies ε-DP iff for all neighboring datasets $D, D'$ and all measurable $S$:

$$
\Pr[\mathcal{M}(D) \in S] \;\le\; e^{\varepsilon}\,\Pr[\mathcal{M}(D') \in S]
$$

**Step 2 — Choose the noise distribution.**
We pick the Laplace distribution with pdf:

$$
p(z \mid b) = \frac{1}{2b}\exp\!\left(-\frac{\vert z \vert}{b}\right), \quad b > 0
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the ratio for an output $z \in S$.**

$$
\frac{p(\mathcal{M}(D) = z)}{p(\mathcal{M}(D') = z)} \;=\; \frac{\exp\!\left(-\frac{\vert z - f(D) \vert}{b}\right)}{\exp\!\left(-\frac{\vert z - f(D') \vert}{b}\right)}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply the reverse triangle inequality.**

$$
\vert z - f(D) \vert \;\le\; \vert z - f(D') \vert + \vert f(D') - f(D) \vert
$$

$$
\Rightarrow\; \frac{\vert z - f(D) \vert - \vert z - f(D') \vert}{b} \;\le\; \frac{\vert f(D) - f(D') \vert}{b} \;\le\; \frac{\Delta f}{b}
$$

**Step 5 — Bound the ratio.**

$$
\frac{p(\mathcal{M}(D) = z)}{p(\mathcal{M}(D') = z)} \;\le\; \exp\!\left(\frac{\Delta f}{b}\right)
$$

**Step 6 — Match to the ε-DP requirement.**

$$
\exp\!\left(\frac{\Delta f}{b}\right) \;\le\; \exp(\varepsilon) \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; b \;\ge\; \frac{\Delta f}{\varepsilon}
$$

**Step 7 — Final closed-form result.**

$$
\boxed{\;\mathcal{M}(D) \;=\; f(D) \;+\; \text{Lap}\!\left(\frac{\Delta f}{\varepsilon}\right)\;}
$$

**Engineering interpretation:** Smaller $\varepsilon$ ⇒ more noise ⇒ better privacy but worse utility. The privacy budget $\varepsilon$ is *consumed* across queries and cannot be reset without exposing more data.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: FGSM Perturbation Bound under an $\ell_\infty$ Constraint

Consider a classifier with loss $J(\theta, x, y)$. We seek the **maximum change in loss** subject to:

$$
\lVert \delta \rVert_\infty \;\le\; \varepsilon
$$

**Step 1 — First-order Taylor expansion of loss.**

$$
J(\theta, x + \delta, y) \;\approx\; J(\theta, x, y) \;+\; \delta^{\top}\nabla_x J(\theta, x, y)
$$

**Step 2 — We want to *maximize* the increase.** This is a linear optimization in $\delta$.

$$
\max_{\lVert \delta \rVert_\infty \le \varepsilon}\; \delta^{\top}\nabla_x J
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the dual norm property.** The dual of $\lVert \cdot \rVert_\infty$ is $\lVert \cdot \rVert_1$, so the maximum is achieved when $\delta$ aligns in sign with each component of the gradient:

$$
\delta_i = \varepsilon \cdot \text{sign}\!\left(\nabla_x J(\theta, x, y)_i\right)
$$

**Step 4 — Final adversarial example.**

$$
\boxed{\;x^{\text{adv}} \;=\; x \;+\; \varepsilon\cdot \text{sign}\!\left(\nabla_x J(\theta, x, y)\right)\;}
$$

**Step 5 — Why the sign?** Because the **$\ell_\infty$ ball** constrains *each coordinate independently* — making sign the optimal direction regardless of gradient magnitude.

**Engineering interpretation:** An attacker with a budget of $\varepsilon = 8/255$ per pixel can flip classification of a stop-sign detector from "Stop" to "Speed Limit 60" by changing < 3% of each pixel's value — invisible to humans, fatal to autonomous cars.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Risk Score from CVSS-Style Triad

For a CPS node with threat likelihood $T$, vulnerability $V$, impact $I \in [0,1]$:

$$
R \;=\; T \times V \times I
$$

**Step 1 — Normalize** each variable to $[0,1]$ (e.g., $T = 0.8$ for an internet-exposed PLC).

**Step 2 — Compute** (example): $R = 0.8 \times 0.6 \times 0.9 = 0.432$.

**Step 3 — Categorize:**

$$
R < 0.2 \Rightarrow \text{Low}, \quad 0.2 \le R < 0.5 \Rightarrow \text{Medium}, \quad R \ge 0.5 \Rightarrow \text{High}
$$

**Step 4 — Iterate** over all (asset, threat) pairs and prioritize remediation by descending $R$.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Mapping Threats → Defenses → ML Methods

| Threat | Mathematical Indicator | Classical Defense | ML-based Enhancement |
|---|---|---|---|
| False Data Injection | $\chi^2$ residual $> \tau$ | State estimation filters | LSTM-based anomaly detection |
| DDoS / DoS | Traffic entropy drop | Rate limiting, queues | Autoencoder traffic fingerprints |
| Replay Attack | Timestamp skew | Nonce + freshness | Sequence-to-sequence detectors |
| Adversarial Misclassification | $\lVert \delta \rVert_\infty \le \varepsilon$ | Input sanitization | Adversarial training, defensive distillation |
| Side-Channel Leakage | $I(X_{\text{key}};Y_{\text{leak})}$ | Masking, constant-time code | ML-based leakage profiling |
| Privacy Inference | $I(\text{User}; \text{Public Data})$ | $k$-anonymity | DP + Federated learning |
| Insider Threat | Behavioral drift | RBAC, separation of duties | User-behavior analytics (UBA) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model]** *Differentiate between CPS security and CPS privacy. Give one real-world example for each.*

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> - **CPS Security** is the protection of computation, communication, and control processes from adversarial actions that compromise *confidentiality, integrity, availability, or safety* of the system. **Example:** An attacker injecting false torque commands into an industrial robot to damage a gearbox — an *integrity/safety* violation.
> - **CPS Privacy** is the protection of *personally identifiable or behavioral data* generated by sensors in a CPS from unauthorized inference, profiling, or re-identification. **Example:** Inferring a patient's medical condition from raw accelerometer data on a fitness wearable — a *confidentiality/inference* violation.
> - **Key distinction (1 mark):** Security is about *protecting the system*; privacy is about *protecting the data subject*. They overlap (e.g., encryption serves both) but are not identical.

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam - Jun 2024 Model]** *List and briefly explain three categories of attacks on Machine Learning models deployed in CPS.*

> **Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark each):**
> 1. **Evasion (Adversarial) Attacks** — Attacker crafts imperceptible perturbations to fool an inference model. *Example:* FGSM attack on a road-sign classifier in an autonomous vehicle.
> 2. **Poisoning Attacks** — Attacker injects malicious samples into the training set so the learned model behaves wrongly. *Example:* Backdoored traffic-classifier in a smart-grid IDS.
> 3. **Model Inversion / Inference Attacks** — Attacker reconstructs private training data from model parameters or outputs. *Example:* Reconstructing a patient's ECG from a deployed arrhythmia model.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **Differential Privacy (DP)** framework for protecting data in CPS. Derive the noise scale required for the **Laplace mechanism** to achieve ε-DP for a query of sensitivity $\Delta f$.

**(b) [7 Marks)** Describe how **Federated Learning (FL)** preserves privacy in a CPS deployment. Discuss the *privacy-utility-communication* trade-off with a labelled diagram.

---

#### Model Solution

**(a) Differential Privacy & Laplace Mechanism Derivation**

Differential privacy is a *mathematical guarantee* that the inclusion or exclusion of any single record in a dataset causes only a bounded change in the output distribution of a query.

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Formal definition. A randomized mechanism $\mathcal{M}$ satisfies ε-differential privacy if for all neighboring datasets $D$ and $D'$ (differing in exactly one record) and all measurable subsets $S$ of outputs:

$$
\Pr[\mathcal{M}(D) \in S] \;\le\; e^{\varepsilon}\Pr[\mathcal{M}(D') \in S]
$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Sensitivity definition.

$$
\Delta f \;=\; \max_{D, D'\,:\,d(D,D')=1} \lVert f(D) - f(D') \rVert_1
$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Laplace distribution choice. We add noise $\eta \sim \text{Lap}(0, b)$ with pdf $\frac{1}{2b}\exp(-\vert \eta \vert / b)$.

**Step 4 (2 marks):** Ratio computation and reverse triangle inequality.

$$
\frac{p(f(D) + \eta)}{p(f(D') + \eta)} \;=\; \exp\!\left(\frac{\vert \eta - (f(D) - f(D')) \vert - \vert \eta \vert}{b}\right) \;\le\; \exp\!\left(\frac{\Delta f}{b}\right)
$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Set this bound $\le e^{\varepsilon}$, giving:

$$
\boxed{\,b \;\ge\; \frac{\Delta f}{\varepsilon}\,} \quad\Rightarrow\quad \mathcal{M}(D) = f(D) + \text{Lap}\!\left(\frac{\Delta f}{\varepsilon}\right)
$$

**(b) Federated Learning for Privacy-Preserving CPS**

**Step 1 (2 marks):** Definition. Federated Learning (FL) is a distributed ML paradigm in which *multiple CPS edge nodes* (e.g., smart meters, vehicles, wearables) train local models on their private data and periodically send *only model updates* (gradients/weights) to a central aggregator. Raw data never leaves the device.

**Step 2 (2 marks):** The FL training loop (refer to §4.4 diagram):
- Local training on each node $k$ with loss $J_k(\theta)$.
- Upload $\Delta \theta_k$ to server.
- Aggregation: $\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \eta \sum_{k=1}^{K} \frac{n_k}{N}\Delta\theta_k$ (FedAvg).
- Broadcast updated $\theta_{t+1}$ to all nodes.

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Privacy benefits — raw sensor data (e.g., patient ECG, household energy profile) never leaves the local CPS node, eliminating many leak vectors.

**Step 4 (2 marks):** Trade-off analysis:
- *Privacy* ⇧: better when updates are noisy (DP) or aggregated securely (Secure Aggregation).
- *Utility* ⇩: more noise ⇒ lower model accuracy.
- *Communication* ⇩: gradient compression and partial participation reduce bandwidth, at the cost of convergence speed.

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Open challenges — gradient leakage attacks, system heterogeneity, Byzantine robustness.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - Jun 2023]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **threat model** for a Cyber-Physical System. Classify the attack surfaces across the *sensing, communication, computation, and control* layers with one example per layer.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain **False Data Injection (FDI) attacks** against state estimation in a smart grid. Show mathematically how a $\chi^2$-detector can be defeated by an *attack vector $a = Hc$* lying in the column space of the Jacobian $H$.

---

#### Model Solution

**(a) Threat Model & Attack Surfaces**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Definition. A CPS threat model is a structured specification of adversary *capabilities, goals, and access*. The Dolev–Yao model is typically extended with *control-loop awareness* (e.g., the attacker knows the system dynamics $x_{t+1} = Ax_t + Bu_t$).

**Step 2 (1 mark):** The five adversary classes — passive, active, insider, physical, adversarial-ML (recall §2.1.1).

**Step 3 (3 marks):** Attack-surface classification:

| Layer | Attack Example | Consequence |
|---|---|---|
| Sensing | GPS spoofing of a drone | Wrong navigation, crash |
| Communication | MitM on Modbus/TCP | Injected SCADA commands |
| Computation | Stuxnet reprogramming PLCs | Centrifuge destruction |
| Control | Bad set-point in chemical plant | Pressure overload, explosion |
| Actuation | Reverse motor direction in CNC | Workpiece/tool damage |

**Step 4 (2 marks):** Counter-measure mapping — defence-in-depth: authentication (HMAC), encryption (TLS), FDI detection ($\chi^2$), ML-based IDS, safe-fail control, physical interlocks.

**(b) False Data Injection & Defeat of $\chi^2$ Detector**

**Step 1 (2 marks):** Setup. The smart-grid state estimator solves the DC power-flow:

$$
z \;=\; Hx + e
$$

where $z \in \mathbb{R}^m$ are meter readings, $x \in \mathbb{R}^n$ is the state vector (voltage angles), $H$ is the Jacobian, $e$ is Gaussian noise.

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Standard bad-data detection:

$$
\hat{x} = (H^\top H)^{-1}H^\top z, \quad r = \lVert z - H\hat{x} \rVert_2, \quad r^2 \sim \chi^2_{m-n}
$$

If $r^2 > \tau$, alarm is raised.

**Step 3 (2 marks):** The attack. The adversary injects $z_{\text{bad}} = z + a$ where $a = Hc$ for some *arbitrary* $c \in \mathbb{R}^n$. The estimator now sees:

$$
\hat{x}_{\text{bad}} = \hat{x} + c
$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Residual remains unchanged:

$$
r_{\text{bad}} = \lVert (z + Hc) - H(\hat{x} + c) \rVert_2 = \lVert z - H\hat{x} \rVert_2 = r
$$

The $\chi^2$ detector is **completely bypassed** — the attack is *unobservable*.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls (Both Questions):**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse $\chi^2$ detection failure with "no detection possible".** A $\chi^2$ *detector* fails, but *other* detectors (e.g., sparse-optimization, ML-based) can still catch the attack — say so for full marks.
> 2. **Differential Privacy derivation:** Students routinely forget the *reverse triangle inequality* step (Step 4 in §3.1). Without it, the bound is unjustified — **lose 2 marks**.
> 3. **Federated Learning:** The *gradient leakage attack* (e.g., DLG — Deep Leakage from Gradients, Zhu et al., 2019) is a known counter-example. Mention it to show awareness.
> 4. **Units & sensitivity:** Always state the units and the privacy budget interpretation. Vague answers lose the "comprehension" mark.
> 5. **FGSM / PGD:** Many students write the formula but forget to mention the $\ell_\infty$ constraint — the formula is *only valid* under that constraint.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — print this before the exam.**

- [ ] **CIA + Safety** is the security triad for CPS (not just CIA).
- [ ] **Five attack surfaces:** Sensing, Communication, Computation, Control, Actuation — *memorize one real incident per surface*.
- [ ] **Threat model = Dolev–Yao + control-loop awareness** (attacker knows $A, B$).
- [ ] **Risk formula:** $R = T \times V \times I$ — used in CVSS scoring.
- [ ] **Differential privacy** is **ε-DP** with **Laplace noise scale $\Delta f / \varepsilon$** — *write the derivation if asked*.
- [ ] **FGSM** perturbation: $x^{\text{adv}} = x + \varepsilon \cdot \text{sign}(\nabla_x J)$ — bounded by $\ell_\infty$ norm.
- [ ] **PGD** is the iterative, stronger variant; *projection step* is mandatory.
- [ ] **FDI attack on smart grid:** $a = Hc$ bypasses the $\chi^2$ detector completely.
- [ ] **Federated learning** keeps *raw data local*; only *gradients* leave the device — vulnerable to *gradient leakage* and *Byzantine* attacks.
- [ ] **Differential privacy + FL** = the gold standard for privacy-preserving ML in CPS.
- [ ] **$k$-anonymity** and **mutual information $I(X;Y)$** are foundational *privacy* metrics.
- [ ] **HMAC** is the standard lightweight authentication tag for resource-constrained CPS nodes.
- [ ] **Adversarial training** (training on FGSM/PGD examples) is the most common defense against evasion.
- [ ] **Defence-in-depth** = layered security: physical, network, application, ML-detection, control-verification, privacy.
- [ ] **Side-channel attacks** leak cryptographic keys through power, EM, or timing — even when algorithms are mathematically secure.
- [ ] **Privacy budget $\varepsilon$ is consumable** — once spent, no more queries without breaching privacy.

> **End of Module 4 — Security and Privacy Concerns in CPS**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Ethical Considerations in ML Applications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT635 - MACHINE LEARNING FOR CPS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Ethical Considerations in ML Applications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Ethical Considerations in Machine Learning (ML) Applications** refer to the systematic identification, analysis, and mitigation of moral, legal, and societal impacts arising from the design, training, deployment, and lifecycle management of ML systems. In the context of Cyber-Physical Systems (CPS) — where ML models directly influence physical actuators, healthcare devices, autonomous vehicles, and critical infrastructure — ethics transcends academic philosophy and becomes a **non-negotiable engineering constraint**.

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020 aligned)** outcome framework, ethical reasoning in ML is mapped to **Course Outcome CO5**: *"Analyze the societal, legal, and ethical implications of deploying ML models in cyber-physical environments and propose responsible AI solutions."*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (Module 4)**
> Core pillars examined: **Fairness & Bias**, **Privacy & Data Protection**, **Transparency & Explainability**, **Accountability**, **Safety & Robustness**, and **Governance & Compliance**.

### 1.2 The Five Pillars of Ethical ML

| Pillar | Engineering Concern | Failure Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Fairness** | Algorithmic discrimination across protected groups | Biased hiring tool rejecting female resumes |
| **Privacy** | Unauthorized inference from training data | Re-identifying patients from anonymized records |
| **Transparency** | Black-box decision opacity | Loan rejection with no recourse |
| **Accountability** | Unclear ownership of model outputs | Autonomous car crash liability |
| **Safety** | Adversarial and distributional robustness | Stop-sign misclassification by 1 sticker |

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition — Bias in ML**
> A systematic skew in model outputs that produces **disparate treatment** (intentional) or **disparate impact** (unintentional) against individuals or groups defined by protected attributes (race, gender, age, caste, religion, disability).

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Self-Driving Car" Thought Experiment

Imagine you are a parent teaching your teenager to drive. You would not just teach them how to operate the car — you would drill three ethical habits:

1. **"Look both ways"** → equivalent to **Fairness auditing** (look for hidden bias in data).
2. **"Don't share our home address with strangers"** → equivalent to **Privacy preservation** (don't leak training data).
3. **"Always explain why you braked suddenly"** → equivalent to **Explainability** (no black-box decisions in safety-critical systems).

An ML model deployed in a CPS — like an autonomous car, a smart insulin pump, or an industrial robotic arm — is that teenager. **It has the capability but not yet the judgment.** Ethical considerations are the "driving lessons" we embed before letting the model loose in the real world.

> [!TIP]
> **Intuition Check**: Ethical ML is *not* a post-hoc checklist. It is a **design-time constraint** — analogous to writing safety interlocks into a nuclear reactor controller, not adding fire extinguishers after commissioning.

---

### 1.4 Why Ethics is a *Hard Engineering* Problem (Not Just Philosophy)

In CPS, ML outputs drive **physical actuation**. An unethical ML model is not a "soft" social issue — it is a **physical hazard**:

- A biased medical diagnostic ML → wrong treatment → **patient harm**.
- A privacy-leaking smart meter ML → exposed household routines → **security breach**.
- An opaque ML controller in a power grid → untraceable cascading failure → **infrastructure collapse**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Board Examiner Insight (KTU 2024)** — When answering ethics questions, never frame them as "philosophical debates." Always ground them in **engineering trade-offs** (accuracy vs. fairness, utility vs. privacy, performance vs. explainability). Examiners reward quantitative reasoning over hand-waving.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Ethical ML Lifecycle — A Structured Walkthrough

The development of any ML system in a CPS must traverse the following ethical checkpoints. Each checkpoint has a **trigger condition**, a **responsible stakeholder**, and a **deliverable artefact** required for KTU 2024 Scheme continuous assessment rubrics.

- **Stage 1 — Problem Formulation**
  - *Why*: Ethical failures often originate in how a problem is *framed*, not in the algorithm.
  - *How*: Apply the **"Should we, not just can we?"** test. Refuse projects whose only purpose is surveillance or manipulation.
  - *Deliverable*: An **Ethics Impact Assessment (EIA)** document.

- **Stage 2 — Data Collection & Curation**
  - *Why*: Models inherit and amplify the biases in their training data.
  - *How*: Audit for **representational harm** (under-representation) and **historical bias** (skewed labels).
  - *Deliverable*: A **Data Sheet for Datasets** (Gebru et al., 2018).

- **Stage 3 — Model Design & Training**
  - *Why*: Loss functions optimized purely for accuracy hide subgroup errors.
  - *How*: Use **fairness-constrained optimization** and **multi-objective loss** (accuracy + fairness penalty).
  - *Deliverable*: A **Model Card** specifying intended use and known limitations.

- **Stage 4 — Validation & Pre-Deployment Audit**
  - *Why*: Aggregate accuracy masks per-group errors.
  - *How*: Disaggregate metrics across protected attributes; run **adversarial robustness tests**.
  - *Deliverable*: A **Bias & Robustness Report** with subgroup confusion matrices.

- **Stage 5 — Deployment & Monitoring**
  - *Why*: Data drift can re-introduce bias post-deployment.
  - *How*: Continuous fairness monitoring with **statistical parity drift detectors**.
  - *Deliverable*: **Incident Response Plan** with rollback triggers.

- **Stage 6 — Decommissioning**
  - *Why*: Models retain learned sensitive attributes (membership inference risk).
  - *How*: Secure data deletion, model unlearning, and stakeholder notification.
  - *Deliverable*: **End-of-Life Compliance Certificate**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Grading Note**: Questions on ethical lifecycles frequently award marks for **labelling stages correctly** and for **mapping specific harms to specific stages**. Memorize the 6 stages above.

### 2.2 Fairness Metrics — The Quantitative Core

Fairness is the *only* ethical dimension that can be rigorously measured. KTU 2024 examiners expect students to know at least the following three formulations.

Let $A$ be a protected attribute (binary: $A=0$ or $A=1$), $\hat{Y}$ the model prediction, and $Y$ the true label.

#### 2.2.1 Demographic Parity (Statistical Parity)

A classifier satisfies **demographic parity** if the positive prediction rate is equal across groups:

$$P(\hat{Y} = 1 \mid A = 0) = P(\hat{Y} = 1 \mid A = 1)$$

The **Disparate Impact Ratio (DIR)** measures how far we are from parity:

$$\text{DIR} = \frac{P(\hat{Y} = 1 \mid A = 0)}{P(\hat{Y} = 1 \mid A = 1)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **The 80% Rule (US EEOC "Four-Fifths Rule")**: A DIR below **0.8** is legally presumed to indicate **adverse impact** in employment contexts.

#### 2.2.2 Equalized Odds (Hardt et al., 2016)

A stricter criterion requiring equal **true positive rates (TPR)** and **false positive rates (FPR)** across groups:

$$P(\hat{Y} = 1 \mid Y = y, A = 0) = P(\hat{Y} = 1 \mid Y = y, A = 1), \quad \forall y \in \{0, 1\}$$

#### 2.2.3 Counterfactual Fairness (Kusner et al., 2017)

A prediction is **counterfactually fair** for an individual if it remains the same in a hypothetical world where the individual's protected attribute were different. Formally, using structural causal models (SCM) with exogenous noise $U$:

$$\hat{Y}_{A \leftarrow a}(U) = \hat{Y}_{A \leftarrow a'}(U), \quad \forall a \neq a'$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Impossibility Theorem (Chouldechova, 2017; Kleinberg et al., 2016)**: When **base rates** of the true label differ across groups, **Demographic Parity**, **Equalized Odds**, and **Predictive Parity cannot all be satisfied simultaneously** (except in trivial degenerate cases). This is a **guaranteed 3–4 mark question** in KTU Module 4. Memorize it.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Metric / Principle | Formula / Definition | Engineering Use-Case | Threshold / Constant |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Demographic Parity** | $P(\hat{Y}=1 \mid A=a)$ equal for all $a$ | Hiring, lending, advertising | DIR $\in [0.8, 1.25]$ |
| **Disparate Impact Ratio (DIR)** | $\text{DIR} = \dfrac{P(\hat{Y}=1 \mid A=0)}{P(\hat{Y}=1 \mid A=1)}$ | Legal compliance audit | $\geq 0.8$ (Four-Fifths Rule) |
| **Equalized Odds** | $P(\hat{Y}=1 \mid Y=y, A=a)$ equal for all $a, y$ | Criminal risk scoring, medical diagnosis | TPR/FPR gap $< 5\%$ |
| **Predictive Parity** | $P(Y=1 \mid \hat{Y}=1, A=a)$ equal for all $a$ | Disease screening, fraud detection | PPV gap $< 5\%$ |
| **Differential Privacy (DP)** | $\Pr[M(D) \in S] \leq e^{\varepsilon} \cdot \Pr[M(D') \in S] + \delta$ | Census, health analytics | $\varepsilon \in [0.1, 10]$ (smaller = more private) |
| **k-Anonymity** | Every record indistinguishable from $k-1$ others | De-identified datasets | $k \geq 5$ typical, $k \geq 20$ for medical |
| **LIME Local Fidelity** | $\mathcal{L}(f, g, \pi_x) = \sum_{z} \pi_x(z) \left( f(z) - g(z) \right)^2$ | Explaining any black-box model | $R^2$ of surrogate $\geq 0.7$ |
| **Adversarial Robustness** | $\min_{\delta : \Vert \delta \Vert_p \leq \epsilon} \mathcal{L}(f(x+\delta), y)$ | CPS sensor hardening | $\epsilon = 8/255$ (CIFAR benchmark) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Caution on Markdown Pipes**: In the formulas above, vertical bars $\vert$ are used (not $\vert$ characters) to prevent markdown table breakage. The condition $\Vert \delta \Vert_p \leq \epsilon$ denotes the $\ell_p$-norm ball of perturbation $\delta$.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Healthcare CPS (e.g., wearable ECG monitors)**: Equalized odds is the only acceptable criterion — a missed arrhythmia (FNR disparity) directly kills.
- **Smart Grid ML**: Differential privacy protects household consumption patterns from inferring presence-at-home.
- **Autonomous Vehicles**: Adversarial robustness against stop-sign stickers is a CPS safety requirement, not an optional add-on.
- **Smart City Surveillance**: Demographic parity fails for crime prediction (because base crime rates *do* differ geographically); predictive parity is preferred with explicit **bias-disparity** audits.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Case Study — The COMPAS Recidivism Algorithm

This is the canonical KTU Module 4 case study. Northpointe's COMPAS was used in the U.S. to predict recidivism risk. **ProPublica (2016)** found that Black defendants had higher false-positive rates than White defendants. We will quantify this using equalized odds.

#### 3.1.1 Data Setup

Let $A=1$ denote Black defendants and $A=0$ denote White defendants. Suppose for a 2-year recidivism prediction the model yields the following confusion matrices:

- **Group $A=0$ (White)**, $n=1000$:

| | Actually Re-offended ($Y=1$) | Did Not ($Y=0$) |
|---|---|---|
| Predicted High Risk ($\hat{Y}=1$) | 280 (TP) | 120 (FP) |
| Predicted Low Risk ($\hat{Y}=0$) | 120 (FN) | 480 (TN) |

- **Group $A=1$ (Black)**, $n=1000$:

| | Actually Re-offended ($Y=1$) | Did Not ($Y=0$) |
|---|---|---|
| Predicted High Risk ($\hat{Y}=1$) | 400 (TP) | 320 (FP) |
| Predicted Low Risk ($\hat{Y}=0$) | 100 (FN) | 180 (TN) |

#### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Computation of Fairness Metrics

**Step 1 — Compute the False Positive Rate (FPR) for each group.**

FPR is the probability of being *incorrectly* flagged as high-risk given the person did *not* re-offend. It is computed as:

$$\text{FPR}_A = \frac{\text{FP}}{\text{FP} + \text{TN}} = \frac{\text{FP}}{n - \text{Actual Positives}}$$

For Group $A=0$ (White):

$$\text{FPR}_{A=0} = \frac{120}{120 + 480} = \frac{120}{600} = 0.2000 \; (\text{or } 20.00\%)$$

For Group $A=1$ (Black):

$$\text{FPR}_{A=1} = \frac{320}{320 + 180} = \frac{320}{500} = 0.6400 \; (\text{or } 64.00\%)$$

**Step 2 — Compute the True Positive Rate (TPR, Sensitivity) for each group.**

TPR is the probability of being *correctly* flagged given the person *did* re-offend:

$$\text{TPR}_A = \frac{\text{TP}}{\text{TP} + \text{FN}}$$

For Group $A=0$ (White):

$$\text{TPR}_{A=0} = \frac{280}{280 + 120} = \frac{280}{400} = 0.7000 \; (\text{or } 70.00\%)$$

For Group $A=1$ (Black):

$$\text{TPR}_{A=1} = \frac{400}{400 + 100} = \frac{400}{500} = 0.8000 \; (\text{or } 80.00\%)$$

**Step 3 — Test for Demographic Parity using the Disparate Impact Ratio.**

The positive prediction rate is the column sum of predicted high-risk divided by total:

$$\text{Selection Rate}_{A=0} = \frac{280 + 120}{1000} = 0.4000$$

$$\text{Selection Rate}_{A=1} = \frac{400 + 320}{1000} = 0.7200$$

The Disparate Impact Ratio is:

$$\text{DIR} = \frac{0.4000}{0.7200} = 0.5556$$

**Step 4 — Test for Equalized Odds.**

Equalized odds require TPR and FPR to be equal across groups:

$$\Delta \text{TPR} = \text{TPR}_{A=0} - \text{TPR}_{A=1} = 0.7000 - 0.8000 = -0.1000$$

$$\Delta \text{FPR} = \text{FPR}_{A=0} - \text{FPR}_{A=1} = 0.2000 - 0.6400 = -0.4400$$

**Step 5 — Interpret the Results.**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Demographic Parity**: The DIR of **0.5556** is **below the 0.80 threshold**. Demographic parity is violated. Black defendants are flagged high-risk at **1.8× the rate** of White defendants.
> - **Equalized Odds**: $\vert \Delta \text{TPR} \vert = 0.10$ and $\vert \Delta \text{FPR} \vert = 0.44$ — both far exceed the 5% engineering tolerance. **Equalized odds is severely violated**.
> - **Conclusion**: COMPAS exhibits a **disparate harm** to Black defendants (wrongly labeling innocent people as future criminals at 3.2× the White FPR). A mitigation strategy of **post-processing threshold adjustment per group** (Hardt et al., 2016) is recommended.

#### 3.1.3 Analytical Matrix — The COMPAS Audit Table

| Fairness Metric | Value for $A=0$ | Value for $A=1$ | Gap | Threshold | Verdict |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Selection Rate** | 0.4000 | 0.7200 | 0.3200 | DIR $\geq 0.8$ | **VIOLATED** |
| **FPR** | 0.2000 | 0.6400 | 0.4400 | $\vert \Delta \vert < 0.05$ | **VIOLATED** |
| **FNR** | 0.3000 | 0.2000 | 0.1000 | $\vert \Delta \vert < 0.05$ | **VIOLATED** |
| **TPR** | 0.7000 | 0.8000 | 0.1000 | $\vert \Delta \vert < 0.05$ | **VIOLATED** |
| **PPV** | 0.7000 | 0.5556 | 0.1444 | $\vert \Delta \vert < 0.05$ | **VIOLATED** |

### 3.2 Worked Example — Differential Privacy Budget Accounting

Suppose a hospital wishes to publish aggregate patient statistics under **$\varepsilon$-Differential Privacy**. They perform three queries: query 1 ($\varepsilon_1 = 0.5$), query 2 ($\varepsilon_2 = 0.7$), query 3 ($\varepsilon_3 = 0.3$). Compute the **total privacy budget**.

**Step 1 — Apply Sequential Composition Theorem.**

The **Sequential Composition Theorem** of differential privacy states that the privacy guarantees of multiple mechanisms compose additively:

$$\varepsilon_{\text{total}} = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \varepsilon_i$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the values.**

$$\varepsilon_{\text{total}} = 0.5 + 0.7 + 0.3 = 1.5$$

**Step 3 — Interpret.**

With a total $\varepsilon_{\text{total}} = 1.5$, the hospital has consumed **moderate privacy budget**. Since strong privacy typically demands $\varepsilon \leq 1.0$ and $\varepsilon \leq 0.1$ is preferred for sensitive medical data, the hospital should:

- (a) Run fewer queries, or
- (b) Use **Parallel Composition** (queries on disjoint data subsets) which composes as $\varepsilon_{\text{total}} = \max(\varepsilon_i)$ instead of sum.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Tip**: When questions on DP appear in KTU Module 4, examiners look for the distinction between **Sequential** ($\sum \varepsilon_i$) and **Parallel** ($\max \varepsilon_i$) composition. Confusing the two is a guaranteed 2-mark loss.

### 3.3 Derived Framework — The Ethical Decision Matrix (EDM)

The EDM is a structured 4-quadrant tool for classifying an ML application's ethical risk before deployment. It scores the application on two axes:

- **Axis X (Severity)**: Reversibility of harm. $1 =$ reversible nuisance, $5 =$ loss of life.
- **Axis Y (Scale)**: Number of people affected. $1 =$ individual, $5 =$ millions.

The **Ethical Risk Score (ERS)** is:

$$\text{ERS} = S \times N$$

where $S$ is severity and $N$ is number of people affected. Higher ERS demands stronger mitigation requirements:

| ERS Range | Risk Class | Required Safeguards |
|---|---|---|
| 1 – 4 | **Low** | Model card, basic logging |
| 5 – 9 | **Moderate** | Bias audit, explainability, human-in-the-loop |
| 10 – 15 | **High** | Differential privacy, formal fairness proofs, continuous monitoring |
| 16 – 25 | **Critical** | External ethics board approval, regulatory clearance (FDA, EU AI Act Tier I) |

> [!EXAMPLE]
> **Application**: A movie recommendation system (severity 1, scale 5) → ERS = 5 → **Moderate**.
> **Application**: An autonomous vehicle pedestrian detector (severity 5, scale 4) → ERS = 20 → **Critical**.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Stage 1: Problem Formulation] --> A1[Ethics Impact Assessment EIA]
    A1 --> A2{PROBLEM APPROVED?}
    A2 -- NO --> A3[REFUSE PROJECT]
    A2 -- YES --> B0[Stage 2: Data Collection and Curation]
    B0 --> B1[Data Sheet Generation]
    B1 --> B2[Bias Audit Representational and Historical]
    B2 --> C0[Stage 3: Model Design and Training]
    C0 --> C1[Fairness Constrained Optimization]
    C1 --> C2[Adversarial Robustness Training]
    C2 --> D0[Stage 4: Validation and Pre Deployment Audit]
    D0 --> D1[Disaggregated Subgroup Metrics]
    D1 --> D2[Model Card Generation]
    D2 --> E0[Stage 5: Deployment and Monitoring]
    E0 --> E1[Continuous Fairness Drift Detection]
    E1 --> E2[Incident Response and Rollback Plan]
    E2 --> F0[Stage 6: Decommissioning]
    F0 --> F1[Secure Data Deletion]
    F1 --> F2[End of Life Compliance Certificate]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram**: Each block is an ethical checkpoint. The diamond node `A2` is the **ethical gate** — the first decision to refuse unethical problem formulations. Any path skipping Stage 4 leads to undetected bias in production.

### 4.2 Fairness Metric Relationships and the Impossibility Triangle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    M1[Demographic Parity] -- IMPLIES --> M2[Group Calibrated Rates Not Required]
    M3[Equalized Odds] -- CONFLICTS WITH --> M1
    M3 -- REQUIRES --> M4[Equal TPR and FPR across groups]
    M5[Predictive Parity] -- CONFLICTS WITH --> M3
    M5 -- REQUIRES --> M6[Equal PPV across groups]
    M1 --- CP{Impossibility Theorem Base Rates Differ}
    M3 --- CP
    M5 --- CP
    CP -- RESULT --> OUT[Cannot Satisfy All Three Simultaneously]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation**: The central node `CP` (Chouldechova-Pleit) is the **Impossibility Theorem**. When base rates differ between groups, only **two of the three** fairness criteria can hold. Choose consciously based on the *harm type* (Type I vs Type II).

### 4.3 Differential Privacy Mechanism Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    D0[Raw Dataset D] --> Q0[Query Function f]
    Q0 --> S0[Sensitivity Computation Delta f]
    S0 --> N0[Laplace or Gaussian Noise Mechanism]
    N0 --> R0[Perturbed Output M of D]
    R0 --> B0[Privacy Budget Tracker Epsilon Accumulator]
    B0 --> CHK{Epsilon within Limit?}
    CHK -- YES --> PUB[Publish Result]
    CHK -- NO --> BLOCK[Block Query and Alert]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Use**: The `B0` budget tracker is critical. Without it, repeated queries can silently drain privacy. In KTU 2024 lab exams, students are expected to *describe* such a tracker in pseudocode or block form.
